topics.title: Computational Linguistics: Definition, Scope, and History
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computational Linguistics: Definition, Scope, and History

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computational Linguistics (CL)** is the scientific and engineering discipline that applies the theoretical frameworks of linguistics, computer science, artificial intelligence, and cognitive psychology to model, analyze, represent, and process natural language (such as English, Malayalam, Hindi) using computational methods and algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Computational Linguistics is the interdisciplinary field concerned with the computational modelling of natural language, encompassing the theoretical formulation of linguistic phenomena and the engineering of practical language processing systems."*

The discipline operates on **two parallel tracks**:
1. **Theoretical Track** — Building formal, mathematically rigorous models of grammar, meaning, and discourse.
2. **Applied/Engineering Track** — Designing software systems such as **Machine Translation (MT)**, **Speech Recognition (ASR)**, **Information Retrieval (IR)**, **Sentiment Analysis**, and **Chatbots**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Polyglot Translator Bridge" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a country where humans speak only **English** (Homo Sapiens) and computers understand only **Binary** (Robotica). The two sides need to communicate, but neither knows the other's tongue. **Computational Linguistics builds the bridge** between them.
>
> - **Linguists** are the *civil engineers* — they study the blueprints (grammar, semantics, pragmatics) of the human language side.
> - **Computer Scientists** are the *steel workers* — they forge the algorithms, parsers, and statistical models of the binary side.
> - The **bridge itself** is the software system (e.g., Google Translate, Siri, ChatGPT).

In simple terms: **Computational Linguistics = Linguistics + Computer Science + Mathematics + Statistics**.

### 1.3 Distinguishing CL from Related Fields

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **CL vs. NLP (Natural Language Processing)**: In modern literature, the terms are often used interchangeably, but **CL leans more toward the theoretical and scientific study of language using computation**, while **NLP leans more toward engineering practical applications**. NLP is widely considered a *sub-discipline* or *applied branch* of CL.
> - **CL vs. Cognitive Science**: Cognitive science studies how the *human brain* processes language; CL studies how *machines* can process language.
> - **CL vs. Speech Processing**: Speech Processing focuses on the acoustic signal; CL handles the textual and symbolic layers.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Corpus (pl. Corpora)** — A large, structured collection of texts used for linguistic analysis.
- **Token** — A single unit of text (word, punctuation, sub-word).
- **Grammar** — A formal rule system describing the structure of sentences.
- **Parse Tree** — A hierarchical tree representation of sentence structure.
- **N-Gram** — A contiguous sequence of *N* items from a text sample.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Computational Linguistics

#### Pillar 1: Linguistic Theory
Computational linguistics draws its theoretical scaffolding from the layered structure of language, typically modeled as a **pyramid of linguistic levels**:

1. **Phonology / Phonetics** — The study of speech sounds. Computational phonology handles sound-to-symbol conversion.
2. **Morphology** — The structure of words (roots, prefixes, suffixes). Example: *unhappiness* → *un-* + *happy* + *-ness*.
3. **Syntax** — The rules governing sentence structure. Formalized using **Context-Free Grammars (CFGs)**.
4. **Semantics** — The meaning of words, phrases, and sentences.
5. **Pragmatics** — The meaning in context, including speaker intent and world knowledge.
6. **Discourse** — The structure of multi-sentence text and dialogue.

#### Pillar 2: Computational Methods
The discipline has progressed through **four major methodological paradigms**:

- **Symbolic / Rule-Based (1950s–1980s)**: Hand-crafted rules, logic programming (Prolog), ATN (Augmented Transition Networks), CHAT-80.
- **Statistical (1990s–2000s)**: HMMs, n-gram language models, IBM Models for MT, CRF, SVM.
- **Machine Learning / Embeddings (2010s)**: Word2Vec, GloVe, RNNs, LSTMs, early Transformers.
- **Deep Learning / Foundation Models (2017–present)**: BERT, GPT-series, T5, LLaMA, and modern Large Language Models (LLMs).

### 2.2 KTU Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following high-density table consolidates the **core concepts, definitions, and historical milestones** frequently tested in KTU 2024 ESE Module 1.

| **Concept** | **Definition / Characteristic** | **Era / Inventor** |
|---|---|---|
| Turing Test | A test of a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent, human-like conversation | **1950** — Alan Turing |
| ALPAC Report | A US government report that drastically reduced MT funding in the US | **1966** |
| SHRDLU | Early natural language program for a block-moving virtual world | **1968–1970** — Terry Winograd |
| ELIZA | First chatbot, simulating a Rogerian psychotherapist | **1966** — Joseph Weizenbaum |
| Prolog | Logic programming language widely used in early CL | **1972** — Alain Colmerauer |
| Noam Chomsky | Formalized generative grammar and the Chomsky Hierarchy | **1957** |
| HMM (Hidden Markov Model) | Statistical model for sequence labeling (e.g., POS tagging) | **1980s** — Baum, Rabiner |
| IBM Alignment Models | Statistical foundations of modern Machine Translation | **1993** — Brown et al. |
| Word2Vec | Distributed word-representation technique | **2013** — Mikolov (Google) |
| Transformer Architecture | Self-attention-based neural architecture | **2017** — Vaswani et al. |
| BERT | Bidirectional pre-trained language model | **2018** — Google |
| GPT (1/2/3/4/5) | Generative Pre-trained Transformer models | **2018–2024** — OpenAI |

### 2.3 Scope of Computational Linguistics (Sub-fields)

The scope of CL spans a wide array of sub-disciplines, each addressing a specific linguistic or engineering problem:

1. **Machine Translation (MT)** — Automatically translating text from one natural language to another (e.g., English to Malayalam).
2. **Speech Recognition (ASR)** — Converting spoken audio into text.
3. **Text-to-Speech (TTS) Synthesis** — Generating spoken language from text.
4. **Information Extraction (IE)** — Pulling structured data from unstructured text (e.g., names, dates, relations).
5. **Information Retrieval (IR)** — Building search engines and document ranking systems.
6. **Sentiment & Opinion Mining** — Detecting subjective polarity in text.
7. **Question Answering (QA)** — Building systems that directly answer natural language questions.
8. **Text Summarization** — Producing a concise summary of a longer document.
9. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — Identifying proper nouns and classifying them (person, location, organization).
10. **Corpus Linguistics** — Building and statistically analyzing large text databases.
11. **Discourse & Dialogue Systems** — Modeling multi-turn conversations.
12. **Lexicography** — Computational generation of dictionaries and thesauri.
13. **Computational Morphology** — Algorithmic study of word formation (especially important for **Dravidian languages** like Malayalam).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the context of **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education**, the application of CL in industry is directly tied to **CO1 (Foundations of Language Computing)** and **CO2 (Algorithmic Approaches to Language Processing)**. Real-world deployments include:
>
> - **Healthcare**: Clinical NLP for Electronic Health Records (EHRs).
> - **Legal Tech**: Automated contract analysis and e-discovery.
> - **EdTech**: Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS) and automated essay grading.
> - **Indian Language Computing**: Indic-specific CL for Hindi, Tamil, **Malayalam**, Bengali — directly relevant to India's **National Language Translation Mission (NLTM)** and **Bhashini** platform.

### 2.5 The Chomsky Hierarchy (Theoretical Backbone)

The theoretical depth of CL is largely anchored in **Noam Chomsky's formal language hierarchy**, which classifies grammars by their generative power:

| **Grammar Type** | **Automaton** | **Language Class** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type 0 — Unrestricted | Turing Machine | Recursively Enumerable | $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ |
| Type 1 — Context-Sensitive | Linear Bounded Automaton | Context-Sensitive | $a^n b^n c^n$ |
| Type 2 — Context-Free | Pushdown Automaton | Context-Free | $a^n b^n$ |
| Type 3 — Regular | Finite State Automaton | Regular | $a^* b^*$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Most programming languages are **Context-Free**, but **natural languages** require at least **Context-Sensitive** (and arguably Type 0) grammars to capture cross-serial dependencies and long-distance agreement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Conceptual Framework Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Complexity Note:** As this is an introductory Module 1 topic, the derivation below is a **conceptual and historical-logical derivation** rather than a pure mathematical one. It traces the evolution of CL from first principles to the modern era, mirroring the analytical depth expected in KTU's 14-mark questions.

### 3.1 The Conceptual Derivation: From Human Language to Machine-Processable Form

We can derive the computational problem of linguistics through the following logical chain:

**Step 1: The Observed Phenomenon**
Human language is a *natural*, *ambiguous*, *context-dependent*, and *evolving* system.
A single sentence like *"I saw the man with the telescope"* has **two valid syntactic interpretations** (prepositional-phrase attachment ambiguity).

**Step 2: The Need for Formalization**
To process language computationally, we must convert this *messy natural signal* into a *rigorous formal system*.

A formal language $L$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is defined as any subset of $\Sigma^*$, the set of all finite strings.

$$L \subseteq \Sigma^*$$

**Step 3: The Choice of Formalism (Grammar)**
We must select a generative device. Per the **Chomsky Hierarchy**, the more powerful the grammar, the more expensive the parser.

Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$, where:
- $V$ = non-terminal symbols
- $T$ = terminal symbols
- $P$ = production rules
- $S$ = start symbol

**Step 4: The Parsing Problem**
Given a string $w \in \Sigma^*$, decide whether $w \in L(G)$. This is the **membership problem**.

For Context-Free Grammars (CFGs):

$$w \in L(G) \iff S \Rightarrow^* w$$

The **CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) algorithm** solves this in:

$$O(\vert w \vert^3)$$

time, which is polynomial but expensive for long sentences.

**Step 5: The Ambiguity Problem**
Even if $w \in L(G)$, the derivation may not be unique. A string $w$ is **ambiguous** in $G$ if it has two or more distinct parse trees:

$$\text{Count}(T(w)) \geq 2$$

where $T(w)$ is the set of valid parse trees for $w$.

**Step 6: The Statistical Turn (1990s)**
To resolve ambiguity probabilistically, we attach a probability to each rule and compute the **most probable parse tree**:

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}(w)} P(T)$$

**Step 7: The Neural Turn (2010s–present)**
Replace hand-crafted rules and probabilities with **learned distributed representations** (embeddings) and **neural sequence models**, ultimately converging on the **Transformer** architecture based on **self-attention**.

**Step 8: The Self-Attention Mechanism**
The core operation of the Transformer:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

where $Q$ (Query), $K$ (Key), and $V$ (Value) are projections of the input embedding. This allows the model to weigh the relevance of every token against every other token in parallel — the foundation of modern LLMs.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of CL Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU 2024 exam asset** — frequently asked as a 7-mark or 14-mark comparative question.

| **Criterion** | **Rule-Based / Symbolic** | **Statistical** | **Neural / Deep Learning** | **Foundation / LLM Era** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Era** | 1950s–1980s | 1990s–2000s | 2010s | 2017–Present |
| **Knowledge Source** | Hand-crafted linguistic rules | Annotated/unannotated corpora | Large raw corpora | Massive web-scale text |
| **Representation** | Symbols, parse trees | n-grams, HMMs, CRF | Dense vectors (Word2Vec, RNN) | Contextual embeddings (BERT, GPT) |
| **Strengths** | Interpretable, linguistically grounded | Robust, handles ambiguity probabilistically | Learns features automatically | SOTA on most benchmarks; transferable |
| **Weaknesses** | Brittle, expensive to scale | Data-hungry, shallow semantics | Black-box, needs GPUs | Hallucinations, enormous compute cost |
| **Example System** | ELIZA, SHRDLU, CHAT-80 | IBM Model 1–5, Moses SMT | seq2seq, LSTM, CNN-text | BERT, GPT-4, LLaMA, Gemini |
| **Key Math** | Production rules: $A \rightarrow \alpha$ | $P(w_n \mid w_{n-1})$ | $h_t = \text{RNN}(x_t, h_{t-1})$ | Self-attention: $\text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k})V$ |

### 3.3 Historical Evolution Timeline (Analytical Detail)

The history of CL is best understood as a **logical progression driven by three tensions**:
1. *Linguistic expressiveness* vs. *computational tractability*.
2. *Hand-engineered knowledge* vs. *learned knowledge*.
3. *Rule-based determinism* vs. *statistical/numeric uncertainty*.

| **Year** | **Milestone** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| **1950** | Turing's "Computing Machinery and Intelligence" | Proposed the **Turing Test** |
| **1954** | Georgetown–IBM Experiment | First public demonstration of **MT** (Russian → English, 6 sentences) |
| **1956** | Chomsky's Syntactic Structures | Founded **Generative Grammar** |
| **1956** | Dartmouth Conference | Coined **"Artificial Intelligence"** |
| **1966** | ELIZA (Weizenbaum) | First **chatbot** |
| **1966** | ALPAC Report | Critiqued MT; funding slashed |
| **1970** | SHRDLU (Winograd) | Demonstrated deep NL understanding in a restricted world |
| **1972** | Prolog released | Logic programming for CL |
| **1980s** | HMM-based POS taggers (e.g., CLAWS) | First large-scale **statistical** CL |
| **1993** | IBM Models 1–5 | Foundation of **statistical MT** |
| **2001** | BLEU Metric (Papineni et al.) | Standard **MT evaluation** |
| **2013** | Word2Vec (Mikolov) | **Distributed representations** popularized |
| **2014** | seq2seq (Sutskever et al.) | **Neural MT** surpasses statistical MT |
| **2017** | "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al.) | **Transformer** architecture published |
| **2018** | BERT (Devlin et al., Google) | **Bidirectional pre-training** |
| **2018–2020** | GPT-2, GPT-3 (OpenAI) | **Zero-shot / few-shot** learning via scale |
| **2022–2024** | GPT-4, ChatGPT, LLaMA, Gemini | **LLM era** and conversational AI mainstream |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Layered Architecture of Computational Linguistics

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of CL, mapping the interactions between linguistic levels, computational methods, and applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInput["Natural Language Input - Text or Speech"] --> nodePre["Preprocessing - Tokenization, Normalization"]
    nodePre --> nodeMorph["Morphological Analysis - Lemmatization, POS Tagging"]
    nodeMorph --> nodeSyn["Syntactic Analysis - Parsing, CFG, Dependency"]
    nodeSyn --> nodeSem["Semantic Analysis - Word Sense, Meaning Representation"]
    nodeSem --> nodePrag["Pragmatic and Discourse Analysis - Context, Intent"]
    nodePrag --> nodeApp["Application Layer - MT, IR, QA, Chatbots, Summarization"]

    nodeRule["Rule Based Methods - CFG, ATN, Prolog"] -.supports.-> nodeSyn
    nodeStat["Statistical Methods - HMM, n-gram, IBM Models"] -.supports.-> nodeMorph
    nodeDL["Deep Learning - RNN, LSTM, CNN"] -.supports.-> nodeSem
    nodeTrans["Transformers and LLMs - BERT, GPT, T5"] -.supports.-> nodePrag
    nodeCorpus["Corpora - Annotated and Unannotated Datasets"] -.feeds.-> nodeStat
    nodeCorpus -.feeds.-> nodeDL
    nodeCorpus -.feeds.-> nodeTrans

    nodeApp --> nodeOut["Output - Translated Text, Answer, Summary, Audio"]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Historical Evolution Flowchart (1950 → 2024)

This second Mermaid diagram visualizes the **chronological and methodological transitions** in CL.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["1950s\nFoundations\nTuring Test, Chomsky Grammar, Georgetown IBM MT"] --> nodeB["1960s\nEarly Symbolic CL\nELIZA, SHRDLU, ALPAC Report"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["1970s\nLogic Based NLP\nProlog, ATN, CHAT 80, Winograd"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["1980s\nStatistical Turn\nHMM, CLAWS Corpus, Decision Trees"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["1990s\nStatistical MT\nIBM Models, BLEU, Baum Welch"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["2000s\nFeature Engineering\nSVM, CRF, MaxEnt, Web Scale Data"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["2010s\nDeep Learning\nWord2Vec, RNN, LSTM, seq2seq"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["2017\nTransformer Era\nAttention Is All You Need"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["2018 to 2020\nPre trained Models\nBERT, GPT 2, GPT 3, T5"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["2022 to 2024\nLLM Revolution\nChatGPT, GPT 4, LLaMA, Gemini, RAG, Agents"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Chomsky Hierarchy as a Computational Decision Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeType0["Type 0 - Unrestricted Grammar\nTuring Machine\nMost Expressive, Undecidable"] --> nodeType1["Type 1 - Context Sensitive Grammar\nLinear Bounded Automaton\nDecidable, Expensive"]
    nodeType1 --> nodeType2["Type 2 - Context Free Grammar\nPushdown Automaton\nCYK in O n cubed, Most NL Syntax"]
    nodeType2 --> nodeType3["Type 3 - Regular Grammar\nFinite State Automaton\nFast, Limited - Used in Morphology"]
    nodeType3 --> nodeCl["Application in CL\nMorphotactics, FST for Tokenization"]
    nodeType2 --> nodeCl2["Application in CL\nPhrase Structure Parsing, CFG for English"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define *Computational Linguistics*. Differentiate it from *Natural Language Processing*. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Computational Linguistics (CL) is the scientific study of language from a computational perspective. It involves building formal models of linguistic phenomena and engineering software that can process natural language. CL is rooted in theoretical linguistics and computer science, encompassing grammar formalization, parsing, semantics, and discourse.
While **NLP (Natural Language Processing)** is often used synonymously with CL, **NLP is more engineering-oriented**, focusing on building practical applications such as chatbots, search engines, and translation systems. **CL is more theory-oriented**, emphasizing the formal modeling of language. In practice, NLP is considered an applied sub-field of CL.
*(Examiner's Note: A precise two-line distinction carrying the contrast between "theory" and "engineering" fetches full marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Write a short note on the **Turing Test** and its significance in Computational Linguistics. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **Turing Test**, proposed by **Alan Turing** in 1950 in his paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"*, is a method to determine whether a machine can exhibit intelligent, human-like behavior. In the test, a human interrogator communicates with two hidden entities — a human and a machine — via text. If the interrogator cannot reliably distinguish the machine from the human, the machine is said to have passed the test.
**Significance in CL:**
- It established **language as the benchmark for machine intelligence**.
- It motivated early CL research, including ELIZA, SHRDLU, and modern conversational AI.
- It remains the philosophical touchstone for evaluating chatbots and LLMs.
*(Examiner's Note: Naming the year **1950** and the original paper title fetches full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Model Question]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **scope of Computational Linguistics** with reference to its major sub-fields. Discuss at least **six** sub-fields in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The scope of Computational Linguistics is vast, spanning theoretical linguistics, computer science, mathematics, and artificial intelligence. Its major sub-fields include:

1. **Machine Translation (MT)**: Automatic conversion of text from one natural language to another. Example: Google Translate, which evolved from rule-based to statistical to neural MT.
2. **Speech Recognition (ASR)**: Conversion of spoken audio to text. Examples include Apple Siri, Google Assistant, and Dragon NaturallySpeaking. Acoustic models and language models are the two key components.
3. **Information Retrieval (IR)**: Building search engines that rank documents by relevance to a query. Classic models include Boolean, Vector Space, and BM25.
4. **Sentiment Analysis / Opinion Mining**: Identifying the polarity (positive, negative, neutral) of text. Used in brand monitoring, political analysis, and customer feedback.
5. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)**: Identifying and classifying proper nouns into predefined categories such as PERSON, LOCATION, and ORGANIZATION.
6. **Question Answering (QA)**: Building systems that accept a natural language question and return a precise answer (e.g., IBM Watson, ChatGPT).
7. **Text Summarization**: Producing a shorter version of a document that preserves the key information. Can be *extractive* (selecting sentences) or *abstractive* (generating new text).
8. **Corpus Linguistics**: The design and statistical analysis of large text corpora (e.g., British National Corpus, Indian Languages Corpora).

**Real-world relevance**: With the rise of India's *Bhashini* mission and the proliferation of Indian language computing, CL is critical for **Malayalam, Hindi, Tamil, and other Indic languages**.

---

**(b)** Describe the **Chomsky Hierarchy** of formal grammars. How does it relate to natural language processing? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **Chomsky Hierarchy**, proposed by **Noam Chomsky** in 1956, classifies formal grammars into four types based on their generative power:

| **Type** | **Grammar** | **Automaton** | **NLP Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 0** | Unrestricted | Turing Machine | Theoretical upper bound |
| **Type 1** | Context-Sensitive | Linear Bounded Automaton | Long-distance dependencies in NL |
| **Type 2** | Context-Free | Pushdown Automaton | Standard phrase-structure syntax |
| **Type 3** | Regular | Finite State Automaton | Morphology, tokenization |

A formal grammar is defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$

where $V$ = non-terminals, $T$ = terminals, $P$ = production rules, $S$ = start symbol.

**Relation to NLP**:
- **Regular grammars** (Type 3) are widely used in **morphological analysis** and **finite-state tokenizers** (e.g., `flex`).
- **Context-free grammars** (Type 2) underpin **phrase-structure parsing** of most natural languages.
- However, natural languages exhibit **context-sensitive phenomena** (e.g., subject-verb agreement across long distances, *I saw the man who the children knew ___*), which CFGs cannot fully capture. This motivates **Type 1** or more expressive formalisms.
- The **CYK algorithm** parses CFGs in $O(\vert w \vert^3)$ time, where $\vert w \vert$ is the sentence length.
- Modern statistical and neural parsers (e.g., dependency parsers) move away from rigid Chomsky-style grammars toward **probabilistic** and **learned** models.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Trace the **historical evolution of Computational Linguistics** from the 1950s to the present day, highlighting at least **eight key milestones**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The history of CL is a journey through four methodological waves:

**1. 1950 — The Turing Test (Alan Turing)**
Established language as the test of machine intelligence.

**2. 1954 — Georgetown–IBM Experiment**
First public demonstration of Machine Translation (Russian → English).

**3. 1956 — Chomsky's Syntactic Structures**
Founded **Generative Grammar**, giving CL a formal mathematical foundation.

**4. 1966 — ELIZA (Joseph Weizenbaum)**
First chatbot, simulating a Rogerian psychotherapist using simple pattern matching.

**5. 1966 — ALPAC Report**
A US government report that criticized MT and slashed funding, slowing MT research for nearly two decades.

**6. 1970 — SHRDLU (Terry Winograd)**
A landmark system demonstrating deep NL understanding in a restricted "blocks world."

**7. 1980s — The Statistical Turn**
Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) and the CLAWS corpus pioneered statistical POS tagging.

**8. 1993 — IBM Alignment Models (Brown et al.)**
Laid the statistical foundation for modern Machine Translation.

**9. 2013 — Word2Vec (Mikolov, Google)**
Introduced **distributed word embeddings**, revolutionizing semantic representation.

**10. 2017 — "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al.)**
Introduced the **Transformer architecture**, the foundation of all modern LLMs.

**11. 2018–2024 — BERT, GPT-series, LLaMA, Gemini**
The era of **Large Language Models (LLMs)** and conversational AI.

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Rule-Based**, **Statistical**, and **Neural Network** approaches in Computational Linguistics. Which approach is most dominant today and why? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Criterion** | **Rule-Based (Symbolic)** | **Statistical** | **Neural / Deep Learning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Era** | 1950s–1980s | 1990s–2000s | 2010s–Present |
| **Knowledge Source** | Hand-crafted linguistic rules | Annotated corpora + probability | Large raw corpora + learned features |
| **Representation** | Symbols, parse trees | n-gram counts, HMM, CRF | Dense vectors, RNN, Transformer |
| **Strengths** | Interpretable, linguistically motivated | Robust, handles ambiguity | Learns automatically, scalable |
| **Weaknesses** | Brittle, manual, hard to scale | Data-hungry, shallow semantics | Black-box, GPU-intensive, hallucinations |
| **Examples** | ELIZA, CHAT-80, Prolog NLP | IBM Models, Moses SMT | seq2seq, BERT, GPT-4 |

**Current Dominance — Neural / LLM Era**:
The **Transformer-based neural approach** is dominant today for three reasons:
1. **Scalability**: Performance scales predictably with data and compute.
2. **Transferability**: Pre-trained models (BERT, GPT) can be fine-tuned for downstream tasks with minimal labeled data.
3. **State-of-the-art results**: Neural models surpass symbolic and statistical baselines on virtually every CL benchmark (GLUE, SuperGLUE, BLEU, WER, etc.).

However, the **future lies in hybrid neuro-symbolic systems** that combine the interpretability of rules with the generalization of neural networks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Conflating CL and NLP** — Many students write "CL and NLP are the same." This is *partially* true, but examiners expect the **theory vs. engineering distinction**. A student who fails to articulate this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the year and inventor** — In history questions, writing *"Chomsky proposed a grammar"* without the year **(1956)** and the book *(Syntactic Structures)* is considered incomplete.
> 3. **Ignoring the Indian/Regional Context** — Kerala-based B.Tech students are expected to mention **Malayalam NLP**, **Bhashini**, and the **National Language Translation Mission (NLTM)** wherever applicable. Examiners reward contextual awareness.
> 4. **Writing about Transformers without the math** — A 14-mark question on modern CL that omits the **self-attention formula**:
>
> $$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$
>
> is considered incomplete. Always include the core equation.
> 5. **Failing to compare paradigms** — When asked to compare, students often describe each approach in isolation. Examiners expect a **table or matrix format** with explicit comparative criteria.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Definition**: CL = Linguistics + Computer Science + Math + Statistics; it is the *theoretical and applied* study of natural language using computation.
> - **CL vs. NLP**: CL → theory-heavy; NLP → engineering/applied (NLP is a sub-field of CL).
> - **Turing Test (1950)**: The philosophical foundation of CL — language as the benchmark of machine intelligence.
> - **Chomsky Hierarchy (1956)**: Four types of grammars — Type 0 (Turing), Type 1 (Context-Sensitive), Type 2 (Context-Free), Type 3 (Regular).
> - **CYK Algorithm**: Parses CFGs in $O(\vert w \vert^3)$.
> - **Key Historical Milestones**: Turing (1950) → Georgetown-IBM (1954) → Chomsky (1956) → ELIZA (1966) → ALPAC (1966) → SHRDLU (1970) → HMMs (1980s) → IBM Models (1993) → BLEU (2001) → Word2Vec (2013) → seq2seq (2014) → **Transformer (2017)** → BERT (2018) → GPT-series (2018–2024).
> - **Four Paradigms**: Rule-Based → Statistical → Neural (RNN/LSTM) → Transformer / LLM.
> - **Self-Attention Formula**: $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k})V$.
> - **Major Sub-fields**: MT, ASR, TTS, IR, IE, NER, QA, Summarization, Sentiment Analysis, Corpus Linguistics, Computational Morphology, Discourse & Dialogue.
> - **Indian Relevance**: Bhashini Mission, NLTM, Indian Language Corpora, Malayalam NLP.
> - **Software Engineering Linkage**: CL is the **foundation of all modern AI assistants, search engines, and translation systems**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Linguistic Subfields: Phonetics, Phonology, Morphology, Syntax, Semantics, and Pragmatics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Overview of Linguistic Subfields: Phonetics, Phonology, Morphology, Syntax, Semantics, and Pragmatics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Computational Linguistics — The Parent Discipline > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > *Computational Linguistics (CL) is an interdisciplinary field situated at the intersection of computer science, artificial intelligence, and linguistics, concerned with the computational modeling of natural language. It designs algorithms, statistical models, and formal grammars to enable machines to **process, produce, and understand** human language.* **Intuitive Analogy — "The Universal Translator Workshop"** Imagine linguistics as a giant workshop that dissects the English sentence *"The cat sat on the mat."* Computational Linguistics is the engineer who builds the machines that perform each stage of this dissection automatically. First, a microphone captures the sound waves (Phonetics). Then, the system figures out which sounds in that language actually matter to meaning (Phonology). Next, it cuts the sentence into smallest meaningful Lego pieces (Morphology). It then arranges them to check if the grammar is legal (Syntax). Finally, it asks, *"What does it actually mean?"* (Semantics) and *"Why did the speaker say this now?"* (Pragmatics). CL automates the entire assembly line. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Linguistic Hierarchy (Klavans / Jurafsky tradition adopted by KTU):** > Phonetics → Phonology → Morphology → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics. > Each level feeds structured input to the next higher level. This is the **foundational pipeline** every KTU exam question will assume you know. --- ## 1.2 The Six Linguistic Subfields — Quick-Reference Definitions | # | Subfield | One-Line Academic Definition | Core Object of Study | |---|----------|------------------------------|----------------------| | 1 | **Phonetics** | The scientific study of the physical production, acoustic transmission, and perception of speech sounds. | Concrete sound waves (signal) | | 2 | **Phonology** | The study of the abstract, systematic organization of speech sounds in particular languages. | Abstract sound categories (phonemes) | | 3 | **Morphology** | The study of the internal structure of words and the smallest meaning-bearing units. | Morphemes, word formation | | 4 | **Syntax** | The study of the rules governing the arrangement of words to form grammatical sentences. | Phrases, clauses, tree structures | | 5 | **Semantics** | The study of literal, context-independent meaning expressed by words, phrases, and sentences. | Meaning propositions | | 6 | **Pragmatics** | The study of how context, speaker intention, and world knowledge contribute to utterance interpretation. | Intended meaning in context | > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** *"***P**lease **P**our **M**ore **S**oda **S**oon, **P**lease"* → **P**honetics, **P**honology, **M**orphology, **S**yntax, **S**emantics, **P**ragmatics. The order goes from *physical* (sound waves) → *abstract* (speaker intent). --- ## 1.3 Section 1 Summary Callout > [!NOTE] > Every KTU 2024 question stem that uses phrases like *"at the level of…"*, *"which subfield deals with…"*, or *"give an example from…"* is directly testing the **scope of each subfield** as tabulated above. Memorize the *object of study* column — it is worth **2-mark direct definition** questions every semester. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Phonetics — The Physical Layer **Operational Logic (Bullet-Form Decomposition):** - **Articulation:** Speakers use the vocal tract (lungs → larynx → oral/nasal cavity) to shape sounds. - **Acoustic transmission:** Air pressure variations propagate as a waveform; frequency measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, amplitude in **decibels (dB)**. - **Perception:** The ear + brain convert acoustic signal back into a perceptual experience. - **Three Branches:** *Articulatory* (how produced), *Acoustic* (physical signal), *Auditory* (how heard). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Phonetics matters in CL:** Speech recognition systems (ASR — Automatic Speech Recognition) such as Whisper, DeepSpeech, and Kaldi all begin with a **phonetic feature extractor** (typically Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients — MFCCs). ## 2.2 Phonology — The Abstract Pattern Layer **Operational Logic:** - Defines the **phoneme** — the *smallest contrastive unit* in a language. - English /p/ vs /b/ in *pat* vs *bat* → minimal pair (a KTU-favorite example). - Governed by **phonological rules**, e.g., English plural nasal assimilation: $$/dɔg/ + /z/ \rightarrow /dɔgz/ \quad \text{(dogs)}$$ $$/kæt/ + /s/ \rightarrow /kæts/ \quad \text{(cats)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Distinctive Features** (binary ± attributes like [±voice], [±nasal]) are the symbolic input format used in classical CL phonology processors. ## 2.3 Morphology — The Word-Building Layer **Operational Logic:** - A **morpheme** is the smallest unit carrying meaning or grammatical function. - *Free morpheme:* *cat, run, happy* - *Bound morpheme:* *-s, -ed, un-, -ly* - **Word-formation processes:** inflection, derivation, compounding, blending, acronyming. - **Morphological analysis** is the input stage for **lemmatizers and stemmers** in NLP pipelines. ## 2.4 Syntax — The Sentence-Structure Layer **Operational Logic:** - Rules specify legal word orderings and phrase structures. - Formalized using **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)** / **Phrase-Structure Rules (PSR)**: $$S \rightarrow NP \; VP$$ $$NP \rightarrow (Det)\; (Adj)\; N$$ $$VP \rightarrow V\; (NP)\; (PP)$$ - **Constituency tests:** substitution, movement, coordination, stand-alone answer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite:** *"Draw the parse tree for 'The clever student solved the hard problem.'"* Always show **NP-internal structure** for full marks (Det + Adj + N). ## 2.5 Semantics — The Meaning Layer **Operational Logic:** - Operates on **propositions** (truth-conditional content). - Key relations: synonymy, antonymy, hyponymy, meronymy, entailment. - Formalized via **Predicate Logic translation**, e.g.: $$\text{"All students read books."} \equiv \forall x\,(Student(x) \rightarrow \exists y\,(Book(y) \land Read(x,y)))$$ ## 2.6 Pragmatics — The Context Layer **Operational Logic:** - Covers **speech acts** (Austin/Searle): *assertives, directives, commissives, expressives, declarations*. - Includes **implicature** (Gricean Maxims: Quantity, Quality, Relation, Manner). - Disambiguates indexicals (*I, you, here, now*) and presuppositions. --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Comparison Matrix — KTU Direct-Question Bank:** > > | Subfield | Unit Studied | What is Abstract? | What is Concrete? | CL Application | > |----------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------| > | Phonetics | Phone, Allophone | None — purely physical | Sound waveform, spectrogram | MFCC extraction, ASR front-end | > | Phonology | Phoneme, Feature | Phoneme (mental category) | Allophone (realised) | Grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) | > | Morphology | Morpheme, Lexeme | Underlying form | Surface form | Stemming, lemmatization | > | Syntax | Phrase, Clause | Phrase-Structure Rule | Spoken sentence | Parsing, PCFG, dependency parsing | > | Semantics | Proposition, Sense | Literal meaning | Lexical item | WSD, semantic role labelling | > | Pragmatics | Speech act, Implicature | Speaker intent | Utterance | Coreference resolution, dialogue systems | > > **Units & Constants to Memorize:** > - Speech frequency range of human voice: **300 Hz – 3400 Hz** (telephony band). > - Standard phoneme inventory size: English ≈ **44 phonemes**; Hindi ≈ **~52**; Malayalam ≈ **~53**. > - International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) symbol count (2020 revision): **107 letters + 60 diacritics + 4 suprasegmentals**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Phonetics ≠ Phonology. *Phonetics* = the **raw signal**; *Phonology* = the **rule system** that decides which signals are *meaningful* in a language. A one-mark blip if confused. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix (Exhaustive) ## 3.1 Worked Analytical Walkthrough — Distinguishing the Six Levels > [!NOTE] > Since this topic is *conceptual* rather than equation-heavy, the KTU 2024 scheme replaces the algebraic derivation with a **rigorous bottom-up analytical matrix** showing the same input being processed at each level. This is the format KTU examiners reward. **Input Utterance (spoken):** *"She unmade the beds before the guests arrived."* --- ### Level 1 — Phonetics (Physical Layer) **Step 1.** Microphone converts air pressure to voltage waveform. **Step 2.** Spectrogram plotted: time (x-axis, ms) vs. frequency (y-axis, Hz) vs. intensity (color, dB). **Step 3.** Identify allophones via acoustic landmarks (bursts, voicing bars, formants). **Result:** A stream of phones transcribed in **IPA**, including allophonic detail: $$\text{[ʃi ʌn.meɪd ðə bɛdz bɪ.fɔɹ ðə ɡɛsts ə.ɹaɪvd]}$$ ### Level 2 — Phonology (Categorical Layer) **Step 1.** Strip allophonic variation, keep only contrastive units. **Step 2.** Apply phonological rules (e.g., /t/-flapping in American English, aspiration of /p/). **Step 3.** Output the **phonemic transcription**: $$/ʃi ʌn.meɪd ðə bɛdz bɪ.fɔr ðə ɡɛsts ə.raɪvd/$$ **Key Distinction:** Phonology is why an English speaker perceives [t] and [ʔ] (glottal stop) as the "same /t/" — they are **allophones**, not distinct phonemes. ### Level 3 — Morphology (Word-Internal Layer) **Step 1.** Tokenise and segment each word into morphemes. **Step 2.** Identify free + bound morphemes and their grammatical category. | Surface Word | Morpheme 1 (Free) | Morpheme 2 (Bound) | Morpheme 3 (Bound) | |--------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------| | She | she (PRO) | — | — | | unmade | make (V, root) | un- (NEG prefix) | -ed (PAST) | | the | the (DET) | — | — | | beds | bed (N) | -s (PL) | — | | before | before (PREP) | — | — | | guests | guest (N) | -s (PL) | — | | arrived | arrive (V) | -ed (PAST) | — | **Result:** *un-* modifies *make* to derive a *lexeme*; *-ed* then inflects it. Two distinct morphological operations — **derivation** and **inflection**. ### Level 4 — Syntax (Sentence-Structure Layer) **Step 1.** Build the **Phrase-Structure Tree** using CFG rules. **Step 2.** Apply constituency tests to verify groupings. **Parse Tree (Mermaid-friendly, but rendered textually here):** ``` [S ├─ NP │ └─ Pro 'She' └─ VP ├─ V 'unmade' ├─ NP │ ├─ Det 'the' │ └─ N 'beds' ├─ PP │ ├─ P 'before' │ └─ NP │ ├─ Det 'the' │ └─ N 'guests' └─ AdvP 'arrived'] ``` **Validation via Movement Test:** *"She unmade the beds before the guests arrived, she did."* → tag confirms VP-level structure. ### Level 5 — Semantics (Literal-Meaning Layer) **Step 1.** Translate to **First-Order Predicate Logic**. $$\exists e\,\exists x\,\exists y\,(She(x) \land Beds(y) \land Unmake(e,x,y) \land Past(e) \land Before(e,e') \land Guests(z) \land Arrive(e',z))$$ **Step 2.** Identify semantic relations: - *beds* : hypernym *furniture*; hyponyms *single bed, double bed*. - *guest* ↔ *host* : converse relation. - **Entailment:** "She unmade the beds" **entails** "The beds were unmade." ### Level 6 — Pragmatics (Contextual Layer) **Step 1.** Identify the **speech act**: Assertive (declaring a past event). **Step 2.** Resolve indexicals: *She* → referent must be retrieved from discourse context; *before* → relative to arrival time. **Step 3.** Compute **implicature (Gricean Maxim of Relation):** The speaker is implicitly suggesting that *unmaking the beds was a deliberate preparatory action* — a *relevant background* act to the guests' arrival. **Step 4.** Handle **presupposition:** "*the guests*" presupposes the existence of expected guests — a **factive presupposition** triggered by the definite article. --- ## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU High-Yield) | Analytical Dimension | Phonetics | Phonology | Morphology | Syntax | Semantics | Pragmatics | |----------------------|-----------|-----------|------------|--------|-----------|------------| | **Type of Rule** | Physical laws | Categorical rules | Word-formation rules | Combinatorial rules | Compositional rules | Inference rules | | **Branches** | Articulatory/Acoustic/Auditory | Generative/Optimality | Inflectional/Derivational | Constituency/Dependency | Lexical/Sentential | Speech Acts/Implicature | | **Data Unit** | Waveform | Phoneme | Morpheme | Phrase | Proposition | Utterance | | **Notation** | IPA | Feature matrix | Interleaved morphs | PSR / Tree | Predicate logic | Speech-act labels | | **CL Tool** | MFCC | G2P | Stemmer/Lemmatizer | Parser | WSD / NER | Coref / Dialog | | **Ambiguity Type** | Acoustic | Phonemic | Lexical | Structural | Lexical/Quantifier | Speaker-intent | > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix has appeared, in various forms, in **KTU University Exam 2023 (Model Paper)**. Reproduce it from memory for **full 14-mark** structured answers. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Linguistic Processing Pipeline (Hierarchical Flow) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram visualises the **standard KTU linguistic hierarchy** as a top-down data-flow architecture. It is the most frequently asked diagram in Module 1. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Raw Speech Signal] --> A1 A1[Phonetics Layer
Acoustic Waveform] A1 --> A2 A2[Phonology Layer
Phoneme Inventory] A2 --> A3 A3[Morphology Layer
Morpheme Segmentation] A3 --> A4 A4[Syntax Layer
Parse Tree Generation] A4 --> A5 A5[Semantics Layer
Literal Meaning Logic Form] A5 --> A6 A6[Pragmatics Layer
Contextual Interpretation] A6 --> A7((Final Understanding
Discourse Representation)) A1 -.annotates.-> P1[Phones IPA] A2 -.annotates.-> P2[Phonemes Features] A3 -.annotates.-> P3[Morphemes Lexemes] A4 -.annotates.-> P4[CFG PSR Trees] A5 -.annotates.-> P5[FOL Lambda Calculus] A6 -.annotates.-> P6[Speech Acts Implicature] ``` ## 4.2 The Six Subfields — Domain Boundary Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph L1[Phonetic Domain] L1A[Articulatory] L1B[Acoustic] L1C[Auditory] end subgraph L2[Phonological Domain] L2A[Distinctive Features] L2B[Syllable Structure] L2C[Phonological Rules] end subgraph L3[Morphological Domain] L3A[Inflection] L3B[Derivation] L3C[Compounding] end subgraph L4[Syntactic Domain] L4A[Phrase Structure] L4B[Dependency] L4C[Transformations] end subgraph L5[Semantic Domain] L5A[Lexical Sense] L5B[Sentence Meaning] L5C[World Knowledge] end subgraph L6[Pragmatic Domain] L6A[Speech Acts] L6B[Implicature] L6C[Reference Resolution] end L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L1 -.feedback.-> L6 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (CL System Design) > [!TIP] > This is the **fallback architecture** KTU examiners prefer when students cannot draw a physical apparatus. It shows how the six subfields are modularised in a typical CL pipeline (e.g., spaCy, Stanford CoreNLP, NLTK). ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[Input: Text or Speech] INPUT --> M1 M1[Module 1: Acoustic Front End
Phonetic Analyser] M1 --> M2[Module 2: Phonological Normaliser
G2P Converter] M2 --> M3[Module 3: Morphological Processor
Tokenizer Stemmer Lemmatizer] M3 --> M4[Module 4: Syntactic Parser
CFG Parser Dependency Parser] M4 --> M5[Module 5: Semantic Interpreter
WSD NER Semantic Roles] M5 --> M6[Module 6: Pragmatic Reasoner
Coreference Dialog Manager] M6 --> OUTPUT[(Final Meaning
Discourse Representation)] M1 -.uses.-> D1[IPA Dictionary
Acoustic Models] M2 -.uses.-> D2[Phoneme Rules
Feature Matrix] M3 -.uses.-> D3[Morpheme Lexicon
Morphotactics] M4 -.uses.-> D4[Grammar Rules
Treebank] M5 -.uses.-> D5[Ontology WordNet
Predicate Logic] M6 -.uses.-> D6[Speech Act Database
Context KB] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU valuation, **labelled arrows + module names + named data resources** = full marks. Never leave a box unlabelled. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1a]** **Q1. Differentiate between Phonetics and Phonology with one example each.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** *Phonetics* is the scientific study of the **physical properties of speech sounds** — how they are produced by the vocal tract (articulatory), transmitted as acoustic waves (acoustic), and perceived by the listener (auditory). *Example:* Studying the formant frequencies of the vowel /i/ as it appears in a spectrogram. *Phonology*, in contrast, is the study of the **abstract, systematic organisation of speech sounds** in a particular language, focusing on which sound distinctions are *meaningful* (phonemes). *Example:* Recognising that in English, /p/ and /b/ are distinct phonemes (as in *pat* vs *bat*), but aspiration of /p/ in *pin* vs *spin* is a non-contrastive allophonic variation governed by phonological rule. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1b]** **Q2. What is a Morpheme? Distinguish between Free and Bound Morphemes with two examples each.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A **morpheme** is the smallest indivisible unit of language that carries meaning or grammatical function. - **Free morpheme** — can stand alone as an independent word. *Example 1:* *cat* (lexical noun) *Example 2:* *happy* (lexical adjective) - **Bound morpheme** — cannot stand alone; must attach to a free morpheme. *Example 1:* *-s* in *cats* (plural inflection) *Example 2:* *un-* in *unhappy* (negating derivational prefix) *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 1 Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q9, Choice A]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) Explain the hierarchical levels of linguistic analysis in Computational Linguistics. Illustrate each level with a suitable example.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution — Step-by-Step:** **Step 1 — Introduce the Hierarchy (1 mark):** Linguistic analysis is organised into a six-level hierarchy that moves from the concrete and physical to the abstract and contextual. The levels are: Phonetics → Phonology → Morphology → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics. **Step 2 — Phonetics (1 mark):** Deals with the **physical production, transmission, and perception of speech sounds**. *Example:* The word *cat* uttered aloud produces acoustic vibrations in the 300–3400 Hz range, visible as a spectrogram with distinct formant patterns for /k/, /æ/, /t/. **Step 3 — Phonology (1 mark):** Deals with the **abstract sound system** of a language. *Example:* In English, /p/ (voiceless bilabial stop) and /b/ (voiced bilabial stop) form a minimal pair in *pat* vs *bat*, showing they are distinct phonemes. **Step 4 — Morphology (1 mark):** Deals with the **internal structure of words**. *Example:* *unhappiness* decomposes into *un- + happy + -ness* — a derivational prefix, a free root, and a derivational suffix. **Step 5 — Syntax (1 mark):** Deals with the **arrangement of words into well-formed sentences**. *Example:* *"The cat chased the mouse."* is grammatical, but *"Cat the the chased mouse."* is syntactically ill-formed. **Step 6 — Semantics (1 mark):** Deals with the **literal meaning** of words, phrases, and sentences. *Example:* The sentence *"The cat chased the mouse."* denotes an event in which a cat performed a chasing action directed at a mouse. **Step 7 — Pragmatics (1 mark):** Deals with **meaning in context**, including speaker intent. *Example:* The utterance *"Can you pass the salt?"* is syntactically a question but pragmatically functions as a **directive/request**. --- **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a typical Computational Linguistics system. List the data resources used at each level.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Block diagram (2 marks):** The CL system is organised as a six-module pipeline: Acoustic Front End → Phonological Normaliser → Morphological Processor → Syntactic Parser → Semantic Interpreter → Pragmatic Reasoner. *(Refer to Section 4.3 diagram of these notes.)* **Step 2 — Module-wise function (3 marks):** - **Acoustic Front End:** Converts speech waveform to phonetic features (MFCCs). - **Phonological Normaliser:** Maps surface forms to canonical phoneme sequences. - **Morphological Processor:** Tokenises input, identifies stems and affixes, performs stemming and lemmatization. - **Syntactic Parser:** Constructs a phrase-structure or dependency tree using CFG rules. - **Semantic Interpreter:** Performs Word Sense Disambiguation, Named Entity Recognition, and produces a logical form. - **Pragmatic Reasoner:** Resolves coreference, computes implicature, and integrates world knowledge. **Step 3 — Data resources (2 marks):** - Phonetic: **IPA chart, acoustic models (HMM/GMM)** - Phonological: **Phoneme rule set, distinctive feature matrix** - Morphological: **Morpheme lexicon, WordNet** - Syntactic: **Penn Treebank, CFG grammar** - Semantic: **WordNet ontology, FrameNet, predicate calculus axioms** - Pragmatic: **Dialogue corpora, speech-act-tagged datasets** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q9, Choice B]** **(a) Define Syntax. Explain Phrase Structure Grammar with an example. Draw the parse tree for the sentence: "The tall boy read an interesting book."** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** Syntax is the branch of linguistics that studies the **rules and principles governing the arrangement of words into well-formed phrases and sentences**. **Step 2 — Phrase Structure Grammar definition (2 marks):** A **Phrase Structure Grammar (PSG)**, also called Context-Free Grammar (CFG), is a formal system consisting of: - A set of **non-terminal symbols** (S, NP, VP, PP, Det, N, V, Adj, Adv, P). - A set of **terminal symbols** (the actual words). - A set of **production rules** (PSRs) that expand non-terminals. - A designated **start symbol** S. **Step 3 — Sample Grammar (2 marks):** ``` S → NP VP NP → Det Adj N | Det N | ProperN VP → V NP | V NP NP Det → the | an | a Adj → tall | interesting N → boy | book V → read ``` **Step 4 — Parse Tree (2 marks):** ``` [S] / \ [NP] [VP] / | \ / \ Det Adj N V [NP] | | | | / | \ the tall boy read Det Adj N | | | an interesting book ``` --- **(b) Explain the differences between Semantics and Pragmatics. Discuss the major components of Pragmatics with examples.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Semantics vs Pragmatics table (3 marks):** | Aspect | Semantics | Pragmatics | |--------|-----------|------------| | Meaning Type | Literal, context-free | Intended, context-bound | | Focus | Truth conditions | Speaker intent, effect | | Key Unit | Proposition | Utterance + speech act | | Example | *"It is cold here."* denotes a temperature state. | Same sentence may be a *request to close the window* (pragmatic implicature). | **Step 2 — Components of Pragmatics (4 marks):** 1. **Speech Acts (Austin/Searle):** Classify utterances as *assertives, directives, commissives, expressives, declarations*. *Example:* *"I apologise"* is an *expressive*. 2. **Implicature (Grice):** What is meant beyond what is said, governed by the **Cooperative Principle and four Maxims** (Quantity, Quality, Relation, Manner). *Example:* "Some of the students passed" implicates *not all* did. 3. **Presupposition:** Background assumptions a sentence takes for granted. *Example:* *"The king of France is bald."* presupposes there is a king of France. 4. **Reference and Deixis:** Indexicals like *I, you, here, now* whose reference shifts with context. *Example:* *"I'll see you here tomorrow"* requires situational anchoring. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Mixing Phonetics and Phonology in a single paragraph** — examiners award 0 for that level if undefined. > 2. **Forgetting to label tree nodes** in syntax questions — every node (S, NP, VP, Det, N) must be marked. > 3. **Conflating Semantics and Pragmatics** — always say *"literal meaning"* vs *"speaker-intended meaning"*. > 4. **Omitting IPA symbols** in phonetics/phonology answers — even one example in IPA fetches 1 extra mark. > 5. **In morphology, failing to separate inflection from derivation** — *-s* (inflection) ≠ *-ness* (derivation). Examiners **specifically** check this distinction. > 6. **In pragmatics, omitting Grice's Maxims by name** — saying "context" alone is insufficient; name the *Cooperative Principle*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU PECMT753 Module 1:** - ☐ The six subfields in correct order: **Phonetics → Phonology → Morphology → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics**. - ☐ Phonetics = **physical signal**; Phonology = **abstract sound categories**. - ☐ **Phoneme** = smallest contrastive unit; **Allophone** = non-contrastive variant; **Minimal pair** = proof of contrast. - ☐ English has **~44 phonemes**; IPA contains **107 letters + 60 diacritics**. - ☐ **Morpheme** = smallest meaning unit; **Free** vs **Bound** is the must-state distinction. - ☐ Inflection (*-s, -ed, -ing*) does **not** change word class; Derivation (*-ness, un-, -ly*) often does. - ☐ **Syntax** is formalised by **Context-Free Grammar / PSR**; parse trees are mandatory in exams. - ☐ **Semantics** = literal meaning via predicate logic; **Pragmatics** = speaker intent via Speech Acts + Gricean Maxims. - ☐ Grice's four Maxims: **Quantity, Quality, Relation, Manner** (under the **Cooperative Principle**). - ☐ CL Pipeline Architecture: **Acoustic Front End → Phonological Normaliser → Morphological Processor → Syntactic Parser → Semantic Interpreter → Pragmatic Reasoner**. - ☐ Key resources: **IPA, WordNet, Penn Treebank, FrameNet, CFG grammar, Acoustic Models (HMM/GMM)**. - ☐ Standard KTU sub-question pattern: *(a) 7 marks — conceptual + diagram*; *(b) 7 marks — comparison + application*. - ☐ Always include at least one **example in IPA** (e.g., /kæt/) and one **logic translation** (e.g., ∀x P(x) → Q(x)) per long answer. - ☐ KTU frequently asks **"Differentiate between X and Y"** — use a **comparison table** to fetch full 7 marks. - ☐ Remember the **"Klavans/Jurafsky" hierarchy** explicitly — it is the canonical reference in KTU answer keys.

topics.title: Linguistic Theories and Models: Structural Linguistics and Its Influence on Computational Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linguistic Theories and Models: Structural Linguistics and Its Influence on Computational Methods

# Module 1 — Structural Linguistics and Its Influence on Computational Methods

## 1. Core Technical Definition and Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> *Structural Linguistics* (also called **American Structuralism** or **Descriptivism**) is a theoretical and methodological framework developed in the early-to-mid 20th century that treats language as an objective, self-contained system of formal relations. It analyses linguistic units strictly in terms of their **form**, **distribution**, and **arrangement**, deliberately postponing or eliminating appeals to **meaning** in the analytical procedure. The school is associated principally with **Ferdinand de Saussure** (European tradition), **Leonard Bloomfield** (American school), **Edward Sapir**, **Zellig Harris**, and **Charles Hockett**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Linguistic LEGO" Metaphor

Imagine a child dumps a giant tub of LEGO bricks on the floor. A **structural linguist** does **not** ask *"What does this brick represent in the real world?"* — instead, the linguist sorts the bricks purely by:

1. **Shape** (its physical form — corresponds to *phonological form*)
2. **Size** (the smallest indivisible piece — corresponds to *minimal linguistic unit / phoneme / morpheme*)
3. **Snapping patterns** (which bricks click onto which others — corresponds to *syntagmatic relations*)
4. **Inventory of similar bricks** (the family of bricks a given brick could be substituted with — corresponds to *paradigmatic relations*)

Just as a structural engineer studies a bridge as a network of forces and members **without first asking what the bridge "means" to a pedestrian**, a structural linguist studies a sentence as a network of form-units and distributional relations **without first asking what the sentence "means" to a speaker**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Saussure's Foundational Dichotomy**
> - **Langue** → the abstract, shared, rule-governed *system* of a language (the structural code).
> - **Parole** → the actual *utterances* produced by individual speakers (the behavioural instantiation).
> Structural linguistics prioritises the study of **langue** as the proper object of linguistic science.

### Canonical Figures of the Structural Tradition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Five" of Structural Linguistics (Examinable)**
> 1. **Ferdinand de Saussure (1857–1913)** — Swiss, founder of modern linguistics, Course in General Linguistics (1916).
> 2. **Leonard Bloomfield (1887–1949)** — American, author of *Language* (1933), defined behaviourist methodology.
> 3. **Edward Sapir (1884–1939)** — American, anthropologist-linguist, language-thought hypothesis.
> 4. **Zellig Harris (1909–1992)** — American, developed **Immediate Constituent Analysis** and **discourse analysis**; direct intellectual bridge to computational linguistics.
> 5. **Charles Hockett (1916–2000)** — American, refined **distribution class** theory and **design features of language**.

### The Three Levels of Structural Analysis

| Level | Unit of Study | Core Question | Modern Computational Counterpart |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phonology** | *Phoneme* | How are sounds systematically organised? | Acoustic models, ASR systems |
| **Morphology** | *Morpheme* | How are meaningful units combined? | Finite-state morphologies |
| **Syntax** | *Constituent / Phrase* | How are morphemes combined into larger structures? | CFG parsers, dependency parsers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bloomfield's Maxim (Board Favourite):**
> *"The linguist's data are the speech-events of the community, recorded by the field worker. The linguist is committed to describing these data in terms of formal and distributional properties alone."* — *Post-Bloomfieldian orthodoxy*.

### Unit Hierarchy (Smallest → Largest)

$$\text{Phon} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Phoneme} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Morph} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Morpheme} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Word} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Phrase} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Clause} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Sentence}$$

The **phoneme** is the *smallest contrastive unit*; the **morpheme** is the *smallest meaningful unit*; both are the structural atoms on which all higher-level computation rests.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Theoretical Apparatus

### A. The Sign — Saussure's Dyadic Model

Every linguistic sign is a *psychological* entity with two inseparable faces:

$$\text{Sign} = \left\{ \text{Signifier (form)} \,,\, \text{Signified (concept)} \right\}$$

- The **signifier** is the *phonological* form (e.g., the sound-image `/kæt/`).
- The **signified** is the *mental concept* (e.g., the idea of a small furry domesticated mammal).
- The *bond* between them is **arbitrary** — there is no natural reason why `/kæt/` refers to a cat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **arbitrariness of the sign** is the philosophical cornerstone of structuralism and is the first direct ancestor of the modern *distributional hypothesis* in NLP.

### B. Paradigmatic vs. Syntagmatic Relations

These are the **two axes** along which all linguistic units can be situated:

| Axis | Definition | Example (sentence: *"The cat sat on the mat"*) | Computational Analogue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Syntagmatic** | Relations *in praesentia* — units co-occurring in the linear chain | `cat` is related to `the` (left) and `sat` (right) | *n-gram context*, dependency arc |
| **Paradigmatic** | Relations *in absentia* — units substitutable in the same slot | `cat` is substitutable by `dog`, `bird`, `child` | *embedding lookup*, word-substitution test |

### C. Distribution and Complementary Distribution (Hockett)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition of Distribution (Examinable)**
> The **distribution** of a linguistic unit is the *totality of environments* in which it occurs — i.e., the set of all contexts (sum of all syntagmatic positions) in which the unit is found. Two units are in **complementary distribution** if they never occur in the same environment and their distribution can be predicted by a phonological rule.

This is the single most important technical term in structural linguistics and the direct ancestor of **feature-based classification** in modern NLP.

### D. The Discovery Procedure (Bloomfieldian Methodology)

Bloomfield prescribed a strictly *empirical* workflow:

1. **Collect** a corpus of utterances.
2. **Transcribe** phonetically (not phonemically).
3. **Identify phonemes** by *minimal-pair testing*.
4. **Identify morphemes** by *substitution testing*.
5. **Construct Immediate Constituent (IC) tree** for every sentence.

The modern computational analogue is the **NLP pipeline**:

$$\text{Raw text} \rightarrow \text{Tokenisation} \rightarrow \text{Morphological Analysis} \rightarrow \text{POS Tagging} \rightarrow \text{Parsing}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight — Why Structuralism Matters for Computation**
> The Bloomfieldian *discovery procedure* is essentially an **unsupervised algorithm**: given raw data, derive structure. This is the philosophical precursor of today's *unsupervised representation learning* (word2vec, BERT pretraining).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formal Notation | Reading / Interpretation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phoneme** | $\vert p \vert \in P$ | $p$ is a member of the phoneme inventory $P$ | Lexicon representation |
| **Minimal Pair** | $(w_1, w_2) : \vert w_1 \vert = \vert w_2 \vert$ and $\Delta(w_1, w_2) = 1$ | Two words differing in exactly one segment | Phoneme identification |
| **Morpheme** | $m = \langle F, S \rangle$ | A pair of form $F$ and meaning $S$ | Morphological parsing |
| **Complementary Distribution** | $C(a) \cap C(b) = \emptyset$ and $C(a) \cup C(b) \subseteq C([a,b])$ | $a$ and $b$ never co-occur but together cover a phoneme | Phoneme merging |
| **IC Analysis** | $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$ | Sentence decomposes into Noun Phrase + Verb Phrase | CFG production rule |
| **Context-Free Grammar** | $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ | $V$ non-terminals, $\Sigma$ terminals, $R$ rules, $S$ start symbol | Syntactic parsing |
| **Finite-State Transducer** | $T = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | $Q$ states, $\delta$ transitions, $F$ accepting states | Morphological generation |
| **Distributional Hypothesis** | $\text{sim}(w_1, w_2) \propto f(c(w_1), c(w_2))$ | Words with similar contexts have similar meanings | Word embeddings |
| **Substitution Test** | $X \to Y$ if $S = \alpha \, X \, \beta$ and $S' = \alpha \, Y \, \beta$ both grammatical | Replace $X$ with $Y$ in the same frame | Constituency testing |
| **n-gram Model** | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-(n-1)}, \ldots, w_{i-1})$ | Probability of a word given $n-1$ predecessors | Statistical language modelling |
| **Production Rule** | $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $A \in V$, $\beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^{\ast}$ | Rewrite non-terminal as string | Grammar induction |
| **Chomsky Hierarchy Level** | $\text{REG} \subset \text{CFG} \subset \text{CSG} \subset \text{REC}$ | Regular ⊊ Context-free ⊊ Context-sensitive ⊊ Recursive | Complexity classification |

Tables may break if the vertical bar is used inside a row. The above table is the *only* place where the symbol is rendered as the LaTeX command $\vert$ for safety.

## 2.3 Why and How — The Engineering Utility

- **Phonology → ASR systems**: Modern speech recognition pipelines begin with *acoustic feature extraction* (MFCC, spectrograms), which is the engineering heir of structuralist *phonetic transcription*.
- **Morphology → Finite-State Morphology**: Xerox-style two-level morphology (Koskenniemi, 1983) is a direct computational realisation of *Bloomfieldian morpheme-based analysis*.
- **Syntax → CFG / Dependency Grammars**: Harris's *Immediate Constituent* analysis is the formal ancestor of **Chomsky Normal Form** CFG rules.
- **Semantics → Distributional Semantics**: Firth's (and later Harris's) *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps"* is the *axiom* of Word2Vec, GloVe, and contextual embeddings.
- **Discourse → Statistical NLP**: Harris's *Methods in Structural Linguistics* (1951) is the first fully algorithmic grammar-induction textbook; Zellig Harris is rightly called *"the father of computational linguistics"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Take-Away (Board Expected):**
> *Structural linguistics converted linguistics from a humanistic discipline into a **formal, data-driven, algorithmic** science — and that conversion is the *birth certificate* of computational linguistics.*

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Phoneme Identification via Minimal Pairs

> **Given corpus (Hypothetical Malayalam-influenced English set):**
> `[pin]`, `[bin]`, `[tin]`, `[din]`, `[sin]`, `[spin]`, `[spin]`, `[span]`

**Step 1 — Align all words into segmental matrices:**

$$\begin{aligned}
p &\,|\, i \,|\, n \\
b &\,|\, i \,|\, n \\
t &\,|\, i \,|\, n \\
d &\,|\, i \,|\, n \\
s &\,|\, p \,|\, i \,|\, n \\
s &\,|\, p \,|\, a \,|\, n
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the minimal-pair test:** Two words differing in *exactly one* segment in *exactly the same* environment constitute a minimal pair; the differing segments are **contrastive phonemes**.

**Step 3 — Tabulate contrasts:**

| Word A | Word B | Differing Segment | Contrast Established |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pin` | `bin` | `/p/ \leftrightarrow /b/` | Voicing contrast |
| `tin` | `din` | `/t/ \leftrightarrow /d/` | Voicing contrast |
| `pin` | `tin` | `/p/ \leftrightarrow /t/` | Place + manner contrast |
| `spin` | `pin` | environment of `/p/` | Allophonic test |

**Step 4 — Inferred phoneme inventory:**

$$P = \{\, /p/, /b/, /t/, /d/, /s/, /i/, /æ/, /n/ \,\}$$

**Step 5 — Distinguish phoneme from allophone:** A pair of sounds in **complementary distribution** is *not* a phonemic contrast but an *allophonic* variation. The structural test is:

$$C(x) \cap C(y) = \emptyset \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x, y \text{ are allophones of the same phoneme}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Morpheme Analysis (English, structural)

> **Sentence:** *"The cats chased the mice but our cat chased nothing."*

**Step 1 — Apply substitution test for the smallest meaningful unit:**

- `cat` → `dog`, `rat`, `bird` (all yield grammatical, meaningful contrast) ⇒ `cat` is a *morpheme* ($m_1$).
- `s` → cannot be removed without losing grammaticality; `-s` after plural noun is a morpheme $m_2$.
- `chase` → `pursue`, `follow` ⇒ $m_3$.
- `-d` (past tense) → cannot be removed ⇒ $m_4$.
- `the`, `but`, `our`, `nothing` are each irreducible.

**Step 2 — Build the morphemic representation:**

$$\text{[The]}_{m_5} \;\; \text{[cat]}_{m_1} \;\; \text{[-s]}_{m_2} \;\; \text{[chase]}_{m_3} \;\; \text{[-d]}_{m_4} \;\; \text{[the]}_{m_5} \;\; \text{[mouse]}_{m_6} \;\; \text{[-s]}_{m_2} \;\; \text{[but]}_{m_7} \;\; \text{[our]}_{m_8} \;\; \text{[cat]}_{m_1} \;\; \text{[chase]}_{m_3} \;\; \text{[-d]}_{m_4} \;\; \text{[no]}_{m_9} \;\; \text{[thing]}_{m_{10}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the canonical morphological operations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Plural:} \quad & \text{Noun} + \text{-s} \\
\text{Past Tense:} \quad & \text{Verb} + \text{-d} / \text{-ed} \\
\text{Negation:} \quad & \text{un-} + \text{Adjective} \;\;\text{or}\;\; \text{no-} + \text{Noun}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Express each rule as a finite-state rewrite:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &\rightarrow \text{cat} \mid \text{dog} \mid \text{mouse} \\
N &\rightarrow N + \text{-s (Pl)} \\
V &\rightarrow \text{chase} \mid \text{follow} \mid \text{pursue} \\
V &\rightarrow V + \text{-d (Past)} \\
\text{Det} &\rightarrow \text{the} \mid \text{a} \mid \text{our}
\end{aligned}$$

This is precisely the *Bloomfieldian morpheme table* and is the formal kernel of **finite-state morphology** in computational systems.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Immediate Constituent (IC) Analysis

> **Sentence:** *"The little girl in the red dress smiled happily."*

**Step 1 — Apply the *substitution* (replacement) test:**

- `The little girl in the red dress` can be replaced by the single pro-form `She` ⇒ this is a **Noun Phrase (NP)**.
- `smiled happily` can be replaced by `slept` ⇒ this is a **Verb Phrase (VP)**.

**Step 2 — Recurse into the NP:**

- `The little girl` can be replaced by `She` ⇒ inner NP.
- `in the red dress` can be replaced by a single prepositional phrase marker ⇒ PP.

**Step 3 — Recurse into the inner NP and PP:**

- `The` → Det; `little` → Adj; `girl` → Noun.
- `in` → Prep; `the` → Det; `red` → Adj; `dress` → Noun.

**Step 4 — IC Tree (rendered in Section 4 as Mermaid).** Mathematically, the bracketing is:

$$\big[\,_{S}\;\; \big[\,_{NP}\;\; [\text{The}]_{Det}\; [\text{little}]_{Adj}\; [\text{girl}]_{N} \;\big]\; \big[\,_{PP}\; [\text{in}]_{P}\; \big[\,_{NP}\; [\text{the}]_{Det}\; [\text{red}]_{Adj}\; [\text{dress}]_{N} \;\big]\; \big]\; \big[\,_{VP}\; [\text{smiled}]_{V}\; [\text{happily}]_{Adv} \;\big]\; \big]$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent CFG productions (the computational translation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \; VP \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \; Adj \; N \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \; Adj \; N \; PP \\
PP &\rightarrow P \; NP \\
VP &\rightarrow V \; Adv
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Verify that the formal grammar is in Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) or near-CNF.** Each rule is either $A \rightarrow B \; C$ (binary) or $A \rightarrow a$ (terminal). This is the *direct inheritance* of structural IC analysis into formal language theory.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Distributional Hypothesis (Formal Statement and Operationalisation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Harris (1954) and Firth (1957), formalised:**
> *Linguistic units that occur in similar contexts have similar meanings.*

**Operational step 1 — Define the context of a word $w$ as a window:**

$$c(w) = \{w_{i-k}, w_{i-k+1}, \ldots, w_{i-1}, w_{i+1}, \ldots, w_{i+k}\}$$

**Operational step 2 — Define a context vector using co-occurrence counts:**

$$\vec{w} = \big(\, f(w, c_1), \; f(w, c_2), \; \ldots, \; f(w, c_n) \,\big)$$

**Operational step 3 — Define semantic similarity via cosine similarity:**

$$\text{sim}(w_1, w_2) = \frac{\vec{w_1} \cdot \vec{w_2}}{\vert \vec{w_1} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{w_2} \vert}$$

**Operational step 4 — Computational inference:**

$$\lim_{\vert C \vert \to \infty} \text{sim}(\text{cat}, \text{dog}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{high, because } c(\text{cat}) \approx c(\text{dog})$$

This is the bridge from **structural distributionalism** to **modern distributional semantics** (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Structural Concept to Computational Method

| Structural Concept | Modern Computational Realisation | Year of Bridging Publication |
|---|---|---|
| Phoneme inventory | Acoustic phoneme recognition (HMM-GMM, DNN) | 1970s–2010s |
| Allophonic rules | Finite-state phonological transducers | Kaplan & Kay, 1981 |
| Morpheme analysis | Two-level morphology | Koskenniemi, 1983 |
| IC analysis | CFG parsing algorithms (CKY, Earley) | 1961–1970 |
| Substitution test | Constituency tests in parser evaluation | 1990s |
| Distributional hypothesis | Word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) | Mikolov 2013, Pennington 2014 |
| Paradigmatic class | Word-class / POS induction | 1990s |
| Syntagmatic chain | n-gram language models | Shannon, 1948 |
| Discourse analysis (Harris) | Coreference resolution, RST | Grosz & Sidner, 1986 |
| Substitution frame | Pattern-matching in IE, regex | 1950s–present |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Take-Away:** Every NLP system you will ever build in this programme — from a regex tokenizer to a Transformer — is the *descendant* of one or more of the ten structural concepts in the matrix above.

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper 1, Module 1)]**
**Define structural linguistics. List its three levels of analysis with one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Structural linguistics is a 20th-century school of linguistic thought that studies language as an objective, self-contained system of formal and distributional relations, postponing semantic interpretation. The three levels are:

1. **Phonology** — study of the sound system. *Example:* Identifying that English `/p/` and `/b/` are two distinct phonemes because they form minimal pairs (e.g., *pin* vs *bin*).
2. **Morphology** — study of the smallest meaningful units. *Example:* Identifying that *unhappiness* decomposes into three morphemes: `un-` (negation), `happy` (root), `-ness` (nominaliser).
3. **Syntax** — study of how morphemes combine into larger units. *Example:* Bracketing *The small boy* as `[Det [Adj] [N]]`.

> **Word-count target:** 80–110 words. *Examiner allocates 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for levels and examples.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper 2, Module 1)]**
**Explain Saussure's distinction between *langue* and *parole* with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Langue** is the abstract, communal, rule-governed system of a language — the shared *code* that all members of a linguistic community internalise. **Parole** is the concrete, individual, situated act of using that code in real speech situations.

**Example:** All Malayalam speakers share the *langue* of Malayalam, which permits the word order *Subject-Object-Verb* in certain constructions. When Mrs. Radhika says *"Raman pustakam vaykkunnu"* (Raman book reads), she is performing an act of *parole*; the rule permitting the SOV order is part of *langue*.

> **Word-count target:** 70–100 words. *1 mark each for definitions, 1 mark for example.*

---

## Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Two questions A and B are printed, the student answers either A or B. Each is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.
