### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question 2024 Scheme]*

> **(a)** Explain the major principles of structural linguistics as developed by Bloomfield and the American school. Discuss the role of the *discovery procedure* in linguistic analysis. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** "Structural linguistics is the philosophical foundation of modern computational linguistics." Critically evaluate this statement with concrete examples of how the structuralist framework influenced the development of finite-state morphology and context-free grammar parsers. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Bloomfieldian Principles:**

1. **Empiricism / Behaviourism** — Only *observable* data (speech signals) are admissible; mentalism is rejected.
2. **Mechanism** — Language is described as a *system of stimuli and responses*; meaning is *"the situation in which the speaker utters it and the response it calls forth in the hearer"*.
3. **Formalist Bias** — Form is primary; meaning is treated as a *black box*.
4. **Discovery Procedure** — A *mechanical, replicable* algorithm for deriving the grammar of a language from a corpus.
5. **Distributionalism** — The *position* of a unit in the linguistic chain defines its identity.

**The Discovery Procedure (Steps):**

1. Collect and phonetically transcribe the corpus.
2. Group sounds into phonemes via minimal-pair testing.
3. Identify morphemes via substitution testing.
4. Bracket every sentence into its Immediate Constituents.
5. Tabulate all morpheme-distribution classes.
6. State the resulting grammar as a set of formal regularities.

> This is *the first known algorithmic grammar-induction procedure* and the direct ancestor of unsupervised NLP.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

The statement is true. Two illustrations:

**(i) Finite-State Morphology (Koskenniemi, 1983):**
The structural insight that morphotactics can be captured as a *finite network of form-representations* directly led to two-level morphology, which is the workhorse of every morphological analyser in NLP (e.g., the Finnish morphology in *Omorfi*, the Arabic morphology in *Buckwalter*).

**(ii) Context-Free Grammar Parsers (Chomsky 1956, CKY 1961):**
Harris's IC analysis was formalised as a *context-free grammar*. The CKY algorithm parses a sentence in $O(n^3)$ time using CNF rules — every modern constituency parser (including the *Stanford Parser*, *Berkeley Parser*) is the direct heir of this.

**Conclusion:** The structuralist commitment to *form*, *distribution*, and *algorithmic recoverability* is precisely the engineering mindset that defines computational linguistics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Board Notification):**
> Students lose marks by:
> 1. Confusing **Bloomfield** with **Chomsky** (they are *opposed*; Chomsky *rejected* structuralism in 1957).
> 2. Writing that structuralism "uses computers" (it predates them; it provided the *theoretical scaffolding* for computer applications).
> 3. Omitting Harris from the lineage. *Harris is the *missing link* between structuralism and computation.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question 2024 Scheme]*

> **(a)** What is *Immediate Constituent Analysis*? Using the sentence *"The tall boy from Kerala quickly opened the heavy wooden door"*, perform a complete IC bracketing and state the equivalent CFG rules. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the *distributional hypothesis* of structural linguistics. Show, with a worked numerical example, how it is operationalised in modern word-embedding models. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Definition:** IC Analysis (Harris, 1946) is a method of syntactic analysis that recursively decomposes a sentence into its *binary* or *multi-way* constituents, identifying the *immediate* (most local) sub-structures first.

**IC Bracketing of the sentence:**

$$\big[\,_{S}\; \big[\,_{NP}\; [\text{The}]_{Det}\; [\text{tall}]_{Adj}\; [\text{boy}]_{N}\; \big[\,_{PP}\; [\text{from}]_{P}\; [\text{Kerala}]_{PN}\; \big]\; \big]\; \big[\,_{VP}\; [\text{quickly}]_{Adv}\; [\text{opened}]_{V}\; \big[\,_{NP}\; [\text{the}]_{Det}\; [\text{heavy}]_{Adj}\; [\text{wooden}]_{Adj}\; [\text{door}]_{N}\; \big]\; \big]\; \big]$$

**Equivalent CFG (Chomsky Normal Form compatible):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \; VP \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \; Adj \; N \; PP \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \; Adj \; Adj \; N \\
PP &\rightarrow P \; NP \\
VP &\rightarrow Adv \; V \; NP
\end{aligned}$$

**Word-level terminals:**

$$\text{Det} \rightarrow \text{the} \quad\quad \text{Adj} \rightarrow \text{tall} \mid \text{heavy} \mid \text{wooden} \quad\quad \text{N} \rightarrow \text{boy} \mid \text{door} \quad\quad \text{PN} \rightarrow \text{Kerala} \quad\quad \text{P} \rightarrow \text{from} \quad\quad \text{V} \rightarrow \text{opened} \quad\quad \text{Adv} \rightarrow \text{quickly}$$

> Each rule maps to a node in the IC tree, and the *yield* of the tree (its terminal leaves read left-to-right) recovers the original sentence — this is the standard *derivation* test.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**The Distributional Hypothesis (Harris 1954, Firth 1957):**
*"Words that occur in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings."*

**Worked numerical example — word embedding via co-occurrence:**

Take a tiny corpus:
`"the cat sat on the mat"`, `"the dog sat on the rug"`, `"the cat chased the mouse"`, `"the dog chased the cat"`.

**Step 1 — Build a co-occurrence matrix $M$ using a symmetric window of size 2:**

| | cat | dog | sat | chased | mat | rug | mouse | on | the |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **cat** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 4 |
| **dog** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 4 |
| **mouse** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2 |

**Step 2 — Compute cosine similarity between `cat` and `dog`:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{\text{cat}} &= (0, 1, 1, 2, 1, 0, 1, 0, 4) \\
\vec{\text{dog}} &= (1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 4) \\
\vec{\text{cat}} \cdot \vec{\text{dog}} &= 0 + 0 + 1 + 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 16 = 19 \\
\vert \vec{\text{cat}} \vert &= \sqrt{0 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 16} = \sqrt{24} \approx 4.899 \\
\vert \vec{\text{dog}} \vert &= \sqrt{1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 16} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472 \\
\text{sim}(\text{cat}, \text{dog}) &= \frac{19}{4.899 \times 4.472} \approx \frac{19}{21.91} \approx 0.867
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with `cat` vs `mouse`:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{\text{cat}} \cdot \vec{\text{mouse}} &= 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 8 = 8 \\
\vert \vec{\text{mouse}} \vert &= \sqrt{1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \\
\text{sim}(\text{cat}, \text{mouse}) &= \frac{8}{4.899 \times 2.236} \approx \frac{8}{10.95} \approx 0.730
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**

$$\text{sim}(\text{cat}, \text{dog}) = 0.867 \;>\; \text{sim}(\text{cat}, \text{mouse}) = 0.730$$

The distributional hypothesis is confirmed: *cat* and *dog* are more similar than *cat* and *mouse* because they share more co-occurring words (`sat`, `chased`, `the`). This is precisely the algorithmic embodiment of the structuralist insight — and the *exact* mechanism (in scaled-up form) used by Word2Vec, GloVe, and BERT.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Board Notification):**
> Students commonly lose marks in Q B(b) by:
> 1. Confusing the *distributional hypothesis* with the *distributional method* (the former is a *claim about meaning*; the latter is a *discovery procedure*).
> 2. Forgetting to *normalise* the co-occurrence vector before computing similarity.
> 3. Writing the answer as pure prose without showing the matrix and the numerical computation — *this is a 7-mark question and demands a worked numerical example*.

---

## Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 1 (Linguistic Theories and Models)**

- **Structural Linguistics** = objective, formal, distributional, meaning-postponed study of language.
- **Three Founding Pillars:** Saussure (Europe), Bloomfield (US), Sapir (US).
- **Langue vs Parole** — abstract system vs concrete utterance; *structural linguistics studies langue*.
- **Sign** = Signifier (form) + Signified (concept); the bond is *arbitrary*.
- **Two Axes:** *Syntagmatic* (chain) and *Paradigmatic* (substitution class).
- **Three Levels:** Phonology (phoneme) → Morphology (morpheme) → Syntax (constituent).
- **Distribution** = total of environments of a unit; *complementary distribution* = allophony.
- **Bloomfield's Discovery Procedure** = the *first algorithmic* grammar-induction method.
- **IC Analysis (Harris 1946):** recursive binary/multi-way decomposition; yields a *constituency tree*.
- **IC Analysis → CFG:** every IC rule maps to a *production rule*; CNF conversion is mechanical.
- **Distributional Hypothesis (Harris, Firth):** similar contexts → similar meanings → *operationalised in word embeddings*.
- **Saussure's Arbitrariness of the Sign** is the philosophical ancestor of *vector representations of meaning*.
- **Zellig Harris = Father of Computational Linguistics** (his 1951 book is the field's founding text).
- **Chomsky (1957) ≠ Structuralist** — he *rejected* structuralism; however, his CFG *formalised* Harris's IC analysis.
- **Engineering descendants:** Finite-State Morphology, CFG Parsers, Distributional Semantics, n-gram models.
- **Board-friendly one-liner:** *"Structural linguistics made language formal; computational linguistics made language machine-tractable."*

> **Last-Month Revision Strategy:** Memorise the **High-Yield Formula Sheet** (Section 2.2), reproduce the **IC bracketing example** from Section 3.3 *from memory*, and rehearse the **cosine-similarity numerical** in Section 3.4 / Q B(b). Together these account for an estimated **60–70 %** of Module 1 marks in the ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chomsky's Hierarchy: Regular Languages, Context-Free Grammars, and Their Computational Relevance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chomsky's Hierarchy: Regular Languages, Context-Free Grammars, and Their Computational Relevance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Chomsky Hierarchy

**Chomsky's Hierarchy** is a foundational containment hierarchy of four classes of formal grammars proposed by the linguist **Avram Noam Chomsky** in 1956. It classifies formal languages into **Type 0, Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3**, ordered by increasing restrictiveness on production rules. Each grammar type corresponds to a specific class of formal language and a specific abstract machine (automaton) that recognizes it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Chomsky's Hierarchy is a classification of formal grammars into four types — Type 0 (Unrestricted), Type 1 (Context-Sensitive), Type 2 (Context-Free), and Type 3 (Regular) — based on the structural constraints imposed on their production rules. The hierarchy establishes a strict containment relationship where each lower type is a proper subset of the type above it."*

The four classes, from most powerful to least powerful, are:

| Hierarchy Level | Grammar Type | Language Class | Recognizing Automaton |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| Type 0 | Unrestricted (Recursively Enumerable) | Recursively Enumerable Languages | Turing Machine |
| Type 1 | Context-Sensitive | Context-Sensitive Languages | Linear-Bounded Automaton |
| Type 2 | Context-Free | Context-Free Languages | Pushdown Automaton |
| Type 3 | Regular | Regular Languages | Finite Automaton |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Language Learning Tower"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Building a Vocabulary Tower**
> Imagine learning a new natural language (say, English):
> - **Type 3 (Regular)** = Learning the **alphabet** and **simple words** (e.g., "cat", "dog") — you can recognize patterns but cannot form nested structures.
> - **Type 2 (Context-Free)** = Learning **sentence structure** — you can match opening and closing brackets, embed clauses inside sentences (`[The [student [who passed]] smiled]`).
> - **Type 1 (Context-Sensitive)** = Learning **meaning-dependent rules** — the form of a word depends on its surroundings (e.g., "I am" vs. "He is").
> - **Type 0 (Unrestricted)** = Learning the **full expressive power** of human language, including contradictions, paradoxes, and self-referential statements.
>
> Each level **contains** the level below it (every regular language is also context-free), but each level adds **more expressive power** and requires a **more powerful machine** to recognize.

## 1.3 Mathematical Set-Theoretic Formulation

The strict containment of the Chomsky hierarchy is expressed mathematically as:

$$
\mathcal{L}_3 \subsetneq \mathcal{L}_2 \subsetneq \mathcal{L}_1 \subsetneq \mathcal{L}_0
$$

Where $\mathcal{L}_i$ denotes the class of languages generated by grammars of Type $i$. The subset symbol $\subsetneq$ indicates **proper subset**, meaning each class is strictly contained within the next broader class.

## 1.4 Definition of a Formal Grammar

A **formal grammar** $G$ is defined as a 4-tuple:

$$
G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)
$$

Where:
- $V$ is a finite set of **non-terminal symbols** (variables).
- $\Sigma$ is a finite set of **terminal symbols** (alphabet), with $V \cap \Sigma = \emptyset$.
- $R$ is a finite set of **production rules** of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$.
- $S \in V$ is the designated **start symbol**.

> [!TIP]
> **Computational Relevance Note:** Every modern programming language compiler, every natural language processing (NLP) pipeline, and every XML/HTML parser ultimately relies on a variant of one of these grammars to determine whether a given string is "valid" in that language. Understanding the hierarchy helps computational linguists choose the right level of grammatical formalism for their parsing task.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Grammar Types — Operational Breakdown

### Type 0 — Unrestricted Grammars

Production rules of the form:

$$
\alpha \rightarrow \beta
$$

Where $\alpha \in (V \cup \Sigma)^+$ (i.e., a non-empty string of terminals and non-terminals) and $\beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^*$ (i.e., any string including the empty string $\varepsilon$).

- **No restrictions** on the left-hand side (LHS) or right-hand side (RHS).
- Generates all **recursively enumerable languages**.
- The membership problem is **undecidable** in the general case.
- **Recognizer:** Turing Machine (TM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Recursively Enumerable"?** Because there exists a Turing Machine that, given any string $w \in \mathcal{L}(G)$, will halt and accept it. However, for strings $w \notin \mathcal{L}(G)$, the TM may loop forever — hence "enumerable" rather than "decidable."

### Type 1 — Context-Sensitive Grammars (CSG)

Production rules of the form:

$$
\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta
$$

Where $A \in V$, $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^*$, and $\gamma \in (V \cup \Sigma)^+$.

Equivalent compact form: productions of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ where $\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$ (the RHS is at least as long as the LHS — this is called **non-contracting**).

- The rule can only be applied if the non-terminal $A$ is surrounded by the specific context $\alpha \_\, \beta$.
- The language $\mathcal{L}(G)$ is called a **Context-Sensitive Language (CSL)**.
- The membership problem is **decidable** but **PSPACE-complete**.
- **Recognizer:** Linear-Bounded Automaton (LBA).
- Cannot generate the empty string $\varepsilon$ (except by convention via $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ if $S$ never appears on the RHS).

### Type 2 — Context-Free Grammars (CFG)

Production rules of the form:

$$
A \rightarrow \gamma
$$

Where $A \in V$ and $\gamma \in (V \cup \Sigma)^*$.

- The LHS must be a **single non-terminal** with no context surrounding it.
- Generates **Context-Free Languages (CFLs)**.
- The membership problem is decidable in polynomial time $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ using algorithms like **CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami)**.
- **Recognizer:** Pushdown Automaton (PDA).
- CFGs are the backbone of **programming language syntax** (e.g., parsing expressions, statements, functions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF):** Every Type 2 grammar can be mechanically transformed into an equivalent CNF where all rules have the form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (where $A, B, C \in V$ and $a \in \Sigma$). This is essential for the CYK parser used in computational linguistics.

### Type 3 — Regular Grammars (RG)

Two equivalent forms:

**Right-Linear Grammar:**

$$
A \rightarrow wB \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow w
$$

**Left-Linear Grammar:**

$$
A \rightarrow Bw \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow w
$$

Where $A, B \in V$ and $w \in \Sigma^*$.

- Generates **Regular Languages (RLs)**.
- The membership problem is decidable in **linear time** $\mathcal{O}(n)$.
- **Recognizer:** Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) or Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA).
- Equivalent in power to **regular expressions** and **regular languages**.

## 2.2 The Automata Correspondence (Chomsky–Schützenberger Theorem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Computational Linguistics:** When you build a tokenizer, a lexical scanner (like `lex`/`flex`), or a regular-expression-based pattern matcher in NLP, you are implicitly using Type 3 grammars and finite automata. When you build a syntactic parser (like those in NLTK, spaCy, or Stanford CoreNLP), you are implicitly using Type 2 grammars and pushdown automata.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Property | Type 3 (Regular) | Type 2 (Context-Free) | Type 1 (Context-Sensitive) | Type 0 (Unrestricted) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Production Rule Form** | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | $A \rightarrow \gamma$ | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ (any) |
| **LHS Constraint** | Single non-terminal | Single non-terminal | Non-terminal with context | Any non-empty string |
| **RHS Constraint** | Terminal + optional non-terminal | Any string of terminals/non-terminals | Non-empty, longer than LHS | Any string (including $\varepsilon$) |
| **Language Class** | $\mathcal{L}_3$ | $\mathcal{L}_2$ | $\mathcal{L}_1$ | $\mathcal{L}_0$ |
| **Recognizer** | DFA / NFA | Pushdown Automaton (PDA) | Linear-Bounded Automaton (LBA) | Turing Machine (TM) |
| **Membership Decidability** | Decidable | Decidable | Decidable | Undecidable |
| **Membership Time Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ (CYK) | PSPACE-complete | Undecidable |
| **Closure under Union** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Closure under Intersection** | Yes | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| **Closure under Complement** | Yes | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| **Pumping Lemma Exists?** | Yes | Yes (for CFLs) | No general form | N/A |
| **Real-World Use** | Lexical analysis, regex | Programming language syntax, NLP parsing | Some natural language phenomena | General computation, $\lambda$-calculus |

**Key Closure Formulas (for board exam short notes):**

- **Regular Languages are closed under:** Union, Intersection, Complement, Concatenation, Kleene Star, Reversal.
- **Context-Free Languages are closed under:** Union, Concatenation, Kleene Star, Reversal.
- **Context-Free Languages are NOT closed under:** Intersection, Complement, Subtraction.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The closure properties are critical when designing NLP pipelines. For example, if you need to compute the difference of two regular languages (e.g., remove stopwords from a regular expression pattern), you can do so directly because RLs are closed under set difference. For CFLs, you must instead use the **Pumping Lemma** to prove non-membership, because set operations may not preserve the CFL property.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Relevance

| Application Domain | Grammar Type Used | Reason |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Lexical Analyzers** (`lex`, `flex`, regex engines) | Type 3 (Regular) | Token patterns are regular; linear-time matching is essential |
| **Programming Language Parsers** (YACC, Bison, ANTLR) | Type 2 (CFG) | Nested structures (if-else, loops, functions) require CFG power |
| **XML / HTML / JSON Validators** | Type 2 (CFG) | Markup languages have nested tag structures |
| **Natural Language Syntax** (Penn Treebank parsers) | Type 2 with feature extensions (TAG, LFG) | Human language exhibits context-free patterns |
| **Computational Phonology / Morphology** | Type 3 (finite-state morphology) | Morphological rules are largely concatenative and regular |
| **DNA / Protein Sequence Analysis** | Type 0 (Hidden Markov Models, grammars) | Biological sequences require more expressive power than regular |
| **Formal Verification / Model Checking** | Type 0 / Type 1 | Program verification needs unrestricted expressive power |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Classifying a Grammar into Chomsky's Hierarchy

**Problem:** Classify each of the following grammars into its Chomsky type. Justify your answer.

**Grammar G1:**

$$
S \rightarrow aS \mid b
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the form of the production rules.

The rules are $S \rightarrow aS$ and $S \rightarrow b$.

**Step 2:** Compare with the canonical forms of each Chomsky type.

The LHS is a single non-terminal $S$. The RHS consists of either a single terminal $b$ or a terminal followed by a non-terminal $aS$.

**Step 3:** Match to the closest canonical form.

This matches the **right-linear grammar** form: $A \rightarrow wB$ or $A \rightarrow w$, where here $w = a$ and $B = S$ for the first rule, and $w = b$ with no $B$ for the second.

**Step 4:** State the conclusion.

$$
G_1 \text{ is a Type 3 (Regular) Grammar.}
$$

The language it generates is $\mathcal{L}(G_1) = \{a^n b \mid n \geq 0\}$, which is a classic regular language.

**Step 5:** Provide the corresponding finite automaton.

A DFA that accepts this language has two states: a start/loop state that reads $a$'s, and an accepting state that reads $b$.

---

**Grammar G2:**

$$
S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the form of the production rules.

The rules are $S \rightarrow aSb$ and $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

**Step 2:** Check if it is regular.

For Type 3, the RHS must be of the form $wB$ or $w$. The rule $S \rightarrow aSb$ has a terminal $a$ on the left and a terminal $b$ on the right of the non-terminal $S$, which violates the right-linear (or left-linear) restriction. So G2 is **not regular**.

**Step 3:** Check if it is context-free.

The LHS is a single non-terminal $S$ with no surrounding context, which matches the Type 2 form $A \rightarrow \gamma$.

**Step 4:** State the conclusion.

$$
G_2 \text{ is a Type 2 (Context-Free) Grammar.}
$$

The language $\mathcal{L}(G_2) = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is a **classic non-regular but context-free** language. This is the canonical example used to demonstrate that CFGs are strictly more powerful than regular grammars.

---

**Grammar G3:**

$$
aSb \rightarrow aacb
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the form of the production rules.

The LHS is $aSb$ which is a string of mixed terminals and non-terminals.

**Step 2:** Check for context-sensitivity.

The rule has a non-terminal $S$ in the context of $a\_\,b$, and the RHS replaces $S$ with $ac$ in that same context. This matches the Type 1 form $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ with $\alpha = a$, $A = S$, $\beta = b$, and $\gamma = ac$.

Also, $\vert aSb \vert = 3 \leq \vert aacb \vert = 4$, satisfying the non-contracting property.

**Step 3:** State the conclusion.

$$
G_3 \text{ is a Type 1 (Context-Sensitive) Grammar.}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Conversion of CFG to Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)

**Problem:** Convert the following CFG to Chomsky Normal Form.

**Original Grammar G:**

$$
S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB
$$

$$
A \rightarrow B \mid S
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b \mid \varepsilon
$$

---

### Step 1: Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions

Find nullable variables (those that can derive $\varepsilon$).

$B$ is nullable because $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

Then any rule containing $B$ on the RHS gives a new nullable variable if all symbols on the RHS are nullable. Looking at $A \rightarrow B$, since $B$ is nullable, $A$ is also nullable.

Now substitute all combinations. Original rules with $B$ or $A$ on RHS:

- $S \rightarrow ASA$ — $A$ is nullable, so add $S \rightarrow SA$ and $S \rightarrow AS$ and $S \rightarrow S$.
- $S \rightarrow aB$ — $B$ is nullable, so add $S \rightarrow a$.
- $A \rightarrow B$ — $B$ is nullable, so add $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- $A \rightarrow S$ — keep as is.
- $B \rightarrow b$ — keep as is.

Removing the $\varepsilon$-productions themselves, the new grammar $G_1$ is:

$$
S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB \mid SA \mid AS \mid S \mid a
$$

$$
A \rightarrow B \mid S \mid \varepsilon
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b
$$

---

### Step 2: Eliminate unit productions (rules of the form $A \rightarrow B$)

Find unit pairs: $(S, S), (S, A), (S, B), (A, A), (A, S), (A, B), (B, B)$.

Expanding each:

- From $(S, S)$: no new rules (self-loop).
- From $(S, A)$: take $A \rightarrow B \mid S \mid \varepsilon$. Add $S \rightarrow B$, $S \rightarrow S$, $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$. But $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ must be handled (we keep it for now and remove later if needed).
- From $(S, B)$: $B \rightarrow b$. Add $S \rightarrow b$.
- From $(A, S)$: take $S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB \mid SA \mid AS \mid a$. Add $A \rightarrow ASA, A \rightarrow aB, A \rightarrow SA, A \rightarrow AS, A \rightarrow a$.
- From $(A, B)$: $B \rightarrow b$. Add $A \rightarrow b$.

The new grammar $G_2$ (removing unit productions and $\varepsilon$-productions) is:

$$
S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB \mid SA \mid AS \mid a \mid b
$$

$$
A \rightarrow b \mid ASA \mid aB \mid SA \mid AS \mid a
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b
$$

---

### Step 3: Replace terminals in mixed rules with new variables

Rules with mixed RHS (terminals + non-terminals) longer than 1:

- $S \rightarrow aB$ — replace $a$ with new variable $C_a$. Rule becomes $S \rightarrow C_a B$. Add $C_a \rightarrow a$.
- $S \rightarrow AS$ — already a single non-terminal after expansion? Wait, $AS$ is two non-terminals, so it is fine for CNF.
- All other rules either end in a single non-terminal (after expansion) or a single terminal.

Wait — for strict CNF, rules must be of form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$. The rule $S \rightarrow AS$ is already in CNF form.

The rule $S \rightarrow aB$ becomes $S \rightarrow C_a B$ with helper rule $C_a \rightarrow a$.

Similarly $A \rightarrow aB$ becomes $A \rightarrow C_a B$.

The new grammar $G_3$ is:

$$
S \rightarrow AS \mid SA \mid ASA \mid C_a B \mid a \mid b
$$

$$
A \rightarrow AS \mid SA \mid ASA \mid C_a B \mid a \mid b
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b
$$

$$
C_a \rightarrow a
$$

---

### Step 4: Break rules with more than 2 non-terminals on RHS

The rule $S \rightarrow ASA$ has three non-terminals. Introduce new variable $C_{AS}$ such that $AS \rightarrow C_{AS}$ and replace $ASA$ with $A \cdot C_{AS}$.

Similarly for $A \rightarrow ASA$.

Final CNF grammar $G_{CNF}$:

$$
S \rightarrow AS \mid SA \mid A C_{AS} \mid C_a B \mid a \mid b
$$

$$
A \rightarrow AS \mid SA \mid A C_{AS} \mid C_a B \mid a \mid b
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b
$$

$$
C_a \rightarrow a
$$

$$
C_{AS} \rightarrow AS
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of CNF properties:**
> - Every rule has the LHS as a single non-terminal.
> - Every rule's RHS is either a single terminal ($S \rightarrow a$, $B \rightarrow b$, $C_a \rightarrow a$) or exactly two non-terminals ($S \rightarrow AS$, $S \rightarrow C_a B$, $C_{AS} \rightarrow AS$, etc.).
> - This satisfies Chomsky Normal Form.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decidability and Closure Across the Hierarchy

| Property | $\mathcal{L}_3$ (Reg) | $\mathcal{L}_2$ (CFL) | $\mathcal{L}_1$ (CSL) | $\mathcal{L}_0$ (RE) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Membership ($w \in L$?) | D, $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | D, $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ | D, PSPACE | U (Undecidable) |
| Emptiness ($L = \emptyset$?) | D | D | U | U |
| Finiteness ($L$ finite?) | D | D | U | U |
| Equivalence ($L_1 = L_2$?) | D | D | U | U |
| Inclusion ($L_1 \subseteq L_2$?) | D | U | U | U |
| Closure under Union | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Closure under Intersection | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Closure under Complement | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Closure under Concatenation | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Closure under Kleene Star | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Closure under Homomorphism | ✓ | ✓ | ND | ND |
| Pumping Lemma | Yes | Yes (Ogden's) | No | No |

Where **D = Decidable**, **U = Undecidable**, **ND = Not Decidable in General**, **✓ = Closed**, **✗ = Not Closed**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Mnemonic — "RCRC":** The properties that are **closed for Regular** (R), **CFL** (C), **RE** (R), and **CSL** (C) follow the pattern: Regular and Recursively Enumerable are closed under intersection, but Context-Free Languages are NOT. This pattern reappears in nearly every computational linguistics exam.

## 3.4 Derivation: Why $\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is NOT Regular

**Setup:** Use the **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages**.

The Pumping Lemma states: If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a **pumping length** $p \geq 1$ such that every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$ can be decomposed as $s = xyz$ satisfying:

1. $\vert xy \vert \leq p$
2. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$
3. $xy^i z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Proof by contradiction:**

**Step 1:** Assume $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is regular. Then by the Pumping Lemma, there exists a pumping length $p$.

**Step 2:** Choose the string $s = a^p b^p$. Note that $\vert s \vert = 2p \geq p$, so the lemma applies.

**Step 3:** By the Pumping Lemma, $s = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$.

Since $\vert xy \vert \leq p$, both $x$ and $y$ must consist entirely of $a$'s. So $y = a^k$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4:** Consider the pumped string $xy^2 z = a^{p+k} b^p$.

This string has $p+k$ a's but only $p$ b's, so $p + k \neq p$. Therefore:

$$
xy^2 z \notin L
$$

**Step 5:** This contradicts the Pumping Lemma's conclusion that $xy^i z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.

**Step 6:** Therefore, our assumption is false.

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L} = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\} \text{ is NOT a regular language.}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\{a^n b^n\}$ is not regular but **is** generated by the CFG $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$, this proves that **Type 2 grammars are strictly more powerful than Type 3 grammars**, confirming the strict containment $\mathcal{L}_3 \subsetneq \mathcal{L}_2$.
