topics.title: Structural Hierarchy and Coping with Syntactic Ambiguity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structural Hierarchy and Coping with Syntactic Ambiguity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Structural Hierarchy — Formal Definition

**Structural Hierarchy** in computational linguistics is the formal, ordered organization of linguistic units from the smallest meaningful elements (**morphemes**) up to the largest coherent structures (**sentences/discourse**). It defines how smaller constituents recursively combine—governed by a formal grammar (typically a **Context-Free Grammar, CFG**, or **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar, PCFG**)—to form larger constituents following a strict tree-like organization.

The canonical hierarchy is:
> **Morpheme → Word → Phrase → Clause → Sentence → Discourse**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Structural hierarchy is implemented computationally through **Phrase Structure Trees (PSTs)** or **Parse Trees**, where every non-terminal node represents a constituent category (NP, VP, PP, S) and every terminal node represents a lexical item (a word).

### 1.2 Syntactic Ambiguity — Formal Definition

**Syntactic ambiguity** (also called *structural ambiguity* or *grammatical ambiguity*) occurs when a single sentence sequence admits **two or more valid phrase-structure parse trees** under a given grammar $G$, leading to more than one distinct syntactic interpretation. Formally, a sentence $w$ is syntactically ambiguous under grammar $G$ iff:

$$\vert \{ T_i \mid T_i \text{ is a valid parse tree of } w \text{ in } G \} \vert \geq 2$$

### 1.3 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Analogy"**
> Think of a sentence as a **building**:
> - **Bricks** = Morphemes (smallest units, e.g., *un-, -happy, -ness*)
> - **Walls** = Words (bricks joined into functional units)
> - **Rooms** = Phrases (NP, VP, PP — each room has a clear function)
> - **Floors** = Clauses (a self-contained unit of meaning)
> - **The entire building** = Sentence
>
> Just as a building blueprint (parse tree) shows how rooms are nested inside floors, a parse tree shows how phrases are nested inside clauses inside a sentence.

> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Traffic Signal" for Ambiguity**
> When a driver hears "I saw the man with the telescope," they must decide *with what* they saw the man — with their own eyes or with a telescope. This is **PP-attachment ambiguity**. The traffic signal here is **context + world knowledge**, which the brain uses automatically. Computational linguistics builds algorithms to mimic this disambiguation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Ambiguity is the *single most important problem* in syntax-based NLP. The famous sentence *"I saw the man with the telescope"* has **two valid parse trees** — a classic exam trap.

### 1.4 Key Metrics (Standard NLP Constants)

- **Average sentence length in English:** $\approx$ **15–20 words**
- **Ambiguity rate in natural language:** $\approx$ **1 ambiguous structure per 4 sentences** (in unrestricted text)
- **Parse tree depth (typical):** **5–8 levels** for an English sentence
- **CYK algorithm complexity:** $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ — cubic in sentence length
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Structural Hierarchy — Layered Breakdown

**Layer 1 — Morpheme Level**
- Smallest meaning-bearing unit; cannot be further divided without losing meaning.
- Example: *unhappiness* → {*un-, happy, -ness*} — three morphemes.

**Layer 2 — Word Level**
- A morpheme (or combination) functioning as a single lexical item with a Part-of-Speech (POS) tag.
- Example: *"cats"* → POS = **NNS** (Noun, Plural).

**Layer 3 — Phrase Level (Constituents)**
- A group of words forming a **single grammatical unit**. Core types:
  - **NP** — Noun Phrase (*"the red car"*)
  - **VP** — Verb Phrase (*"quickly ran away"*)
  - **PP** — Prepositional Phrase (*"on the table"*)
  - **AP** — Adjectival Phrase (*"very tall"*)
  - **AdvP** — Adverbial Phrase (*"extremely quickly"*)

**Layer 4 — Clause Level**
- A clause contains a **subject + predicate** unit. Types: independent (main) and dependent (subordinate).

**Layer 5 — Sentence Level**
- The maximal unit of syntactic analysis, often denoted **S** or **IP** (Inflectional Phrase).

**Why this matters:** Each layer provides **compositional meaning** — meaning is built bottom-up via the principle of **compositionality** (Frege's Principle): *the meaning of a whole is a function of the meanings of its parts and the way they are syntactically combined.*

### 2.2 Types of Syntactic Ambiguity

| **Type** | **Description** | **Classic Example** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Attachment Ambiguity** | A phrase (esp. PP) can attach at multiple nodes | *"I saw the man with the telescope"* |
| **Coordination Ambiguity** | Scope of the conjunction (*and/or*) is unclear | *"old men and women"* — *old [men and women]* vs. *[old men] and [women]* |
| **Scope Ambiguity** | Scope of quantifiers/negation is unclear | *"Every student read a book"* — *one book* or *a different book per student*? |
| **Garden-Path Ambiguity** | Initial parse misleads the reader | *"The horse raced past the barn fell."* |
| **NP/VP Bracketing Ambiguity** | Binary bracketing of constituents unclear | *"tall students and teachers"* |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Rule** | **Meaning** | **Use Case** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | Context-Free Grammar: terminals, non-terminals, productions, start symbol | Defining formal grammar |
| $w \in L(G)$ iff $S \Rightarrow^* w$ | Sentence $w$ is in language $L$ iff derivable from $S$ | Membership test |
| $P(\text{parse}) = \prod_i P(\text{rule}_i)$ | PCFG probability = product of rule probabilities | Probabilistic disambiguation |
| $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T P(T \mid w)$ | Best parse = argmax probability tree for sentence | Statistical parsing |
| $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Correct constituents / total gold constituents | Parser evaluation |
| $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Correct constituents / total predicted constituents | Parser evaluation |
| $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall | Parser evaluation |
| $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ | Worst-case CFG parsing (CYK/Early) | Complexity analysis |
| $\text{Cross-Entropy}(H) = -\sum p(x) \log q(x)$ | Probabilistic model evaluation | LM-based parsing |
| $P(\text{attach to NP}) = \frac{\text{count(NP-att)}}{\text{count(PP-att total)}}$ | Lexical preference probability | PP-attachment resolution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Students:** When writing absolute values or set cardinalities, always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ (with `\vert` or `\mid`) — never the bare `|` pipe — to maintain correct LaTeX rendering in your KTU answer sheets when transcribed digitally.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Translation (Google Translate, IndicTrans):** Disambiguating source-language structure to pick correct target-language word order.
- **Information Extraction:** Resolving which entity a modifier attaches to in biomedical text.
- **Question Answering Systems:** Determining *what* the question is asking (subject, object, instrument).
- **Sentiment Analysis:** *Not all good food* — scope of negation determines sentiment polarity.
- **Grammar Checkers (Grammarly, LanguageTool):** Detect and flag structural ambiguities.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Building a Parse Tree from CFG Productions

**Problem:** Parse the sentence *"The cat sleeps"* using the CFG $G$ with productions $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow NP \; VP \\
NP &\rightarrow Det \; N \\
VP &\rightarrow V \\
Det &\rightarrow \text{``the''} \\
N &\rightarrow \text{``cat''} \\
V &\rightarrow \text{``sleeps''}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & S \\
\text{Apply } S \rightarrow NP \; VP: \quad & NP \; VP \\
\text{Apply } NP \rightarrow Det \; N: \quad & Det \; N \; VP \\
\text{Apply } Det \rightarrow \text{``the''}: \quad & \text{``the''} \; N \; VP \\
\text{Apply } N \rightarrow \text{``cat''}: \quad & \text{``the''} \; \text{``cat''} \; VP \\
\text{Apply } VP \rightarrow V: \quad & \text{``the''} \; \text{``cat''} \; V \\
\text{Apply } V \rightarrow \text{``sleeps''}: \quad & \text{``the''} \; \text{``cat''} \; \text{``sleeps''} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting Parse Tree (textual):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[S \; [NP \; [Det \; \text{``the''}] \; [N \; \text{``cat''}]] \; [VP \; [V \; \text{``sleeps''}]]]
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Demonstrating Syntactic Ambiguity (Two Trees)

**Sentence:** *"I saw the man with the telescope"*

**Grammar Extension (additions to $G$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP &\rightarrow P \; NP \\
P &\rightarrow \text{``with''} \\
VP &\rightarrow VP \; PP \\
NP &\rightarrow NP \; PP
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parse Tree 1 (PP attaches to VP — "telescope" is the instrument of seeing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& [S \; [NP \; [Pron \; \text{``I''}]] \; [VP \; [V \; \text{``saw''}] \\
& \qquad [NP \; [Det \; \text{``the''}] \; [N \; \text{``man''}] \; [PP \; [P \; \text{``with''}] \\
& \qquad\qquad [NP \; [Det \; \text{``the''}] \; [N \; \text{``telescope''}]]]]]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parse Tree 2 (PP attaches to NP — "man" possesses the telescope):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& [S \; [NP \; [Pron \; \text{``I''}]] \; [VP \; [V \; \text{``saw''}] \\
& \qquad [NP \; [Det \; \text{``the''}] \; [N \; \text{``man''}] \; [PP \; [P \; \text{``with''}] \\
& \qquad\qquad [NP \; [Det \; \text{``the''}] \; [N \; \text{``telescope''}]]] \\
& \qquad\qquad [PP \; \ldots]]
\end{aligned}
$$

Both trees are **grammatically valid** — confirming syntactic ambiguity.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: PCFG Disambiguation by Probability Maximization

**Setup:** Given two valid parse trees $T_1$ and $T_2$ for the same sentence $w$, choose the one with maximum probability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T_1 \mid w) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n_1} P(r_i) \\
P(T_2 \mid w) &= \prod_{j=1}^{n_2} P(r_j) \\
\hat{T} &= \arg\max_{T \in \{T_1, T_2\}} P(T \mid w)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose the rule probabilities (estimated from a treebank) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(VP \rightarrow V \; NP) &= 0.30 \\
P(VP \rightarrow V \; NP \; PP) &= 0.05 \\
P(NP \rightarrow NP \; PP) &= 0.20 \\
P(PP \rightarrow P \; NP) &= 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

For the sentence *"I saw the man with the telescope"*:

**Tree 1 (PP-attaches-to-VP, instrument reading):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T_1) &= P(S \rightarrow NP \; VP) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow PRP) \cdot P(VP \rightarrow V \; NP \; PP) \\
&\quad \cdot P(V \rightarrow \text{``saw''}) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow Det \; N) \\
&\quad \cdot P(PP \rightarrow P \; NP) \cdot P(P \rightarrow \text{``with''}) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow Det \; N) \\
&\quad \cdot P(Det \rightarrow \text{``the''}) \cdot P(N \rightarrow \text{``man''}) \cdot P(N \rightarrow \text{``telescope''}) \\
&\quad \cdot P(Det \rightarrow \text{``the''})
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming lexical probabilities: $P(\text{``saw''} \mid V) = 0.001$, $P(\text{``the''} \mid Det) = 0.004$, $P(\text{``man''} \mid N) = 0.0001$, $P(\text{``telescope''} \mid N) = 0.00005$, $P(PRP \rightarrow \text{``I''}) = 0.002$, $P(S \rightarrow NP \; VP) = 1.0$, $P(P \rightarrow \text{``with''}) = 0.01$, $P(NP \rightarrow Det \; N) = 0.6$, $P(NP \rightarrow PRP) = 0.1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T_1) &= 1.0 \times 0.1 \times 0.05 \times 0.001 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.01 \times 0.6 \\
&\quad \times 0.004 \times 0.0001 \times 0.004 \times 0.00005 \\
P(T_1) &\approx 1.44 \times 10^{-20}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tree 2 (PP-attaches-to-NP, possessive reading):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T_2) &= P(S \rightarrow NP \; VP) \cdot P(VP \rightarrow V \; NP) \cdot P(NP \rightarrow NP \; PP) \\
&\quad \cdot P(PP \rightarrow P \; NP) \cdot \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision rule:** Compare $\log P(T_1)$ vs. $\log P(T_2)$; the tree with the higher log-probability wins. PCFGs prefer **VP-attachment** in this case because lexical statistics in treebanks show that prepositional phrases modifying the verb (instrument/locative) are more frequent than those modifying a noun immediately after another noun.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Disambiguation Strategies

| **Strategy** | **Principle** | **Data Required** | **Limitation** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Selectional Restrictions** | Verb selects semantically compatible arguments | Lexicon + semantic classes | Rule maintenance cost |
| **Subcategorization** | Each verb specifies valid VP complements | Subcat. frames | Sparse for rare verbs |
| **Frequency / PCFG** | Most common parse wins | Treebank statistics | Ignores lexical semantics |
| **Lexicalized Parsing** | Specific head-word attachments weighted | Lexically annotated corpus | High model complexity |
| **Dependency Distance** | Prefer parses with shorter dependencies | None (structural) | Fails on long-range deps |
| **Right Association** | Attach new constituent to lowest/rightmost node | None (heuristic) | Violated often in real text |
| **Machine Learning (SVM/NN)** | Learn attachment from features | Annotated training data | Black-box; needs large data |
| **Transformer Parsers** | End-to-end neural parsing | Large parse corpus | Computationally expensive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Structural Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Discourse"] --> B["Sentence S"]
    B --> C1["Clause 1"]
    B --> C2["Clause 2 (subordinate)"]
    C1 --> D1["Verb Phrase VP"]
    C1 --> D2["Noun Phrase NP"]
    D1 --> E1["Verb V"]
    D1 --> E2["Adverbial Phrase AdvP"]
    D2 --> E3["Determiner Det"]
    D2 --> E4["Noun N"]
    E4 --> F1["Morpheme: cat"]
    E4 --> F2["Morpheme: -s plural"]
    F1 --> M1["cat (root)"]
    F2 --> M2["-s (inflectional)"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Two Parse Trees for an Ambiguous Sentence

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph T1["Tree 1: PP attaches to VP (instrument)"]
        S1["S"] --> NP1["NP: I"]
        S1 --> VP1["VP"]
        VP1 --> V1["V: saw"]
        VP1 --> NP2["NP: the man"]
        VP1 --> PP1["PP: with the telescope"]
        PP1 --> P1["P: with"]
        PP1 --> NP3["NP: the telescope"]
    end
    subgraph T2["Tree 2: PP attaches to NP (possessive)"]
        S2["S"] --> NP4["NP: I"]
        S2 --> VP2["VP: saw"]
        VP2 --> NP5["NP: the man"]
        NP5 --> PP2["PP: with the telescope"]
        PP2 --> P2["P: with"]
        PP2 --> NP6["NP: the telescope"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a Disambiguation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Raw Sentence Input"] --> B["Tokenization Module"]
    B --> C["POS Tagger"]
    C --> D["CFG Parser (CKY/Early)"]
    D --> E["Multiple Candidate Parse Trees"]
    E --> F["Feature Extractor"]
    F --> G["PCFG Probability Scorer"]
    F --> H["Lexical Preference Module"]
    F --> I["Selectional Restriction Checker"]
    G --> J["Score Aggregator"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Argmax Selector"]
    K --> L["Best Disambiguated Parse Tree Output"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Disambiguation Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Ambiguous Sentence Detected"] --> Q1{"Multiple Parse Trees?"}
    Q1 -->|No| Out1["Return Unique Parse"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Treebank Available?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PCFG["Apply PCFG: argmax P(T|w)"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Semantic Lexicon Available?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Sem["Apply Selectional Restrictions"]
    Q3 -->|No| Heur["Apply Right-Association Heuristic"]
    PCFG --> Result["Best Parse Selected"]
    Sem --> Result
    Heur --> Result
    Result --> End["Output: Disambiguated Parse"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define syntactic ambiguity. Give one example of attachment ambiguity and explain why it is syntactically valid to parse it in more than one way.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Syntactic ambiguity arises when a sentence has **two or more valid phrase-structure parse trees** under a given grammar, leading to multiple grammatical interpretations.
Example: *"I saw the man with the telescope."* The PP *"with the telescope"* can syntactically attach either to the **VP** (it is the instrument of seeing — I used the telescope to see him) or to the **NP** (the man possesses the telescope — I saw the man who had the telescope). Both attachments satisfy the CFG rules $VP \rightarrow VP \; PP$ and $NP \rightarrow NP \; PP$, hence both parses are grammatically valid. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Briefly explain the concept of structural hierarchy in language with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Structural hierarchy is the **ordered layered organization of linguistic units** from smallest to largest: Morpheme → Word → Phrase → Clause → Sentence. Each higher layer is composed of units from the lower layer according to grammar rules.
Example: For *"The small child runs quickly,"* the morpheme *-s* (plural) combines with *child* to form the word *children*; this combines with the determiner *the* and adjective *small* to form the **NP** *"the small child"*; the NP combines with the **VP** *"runs quickly"* to form the entire sentence. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **structural hierarchy of language** in computational linguistics. Describe each level with a suitable example. How is this hierarchy represented formally using a Context-Free Grammar (CFG)? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **syntactic ambiguity**? Explain the different **types of syntactic ambiguity** (attachment, coordination, scope) with one example each. How does a **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)** help in choosing the correct parse? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Structural hierarchy refers to the systematic, recursive organization of language units where larger constituents are built from smaller ones. The hierarchy has five levels:

1. **Morpheme** — the smallest meaning-bearing unit, e.g., *un-, happy, -ness* in *unhappiness*.
2. **Word** — a morpheme or combination functioning as a single lexical item, e.g., *"cats"* (POS = NNS).
3. **Phrase** — a group of words forming a single grammatical function, e.g., **NP**: *"the red car"*, **VP**: *"ran quickly"*, **PP**: *"on the table"*.
4. **Clause** — a unit containing a subject-predicate structure, e.g., *"because he arrived late"*.
5. **Sentence** — the maximal syntactic unit, e.g., *"The red car ran quickly."*

**Formal Representation (3 marks):** A **CFG** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where:
- $V$ = set of non-terminals (e.g., $\{S, NP, VP, PP, Det, N, V\}$)
- $T$ = set of terminals (lexicon, e.g., $\{$*the, cat, saw*\ \}$)
- $P$ = set of production rules (e.g., $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$)
- $S$ = start symbol

**Example derivation (2 marks):** The sentence *"The cat saw the mouse"* derives as $S \Rightarrow NP \; VP \Rightarrow Det \; N \; VP \Rightarrow \ldots$ culminating in the terminal string.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Syntactic ambiguity occurs when a sentence has multiple valid parse trees under the same grammar.

**Types (4 marks):**
- **Attachment ambiguity:** PP can attach to multiple nodes. Example: *"I saw the man with the telescope"* — PP attaches to VP (instrument) or NP (possessive).
- **Coordination ambiguity:** Scope of the conjunction is unclear. Example: *"old men and women"* — *old [men and women]* vs. *[old men] and [women]*.
- **Scope ambiguity:** The scope of quantifiers or negation is unclear. Example: *"Every student did not read a book"* — *some* or *no* student read a book.

**PCFG Disambiguation (2 marks):** A PCFG augments each CFG rule $r_i$ with a probability $P(r_i)$ estimated from a treebank. The probability of a parse tree $T$ is the product of its rule probabilities:

$$
P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(r_i)
$$

The best parse is selected as $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T P(T \mid w)$. This assigns higher probability to the more frequent structural pattern seen in training data, effectively choosing the most likely interpretation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **Phrase Structure Grammar**. With neat examples, illustrate how a **parse tree** is constructed for a simple English sentence. List any **five CFG production rules** used in your example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **various techniques used to cope with syntactic ambiguity** in NLP, including selectional restrictions, subcategorization, PCFGs, and modern neural approaches. Mention at least two limitations of purely rule-based disambiguation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Phrase Structure Grammar (PSG) (2 marks):** PSG, also called constituency grammar, describes sentence structure as a hierarchical grouping of words into nested constituents. It is formalized as a CFG where each rule expands a non-terminal into a sequence of terminals and/or non-terminals.

**Parse Tree Construction (3 marks):** Consider: *"The clever boy solved the puzzle."*

```
                    S
                   / \
                 NP    VP
                / \   / \
              Det  N  V   NP
               |  |  |   / \
              The boy solved Det  N
                            |   |
                          the puzzle
```

**Five CFG Rules used (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R1: } & S \rightarrow NP \; VP \\
\text{R2: } & NP \rightarrow Det \; Adj \; N \\
\text{R3: } & VP \rightarrow V \; NP \\
\text{R4: } & Det \rightarrow \text{``The''} \mid \text{``the''} \\
\text{R5: } & N \rightarrow \text{``boy''} \mid \text{``puzzle''} \\
\text{R6: } & V \rightarrow \text{``solved''} \\
\text{R7: } & Adj \rightarrow \text{``clever''}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Disambiguation Techniques (5 marks):**

1. **Selectional Restrictions:** Semantic constraints on verb arguments. E.g., the verb *eat* requires its object to be *edible* — this rules out *"I ate the concept."*
2. **Subcategorization Frames:** Each verb specifies the syntactic frames it accepts. E.g., *give* requires NP + NP (*"give him a book"*), not just *NP*.
3. **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs):** Use rule probabilities derived from treebanks; the parse with the highest probability is selected.
4. **Lexicalized Parsing:** Head-to-head dependencies are annotated with statistics (e.g., Collins Parser, Charniak Parser).
5. **Neural / Transformer Parsers:** End-to-end models (e.g., Biaffine Parser, BERT-based parsers) learn contextual embeddings to score parses, achieving SOTA accuracy.
6. **Right Association Heuristic:** Attach new constituents to the lowest, right-most possible node (a default preference).
7. **Frequency-Based Disambiguation:** The most common structural reading in a corpus is preferred.

**Limitations of Rule-Based Disambiguation (2 marks):**
- **Coverage problem:** Hand-crafted rules cannot cover all linguistic phenomena, especially in open-domain text.
- **Maintenance cost:** As language evolves and new domains appear, rule sets must be continuously updated.
- **Conflict resolution:** Rules may contradict each other, and there is no principled mechanism to resolve such conflicts without probabilistic scoring.
- **Domain sensitivity:** Rules tuned for one domain (e.g., newswire) often fail on others (e.g., biomedical text or social media).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing syntactic vs. lexical ambiguity:** Lexical ambiguity = a *word* has multiple meanings (*"bank"* — river/money). Syntactic ambiguity = the *structure* admits multiple parses. Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting tree levels in parse trees:** A common error is omitting intermediate nodes (e.g., drawing NP directly to words without the Det/N level). Always show **every non-terminal node**.
> 3. **Not mentioning "valid grammar" in ambiguity definition:** A sentence is ambiguous only if **both parses are valid under the same grammar**. Random alternative parsings are not counted.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps:** In PCFG questions, students often write the formula $P(T) = \prod P(r_i)$ but forget to compute it numerically. Always show **at least one numerical substitution**.
> 5. **Mixing up attachment types:** "PP-attachment" and "NP-bracketing" are distinct ambiguity classes — do not interchange them.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Structural hierarchy = 5 levels:** Morpheme → Word → Phrase → Clause → Sentence. Memorize the order.
- ✅ **CFG formal definition:** $G = (V, T, P, S)$ — write this in every CFG-based question.
- ✅ **Syntactic ambiguity requires $\geq 2$ valid parse trees under the same grammar.**
- ✅ **Four main types of syntactic ambiguity:** Attachment, Coordination, Scope, Garden-Path.
- ✅ **PCFG disambiguation rule:** $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T \prod_i P(r_i)$.
- ✅ **CYK parsing complexity:** $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ — must know for theory questions.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics:** Precision, Recall, $F_1$ — applicable to parsers.
- ✅ **Disambiguation techniques (know at least 5):** PCFG, selectional restrictions, subcategorization, lexicalized parsing, right association, neural parsers.
- ✅ **Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ with `\vert` in LaTeX** — never the bare `|` pipe inside math expressions.
- ✅ **Key example sentences for exam:** *"I saw the man with the telescope"* (PP-attachment), *"old men and women"* (coordination), *"Every student read a book"* (scope).
- ✅ **Compositionality principle:** Meaning of whole = f(meanings of parts, syntax) — always cite this for hierarchy questions.
- ✅ **Draw full parse trees** — never skip intermediate non-terminal nodes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantic Representation: Logicist Approaches and Statistical Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semantic Representation: Logicist Approaches and Statistical Semantics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Semantic Representation?

**Semantic Representation** refers to the formal encoding of meaning of natural language expressions in a structured, machine-tractable form. In Computational Linguistics, it is the bridge between surface-level words/sentences and the underlying meaning that a machine can reason about, query, or transform. The KTU 2024 syllabus divides semantic representation into two principal paradigms:

- **Logicist (Symbolic) Approaches** — meaning is represented through formal logical formulas (predicates, arguments, logical connectives).
- **Statistical Semantics** — meaning is learned from large corpora as dense numerical vectors derived from distributional co-occurrence patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** Semantic representation is the systematic mapping from linguistic expressions (words, phrases, sentences, discourses) onto a formal mathematical or logical formalism that preserves referential, predicative, and inferential properties of natural language meaning.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Library Card Catalogue vs. Google Search:**
> - **Logicist Approach** = a librarian who classifies every book using the **Dewey Decimal System** — precise, hand-coded, rule-based, requires an expert to design categories.
> - **Statistical Semantics** = **Google's search engine** — it does not "understand" the book; it just learns that words appearing in similar contexts share meaning, building an implicit map of associations.
> - Modern systems (BERT, GPT) = a **hybrid librarian** who uses the librarian's precision but learns patterns from millions of catalogue entries automatically.

---

## 1.3 The Two Paradigms at a Glance

| Aspect | Logicist (Symbolic) | Statistical (Distributional) |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Philosophy, formal logic (Aristotle, Frege, Montague) | Linguistics + Information Retrieval (Harris, Firth) |
| **Representation** | Symbolic formulas, predicates, lambda terms | Real-valued dense vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Knowledge Source** | Hand-crafted, ontologies, lexicons | Automatic from raw text corpora |
| **Inference** | Sound logical deduction | Geometric similarity, probabilistic scoring |
| **Strength** | Compositionality, interpretability | Robustness, coverage, scalability |
| **Weakness** | Knowledge acquisition bottleneck | Black-box, lacks formal semantics |
| **Key Method** | FOL, $\lambda$-calculus, DRT | LSA, Word2Vec, BERT embeddings |
| **Era** | 1960s–1990s | 1990s–Present |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** A logicist says *"Paris IS-A capital-of France"* (symbolic relation). A statistical system says *"Paris and London appear in similar sentences, so their vectors point in similar directions"* (geometric similarity).

---

## 1.4 Historical Lineage (KTU High-Yield)

- **1950s–60s** — Machine Translation reveals need for semantic representation; **Katz & Fodor** propose selectional feature systems.
- **1960s–70s** — **Richard Montague** introduces *Montague Semantics* — natural language fragments are interpreted as formal logical languages with **compositionality**.
- **1970s–80s** — **Kamp (1981)** and **Heim (1982)** develop **Discourse Representation Theory (DRT)** to handle anaphora and discourse.
- **1954** — **Zellig Harris** articulates the *Distributional Hypothesis*: *"Words occurring in similar contexts have similar meanings."*
- **1957** — **J.R. Firth** popularises: *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."*
- **1988** — **Deerwester et al.** introduce **Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA)**.
- **2013** — **Mikolov** releases **Word2Vec** (CBOW + Skip-gram).
- **2018+** — Contextual embeddings (BERT, ELMo) unify both paradigms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logicist (Symbolic) Tradition

### 2.1.1 First-Order Logic (FOL) for Semantics

FOL represents meaning as a set of well-formed formulas built from **predicates, constants, variables, functions, and logical connectives**.

**Building Blocks:**
- **Constants**: $Paris$, $John$, $India$ — denote specific entities.
- **Predicates**: $Capital(x, y)$ — "$x$ is the capital of $y$".
- **Variables**: $x, y, z$ — range over entities.
- **Logical Connectives**: $\land$ (and), $\lor$ (or), $\neg$ (not), $\Rightarrow$ (implies), $\Leftrightarrow$ (iff).
- **Quantifiers**: $\forall$ (universal), $\exists$ (existential).

**Compositional Translation Example:**
> *"Paris is the capital of France."*
> $$\text{Capital}(Paris, France)$$
> *"All cities have capitals."*
> $$\forall x \,[\,City(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, Capital(y, x)\,]$$

---

### 2.1.2 Lambda Calculus (Montague Semantics)

**Type-Logical Foundation.** Invented by Church (1936), adopted by Montague (1970) to give natural language the same compositional structure as formal logic.

**Syntax:**
- **Variables**: $x, y, z$
- **Abstraction**: $\lambda x . P(x)$ — a function that maps $x$ to a property
- **Application**: $(F \, a)$ — apply function $F$ to argument $a$

**Compositional Rule (Functional Application):**
$$\text{MEANING}(A \, B) = \text{MEANING}(A)(\text{MEANING}(B))$$

**Worked Example:**
> *"Every dog barks."*

Type-theoretic composition:

$$\text{All} = \lambda P \, \lambda Q . \forall x [P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)]$$

$$P = \lambda x . Dog(x) \quad ; \quad Q = \lambda x . Bark(x)$$

$$\text{All}(P)(Q) = \forall x [Dog(x) \Rightarrow Bark(x)]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Montague's Thesis:** *"There is no important theoretical difference between natural languages and the artificial languages of logicians."* This gives rise to the **Principle of Compositionality** — the meaning of a complex expression is a function of the meanings of its parts and the way they are syntactically combined.

---

### 2.1.3 Discourse Representation Theory (DRT)

DRT, introduced by **Hans Kamp (1981)**, extends FOL to handle **discourse-level phenomena** (anaphora, presupposition, donkey sentences).

**Key Construct — DRS (Discourse Representation Structure):**
A DRS is an ordered pair $\langle U, Con \rangle$ where:
- $U$ = a set of **discourse referents** (variables for entities introduced).
- $Con$ = a set of **conditions** (predicates, equalities, negations).

**Example:**
> *"Alex owns a dog. It barks loudly."*

$$\text{DRS} = \langle \{x, y\},\ \{Alex(x), Dog(y), Owns(x,y), Barks(y), Loudly(y)\} \rangle$$

The pronoun *"It"* is resolved by **accommodation** — bound to the most salient discourse referent $y$.

---

## 2.2 The Statistical (Distributional) Tradition

### 2.2.1 The Distributional Hypothesis

> *"Words that occur in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings."* — Harris (1954), Firth (1957)

This converts **meaning** into a **geometric problem**: semantic similarity $\approx$ spatial proximity in vector space.

---

### 2.2.2 Vector Space Model (VSM)

Each word $w$ is represented as a row vector $\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ derived from its co-occurrence statistics with context words.

**Co-occurrence Matrix Construction:**
- $C$ = word $\times$ context matrix.
- $C_{ij}$ = number of times word $w_i$ co-occurs with context word $c_j$ within a window of size $\pm k$.

**Document-Term vs. Word-Word:**
- **Document-Term Matrix** (used in LSA) — rows = documents, columns = terms.
- **Word-Context Matrix** (used in Word2Vec) — rows = words, columns = context words.

---

### 2.2.3 Key Similarity Metric — Cosine Similarity

$$\boxed{\text{cos}(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\|\vec{u}\| \, \|\vec{v}\|} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i v_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i^2}}}$$

**Range:** $\text{cos} \in [-1, +1]$
- $+1$ = identical direction (semantically equivalent).
- $0$ = orthogonal (no semantic relation).
- $-1$ = opposite (rare, mostly for negation-trained vectors).

---

### 2.2.4 Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)

Measures how strongly a target word $w$ and a context word $c$ co-occur more than chance:

$$\text{PMI}(w, c) = \log_2 \frac{P(w, c)}{P(w) \, P(c)}$$

Where probabilities are estimated from corpus counts:
$$P(w, c) \approx \frac{C_{wc}}{N} \quad , \quad P(w) \approx \frac{C_w}{N} \quad , \quad P(c) \approx \frac{C_c}{N}$$

**Positive PMI (PPMI)** — negative values are replaced by $0$:

$$\text{PPMI}(w, c) = \max(0, \text{PMI}(w, c))$$

---

### 2.2.5 Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA)

LSA applies **Truncated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** to the term-document matrix to discover **latent semantic dimensions**.

Given matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ (terms $\times$ documents):

$$X = U \, \Sigma \, V^{\top}$$

Where:
- $U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ — left singular vectors (terms in latent space).
- $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ — diagonal of singular values $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq 0$.
- $V^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — right singular vectors (documents in latent space).

**Truncation** keeps only top-$k$ singular values to remove noise:

$$X_k = U_k \, \Sigma_k \, V_k^{\top}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Synonyms and related terms project onto the **same latent dimensions** because they appear in similar documents — even when they never co-occur literally.

---

### 2.2.6 Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013)

Two shallow neural architectures:

**1. CBOW (Continuous Bag-of-Words):**
Predicts the target word from surrounding context.

$$P(w_t \mid c) \quad \text{where} \quad c = \{w_{t-k}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+k}\}$$

**2. Skip-gram:**
Predicts context words from a target word.

$$P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t) \quad \text{for } j \in \{-k, \dots, -1, +1, \dots, +k\}$$

**Negative Sampling** objective (efficient approximation):

$$\mathcal{L} = \log \sigma(\vec{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)}[\log \sigma(-\vec{v}_{w_i}^{\top} \vec{v}_{w_I})]$$

Where $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ is the sigmoid function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Famous Word2Vec Algebraic Property:**
> $$\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$$
> This emerges purely from corpus statistics — no symbolic rule is hard-coded.

---

### 2.2.7 GloVe (Global Vectors, Pennington et al., 2014)

Combines global co-occurrence counts with local context windows. The objective:

$$\mathcal{J} = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij}) \left(\vec{w}_i^{\top} \tilde{\vec{w}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij}\right)^2$$

Where $f(x) = \min\left(1, \left(\frac{x}{x_{\max}}\right)^{\alpha}\right)$ caps the influence of very frequent co-occurrences (default $\alpha = 0.75$, $x_{\max} = 100$).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat-Sheet)

| **Symbolic** | **Formula / Concept** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Capital}(a, b)$ | Predicate logic atomic formula | FOL semantics |
| $\forall x [P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)]$ | Universal quantification | FOL |
| $\lambda x . P(x)$ | Lambda abstraction | Montague |
| $\text{FA}: \text{M}(A\,B) = \text{M}(A)(\text{M}(B))$ | Functional Application | Compositionality |
| $\langle U, Con \rangle$ | DRS structure | DRT |
| $\text{cos}(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{\|\vec{u}\|\|\vec{v}\|}$ | Cosine similarity | VSM |
| $\text{PMI}(w,c) = \log_2 \frac{P(w,c)}{P(w)P(c)}$ | Pointwise MI | Statistical Semantics |
| $\text{PPMI}(w,c) = \max(0, \text{PMI}(w,c))$ | Positive PMI | VSM |
| $X = U \Sigma V^{\top}$ | SVD decomposition | LSA |
| $X_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$ | Truncated SVD (rank-$k$) | LSA |
| $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | Sigmoid | Word2Vec |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{NEG}}$ | Negative sampling loss | Word2Vec |
| $\mathcal{J}_{\text{GloVe}}$ | Weighted least-squares loss | GloVe |
| $f(x) = \min(1, (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha})$ | GloVe weighting cap | GloVe |
| $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$ | Vector analogy | Word2Vec/GloVe |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field** | **Application of Semantic Representation** |
|---|---|
| Search Engines (Google, Bing) | Query-document matching via embeddings |
| Machine Translation (Google Translate) | Cross-lingual semantic spaces |
| Chatbots & Virtual Assistants | Intent classification, slot filling |
| Healthcare NLP (IBM Watson Health) | Clinical entity linking, drug-interaction reasoning |
| Legal Tech | Contract clause similarity, precedent retrieval |
| Information Extraction | Named entity recognition, relation extraction |
| Question Answering (SQuAD, ChatGPT) | Contextual semantic matching |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Derivation #1: Translating a Sentence to FOL

**Sentence:** *"Every student who reads a novel admires some author."*

We proceed step-by-step using the **Principle of Compositionality**.

### Step 1 — Identify Lexical Predicates
- $Student(x)$: "$x$ is a student"
- $Reads(x, y)$: "$x$ reads $y$"
- $Novel(y)$: "$y$ is a novel"
- $Admires(x, z)$: "$x$ admires $z$"
- $Author(z)$: "$z$ is an author"

### Step 2 — Identify Logical Structure
The sentence has the logical form:

> $\forall x \, [\, Student(x) \land \exists y \, (Novel(y) \land Reads(x, y)) \Rightarrow \exists z \, (Author(z) \land Admires(x, z))\, ]$

### Step 3 — Derive via Syntactic Tree

$$\text{S} \to \text{NP}_{every} \; \text{VP}$$

**NP$_{every}$** = $\lambda P \, \lambda Q . \forall x [P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)]$

**VP** = "admires some author" applied to "reads a novel"

$$\text{Inner VP} = \lambda x . \exists z [Author(z) \land Admires(x, z)]$$

$$\text{Modifier "reads a novel"} = \lambda x . \exists y [Novel(y) \land Reads(x, y)]$$

### Step 4 — Final Compositional Build

$$\text{FA} \Rightarrow \forall x \, \Big[\, Student(x) \land \exists y [Novel(y) \land Reads(x, y)] \;\Rightarrow\; \exists z [Author(z) \land Admires(x, z)]\, \Big]$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Derivation #2: Cosine Similarity Between Two Word Vectors

Suppose we have learned the following toy embeddings (after dimensionality reduction) from a small corpus:

$$vec(king)  = (0.5, \; 0.8, \; 0.1, \; -0.2)$$
$$vec(queen) = (0.6, \; 0.7, \; 0.3, \; -0.1)$$
$$vec(apple) = (0.1, \; 0.0, \; 0.9, \;  0.4)$$

### Step 1 — Compute the dot product of $vec(king)$ and $vec(queen)$

$$\vec{king} \cdot \vec{queen} = (0.5)(0.6) + (0.8)(0.7) + (0.1)(0.3) + (-0.2)(-0.1)$$

$$= 0.30 + 0.56 + 0.03 + 0.02 = 0.91$$

### Step 2 — Compute magnitudes

$$\|\vec{king}\| = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.8^2 + 0.1^2 + (-0.2)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{0.25 + 0.64 + 0.01 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.94} \approx 0.9695$$

$$\|\vec{queen}\| = \sqrt{0.6^2 + 0.7^2 + 0.3^2 + (-0.1)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{0.36 + 0.49 + 0.09 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.95} \approx 0.9747$$

### Step 3 — Compute cosine similarity

$$\cos(\vec{king}, \vec{queen}) = \frac{0.91}{(0.9695)(0.9747)} = \frac{0.91}{0.9450} \approx 0.9630$$

### Step 4 — Repeat for $king$ vs $apple$

$$\vec{king} \cdot \vec{apple} = (0.5)(0.1) + (0.8)(0.0) + (0.1)(0.9) + (-0.2)(0.4)$$

$$= 0.05 + 0.00 + 0.09 - 0.08 = 0.06$$

$$\|\vec{apple}\| = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.00 + 0.81 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.98} \approx 0.9899$$

$$\cos(\vec{king}, \vec{apple}) = \frac{0.06}{(0.9695)(0.9899)} = \frac{0.06}{0.9597} \approx 0.0625$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

$$\boxed{\cos(\vec{king}, \vec{queen}) \approx 0.96 \gg \cos(\vec{king}, \vec{apple}) \approx 0.06}$$

**Conclusion:** *king* and *queen* are semantically far more similar than *king* and *apple* — a direct validation of the distributional hypothesis.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Derivation #3: PMI Computation from a Corpus

Consider a tiny corpus of **N = 1000** word pairs and the following counts:

| Pair $(w, c)$ | $C_{wc}$ |
|---|---|
| (data, science) | 50 |
| (data, the)     | 800 |
| (science, the)  | 100 |
| (banana, science) | 0 |
| (banana, the)   | 5 |

Marginal counts:
- $C_{data} = 850$, $C_{science} = 150$, $C_{banana} = 5$
- $C_{the} = 905$

### Step 1 — Compute PMI(data, science)

$$P(data, science) = \frac{50}{1000} = 0.050$$

$$P(data) = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.850, \quad P(science) = \frac{150}{1000} = 0.150$$

$$\text{PMI}(data, science) = \log_2 \frac{0.050}{(0.850)(0.150)} = \log_2 \frac{0.050}{0.1275}$$

$$= \log_2 (0.3922) \approx -1.350 \; \text{bits}$$

### Step 2 — Compute PMI(data, the)

$$P(data, the) = \frac{800}{1000} = 0.800, \quad P(the) = \frac{905}{1000} = 0.905$$

$$\text{PMI}(data, the) = \log_2 \frac{0.800}{(0.850)(0.905)} = \log_2 \frac{0.800}{0.7693}$$

$$= \log_2 (1.040) \approx 0.056 \; \text{bits}$$

### Step 3 — Apply PPMI

$$\text{PPMI}(data, science) = \max(0, -1.350) = 0$$

$$\text{PPMI}(data, the) = \max(0, 0.056) = 0.056$$

### Step 4 — Interpret

- $data$-$science$ **co-occurs less than chance** despite high raw count → PPMI suppresses it.
- $data$-$the$ is a near-chance pair → tiny positive PMI.
- This is exactly why raw counts fail and **re-weighting** (PMI/PPMI) is essential for semantic tasks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Criterion** | **FOL / Montague** | **LSA** | **Word2Vec** | **GloVe** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compositionality | ✅ Yes (lambda calculus) | ❌ No (bag-of-words) | ❌ No (context-window) | ❌ No (count-based) |
| Captures Polysemy | ❌ Single sense per symbol | ❌ Single vector | ❌ Single vector (pre-BERT) | ❌ Single vector |
| Inference | ✅ Sound deduction | ⚠ Geometric only | ⚠ Geometric only | ⚠ Geometric only |
| Training Cost | Manual annotation | One SVD pass | Iterative SGD | Iterative SGD |
| Captures Subtlety | ⚠ With effort | ⚠ Latent factors | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong |
| Scalability | ❌ Knowledge bottleneck | ✅ Truncated SVD | ✅ Very scalable | ✅ Very scalable |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests the **complementary strengths** of the two paradigms: logicist = *precision*, statistical = *coverage*.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Hybrid Semantic Representation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input["Raw Natural Language Text"] --> tokenize["Tokenisation and POS Tagging"]
    tokenize --> branchA{"Logicist Path"}
    tokenize --> branchB{"Statistical Path"}
    branchA --> parse["CCG / HPSG Syntactic Parse"]
    parse --> lambda["Lambda-Calculus Compositional Build"]
    lambda --> fol["First-Order Logic Formulae"]
    fol --> reasoner["Automated Theorem Prover / Inference Engine"]
    branchB --> cooccur["Co-occurrence Matrix Construction"]
    cooccur --> svd["Truncated SVD LSA"] 
    cooccur --> nn["Skip-gram or CBOW Word2Vec"]
    nn --> glove["Global Co-occurrence GloVe"]
    svd --> vsm["Vector Space Model"]
    glove --> vsm
    vsm --> cosim["Cosine Similarity / KNN Search"]
    reasoner --> output["Semantic Interpretation Output"]
    cosim --> output
    output --> apps["Downstream Applications: QA MT IR Chatbots"]
```

## 4.2 Logicist Compositional Stack (Montague-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lex["Lexicon: Word to Logical Type Mapping"] --> syn["Syntax: Combinatory Categorial Grammar"]
    syn --> types["Type-Driven Composition: FA, Type Raising"]
    types --> lambda["Lambda-Abstraction over Variables"]
    lambda --> model["Model-Theoretic Interpretation: Domain D, Interpretation Function I"]
    model --> truth["Truth-Conditional Semantics: Sentence true iff Model satisfies Formula"]
    truth --> entail["Entailment and Inference"]
```

## 4.3 Statistical Semantics Pipeline (Vector Path)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    corpus["Large Text Corpus"] --> token2["Token Stream"]
    token2 --> win["Sliding Context Window +/- k words"]
    win --> counts["Co-occurrence Counts C wi cj"]
    counts --> pmi["PMI / PPMI Reweighting"]
    counts --> svd2["Singular Value Decomposition"]
    counts --> skip["Skip-gram Negative Sampling"]
    pmi --> vecs["Word Vectors in R d"]
    svd2 --> vecs
    skip --> vecs
    vecs --> sim["Cosine Similarity / Vector Algebra"]
    sim --> analogy["Analogy: King - Man + Woman approx Queen"]
```

## 4.4 DRT Anaphora Resolution Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Sentence 1: Alex owns a dog"] --> drs1["DRS 1: U1 = x, y; Con1 = Alex x, Dog y, Owns x y"]
    s2["Sentence 2: It barks loudly"] --> lookup["Pronoun 'It' triggers Discourse Referent Lookup"]
    drs1 --> lookup
    lookup --> bind["Bind to most salient referent y in U1"]
    bind --> drs2["DRS 2 incorporates: Barks y, Loudly y"]
    drs2 --> truth2["Model-Theoretic Truth Evaluation"]
```

## 4.5 Side-by-Side: Logicist vs Statistical Reasoning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOGICIST["LOGICIST SEMANTIC REPRESENTATION"]
        L1["Symbolic Sentence: All x Dog x implies Bark x"] --> L2["Apply logical rules Modus Ponens Universal Elimination"]
        L2 --> L3["Conclude: Fido barks"]
    end
    subgraph STATISTICAL["STATISTICAL SEMANTIC REPRESENTATION"]
        S1["Query: What is similar to dog?"] --> S2["Project vec(dog) into R 300"]
        S2 --> S3["Compute cosine with all word vectors"]
        S3 --> S4["Top-K neighbours: puppy, canine, wolf, bark, pet"]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OBE pattern**: 3-mark short answers (Part A) and 14-mark long answers with internal choice (Part B), mapped to Course Outcomes CO2 and CO3 of PECMT753.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
**"Differentiate between the Distributional Hypothesis and the Compositionality Principle in semantic representation." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **Distributional Hypothesis** (Harris, Firth) — the meaning of a word is determined by the company it keeps, i.e., words appearing in similar **contexts** share meaning; it is a *statistical* principle based on **co-occurrence**.
2. **Compositionality Principle** (Frege, Montague) — the meaning of a complex expression is a *function* of the meanings of its parts and the syntactic rule used to combine them; it is a *symbolic* principle based on **functional application**.
3. **Contrast** — Distributionalism derives meaning from corpus statistics (vectors), Compositionality derives it from logical/syntactic rules (formulas). They are complementary, not competing.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapt.]
**"What is Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI)? Why is PPMI preferred over raw PMI in semantic vector construction?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **Definition:** $\text{PMI}(w, c) = \log_2 \frac{P(w, c)}{P(w) P(c)}$ measures the deviation of observed joint probability from expected probability under independence.
2. **Raw PMI problem:** pairs that co-occur *less* than chance yield **negative values**, which are uninformative and noisy when computing vector similarities.
3. **PPMI fix:** $\text{PPMI}(w, c) = \max(0, \text{PMI}(w, c))$ — negative entries are clipped to zero, retaining only semantically *positively* associated pairs, which improves word-similarity benchmarks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Theme]

**(a)** Explain the **First-Order Logic (FOL)** approach to semantic representation. Using suitable examples, demonstrate how English sentences can be systematically translated into FOL formulas. Discuss the **limitations** of pure FOL in handling natural language semantics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Montague Semantics** and the role of **Lambda Calculus** in achieving compositionality. With a detailed worked example, show how *"Every clever student solves some problem"* can be compositionally translated into a logical formula. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) FOL Approach to Semantic Representation — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** First-Order Logic is a formal symbolic system using predicates, constants, variables, quantifiers, and connectives to represent the meaning of natural language in a machine-tractable form.

**Key Components (2 marks):**
- **Constants** ($a, b, c$) — denote individual entities: $John$, $Paris$.
- **Predicates** ($P, Q, R$) — denote properties or relations: $Capital(x, y)$, $Loves(x, y)$.
- **Variables** ($x, y, z$) — range over entities.
- **Connectives** ($\land, \lor, \neg, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$).
- **Quantifiers** ($\forall, \exists$).

**Translation Examples (2 marks):**

> *"Ravi loves mangoes."* $\quad \Longrightarrow \quad Loves(Ravi, Mangoes)$

> *"Every dog barks."* $\quad \Longrightarrow \quad \forall x [Dog(x) \Rightarrow Bark(x)]$

> *"Some boy owns a red car."* $\quad \Longrightarrow \quad \exists x \exists y [Boy(x) \land Red(y) \land Car(y) \land Owns(x, y)]$

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Quantification Ambiguity** — natural language quantifiers ("most," "many," "few") lack direct FOL equivalents.
- **Discourse / Anaphora** — FOL has no native mechanism for pronoun binding across sentences (motivates DRT).
- **Knowledge Acquisition Bottleneck** — predicates must be hand-coded.
- **Polysemy and Context-Sensitivity** — FOL treats a symbol as a single fixed denotation.

---

**(b) Montague Semantics & Lambda Calculus — 7 Marks**

**Montague's Thesis (1 mark):** Natural languages can be treated as **formal languages** with **compositional semantics**; the meaning of a complex expression is a function of the meanings of its parts.

**Lambda Calculus Basics (2 marks):**
- **Abstraction:** $\lambda x . P(x)$ — forms a function.
- **Application:** $(F \, a)$ — applies $F$ to argument $a$.
- **$\beta$-Reduction:** $(\lambda x . P(x)) \, a \Rightarrow P(a)$.

**Worked Example: "Every clever student solves some problem" (4 marks):**

Lexical translations:
- $every = \lambda P \lambda Q . \forall x [P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)]$
- $clever = \lambda x . Clever(x)$
- $student = \lambda x . Student(x)$
- $solves = \lambda y \lambda z . Solves(z, y)$
- $some = \lambda P \lambda Q . \exists x [P(x) \land Q(x)]$
- $problem = \lambda y . Problem(y)$

**Step 1:** Combine $clever$ and $student$ via function composition (type intersection):
$$\lambda x . [Clever(x) \land Student(x)]$$

**Step 2:** Apply $solves$ to $problem$ to form the VP:
$$\lambda z . \exists y [Problem(y) \land Solves(z, y)]$$

**Step 3:** Apply $every$ to the modified NP and the VP:
$$\forall x \big[ Clever(x) \land Student(x) \;\Rightarrow\; \exists y [Problem(y) \land Solves(z, y)] \big]$$

**Conclusion:** Montague's framework makes **Principle of Compositionality** mathematically precise using typed lambda calculus and **type-driven rules** (Functional Application, Type Raising, Composition).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**(a)** Explain the **Distributional Hypothesis** and the **Vector Space Model (VSM)**. With a clear diagram, describe the construction of a **co-occurrence matrix** and explain how **cosine similarity** is used to measure semantic relatedness. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA)** algorithm. Explain **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** and demonstrate with a numerical example how **truncated SVD** reduces dimensionality and reveals latent semantic dimensions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Distributional Hypothesis & VSM — 7 Marks**

**Distributional Hypothesis (2 marks):**
- **Harris (1954) & Firth (1957):** *"Words occurring in similar contexts have similar meanings."*
- This converts the abstract notion of *meaning* into an empirical, corpus-derived property — **contextual co-occurrence**.

**Vector Space Model (3 marks):**
- Every word is represented as a point in $\mathbb{R}^d$ (a dense or sparse vector).
- **Co-occurrence matrix** $C \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times |V|}$: rows = target words, columns = context words, $C_{ij}$ = number of co-occurrences within window $\pm k$.

|  | science | the | data | king |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **data** | 50 | 800 | 5 | 0 |
| **science** | 2 | 100 | 30 | 0 |
| **king** | 0 | 200 | 0 | 1 |

- **Cosine Similarity Formula:**
$$\text{cos}(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\|\vec{u}\| \, \|\vec{v}\|}$$
- High cosine = high semantic similarity; used for nearest-neighbour retrieval, document clustering, paraphrase detection.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the **Vector Space Diagram** below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    origin["0,0 Origin"] --> vking["vec king"]
    origin --> vqueen["vec queen"]
    origin --> vapple["vec apple"]
    vking -. "cos approx 0.96 High Similarity" .-> vqueen
    vking -. "cos approx 0.06 Low Similarity" .-> vapple
```

---

**(b) Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) — 7 Marks**

**LSA Overview (2 marks):**
- Proposed by **Deerwester et al. (1988)**.
- Applies **Truncated SVD** to a term-document (or word-context) matrix $X$.
- Goal: uncover **latent semantic dimensions** that group synonyms and related terms.

**SVD Definition (2 marks):**
Given $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$:
$$X = U \Sigma V^{\top}$$
- $U$: left singular vectors (rows = terms in latent space).
- $\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \dots)$: singular values in descending order.
- $V$: right singular vectors (columns = documents in latent space).

**Truncation to rank $k$ (2 marks):**
- Keep only top-$k$ singular values: $X_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$.
- $k$ typically 100–300 for LSA; smaller than $\min(m, n)$.
- Reduces noise from rare terms; collapses synonymy into shared dimensions.

**Numerical Example (1 mark):**
$$X = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow X = U \Sigma V^{\top}$$
After SVD (numerical):
$$U \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.71 & -0.43 \\ 0.71 & 0.43 \\ 0.0 & 0.86 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \Sigma \approx \begin{pmatrix} 3.16 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.41 \end{pmatrix}, \quad V^{\top} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.71 & 0.71 \\ -0.71 & 0.71 \end{pmatrix}$$
Rank-1 truncation: $X_1 = U_1 \Sigma_1 V_1^{\top} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.6 & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & 1.6 \\ 0.0 & 0.0 \end{pmatrix}$ — capturing the dominant semantic axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Statistical Semantics Questions):**
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator of cosine similarity** — students often write $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}$ alone and lose 2 marks. Always normalise by magnitudes.
> 2. **Confusing LSA dimensions with Word2Vec dimensions** — LSA produces one vector per *document* and per *term*; Word2Vec produces one vector per *word type*. Examiners penalise this mix-up.
> 3. **Skipping the $\max(0, \cdot)$ step in PPMI** — you must state that negative values are clipped.
> 4. **Forgetting the singular values are sorted in descending order** in SVD — examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Writing `cos(u,v) = |u·v| / (|u||v|)`** — bars are *not* used in cosine; this is a common KTU deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Two paradigms of semantic representation** — *Logicist (Symbolic)* and *Statistical (Distributional)*.
- **Logicist foundations:** FOL, Montague Semantics (Lambda Calculus), DRT.
- **Principle of Compositionality** (Frege–Montague) — meaning of whole = function of parts + syntax.
- **Montague's key claim** — natural language fragments can be interpreted as typed formal languages.
- **Discourse Representation Theory (Kamp, Heim)** — handles anaphora via $\langle U, Con \rangle$ DRS pairs.
- **Distributional Hypothesis** (Harris, Firth) — meaning arises from co-occurrence context.
- **Vector Space Model** — each word → vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$.
- **Cosine similarity** $\in [-1, +1]$ — primary metric for semantic relatedness.
- **PMI** = $\log_2 \frac{P(w,c)}{P(w)P(c)}$; **PPMI** = $\max(0, \text{PMI})$.
- **LSA** uses **Truncated SVD**: $X_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$.
- **Word2Vec** has two architectures: **CBOW** and **Skip-gram**; uses **negative sampling**.
- **GloVe** combines global co-occurrence with local context windows; weighted least-squares loss.
- **Famous analogy:** $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$.
- **Logicist strengths:** compositional, interpretable, sound inference.
- **Statistical strengths:** scalable, robust, learns from raw text.
- **Modern systems (BERT, GPT) = hybrid** — symbolic structure + statistical training.
- **Sigmoid function:** $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ — central to neural distributional models.
- **GloVe weighting cap:** $f(x) = \min(1, (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha})$, $\alpha = 0.75$.
- **DRS:** discourse referents $U$ + conditions $Con$ — bound pronouns to $U$ set.
- **SVD singular values sorted descending:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq 0$.
- **Cosine is bounded** by Cauchy–Schwarz: $-1 \leq \cos \leq 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Aid (for KTU last-minute revision):**
> *"Logicists **logically compose**; Statisticians **statistically co-occur**."*

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping Syntactic Trees to Logical Forms** is the formal semantic interpretation process in computational linguistics wherein a surface syntactic parse tree (generated by a Context-Free Grammar, PCFG, or dependency parser) is systematically traversed and converted into a *logical form* — a symbolic, machine-manipulable representation of sentence meaning expressed in **First-Order Predicate Calculus (FOPC)**, **Lambda Calculus (λ-calculus)**, or **Neo-Davidsonian Event Logic**.

The logical form (LF) is the output of a **semantic interpretation function** `[[·]]` (the double-bracket notation from Montague Grammar) that maps a syntactic tree to a **typed logical expression**, satisfying the **Principle of Compositionality** — the meaning of the whole is a function of the meanings of its parts and the way they are syntactically combined.

Formally, if `T` is a parse tree and `L` is the resulting logical form:

$$[[T]] = L \in \mathcal{L}_{\text{FOPC}}$$

where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{FOPC}}$ is the set of well-formed formulas of first-order predicate logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** This topic is foundational for the *semantic analysis* stage of the NLP pipeline. It directly bridges **Module 2 (Syntax & Semantics)** with downstream applications in Question Answering, Information Extraction, and Knowledge Base Construction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a beautifully assembled **LEGO structure** (the syntactic tree) — where each LEGO brick is a word, the colors represent parts-of-speech, and the way bricks are glued together represents grammatical relations (subject-of, object-of, modifier-of).

Now, this LEGO structure is *only* a shape — it does not yet *say* anything meaningful. To make it speak, we need a **translator who reads the assembly instructions and writes down a precise, unambiguous sentence in a logical language** that a computer can reason with.

That translator is the **semantic interpreter**, and the precise sentence it writes is the **Logical Form**.

| LEGO Analogy | Computational Linguistics Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Individual LEGO brick | Lexical item (word with its lemma) |
| Brick connections | Syntactic dependencies (NP, VP, PP) |
| Final assembled structure | Parse tree `T` |
| Translated logical sentence | Logical Form `L` (e.g., $\lambda x.\text{see}(x, \text{man})$) |
| Instruction manual | Grammar rules + Lexicon |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** *"Tree tells the structure; Logic tells the truth."* A parse tree tells us *how* words combine; the logical form tells us *what the combination means* in a form usable for inference.

---

## 1.3 Core Entities & Terminology Inventory

Before diving into mappings, let us lock down the key entities a KTU examiner will expect you to recognize:

- **Syntactic Tree (Parse Tree):** A hierarchical tree `T = (V, E, root)` where leaves are terminal symbols (words) and internal nodes are non-terminal categories (S, NP, VP, PP, etc.).
- **Logical Form (LF):** A well-formed formula in a target meaning representation language.
- **Compositionality (Frege–Montague Principle):** The meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its constituent expressions and the rules used to combine them.
- **Type:** A semantic category assigned to each subtree (e.g., `e` for entity, `t` for truth value, `(e \rightarrow t)` for predicate).
- **Lambda Abstraction:** A notation for creating functions, used to handle unbounded constituents (e.g., quantifiers, relative clauses).
- **Semantic Head:** The word in a phrase that determines its primary semantic contribution (typically the head child in the syntactic tree).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Trap:** Students often confuse the *syntactic* head with the *semantic* head. In "the red car", the syntactic head is *"car"* (the noun), but the **semantic head is also "car"** (the noun contributes the `thing-being-red` predicate's argument). However, in a sentence like *"John runs quickly"*, the verb *"runs"* is the semantic head that selects its arguments and adjuncts.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (KTU Reference)

In the context of logical form construction, the following **standard semantic-type symbols** are universally adopted in KTU-evaluated syllabi (per Heim & Kratzer 1998, the standard KTU-referenced textbook framework):

| Symbol | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **e** | Entity type | Refers to an individual (e.g., `John`, `a man`) |
| **t** | Truth-value type | Refers to a proposition (True/False) |
| **⟨e, t⟩** | Predicate type | Function from entity to truth value (e.g., `red(x)`) |
| **⟨e, ⟨e, t⟩⟩** | Two-place predicate | Function from entity to entity-predicate (e.g., `love`) |
| **⟨⟨e, t⟩, t⟩** | Generalized quantifier | Type of `every`, `some`, `no` |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern KTU notation, the angle-bracket type `⟨σ, τ⟩` is equivalent to the older `σ → τ` arrow notation. Both are accepted in the 2024 scheme answer scripts. For maximum examiner friendliness, **use the arrow notation** `e → t` or `(e → t)` in written exams.

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Architecture of Tree-to-Logic Mapping

The mapping of a syntactic tree to a logical form is not a single step — it is a **three-stage pipeline** that the KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests. Master these stages and you can answer any derivation question.

### **Stage 1 — Lexical Semantics (Word → Atomic Predicate)**

Each terminal word `w_i` in the lexicon is assigned an **atomic logical predicate** of appropriate type, plus a list of lambda-bound variables that the word introduces or consumes.

**Examples (Word → Atomic Form):**

| Word | Atomic Logical Form | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `John` | `john` | `e` |
| `man` | `λx.man(x)` | `e → t` |
| `sees` | `λy.λx.see(x, y)` | `e → (e → t)` |
| `every` | `λP.λQ.∀x[P(x) → Q(x)]` | `(e → t) → ((e → t) → t)` |
| `not` | `λp.¬p` | `t → t` |
| `sleeps` | `λx.sleep(x)` | `e → t` |
| `quickly` | `λR.λx.quickly(R(x))` | Takes a VP-type |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** The order of `λ`-binders matters and indicates **argument selection order**. For transitive verbs, the convention is `λy.λx.verb(x,y)` (object first, then subject) — this is the **Montagovian convention** universally expected in KTU valuations. Reversing the order costs marks.

---

### **Stage 2 — Type-Driven Composition (Rule-to-Rule Hypothesis)**

This is the **heart of the mapping**. Following the **Rule-to-Rule Hypothesis** (Bach, 1976 — a foundational KTU reference), *every syntactic rule in the CFG has a corresponding semantic composition rule* applied bottom-up over the parse tree.

The generic rule schema is:

$$\frac{\text{Parent node} \quad \text{with children having LFs } L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n}{\text{Parent LF} = f(L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n)}$$

**Key Composition Operations:**

| Operation Name | Syntactic Configuration | Semantic Operation |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Application (FA)** | `VP → V NP` | `V_LF (NP_LF)` (apply verb's λ to NP) |
| **Predicate Modification** | `NP → AP N` or `VP → AdvP VP` | `λx.[Adj_LF(x) ∧ Head_LF(x)]` (intersective) |
| **Quantifier Raising (QR)** | `S → NP VP`, NP is a QP | Move `NP_LF` outside `VP_LF`, bind via `∀` or `∃` |
| **Conjunction** | `XP → XP and XP` | `λx.[L1(x) ∧ L2(x)]` (intersective) |
| **Type Raising** | `NP` in subject position | `λP.P(john)` — raises `john: e` to `(e → t) → t` |

---

### **Stage 3 — Quantifier Scope Resolution**

The final LF may contain multiple quantifiers whose **scope** must be unambiguously determined. KTU examiners expect the **Cooper Storage** method or simple **Quantifier Raising (QR)** to be applied.

**Example:** For *"Every man loves a woman"*, the LF is:

$$L = \forall x[\text{man}(x) \rightarrow \exists y[\text{woman}(y) \land \text{love}(x, y)]]$$

The QR operation moves the QP *"a woman"* from object position up to a scope position above the verb, where it can take wide scope over *"every man"*.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Gold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your revision bible for the 14-mark Part B questions. Memorize the LFs of the common closed-class words.**

| Word/Category | Logical Form (LF) | Type Signature |
|---|---|---|
| Proper noun `John` | `john` | `e` |
| Intransitive Verb `sleeps` | `λx.sleep(x)` | `e → t` |
| Transitive Verb `sees` | `λy.λx.see(x, y)` | `e → (e → t)` |
| Ditransitive Verb `gives` | `λz.λy.λx.give(x, y, z)` | `e → (e → (e → t))` |
| Common Noun `dog` | `λx.dog(x)` | `e → t` |
| Adjective `red` | `λx.red(x)` | `e → t` |
| Adverb `quickly` | `λR.λx.quickly(R(x))` | `(e → t) → (e → t)` |
| Determiner `every` | `λP.λQ.∀x[P(x) → Q(x)]` | `(e → t) → ((e → t) → t)` |
| Determiner `a`/`some` | `λP.λQ.∃x[P(x) ∧ Q(x)]` | `(e → t) → ((e → t) → t)` |
| Determiner `no` | `λP.λQ.¬∃x[P(x) ∧ Q(x)]` | `(e → t) → ((e → t) → t)` |
| Negation `not` | `λp.¬p` | `t → t` |
| Pronoun `he` | `λP.P(he)` (type-raised) | `(e → t) → t` |

---

### **Type Signature Rules (Why it works):**

For Functional Application to be valid:

$$\text{If } f : \alpha \rightarrow \beta \text{ and } a : \alpha, \text{ then } f(a) : \beta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If a student's type signature does not match (e.g., applying a `e → t` predicate to a `t` truth value), the valuation examiner will deduct **2 marks** for a "type mismatch error" even if the final formula looks plausible.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mapping from syntactic trees to logical forms is **not a theoretical exercise** — it powers every major semantic-AI system deployed today:

1. **Knowledge Base Population (KBP):** Systems like IBM's *Watson* convert parsed natural-language queries into FOPC, then submit them to SPARQL / Neo4j databases.
2. **Question Answering (QA):** For the question *"Who founded Microsoft?"*, the LF `λx.found(x, microsoft)` is generated and matched against a knowledge base.
3. **Information Extraction (IE):** Event-extraction pipelines (used in financial intelligence, e.g., Bloomberg's news engine) map dependency parses to **Neo-Davidsonian event logics** like `∃e[founding(e) ∧ agent(e, Bill_Gates) ∧ theme(e, Microsoft)]`.
4. **Semantic Parsing in Robotics:** Service robots (e.g., SoftBank's Pepper) parse commands like *"Bring me the red cup from the kitchen"* into LFs that are then grounded in world-state predicates.
5. **Legal & Medical NLP:** Logical forms are used to detect contradictions and entailments in clinical texts and contract law.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip — Linking Theory to Application:** When a KTU question asks *"What is the real-world utility of LF mapping?"*, structure your answer in 3 bullet points: (1) enables formal inference, (2) supports DB-style querying, (3) supports entailment / contradiction detection. This scores full marks in application-based CO questions.

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive** — every lambda-reduction step and every type-justification line is shown. KTU examiners reward this level of detail in 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: "John sees Mary"

This is the **classic KTU textbook example** — expect it (or a close variant) in every exam cycle.

### **Step 1 — Lexical Assignments**

From the lexicon (per Section 2.1 table):

- `John` ⟶ `john : e`
- `sees` ⟶ `λy.λx.see(x, y) : e → (e → t)`
- `Mary` ⟶ `mary : e`

### **Step 2 — Syntactic Parse Tree**

```
S
├── NP
│   └── John
└── VP
    ├── V
    │   └── sees
    └── NP
        └── Mary
```

### **Step 3 — Bottom-Up Composition**

We start at the leaves and apply the appropriate semantic composition rule at each internal node.

**Node `NP` (left, "John"):**

$$[[\text{NP}_{\text{John}}]] = \text{john} \quad : e$$

**Node `NP` (right, "Mary"):**

$$[[\text{NP}_{\text{Mary}}]] = \text{mary} \quad : e$$

**Node `VP` (built from `V + NP`):** The verb `sees` is a transitive verb expecting an object NP of type `e`. We apply **Functional Application (FA)**:

The verb LF is `λy.λx.see(x, y)`. Its outermost λ binds `y` (the object).

Apply the verb to the right NP (`mary`):

$$[[V]([[NP_{\text{Mary}}]])] = (\lambda y. \lambda x.\text{see}(x, y))(\text{mary})$$

Substitute `y` with `mary`:

$$= \lambda x.\text{see}(x, \text{mary}) \quad : e \rightarrow t$$

This becomes the VP's LF (a one-place predicate awaiting a subject).

**Node `S` (built from `NP_{subj} + VP`):** The subject NP `john` of type `e` is applied to the VP's LF of type `e → t`:

$$[[S]] = [[VP]]([[NP_{\text{John}}]]) = (\lambda x.\text{see}(x, \text{mary}))(\text{john})$$

Substitute `x` with `john`:

$$= \text{see}(\text{john}, \text{mary}) \quad : t$$

### **Final Logical Form:**

$$\boxed{L = \text{see}(\text{john}, \text{mary})}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Win:** The result is a closed proposition of type `t` (a truth value), which is exactly what a *declarative sentence* should yield. This is the **Klein–Bach closure check** — a sentence's LF must close to type `t`; if it does not, the parse is semantically ill-formed.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: "Every dog barks"

This example introduces **quantifier scope** — a high-frequency KTU question.

### **Step 1 — Lexical Assignments**

- `every` ⟶ `λP.λQ.∀x[P(x) → Q(x)] : (e → t) → ((e → t) → t)`
- `dog` ⟶ `λx.dog(x) : e → t`
- `barks` ⟶ `λx.bark(x) : e → t`

### **Step 2 — Compose the Noun Phrase "every dog"**

The determiner `every` takes two arguments of type `e → t` (the restrictor and the scope). We first build the restrictor NP from `dog`:

$$[[\text{dog}]] = \lambda x.\text{dog}(x)$$

Apply `every` to `dog`:

$$(\lambda P.\lambda Q.\forall x[P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)])(\lambda x.\text{dog}(x))$$

Substitute `P` with `λx.dog(x)`:

$$= \lambda Q.\forall x[\text{dog}(x) \rightarrow Q(x)] \quad : (e \rightarrow t) \rightarrow t$$

This is a **Generalized Quantifier (GQ)** — a set of sets of entities.

### **Step 3 — Compose the Verb Phrase "barks"**

$$[[\text{VP}_{\text{barks}}]] = \lambda x.\text{bark}(x) \quad : e \rightarrow t$$

### **Step 4 — Apply the QP "every dog" to the VP**

The QP is of type `(e → t) → t` and is applied to the VP of type `e → t`. This is valid FA.

$$(\lambda Q.\forall x[\text{dog}(x) \rightarrow Q(x)])(\lambda x.\text{bark}(x))$$

Substitute `Q` with `λx.bark(x)`:

$$= \forall x[\text{dog}(x) \rightarrow \text{bark}(x)] \quad : t$$

### **Final Logical Form:**

$$\boxed{L = \forall x\,[\text{dog}(x) \rightarrow \text{bark}(x)]}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: "Every man loves a woman" (Quantifier Scope)

### **Step 1 — Lexical LF**

- `every` ⟶ `λP.λQ.∀x[P(x) → Q(x)]`
- `man` ⟶ `λx.man(x)`
- `loves` ⟶ `λy.λx.love(x, y)`
- `a` ⟶ `λP.λQ.∃x[P(x) ∧ Q(x)]`
- `woman` ⟶ `λx.woman(x)`

### **Step 2 — Compose the VP "loves a woman"**

We need to handle the fact that `a woman` is **inside the VP**, not at the top. We use **Type Raising** on the object NP, or equivalently, we delay the quantifier's binding via **Cooper Storage**.

For simplicity, let's apply **Quantifier Raising (QR)**: move the QP "a woman" to a position where it can take scope.

**Step 2a — Build the QP "a woman":**

$$(\lambda P.\lambda Q.\exists x[P(x) \land Q(x)])(\lambda x.\text{woman}(x)) = \lambda Q.\exists x[\text{woman}(x) \land Q(x)]$$

**Step 2b — Apply to the unsaturated VP.** The unsaturated VP (after combining with `loves`) expects an object of type `e`, but the QP is of type `(e → t) → t`. We type-raise the object position and store the QP.

**Step 2c — Composition (illustrative, after QR):** The QP takes scope over the whole sentence. Applying both quantifiers (universal first, then existential — by convention of subject-before-object QR):

$$L = \forall x[\text{man}(x) \rightarrow \exists y[\text{woman}(y) \land \text{love}(x, y)]]$$

### **Alternative Scope (Object > Subject):**

By allowing the existential QP to QR *higher* than the universal QP, we obtain:

$$L' = \exists y[\text{woman}(y) \land \forall x[\text{man}(x) \rightarrow \text{love}(x, y)]]$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Scope Ambiguity Pitfall:** *"Every man loves a woman"* is genuinely **scope-ambiguous**. The default KTU-accepted answer is the **subject-wide-scope** reading (∀ > ∃), but a complete answer must acknowledge the alternative. Skipping the alternative costs 2 marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Composition Rules at a Glance

| Syntactic Rule | Semantic Rule (in pseudo-form) | Type Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| `S → NP VP` | `VP_LF(NP_LF)` | NP : `σ`, VP : `σ → t` |
| `VP → V NP` | `V_LF(NP_LF)` | V : `σ → (τ → t)`, NP : `τ` |
| `VP → V NP NP` | `V_LF(NP2_LF)(NP1_LF)` | V : `τ → (σ → (ρ → t))` |
| `NP → Det Nom` | `Det_LF(Nom_LF)` | Det : `(e→t) → ((e→t)→t)`, Nom : `e→t` |
| `NP → AP Nom` | `λx.[AP_LF(x) ∧ Nom_LF(x)]` | Both : `e → t` |
| `AP → Adv AP` | `λx.[Adv_LF(x) ∧ AP_LF(x)]` | Both : `e → t` |
| `VP → AdvP VP` | `AdvP_LF(VP_LF)` | AdvP : `(e→t) → (e→t)`, VP : `e → t` |
| `S → S and S` | `λx.[S1_LF(x) = T ∧ S2_LF(x) = T]` | Propositional conjunction |
| `S → not S` | `¬[S_LF]` | Negation of a `t` |
| `NP → Pronoun` | `λP.P(he_i)` (type-raised) | Pronoun : `(e→t) → t` |
