## 4.1 Top-Level System Architecture — Tree-to-Logic Pipeline

This diagram maps the *full* operational pipeline from raw sentence to logical form, including the role of each subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Sentence] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[POS Tagger]
    C --> D[Parser CFG or PCFG]
    D --> E[Syntactic Parse Tree]
    E --> F[Lexical Semantics Module]
    F --> G[Atomic Predicate Lookup]
    G --> H[Tree Traversal Engine]
    H --> I[Type Driven Composition]
    I --> J[Quantifier Scope Resolution]
    J --> K[Final Logical Form LF]
    K --> L[Downstream Application Layer]
    L --> M1[Knowledge Base Query]
    L --> M2[Entailment Engine]
    L --> M3[Information Extraction]
    L --> M4[Question Answering]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Steps **A → E** belong to the *syntactic* phase (parser output).
- Steps **F → K** are the *semantic interpretation* phase — **this is what the topic covers**.
- Steps **L → M*** are downstream consumers, illustrating the engineering utility.

---

## 4.2 Bottom-Up Tree Traversal — Step-by-Step Schematic

The diagram below illustrates how `[[·]]` (the double-bracket interpretation function) is recursively applied to a sample sentence *"Every dog barks"*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    leafA[John / Lexicon: john e] --> npLeft[NP Left LF: john e]
    leafB[sees / Lexicon: lambda y lambda x see x y e to e to t] --> vNode[V LF: lambda y lambda x see x y e to e to t]
    leafC[Mary / Lexicon: mary e] --> npRight[NP Right LF: mary e]
    npRight -->|FA: apply V to right NP| vpNode[VP LF: lambda x see x mary e to t]
    npLeft -->|FA: apply VP to left NP| sNode[S LF: see john mary t]
    vNode --> vpNode
```

**Key callouts:**

- The lambda reduction happens *at* the `vpNode` step (substituting `y := mary`).
- The second lambda reduction happens *at* the `sNode` step (substituting `x := john`).
- Final result is type `t` — closure check passes.

---

## 4.3 Quantifier Scope Resolution via Cooper Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sent1[Input Sentence: Every man loves a woman] --> parse[Syntactic Tree Built]
    parse --> compRestrict[Build Restrictor: lambda x man x]
    parse --> compScope[Build VP Scope Predicate: lambda x love x Y]
    compRestrict --> qp1[QP1: lambda Q forall x man x to Q x]
    qp1 --> store1[Cooper Store: Store QP1]
    compScope --> vplift[VP Lifted to Predicate over Y]
    store1 --> retrieve1[Retrieve and Apply QP1]
    vplift --> retrieve1
    retrieve1 --> outFinal[Final LF: forall x man x to exists y woman y and love x y]
```

This schematic illustrates the **two-store version of Cooper Storage** for the sentence *"Every man loves a woman"*. The subject QP is stored first, then the VP is built, then the stored QP is retrieved and applied — yielding the final LF with proper subject-wide-scope.

---

## 4.4 The Principle of Compositionality — Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Word Level LFs] --> B[Phrase Level LFs]
    B --> C[Clause Level LFs]
    C --> D[Sentence Level LF type t]
    A -. Meaning of Parts .-> D
    B -. Combination Rules .-> D
    C -. Syntactic Structure .-> D
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Answer in 2–4 sentences, with one formal definition and one example. Target time: 3 minutes per question.*

### **Q1.** Define the term "Logical Form" in the context of computational linguistics. Why is it necessary as an intermediate representation? **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Logical Form (LF)** is a formal symbolic representation of the meaning of a natural-language sentence, expressed in a logical language such as First-Order Predicate Calculus (FOPC) or Lambda Calculus, in which predicates denote properties/relations, constants denote individuals, and quantifiers express scope.

It is necessary as an intermediate representation because:
1. **It disambiguates** semantic scope (e.g., quantifier scope) that natural language leaves implicit.
2. **It enables formal inference** via theorem provers (resolution, model checking).
3. **It allows database-style querying** of unstructured text by translating NL to structured logic queries (as in semantic parsing for QA systems).

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for any two reasons, 1 mark for a clean concluding sentence.)*

---

### **Q2.** State the **Principle of Compositionality** as proposed by Frege and Montague. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Principle of Compositionality** (Frege, 1892; formalized by Montague, 1970) states that:

> *"The meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its constituent parts and the rules used to combine them."*

Formally, the meaning function `[[·]]` is a **homomorphism** from the syntactic algebra (parse trees under tree-combination) to the semantic algebra (logical forms under functional application). This principle underlies the entire *rule-to-rule* hypothesis of semantic interpretation: every syntactic rule in the CFG has a corresponding semantic composition rule that is applied bottom-up over the parse tree. A direct corollary is that the LF of a sentence can be computed in **polynomial time** with respect to the size of the parse tree.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the principle statement, 1 mark for the rule-to-rule consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Derivation Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *Part B questions follow the KTU 2024 ESE pattern: sub-part (a) carries 7 marks and sub-part (b) carries 7 marks. Internal choice means you answer EITHER Question A OR Question B, not both.*

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Given the sentence: **"No student read every book"**, build the syntactic tree, assign lexical LFs, and derive the final logical form step-by-step using the rule-to-rule hypothesis.*

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Lexicon (atomic predicates):**

| Word | LF | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `No` | `λP.λQ.¬∃x[P(x) ∧ Q(x)]` | `(e→t)→((e→t)→t)` |
| `student` | `λx.student(x)` | `e → t` |
| `read` | `λy.λx.read(x, y)` | `e → (e → t)` |
| `every` | `λP.λQ.∀x[P(x) → Q(x)]` | `(e→t)→((e→t)→t)` |
| `book` | `λx.book(x)` | `e → t` |

**Step 2 — Syntactic Tree:**

```
S
├── NP
│   ├── Det: No
│   └── N: student
└── VP
    ├── V: read
    └── NP
        ├── Det: every
        └── N: book
```

**Step 3 — Compose the Object QP "every book":**

Apply `every` to `book`:

$$(\lambda P.\lambda Q.\forall x[P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)])(\lambda x.\text{book}(x)) = \lambda Q.\forall x[\text{book}(x) \rightarrow Q(x)]$$

**Step 4 — Compose the VP "read every book":**

Apply the verb `read` (type `e → (e → t)`) to the QP. To make types match, we use **Type Raising** on the QP or equivalently, we apply `read` after Quantifier Raising.

Raise QP to a function: `λR.∀x[book(x) → R(x)]` of type `((e → t) → t)`.

Apply `read` (after QR, treating object as bound variable):

$$(\lambda y.\lambda x.\text{read}(x, y))(\text{book-bound-var}) \Rightarrow \text{read semantics with object quantifier}$$

Final VP LF (after applying existential to ∀ over `book`):

$$\lambda x.\forall y[\text{book}(y) \rightarrow \text{read}(x, y)]$$

**Step 5 — Compose the Subject QP "No student":**

Apply `No` to `student`:

$$\lambda Q.\neg\exists x[\text{student}(x) \land Q(x)]$$

**Step 6 — Final S Composition:**

Apply the subject QP to the VP:

$$(\lambda Q.\neg\exists x[\text{student}(x) \land Q(x)])(\lambda x.\forall y[\text{book}(y) \rightarrow \text{read}(x, y)])$$

### **Final Logical Form:**

$$\boxed{L = \neg\exists x\,[\text{student}(x) \land \forall y\,[\text{book}(y) \rightarrow \text{read}(x, y)]]}$$

Equivalently (after quantifier negation):

$$L = \forall x\,[\text{student}(x) \rightarrow \neg\forall y\,[\text{book}(y) \rightarrow \text{read}(x, y)]]$$

$$L = \forall x\,[\text{student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y\,[\text{book}(y) \land \neg\text{read}(x, y)]]$$

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for lexicon, 2 marks for tree + composition, 2 marks for step-by-step application, 2 marks for final LF with proper type closure.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the **Rule-to-Rule Hypothesis** (Bach, 1976) and discuss its strengths and limitations in mapping syntactic trees to logical forms.*

**Model Solution:**

The **Rule-to-Rule Hypothesis**, proposed by Emmon Bach (1976), postulates a **one-to-one correspondence** between the rules of a Context-Free Grammar (CFG) and the semantic composition rules of the interpretation function `[[·]]`. Every syntactic rule that combines smaller constituents into a larger one has a paired semantic operation that combines their LFs into the LF of the larger constituent.

**Formal Statement:**

$$\forall R \in \text{CFG rules} \; \exists f_R \in \text{Semantic rules such that } [[R(c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n)]] = f_R([[c_1]], [[c_2]], \ldots, [[c_n]])$$

**Strengths:**

1. **Modularity:** Each syntactic rule has a local semantic companion, simplifying grammar engineering.
2. **Polynomial-time computability:** The LF can be computed in O(|T|) time where |T| is the tree size.
3. **Predictive power:** New sentences unseen in training data can be interpreted, provided their parse tree is well-formed.
4. **Formal elegance:** Aligns directly with Montague's compositionality principle.

**Limitations:**

1. **Idioms and multiword expressions** (e.g., "kick the bucket") violate rule-to-rule — their meaning is non-compositional.
2. **Scope ambiguities** (e.g., "Every man loves a woman") require post-composition scope resolution (QR / Cooper Storage) outside the strict rule-to-rule framework.
3. **Lexical selectional restrictions** (e.g., "The book slept" is syntactically valid but semantically anomalous) require type-and-feature checks beyond simple FA.
4. **Discourse phenomena** (anaphora, presupposition) are not captured by tree-local rules.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for hypothesis statement, 2 marks for strengths, 2 marks for limitations, 1 mark for a concluding remark on how modern systems like HPSG or CCG extend the hypothesis.)*

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Build the syntactic tree for the sentence: **"A red car parked quickly in the garage"** and derive its complete logical form, showing all composition steps and the final type-closed expression.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lexicon:**

| Word | LF | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `A` | `λP.λQ.∃x[P(x) ∧ Q(x)]` | `(e→t)→((e→t)→t)` |
| `red` | `λx.red(x)` | `e → t` |
| `car` | `λx.car(x)` | `e → t` |
| `parked` | `λx.park(x)` | `e → t` |
| `quickly` | `λR.λx.quickly(R(x))` | `(e→t) → (e→t)` |
| `in` | `λP.λx.in(garage, x) ∧ P(x)` (or relational) | `((e→t)→(e→t))` |
| `the` | Omitted for brevity, or definite `ιx` (iota) | |
| `garage` | `λx.garage(x)` | `e → t` |

**Step 2 — Build the inner AP "red car" (intersective modification):**

$$[[\text{red}]] \text{ AND } [[\text{car}]] = \lambda x.[\text{red}(x) \land \text{car}(x)]$$

**Step 3 — Build the QP "A red car":**

$$(\lambda P.\lambda Q.\exists x[P(x) \land Q(x)])(\lambda x.[\text{red}(x) \land \text{car}(x)]) = \lambda Q.\exists x[\text{red}(x) \land \text{car}(x) \land Q(x)]$$

**Step 4 — Build the PP "in the garage":**

$$[[\text{PP}]] = \lambda P.\lambda y.[P(y) \land \text{in}(\text{garage}, y)]$$

**Step 5 — Build the AdvP-modified VP "parked quickly in the garage":**

Apply adverb to VP, then PP:

$$[[\text{VP}]] = \lambda x.[\text{park}(x) \land \text{quickly}(x) \land \text{in}(\text{garage}, x)]$$

**Step 6 — Final S composition:**

$$[[S]] = \exists x[\text{red}(x) \land \text{car}(x) \land \text{park}(x) \land \text{quickly}(x) \land \text{in}(\text{garage}, x)]$$

### **Final Logical Form:**

$$\boxed{L = \exists x\,[\text{red}(x) \land \text{car}(x) \land \text{park}(x) \land \text{quickly}(x) \land \text{in}(\text{garage}, x)]}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Compare and contrast the use of **First-Order Predicate Calculus (FOPC)** and **Lambda Calculus** as target representation languages for logical forms. When would you prefer one over the other in an engineering system?*

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | FOPC | Lambda Calculus |
|---|---|---|
| **Expressivity** | Full FOPC supports quantifiers, predicates, functions, equality. | Lambda calculus is a *function-definition* language; quantifiers must be encoded via combinators or extended (typed lambda calculus). |
| **Natural for Tree-to-Logic?** | Requires intermediate lambda steps during composition. | Natural fit: each rule produces a lambda expression that is reduced at parent nodes. |
| **Inference Support** | Excellent — mature theorem provers (Vampire, E, Prover9) and SMT solvers. | Limited — requires translation to FOPC or higher-order logic. |
| **Compositionality** | Achievable but cumbersome (skolemization needed). | Native — composition IS function application. |
| **Type System** | Weak typing (sorts only). | Strong typing via Church-style simple types. |
| **Engineering Preference** | **Best for:** KB querying, deductive QA, formal verification. | **Best for:** intermediate semantic representation during parsing. |

**Conclusion for Engineering Systems:**

- **Use Lambda Calculus as the *intermediate* LF** during compositional interpretation (because it is type-driven and matches tree structure).
- **Translate to FOPC as the *final* LF** for downstream inference, KB querying, or entailment tasks.
- Modern systems (e.g., Google's SLING, AllenNLP's Open-SES) follow exactly this **two-stage Lambda → FOPC** architecture.

*(Examiner's note: 3 marks for the table content, 2 marks for the comparative analysis, 2 marks for the engineering recommendation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in Tree-to-Logic questions:**
>
> 1. **Type signature mismatch (–2 marks):** Forgetting to assign type signatures to intermediate LFs. *Always* annotate `[[·]]` with its type at each composition step.
> 2. **Lambda binder order reversed (–1 mark):** Writing `λx.λy.love(y, x)` instead of `λy.λx.love(x, y)`. **Montagovian convention is non-negotiable in KTU valuations.**
> 3. **No closure check (–1 mark):** Forgetting to verify that the final LF has type `t`. The examiner explicitly looks for this verification.
> 4. **Skipping scope alternatives (–2 marks):** For sentences with multiple quantifiers, only giving one scope reading. Always state both `∀ > ∃` and `∃ > ∀` when both are grammatically possible.
> 5. **Confusing LF with syntax (–1 mark):** Writing the *parse tree* as the "logical form" — the LF must be a **logical expression**, not a tree diagram.
> 6. **Missing quantifier raising step (–2 marks):** When a QP is buried inside a VP, students often forget to apply QR. State explicitly: *"We apply QR to lift the QP to sentence scope."*
> 7. **No real-world linkage (–1 mark):** On 7-mark "discuss/explain" sub-parts, omitting the *application* dimension. Always close your answer with a one-line application note.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-second revision before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **Compositionality Principle:** Meaning of whole = f(meanings of parts, combination rules). This is the *guiding philosophy* of all tree-to-logic mapping.
- ✅ **Three-Stage Pipeline:** Lexical Semantics → Type-Driven Composition → Scope Resolution. Memorize this three-word mantra: **"Lex → Comp → Scope"**.
- ✅ **Lexicon is King:** Commit to memory the LFs of `every`, `a/some`, `no`, `not`, transitive verbs, intransitive verbs, adjectives, and adverbs (see Section 2.2 cheat sheet).
- ✅ **Montagovian Convention:** Transitive verb LF is `λy.λx.V(x, y)` — object binds first, subject binds second. Reversing = –1 mark.
- ✅ **Functional Application (FA):** If `f : α → β` and `a : α`, then `f(a) : β`. This is the *only* composition operation you need for 80% of KTU derivation questions.
- ✅ **Predicate Modification is Intersection:** `λx.[Adj(x) ∧ N(x)]` for `AP + N`, and `λx.[Adv(x) ∧ VP(x)]` for `AdvP + VP`.
- ✅ **Type Raising for Pronouns:** `he ⟶ λP.P(he)` of type `(e → t) → t`. This is a frequent 3-mark sub-question.
- ✅ **Quantifier Raising (QR):** QPs in object position must be **lifted** to sentence scope before FA. State this explicitly.
- ✅ **Cooper Storage:** For complex scope interactions, the store mechanism is the standard KTU-accepted solution.
- ✅ **Final Type Check:** A well-formed declarative sentence must produce a closed LF of type `t`. If yours doesn't, **re-derive**.
- ✅ **Two-Stage Engineering Architecture:** Use **Lambda Calculus** for intermediate LF (composition is native), translate to **FOPC** for inference and KB querying.
- ✅ **Real-World Hooks:** Map your answer to at least one application — QA, IE, KB construction, entailment, robotics — to score the *application marks* in CO-3 / CO-5 questions.
- ✅ **Exam Closure:** Always end every derivation with a boxed final LF and a one-line **semantic gloss** (English interpretation of the formula).

---

> *End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Mapping Syntactic Trees to Logical Forms*
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PECMT753 — Computational Linguistics*
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 Aligned)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling Semantic Ambiguity and Under-Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Handling Semantic Ambiguity and Under-Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Semantic Ambiguity — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semantic Ambiguity** is a linguistic phenomenon wherein a single linguistic expression (word, phrase, or sentence) is interpretable in more than one distinct way at the level of **meaning (semantics)**, even when its syntactic structure is fully determined. The expression carries **multiple plausible truth-conditional interpretations** that can only be resolved by appeal to context, world knowledge, or pragmatic inference.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) framework for Computational Linguistics, semantic ambiguity is treated as a **central computational bottleneck**. A robust NLP pipeline cannot assign a *single* meaning to an ambiguous utterance without first **disambiguating** it, because downstream tasks (machine translation, question answering, sentiment analysis, information extraction) depend on a *canonical logical form*.

### 1.2 Under-Specification — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Under-Specification (US)** is a representational strategy in formal/computational semantics in which a linguistic expression is assigned a meaning representation that **deliberately leaves certain semantic choices open**, encoding only the information that is *common to all* possible readings. The result is a **partial logical form** that can be subsequently refined into a set of fully-specified readings.

The core idea: **Don't commit to one interpretation too early.** Build a compact, factored representation and unpack it only when context (or downstream processing) demands it.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Menu Analogy**
>
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant and the waiter says: *"I saw her duck near the bank."* You pause — but a smart menu (the **semantic processor**) lists all possible "dishes" (meanings) this phrase *could* yield:
> - *Duck* (the bird) near a *bank* (river edge) — **lexical polysemy**
> - *Duck* (the verb, to lower) near a *bank* (financial institution) — **lexical homonymy + structural ambiguity**
> - *I* (the speaker) — but *who is "her"?* — **anaphoric under-specification**
>
> An **under-specified representation** is like writing all these possibilities on a *single index card* with **placeholders (holes)** that get filled in only when the waiter confirms your table. **Semantic disambiguation** is the act of crossing off the wrong "dishes" until only one remains.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in WSD / Ambiguity Resolution

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (English) | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polysemy Degree** | $P_w$ | **1.0 – 7.0 senses/word** | Average number of senses per word in a lexicon |
| **Entropy of Sense Distribution** | $H(S)$ | **0 – log K bits** | Uncertainty in choosing a sense $s$ for word $w$ |
| **Coverage** | $C$ | $\geq 90\%$ | Fraction of text tokens receiving a sense |
| **Precision / Recall** | $P, R$ | **0 – 1** | Accuracy of disambiguator |

These four quantitative anchors are used universally in **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** evaluation — a sub-task of handling semantic ambiguity.

### 1.5 Why the KTU 2024 Syllabus Cares About This Topic

- **Module 2 Goal (PECMT753):** Build the *semantic layer* of an NLP pipeline.
- **Course Outcome Alignment:** This topic directly serves **CO2** — *"Apply formal semantic representations to model linguistic meaning."*
- **Bloom's Level:** *Analyze (L4)* and *Evaluate (L5)* — students are expected to **classify ambiguity types** and **choose appropriate disambiguation strategies**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Semantic Ambiguity

Ambiguity in natural language is **systematic** and can be decomposed into well-defined categories. Mastering this taxonomy is *high-yield* for KTU Part-A questions.

#### 2.1.1 Lexical Ambiguity
A single word form maps to multiple unrelated or related senses.

- **Homonymy** — Same form, *unrelated* meanings, different etymologies.
$$w \rightarrow \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}, \quad s_i \perp s_j \;\; (i \neq j)$$
*Example:* `bank` (river) vs `bank` (financial) — same spelling, same pronunciation, no shared meaning.

- **Polysemy** — Same form, *related* meanings, shared semantic core.
$$w \rightarrow \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}, \quad \exists\; s_i, s_j : \text{related}(s_i, s_j)$$
*Example:* `head` (of body), `head` (of department), `head` (of beer) — all share the schema *{top/front/leader entity}*.

#### 2.1.2 Structural (Syntactic) Ambiguity
The same string admits **multiple parse trees**, each yielding a different meaning.

- *Example:* `"I saw the man with the telescope."`
    - Reading 1: I used a telescope to see the man. (PP-attaches to VP)
    - Reading 2: I saw the man who had the telescope. (PP-attaches to NP)

#### 2.1.3 Scope Ambiguity
Affects the **relative semantic scope** of operators such as negation `¬`, quantifiers `∀`, `∃`, and modals.

*Example:* `"Every student read a book."`
- $\forall x.\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y.\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Read}(x,y)$ — *each student read possibly different books*
- $\exists y.\text{Book}(y) \wedge \forall x.\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x,y)$ — *one specific book read by all students*

#### 2.1.4 Attachment Ambiguity
A sub-phrase (typically a **PP**, *prepositional phrase*) can attach at multiple positions in the parse tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **PP-attachment** is statistically the **most frequent** structural ambiguity in English newspaper corpora. Ratios reported in the literature: **NP-attachment ≈ 52%**, **VP-attachment ≈ 34%**, others ≈ 14%.

#### 2.1.5 Anaphoric / Reference Ambiguity
A pronoun or referring expression has **multiple possible antecedents**.

*Example:* `"John told Mike that he had won."` — *who is "he"? John or Mike?*

#### 2.1.6 Semantic-Field / Selectional Ambiguity
A predicate can combine with arguments of multiple semantic types, even when the syntax is fixed.

*Example:* `"The newspaper has a baby."* — *has* normally takes `<Loc, Theme>` or `<Owner, Owned>`, but `baby` violates typical selectional restrictions.

---

### 2.2 Under-Specification Theory — The 'How' and 'Why'

**Why** under-specify? Three engineering reasons:
1. **Efficiency** — Avoids combinatorial blow-up when multiple ambiguities interact.
2. **Modularity** — Disambiguation is postponed to a component (e.g., a discourse model) that has the relevant knowledge.
3. **Cognitive plausibility** — Mirrors incremental human comprehension, where listeners hold multiple readings in parallel.

**How** is it achieved? Two foundational techniques:

#### A. Hole Semantics (dominant in MRS — Minimal Recursion Semantics)
The semantic representation is a graph of **predicates (nodes)** connected by **relations (edges)**, with **HOLE variables** (`h1`, `h2`, …) marking slots that may be filled in different ways.

$$\text{USR} = \langle \text{Predicates}, \text{ARG roles}, \text{HOLEs} \rangle$$

Each hole can be resolved to one of $k$ sub-structures, yielding $k$ fully-specified readings.

#### B. Dominance Constraints (Copestake, Crysmann)
Encode partial hierarchical information using **inequalities** over tree nodes — neither a full tree nor a forest, but a *partial order*.

$$d(n_1, n_2) \;\;\text{means node } n_1 \text{ dominates } n_2$$

#### C. Underspecified Logic (US-LFG — Wedekind)
Distinguishes **descriptive** content (always true) from **constructive** content (variable). Under-specified clauses are "holes" in the logical form.

---

### 2.3 Disambiguation Strategies — The Strategic Toolkit

| Strategy | Knowledge Source | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Selectional Restrictions** | Lexicon / Ontology | Cheap, fast | Fails for creative/metonymic usage |
| **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** — Supervised | Annotated corpus | High precision | Data-hungry |
| **WSD — Knowledge-based (Lesk, Walker)** | Dictionary / WordNet | No training data | Moderate accuracy |
| **WSD — Semi-supervised / Bootstrapping** | Small seed set + corpus | Good coverage | Sensitive to seed choice |
| **Statistical / ML (Naïve Bayes, SVM, Neural)** | Annotated + features | State-of-the-art | Black box |
| **Discourse / Coreference Models** | Centering theory, salience | Handles anaphora | Domain-sensitive |
| **World Knowledge (Knowledge Graphs)** | Wikidata, ConceptNet | Rich inference | Incomplete |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Module 2, PECMT753**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Polysemy Degree** | $P_w = \frac{1}{\vert W \vert}\sum_{w \in W} \vert S(w) \vert$ | $\vert S(w) \vert$ = number of senses of $w$ |
| 2 | **Sense Entropy** | $H(S \mid w) = -\sum_{i=1}^{k} p(s_i \mid w)\log_2 p(s_i \mid w)$ | $p(s_i \mid w)$ from sense-tagged corpus |
| 3 | **Lesk Overlap Score** | $\text{Lesk}(s_i, c) = \vert \text{gloss}(s_i) \cap \text{context}(c) \vert$ | Naive Lesk algorithm |
| 4 | **Bayes WSD** | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_i} P(s_i \mid c) = \arg\max_{s_i} P(c \mid s_i) P(s_i)$ | Naïve Bayes with bag-of-context features |
| 5 | **Precision / Recall** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Standard IR metrics |
| 6 | **F1-Measure** | $F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 7 | **Quantifier Scope Reading Count** | $\binom{n}{2}$ for $n$ quantifiers | 2 quantifiers → 2 readings |
| 8 | **PP-Attachment Likelihood** | $P(\text{NP-att}) \approx 0.52, \;\; P(\text{VP-att}) \approx 0.34$ | Empirical (Ratnaparkhi 1998) |
| 9 | **US-Reading Count** | $\prod_{i=1}^{m} k_i$ for $m$ holes, $k_i$ resolutions each | Combinatorial explosion worst case |
| 10 | **Mutual Information (for collocation)** | $MI(x, y) = \log \frac{P(x, y)}{P(x) P(y)}$ | Used in selectional preferences |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Translation (Google Translate, Indic systems):** WSD on `bank` decides if the Tamil equivalent is `நதிக்கரை` (river) or `வங்கி` (financial).
- **Search Engines (Elasticsearch, Lucene):** Query expansion uses disambiguation to map `java` → [island | coffee | programming language] before retrieval.
- **Clinical NLP (cTAKES, MetaMap):** Under-specification prevents premature commitments to ICD codes when symptoms are reported in ambiguous natural language.
- **Dialogue Systems (Rasa, Dialogflow):** Intent classification depends critically on resolving *attachment* and *scope* ambiguities in user utterances.
- **Knowledge Graph Construction (DBpedia, Wikidata):** Entity linking is essentially a disambiguation task over millions of candidate senses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Scope Ambiguity Resolution (Cooper Storage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooper's Storage Mechanism** is the *canonical* under-specification technique for quantifier scope. We now derive its operation step-by-step on the sentence *"Every student read a book."*

---

#### Step 1 — Identify Quantifiers and their Binders

The sentence contains two quantifier-bearing NPs:
- `Every student` — bound by the **universal quantifier** $\forall$
- `a book` — bound by the **existential quantifier** $\exists$

For $n$ quantifiers, the number of scope readings is at most $n!$ (after eliminating impossible permutations such as $\forall$ binding a pronoun outside its scope). For 2 quantifiers, we have **2 readings**.

---

#### Step 2 — Build the Initial Semantic Skeleton (Ignoring Scope)

We first compute the **predicate-argument structure** with **placeholders (holes)** for the quantifier-bound variables.

$$
\lambda x.\lambda y.\text{read}(x, y)
$$

Here `read` is the 2-place predicate. Both `x` (subject) and `y` (object) are **holes** waiting to be filled by quantifier introductions.

---

#### Step 3 — Introduce Quantifiers into Cooper Storage

Cooper's storage $S$ is a **set of pending quantifier-term pairs**. Each entry is of the form $\langle Q, t \rangle$ where $Q$ is a quantifier and $t$ is the term it binds.

For our sentence:

$$
S = \{\, \langle \forall x, \text{Student}(x)\rangle,\; \langle \exists y, \text{Book}(y)\rangle \,\}
$$

The semantic skeleton is stored as a *term in the storage* too:

$$
\text{Sk} = \lambda x.\lambda y.\text{read}(x, y)
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Generate All Scope Readings by Permuting Storage

We extract quantifiers from the store in **every possible order** and apply them to the skeleton, building a full **typed logical form**.

**Reading 1 — $\forall$ outside $\exists$ (object-wide / distributive):**

$$
\forall x.\big(\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y.\big(\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{read}(x, y)\big)\big)
$$

**Reading 2 — $\exists$ outside $\forall$ (subject-wide / collective):**

$$
\exists y.\big(\text{Book}(y) \wedge \forall x.\big(\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{read}(x, y)\big)\big)
$$

Each reading is obtained by **choosing the next quantifier to apply** (the next element of the permutation of storage), and the partial application of the skeleton.

---

#### Step 5 — Encode the Under-Specified Representation (USR)

Instead of materializing both readings, we encode the *common structure* plus the *alternatives*:

$$
\text{USR} = \langle \text{Store} = S, \; \text{Skeleton} = \text{Sk}, \; \text{HOLEs} = \{h_\forall, h_\exists\} \rangle
$$

with constraints:

$$
\text{scope}(h_\forall) \in \{\text{outer}, \text{inner}\}, \quad
\text{scope}(h_\exists) \in \{\text{outer}, \text{inner}\}, \quad
\text{scope}(h_\forall) \neq \text{scope}(h_\exists)
$$

The two concrete readings are obtained by **resolving** these hole variables.

---

#### Step 6 — Cost of the USR vs Concrete Representations

Let $n$ = number of quantifiers, $k$ = average resolution options per hole.

$$
\text{Storage size} = \mathcal{O}(n), \quad \text{USR size} = \mathcal{O}(n + k), \quad \text{Concrete readings} = \mathcal{O}(n! \cdot k^n)
$$

For $n = 4, k = 2$:

$$
\text{USR size} \approx 6 \text{ terms}, \quad \text{Concrete} = 24 \times 16 = 384 \text{ terms}
$$

This **64× reduction** is exactly the engineering motivation for under-specification.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Disambiguation Decision Table

| Ambiguity Type | Linguistic Cue | Default Disambiguator | Fallback |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lexical (Homonymy)** | Distinct POS tags | WordNet first-sense (MFS) | Contextual embeddings (BERT) |
| **Lexical (Polysemy)** | Shared semantic core | Topic model (LDA) | Knowledge-graph overlap |
| **Structural / PP-attachment** | Verb sub-categorization | Lexicalized PCFG (Collins) | Neural parser (Stanford) |
| **Scope** | Quantifier word order | Cooper Storage + preference | Surface-scope heuristic (subject < object) |
| **Anaphoric** | Gender/number agreement | Hobbs algorithm (syntactic) | Centering Theory + salience |
| **Selectional** | Semantic features | Selectional Association (Resnik) | Distributional similarity (word2vec) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **surface-scope heuristic** (object NP out-scopes subject NP unless strong cues dictate otherwise) is the *de-facto* default in statistical MT systems when no disambiguation module is available.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Applying Naïve Bayes WSD

Consider the sentence: *"`Put your money in the bank.`"* We wish to choose the correct sense of `bank` (river vs financial).

**Step 1 — Gather context features:**

$$
c = \{\text{put, money, in, the, bank}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute priors** from a sense-tagged corpus (e.g., SemCor):

$$
P(\text{financial}) = 0.65, \quad P(\text{river}) = 0.35
$$

**Step 3 — Compute likelihoods** for each context word $w_i$ given each sense $s$:

$$
P(\text{money} \mid \text{financial}) = 0.08, \quad P(\text{money} \mid \text{river}) = 0.001
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Rule (Naïve Bayes assumption of feature independence):**

$$
P(s_i \mid c) \propto P(s_i) \cdot \prod_{w_j \in c} P(w_j \mid s_i)
$$

$$
P(\text{financial} \mid c) \propto 0.65 \times 0.08 \times \cdots
$$

$$
P(\text{river} \mid c) \propto 0.35 \times 0.001 \times \cdots
$$

**Step 5 — Argmax:**

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_i} P(s_i \mid c) = \text{financial}
$$

The sense **financial** wins decisively — the contextual cue `money` is a near-perfect discriminator.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Lesk Algorithm (Dictionary-Based WSD)

**Step 1 — Retrieve glosses** for each sense of `bank` from WordNet:

- **Sense 1 (financial):** *"a financial institution … accepts deposits and channels them into lending activities"*
- **Sense 2 (river):** *"sloping land … beside a body of water"*

**Step 2 — Tokenize the context** of `bank` in the sentence:

$$
c = \{\text{put, money, in, the, bank}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute overlap** between context and each gloss (Naive Lesk):

$$
\text{Lesk}(s_1, c) = \vert \{\text{in}\} \cap \{\text{accepts, deposits, channels, lending, institution, ...}\} \vert = 1
$$

Wait — *bank* itself appears in *financial* glosses. Many implementations **augment** glosses with glosses of related synsets (hypernyms, hyponyms). After augmentation:

$$
\text{Lesk}(s_1, c) = 4, \quad \text{Lesk}(s_2, c) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Argmax:**

$$
\hat{s} = s_1 = \text{financial}
$$

Same correct outcome, achieved without any training data — the *primary advantage* of knowledge-based WSD.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Evaluation Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Benchmark** (English all-words WSD, standard test sets)

| System | Knowledge Source | Precision | Recall | F1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Most-Frequent-Sense (MFS) baseline | Corpus statistics | 65.1 | 65.1 | 65.1 |
| Naïve Lesk | WordNet glosses | 58.0 | 58.0 | 58.0 |
| Adapted Lesk (Banerjee-Pedersen) | Extended WordNet | 64.2 | 64.2 | 64.2 |
| Supervised SVM (Yarowsky-style) | SemCor | 69.0 | 67.3 | 68.1 |
| **BERT-based WSD (state-of-the-art)** | Pre-trained LM | 80.3 | 79.6 | 79.9 |

*(Numbers illustrative; actual results vary by test set — SemEval-2 / SemEval-3 / WSD framework.)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Diagram — Taxonomy of Semantic Ambiguity

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SEMANTIC AMBIGUITY] --> B[Lexical Ambiguity]
    A --> C[Structural Ambiguity]
    A --> D[Scope Ambiguity]
    A --> E[Anaphoric Ambiguity]
    A --> F[Selectional Ambiguity]

    B --> B1[Homonymy - Unrelated Senses]
    B --> B2[Polysemy - Related Senses]
    B1 --> B1a[Example: bank - river vs finance]
    B2 --> B2a[Example: head - body vs department]

    C --> C1[PP Attachment]
    C --> C2[Coordination Ambiguity]
    C --> C3[NP vs VP Attachment]
    C1 --> C1a[Example: saw man with telescope]

    D --> D1[Quantifier Scope]
    D --> D2[Negation Scope]
    D1 --> D1a[Example: every student read a book]
    D2 --> D2a[Example: not all students passed]

    E --> E1[Pronoun Reference]
    E --> E2[Definite NP Reference]
    E1 --> E1a[Example: John told Mike he won]

    F --> F1[Verb Subcategorization]
    F --> F2[Adjective Selection]
    F1 --> F1a[Example: newspaper has a baby]
```

### 4.2 Under-Specification Pipeline — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Input Sentence] --> TOK[Tokenizer and POS Tagger]
    TOK --> PARS[Parser - Constituency or Dependency]
    PARS --> SEMGEN[Semantic Generator - Type Logic or Montague]
    SEMGEN --> USR[Under-Specified Representation - USR with HOLES]
    USR --> STORE[Cooper Storage - Quantifier Term Pairs]
    USR --> HOLES[Hole Variables h1 h2 h3]
    STORE --> RESOLVER[Contextual Resolver - World Knowledge and Discourse]
    HOLES --> RESOLVER
    RESOLVER --> READ1[Reading 1 - Fully Specified LF]
    RESOLVER --> READ2[Reading 2 - Fully Specified LF]
    RESOLVER --> READN[Reading N - Fully Specified LF]
    READ1 --> RANK[Ranking Module - Probabilistic or Preference Based]
    READ2 --> RANK
    READN --> RANK
    RANK --> BEST[Best Interpretation - Output]
    RANK --> ALT[Alternative Interpretations - Kept for Backtracking]
```

### 4.3 Disambiguation Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Ambiguous Expression Detected] --> Q1{Type of Ambiguity?}
    Q1 -->|Lexical| Q2{WordNet Available?}
    Q1 -->|Scope| Q3{Number of Quantifiers?}
    Q1 -->|PP Attachment| Q4{Verb Subcat Known?}
    Q1 -->|Anaphoric| Q5{Gender or Number Cue?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| L1[Lesk Algorithm]
    Q2 -->|No| L2[Distributional Similarity from Embeddings]

    Q3 -->|Small less than 3| S1[Exhaustive Cooper Storage]
    Q3 -->|Large gte 3| S2[Surface Scope Heuristic]

    Q4 -->|Yes| P1[Lexicalized PCFG Probability]
    Q4 -->|No| P2[Default NP Attachment]

    Q5 -->|Yes| A1[Hobbs Algorithm or Centering]
    Q5 -->|No| A2[Salience Based Pronoun Resolution]

    L1 --> OUT[Disambiguated Sense]
    L2 --> OUT
    S1 --> OUT
    S2 --> OUT
    P1 --> OUT
    P2 --> OUT
    A1 --> OUT
    A2 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 MRS-Style Under-Specified Representation (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph USR [Under-Specified Representation]
        P1[Predicate: read - Arg0 x Arg1 y]
        P2[Predicate: student - Arg0 x]
        P3[Predicate: book - Arg0 y]
        H1[HOLE h1 - bound by universal]
        H2[HOLE h2 - bound by existential]
        Q1[Quantifier: forall x Student x]
        Q2[Quantifier: exists y Book y]
    end

    Q1 --> H1
    Q2 --> H2
    H1 --> P1
    H2 --> P1
    P2 --> H1
    P3 --> H2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The two HOLEs ($h_1$, $h_2$) represent *unbound* argument positions in the `read` predicate. Each hole is constrained to be filled by exactly one of the two quantifiers from storage. The *resolution order* (i.e., which quantifier takes outer scope) is left open — that is the essence of under-specification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Differentiate between homonymy and polysemy with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

Homonymy refers to words that share the same **spelling and/or pronunciation** but have **unrelated meanings** originating from **different etymological roots**. The senses are semantically disjoint. *Examples:* `bank` (river edge) vs `bank` (financial institution); `bark` (dog sound) vs `bark` (tree covering).

Polysemy refers to a **single word** that carries **multiple but related meanings** sharing a common semantic core. The senses are historically and semantically connected. *Examples:* `head` (of a body), `head` (of an organization), `head` (of a beer) — all share the schema *{topmost, leading, foremost entity}*; `mouth` (of a person) vs `mouth` (of a river).

The key test: *can the senses be distinguished by etymology?* If **yes** → homonymy; if **no** → polysemy. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is under-specification in formal semantics? Why is it computationally advantageous?**

**Model Answer:**

Under-specification is a representational technique in which a linguistic expression is assigned a **partial logical form** that encodes only the meaning components **common to all** its possible readings, leaving ambiguous parts as **placeholders (holes)** to be resolved later by a contextual or pragmatic module.

**Computational advantages:**
1. **Avoids combinatorial explosion** — for $n$ ambiguous slots with $k$ resolutions each, an under-specified representation requires $\mathcal{O}(n + k)$ storage instead of $\mathcal{O}(k^n)$ concrete readings.
2. **Modularity** — disambiguation is delegated to a component (discourse model, knowledge base) that possesses the relevant evidence, allowing clean separation of concerns in the NLP pipeline.
3. **Cognitive plausibility** — mirrors human incremental comprehension, where listeners entertain multiple readings in parallel and prune as evidence accumulates.
4. **Reusability** — the same USR can be refined differently in different contexts (e.g., the same ambiguous news headline processed by an MT system vs a QA system). *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, Q-B1]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of **semantic ambiguity** with a suitable example for each. Discuss how **scope ambiguity** arises in natural language sentences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an **under-specified representation (USR)** system. Describe the role of **Cooper's Storage** in handling quantifier scope ambiguity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Types of Semantic Ambiguity:**

1. **Lexical Ambiguity:** A single word has multiple senses.
   - *Homonymy* — unrelated senses: `bat` (animal) vs `bat` (sports equipment)
   - *Polysemy* — related senses: `mouth` (of cave / of river / of person)

2. **Structural (Syntactic) Ambiguity:** Same string, multiple parse trees.
   - *"I saw the man with the telescope"* — PP attaches to VP or NP.

3. **Scope Ambiguity:** Quantifier / operator scope order is indeterminate.
   - *"Every student read a book"* — $\forall \exists$ or $\exists \forall$

4. **Anaphoric Ambiguity:** Reference of pronoun / NP is indeterminate.
   - *"John told Mike that he won"* — antecedent of *he* unclear.

5. **Selectional Ambiguity:** Predicate-argument combination violates or extends typical selectional restrictions.
   - *"The newspaper has a baby"* — `baby` is an unusual argument for `have`.

**Scope Ambiguity — Detailed Discussion:**

Scope ambiguity arises when **two or more operators** (quantifiers $\forall$, $\exists$, negation $\neg$, modals) can each take **outer or inner scope** over the others. The syntactic surface order often does not determine the semantic scope.

*Worked example:* `"Every student read a book."`

- **Surface order:** subject `Every student` precedes object `a book` → suggests `∀` out-scopes `∃`
- **But syntax does not uniquely determine scope.** Two readings are logically possible:
    - **Reading 1 (distributive):** $\forall x.\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y.\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Read}(x,y)$ — *each student read some (possibly different) book*
    - **Reading 2 (collective):** $\exists y.\text{Book}(y) \wedge \forall x.\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Read}(x,y)$ — *one specific book read by all students*

*Disambiguation cues:*
- **Syntactic** — word order (subject < object is the surface-scope default)
- **Lexical** — quantifier type (`every` strongly out-scopes `a`)
- **Discourse** — focus, topic, previous mention
- **Pragmatic** — real-world plausibility (e.g., "a book" is more likely collective if `book` is definite-like)

*(7 marks — distributed: 3 marks taxonomy + 4 marks scope-ambiguity discussion)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**USR Architecture (refer to Section 4.4 block diagram for the visual):**

The USR pipeline has three logical layers:

1. **Skeleton layer** — contains the *shared* semantic content: predicate-argument structure, type information, and lexical-concept labels. Independent of scope and attachment decisions.

2. **Hole / Variable layer** — declares the *open slots* $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_m$ corresponding to ambiguous positions (e.g., quantifier bound variables, PP attachment points).

3. **Constraints layer** — encodes *which hole can be filled by what*: dominance constraints, equality / inequality on hole variables, and type constraints on fillers.

**Role of Cooper's Storage:**

Cooper's Storage is the **canonical mechanism for handling quantifier scope ambiguity** under the USR framework.

**Mechanism (exhaustive steps):**

1. The parser produces a syntactic tree.
2. The semantic component builds the **predicate-argument skeleton** of the sentence with **typed holes** for each quantifier-bound variable.
3. Each quantifier-bearing NP contributes a **store entry** $\langle Q, t \rangle$ to the **Cooper Store**, where $Q$ is the quantifier and $t$ is the term it binds.
4. The resolver **extracts quantifiers from the store in all possible orders** and applies them to the skeleton, producing a set of fully-specified logical forms.
5. The number of generated readings is bounded by the number of permutations of the store (i.e., $n!$ for $n$ quantifiers, modulo grammatical constraints).

**Example:** For *"Every student read a book"* with 2 quantifiers, the store is:

$$
S = \{\langle \forall x, \text{Student}(x)\rangle, \langle \exists y, \text{Book}(y)\rangle\}
$$

Two orderings yield the **distributive** and **collective** readings discussed above. The USR captures both via a single compact representation with two holes; the resolver expands them on demand.

**Advantage:** A system can process the ambiguous input **once**, store the USR compactly, and resolve the scope *only when* downstream components (e.g., the discourse model) have the evidence to disambiguate — saving both computation and representational bandwidth.

*(7 marks — distributed: 3 marks architecture + 4 marks Cooper's storage explanation)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, Q-B2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Lesk algorithm** for Word Sense Disambiguation with an example. State one major limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **PP-attachment ambiguity** in detail. Compare the **NP-attachment** and **VP-attachment** hypotheses, with at least one example. How can statistical / ML methods be used to resolve this ambiguity? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Lesk Algorithm — Concept:**

The Lesk algorithm (1986) is a **knowledge-based WSD** method that uses the glosses (definitions) and examples in a machine-readable dictionary (typically **WordNet**) to disambiguate a polysemous word by finding the sense whose definition has the **largest overlap** with the surrounding context of the word.

**Original Lesk Algorithm — Step by Step:**

1. For an ambiguous word $w$ in context $c$, retrieve the gloss + examples of **every sense** $s_i$ of $w$ from the dictionary.
2. Tokenize the gloss of each sense and the context of $w$.
3. Compute the **overlap score** = number of **common content words** (after stop-word removal and stemming) between context and gloss:

$$
\text{Lesk}(s_i, c) = \vert \text{gloss}(s_i) \cap \text{context}(c) \vert
$$

4. Select the sense with the **highest overlap**:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_i} \text{Lesk}(s_i, c)
$$

5. In case of tie, break arbitrarily or by Most-Frequent-Sense fallback.

**Worked Example:**

*Sentence:* `"The bank can guarantee deposits will never lose value."`

*Context words (after stop-word removal):* `{bank, guarantee, deposits, never, lose, value}`

*Sense 1 (financial institution):* gloss = *"a financial institution that accepts deposits and channels those deposits into lending activities"*

*Sense 2 (river edge):* gloss = *"sloping land beside a body of water"*

- Overlap with Sense 1: `{deposits}` → **score = 1**
- Overlap with Sense 2: `{}` → **score = 0**

**Predicted sense:** Sense 1 (financial institution). ✓

**Major Limitation — Short Context vs. Short Gloss:**

The **Naive Lesk** suffers from a *very low overlap rate* because dictionary glosses are extremely short (5–15 words) and modern contexts may have hundreds of words — most of which are content words unrelated to any sense. This results in **frequent ties** and low accuracy (~50–58% on standard test sets).

**Improved variants:** *Adapted Lesk* (Banerjee & Pedersen, 2002) augments each gloss with the glosses of related synsets (hypernyms, hyponyms, holonyms) — boosting F1 to ~64%. *Corpus Lesk* uses corpus-derived sense signatures instead of glosses.

*(7 marks — distributed: 1 mark concept + 3 marks algorithm steps + 2 marks example + 1 mark limitation)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**PP-Attachment Ambiguity — Concept:**

A **prepositional phrase (PP)** is a phrase consisting of a preposition followed by a complement (NP, S, etc.). In many English sentences, a PP can logically attach at **multiple positions** in the parse tree, generating **structurally distinct** parses, each with a different meaning. This is the **PP-attachment ambiguity**.

**NP-Attachment vs. VP-Attachment:**

Consider the sentence:
> *"He ate the pizza with olives."*

- **Reading 1 — NP-attachment:** `with olives` modifies `the pizza` → *the pizza (which had olives) was eaten*
- **Reading 2 — VP-attachment:** `with olives` modifies `ate` → *the act of eating was done using olives (as utensil or accompaniment)*

Another classic:
> *"I saw the man with the telescope."*
- **NP-attachment:** *I saw the man [who had the telescope]*
- **VP-attachment:** *I [used the telescope to] see the man*

**Empirical Distribution (Ratnaparkhi 1998, WSJ corpus):**
- NP-attachment: **~52%**
- VP-attachment: **~34%**
- Other (S-, ADJP-attachment): **~14%**

**Statistical / ML Approach to Resolution:**

The de-facto approach is to train a **supervised classifier** (Maximum Entropy, SVM, or neural) on features extracted from the **attachment site**.

**Standard feature set:**

| Feature | Example | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Head noun of NP** | `pizza`, `telescope` | Lexicalized preference |
| **Head verb of VP** | `ate`, `saw` | Sub-categorization |
| **Preposition identity** | `with`, `in`, `on` | Strong cue (e.g., `with` prefers NP) |
| **Distance from head** | 2 words, 4 words | Closer → more likely NP-attachment |
| **Definiteness of NP** | `the pizza` vs `a pizza` | Definite NPs favor NP-attachment |

**Model architectures (in increasing complexity):**

1. **Lexicalized PCFG** (Collins 1999) — adds head-word annotations to grammar rules; uses smoothed probabilities of attachment configurations.
2. **Maximum Entropy classifier** (Ratnaparkhi 1998) — combines dozens of overlapping features with log-linear model:

$$
P(\text{attach} \mid \text{context}) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\left(\sum_{i} \lambda_i f_i(\text{context}, \text{attach})\right)
$$

3. **Neural models** (Recurrent / Transformer encoders of the candidate parse fragments) — current state-of-the-art, F1 ~95%+ on standard benchmarks.

**Why NP-attachment is the default in absence of cues:** Linguistic and cognitive research suggests that **hearers prefer NP-attachment** as the lowest-cost parse — known as **Right Association** or **Late Closure** (Frazier & Fodor 1978).

*(7 marks — distributed: 2 marks concept + 2 marks NP/VP comparison + 3 marks ML resolution)*

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS**
>
> 1. **Confusing "ambiguity" with "vagueness":** *Vagueness* is a property of a single expression with indeterminate boundaries (e.g., `tall`, `heap`). *Ambiguity* involves **discrete** alternative readings. Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks for mixing them.
> 2. **Wrong example for polysemy vs homonymy:** A common error is treating `bank` (river/finance) as polysemy. **It is homonymy** — the two senses have *no shared semantic core* and different etymologies. The classic polysemy example is `head`.
> 3. **Confusing "lexical" with "structural" ambiguity:** "Bank" alone is *lexically* ambiguous; "I saw the man with the telescope" is *structurally* ambiguous. Don't conflate them.
> 4. **Scope of `not`:** The sentence *"All students did not pass"* is **ambiguous** between $\neg \forall$ and $\forall \neg$. Many students write a single logical form — examiners expect *both* readings.
> 5. **Stating "More Frequent Sense = Correct Sense" without justification:** The MFS heuristic is a *baseline*, not a guarantee. Acknowledge it as a *fall-back*, not a theory.
> 6. **In USR question:** Students often forget to mention the **Constraints Layer** (the third component alongside Skeleton and Holes). Examiners specifically test for completeness of the USR architecture.
> 7. **Mis-stating Cooper's Storage:** Cooper's storage deals **only with quantifier scope ambiguity** — not with PP-attachment, not with lexical ambiguity. Misattributing it is a 1-mark deduction.
> 8. **No examples in taxonomy question:** Always give **at least one example per category**. A taxonomy without examples gets partial credit only.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Handling Semantic Ambiguity and Under-Specification**

- **Semantic Ambiguity = one expression → multiple meanings.** Always specify whether it is *lexical*, *structural*, *scope*, *anaphoric*, or *selectional*.
- **Homonymy vs. Polysemy:** Etymology is the *test* — different origins = homonymy, shared origin and related senses = polysemy.
- **Scope ambiguity** arises with multiple operators (quantifiers, negation). For $n$ quantifiers, up to $n!$ readings exist. *Surface order is a default, not a rule.*
- **PP-attachment default = NP-attachment** (~52% of cases). *Late Closure* and *Right Association* are the cognitive justifications.
- **Under-Specification (USR)** = partial logical form with **holes** + **constraints** + **skeleton**. *Don't disambiguate prematurely.*
- **Cooper's Storage** is the **specific** mechanism for *quantifier-scope* USR. It contains $\langle Q, t \rangle$ pairs; permutations yield readings.
- **MRS (Minimal Recursion Semantics)** is the modern realisation of USR in HPSG-style grammars; uses hole variables $h_i$ with dominance constraints.
- **Lesk Algorithm** = dictionary-gloss overlap WSD. *Naive Lesk* is weak (~58% F1); *Adapted Lesk* (augmented glosses) improves to ~64% F1.
- **Bayesian WSD** = $\hat{s} = \arg\max P(s_i) \prod P(c_j \mid s_i)$. *Bag-of-context features*, Naïve Bayes assumption of conditional independence.
- **MFS baseline (Most-Frequent-Sense)** achieves ~65% F1 — surprisingly strong, often a *tough-to-beat* baseline.
- **Modern state-of-the-art WSD** uses contextual embeddings (BERT, ELMo) — F1 ~80% on SemEval benchmarks.
- **Selectional restrictions** are an *unsupervised* disambiguation tool: the verb `eat` prefers `<animate>` for its object; violation signals unusual usage.
- **Three engineering benefits of USR:** (i) compactness $\mathcal{O}(n + k)$ vs. $\mathcal{O}(k^n)$, (ii) modularity (delegated disambiguation), (iii) cognitive plausibility.
- **Real-world applications:** MT (sense choice affects target word), IR (query expansion), clinical NLP (ICD code mapping), dialogue systems (intent classification), KG construction (entity linking).
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
    - Sense entropy: $H(S \mid w) = -\sum_i p(s_i \mid w) \log_2 p(s_i \mid w)$
    - F1-measure: $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$
    - USR cost: $\prod_{i=1}^{m} k_i$ readings from $m$ holes
    - Lesk overlap: $\text{Lesk}(s, c) = \vert \text{gloss}(s) \cap \text{context}(c) \vert$
- **Examiners love:** taxonomy tables, worked WSD examples (Lesk / Naïve Bayes), and USR diagrams with **Hole variables clearly labeled**. Always draw the block diagram for USR questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD): Techniques for Resolving Word Meaning Ambiguities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD): Techniques for Resolving Word Meaning Ambiguities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)

**Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** is the computational task of identifying the correct meaning (*sense*) of a polysemous or homonymous word when it appears in a particular context, by selecting the appropriate sense from a predefined sense inventory (such as WordNet, BabelNet, or a domain-specific thesaurus).

Formally, given a word $w$ with a set of senses $S_w = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}$ drawn from a sense inventory, and a context $C = \{w_{-n}, \ldots, w_{-1}, w, w_{+1}, \ldots, w_{+n}\}$ of surrounding words, the WSD task is to determine a function:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S_w} \; P(s_i \mid C)
$$

That is, the system must choose the sense $s_i$ that maximises its posterior probability given the surrounding linguistic context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** WSD is classified as an **AI-hard problem** (Navigli, 2009) because it often requires deep semantic and world knowledge. A classic WSD benchmark accuracy ceiling for *supervised* systems is **~65–70%** on the **Senseval/SemEval** datasets, while all-words WSD on *All-Words Task* still hovers around **~70–80%** for state-of-the-art neural systems (BERT/ELMo/GLM-based).

---

## 1.2 Types of Lexical Ambiguity

Ambiguity is the *root cause* of the WSD problem. We distinguish:

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Homonymy** | Identical spelling/sound, *unrelated* meanings (different etymologies) | **Bank** (river edge) vs **Bank** (financial institution) |
| **Polysemy** | Identical form, *related* senses that have radiated historically | **Head** (body part) → (head of state), (head of a queue) |
| **Metaphoric ambiguity** | Word used figuratively, not in its literal sense | "The economy is **growing**" |
| **Categorial ambiguity** | Word belongs to multiple syntactic categories | "**Book** a book" (verb vs noun) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syntactic ambiguity** (e.g., "I saw the man with the telescope") is *not* the primary target of WSD — that is the responsibility of **Parsing** modules. WSD specifically addresses *lexical* (word-level) ambiguity of meaning.

---

## 1.3 The Two WSD Task Variants

KTU examinations often test the *task formulation* as well. WSD has two canonical formulations:

**1. Lexical Sample Task**
- A small set of target words is pre-selected.
- For each occurrence of a target word, classify it into one of its senses.
- Example: Only the word "**plant**" needs to be disambiguated across a corpus.

**2. All-Words Task**
- *Every* ambiguous word in a running text must be disambiguated.
- The set of words is **open-vocabulary** and not pre-defined.
- Closer to real NLP pipelines (MT, IE, Q&A).

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — WSD as a "GPS for Words"

Imagine you are a tourist in a city with **thousands of streets all named "Main Street"** — but every "Main Street" actually leads to a completely different place. When someone says *"I live on Main Street"*, you cannot tell *which* Main Street they mean unless you look at **contextual clues**: their city, the landmarks mentioned, the time of day, the speaker's accent.

**WSD is exactly this GPS problem for words.**
- The street name ("Main Street") = the **ambiguous word form**.
- The cities/contexts = the **surrounding words / discourse**.
- The address book = the **sense inventory (WordNet)**.
- The "right" interpretation = the **sense label**.

A human resolves the ambiguity in milliseconds using background knowledge. A machine, however, has to *learn* this background knowledge from corpora or knowledge bases — and this is precisely the engineering challenge WSD tackles.

---

## 1.5 Why WSD Matters — KTU Engineering Relevance

WSD is **not** an isolated academic exercise. It is a **foundational pre-processing module** for nearly every downstream NLP application:

- **Machine Translation (MT):** Translating *"bank"* to French as *"rive"* (river bank) vs *"banque"* (financial bank).
- **Information Retrieval (IR):** Search engine ranking improves when "Jaguar car" vs "Jaguar animal" are distinguished.
- **Question Answering & Chatbots:** Correctly interpreting user intent.
- **Sentiment Analysis:** "Unbiased review" vs "biased toward X" hinge on subtle sense choices.
- **Text Mining & Knowledge Graph Construction:** Entity disambiguation directly depends on WSD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without WSD, downstream NLP systems inherit the *propagation-of-error* problem — a single wrong sense assignment can flip the meaning of an entire sentence in machine translation or sentiment scoring.

---

## 1.6 Sense Inventories — The Reference Data

WSD systems are only as good as their **sense inventory**. The most common ones tested in KTU 2024:

- **WordNet (Princeton):** Hierarchical lexical database with ~117,000 synsets for English. Each sense = a *synset* (synonym set).
- **BabelNet:** Multilingual, encyclopedic, integrates WordNet + Wikipedia.
- **UMLS (Unified Medical Language System):** Domain-specific for biomedical WSD.
- **Senseval/SemEval Gold Standards:** Manually annotated corpora used as training/test data.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of WSD Approaches

WSD techniques are classified into **four major families**. The KTU examiner expects you to know *all four* and the canonical algorithms under each:

```
WSD Approaches
├── 1. Knowledge-Based (Unsupervised, KB-only)
│     ├── Lesk Algorithm (1986)
│     ├── Extended/Adapted Lesk
│     ├── Selectional Restrictions (Wilks)
│     ├── Conceptual Density (Agirre & Rigau)
│     └── Graph-based (Navigli & Lapata)
│
├── 2. Supervised Machine Learning
│     ├── Naive Bayes
│     ├── Decision Lists (Yarowsky)
│     ├── SVM / k-NN
│     ├── Neural Networks (CNN, BiLSTM, BERT)
│     └── Memory-Based Learning (MBL)
│
├── 3. Semi-Supervised (Bootstrapping)
│     ├── Yarowsky's Bootstrapping (1995)
│     ├── Co-Training (Blum & Mitchell)
│     └── Self-Training
│
└── 4. Unsupervised
      ├── Contextual Clustering
      ├── Word Sense Induction (LSI/LSA, LDA)
      ├── Co-occurrence Graphs
      └── Neural Embeddings (Word2Vec, BERT contextual)
```

---

## 2.2 Knowledge-Based: Lesk Algorithm (1986)

**Original Lesk Algorithm** — Michael Lesk, 1986:

**Core Idea:** The correct sense of a word in a context is the one whose dictionary definition (gloss) shares the *maximum number of words* with the context words of the target word.

**Steps:**
1. For word $w$ in context $C$, retrieve dictionary definitions (glosses) $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_k$ of all candidate senses from the sense inventory.
2. Compute the *overlap score* between the words in $C$ and the words in $G_i$.
3. Select the sense $G_i$ with the highest overlap.

**Formal overlap score:**

$$
\text{Lesk}(s_i) = \left\vert \; \text{Tokens}(G_i) \;\cap\; \text{Tokens}(C) \;\right\vert
$$

**Variants:**
- **Simplified Lesk:** Removes stopwords before counting overlap.
- **Extended Lesk (Banerjee & Pedersen, 2002):** Expands the comparison to include glosses of related concepts in the WordNet hierarchy (hypernyms, hyponyms, meronyms).
- **Adapted Lesk (Basile et al., 2014):** Uses distributional semantics and word embeddings to compute a *weighted* overlap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation of Lesk:** Suffers from the *short-gloss problem* — WordNet definitions are typically only **10–20 words long**, so the overlap is noisy. Word Sense Disambiguation using Random Walk (Navigli & Lapata) and graph-connectivity methods were developed to address this.

---

## 2.3 Supervised: Naive Bayes Classifier for WSD

The most common *textbook* supervised approach.

**Generative model:** For context features (surrounding words / POS tags / collocations), compute the posterior probability of each sense:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S_w} \; P(s_i) \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(f_j \mid s_i)
$$

where:
- $P(s_i)$ = prior probability of sense $s_i$ in training data.
- $P(f_j \mid s_i)$ = probability of observing feature $f_j$ given sense $s_i$.
- The **naive** (conditionally independent) assumption is that features $f_j$ are independent given the sense.

**Smoothing (Laplace/Add-One):**

$$
P(f_j \mid s_i) = \frac{\text{count}(f_j, s_i) + 1}{\sum_{f'} \text{count}(f', s_i) + \vert V \vert}
$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size.

**Features typically used:**
- **Collocations:** Words at positions $-2, -1, +1, +2$ around the target.
- **Bag-of-words in context window.**
- **POS tags** of surrounding words.
- **Syntactic dependencies** (e.g., subject/object relations).

---

## 2.4 Supervised: Yarowsky's Decision Lists (1994)

A *rule-based* ML approach. For each sense $s_i$ and feature $f$, compute the log-likelihood ratio:

$$
\text{LLR}(f, s_i) = \log \frac{P(f \mid s_i)}{P(f \mid s_j)} \quad \text{for } j \neq i
$$

A decision list is an **ordered** set of (feature, sense) rules, sorted descending by $\vert \text{LLR} \vert$. The first matching rule wins.

> [!NOTE]
> Decision Lists are the foundation of **Yarowsky's Bootstrapping algorithm** for semi-supervised WSD.

---

## 2.5 Semi-Supervised: Yarowsky's Bootstrapping (1995)

**Procedure:**
1. Seed the algorithm with a small set of high-confidence examples (e.g., the word "*plant*" in biology texts → life-form sense; in industrial texts → factory sense).
2. Train a WSD classifier on the seed set.
3. Apply the classifier to the unlabelled corpus.
4. Add high-confidence predictions to the training set.
5. Re-train and iterate.

This leverages the *one-sense-per-discourse* and *one-sense-per-collocation* heuristics as constraints.

---

## 2.6 Unsupervised: Contextual Clustering & Topic Models

When labelled data is unavailable, WSD becomes *Word Sense Induction* (WSI):
- Cluster the contexts of the target word.
- Each cluster is treated as a distinct sense.
- Techniques: **LSA (Latent Semantic Analysis)**, **LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation)**, **contextualized embeddings** (BERT, ELMo).

---

## 2.7 Evaluation Metrics for WSD

KTU may ask for the standard evaluation formulae. The two principal metrics are:

**1. Recall / Accuracy (for lexical sample):**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Number of correctly disambiguated instances}}{\text{Total number of test instances}}
$$

**2. F1-Score (for all-words / multi-class):**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}, \quad P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}
$$

**3. MFS (Most Frequent Sense) Baseline:**

$$
\text{MFS Accuracy} = \frac{\text{count of majority sense in test}}{\text{Total test instances}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A supervised WSD system is considered **useful** only if it beats the **MFS baseline** by a statistically significant margin. This is the KTU exam's favourite comparison question.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise this table — it covers ~80% of formula-based questions in the 14-mark problems.

| # | Technique | Core Formula | Variables & Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bayes Decision Rule for WSD** | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_i} P(s_i \mid C)$ | $s_i$ = candidate sense, $C$ = context |
| 2 | **Naive Bayes (WSD)** | $P(s_i \mid C) \propto P(s_i) \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(f_j \mid s_i)$ | $f_j$ = context feature, independence assumed |
| 3 | **Laplace Smoothing** | $P(f_j \mid s_i) = \dfrac{\text{count}(f_j, s_i) + 1}{\sum_{f'}\text{count}(f', s_i) + \vert V \vert}$ | $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size |
| 4 | **Lesk Overlap Score** | $\text{Lesk}(s_i) = \vert \text{Tokens}(G_i) \cap \text{Tokens}(C) \vert$ | $G_i$ = sense gloss, $C$ = context |
| 5 | **Cosine Similarity (Vector Space WSD)** | $\text{sim}(u, v) = \dfrac{u \cdot v}{\Vert u \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert v \Vert_2}$ | $u, v$ = sense/context vectors |
| 6 | **Pointwise Mutual Information** | $\text{PMI}(w, s) = \log \dfrac{P(w, s)}{P(w) P(s)}$ | $w$ = word, $s$ = sense |
| 7 | **Information Content (Resnik, 1995)** | $\text{IC}(s) = -\log_2 P(s)$ | $P(s)$ = probability of synset $s$ in a corpus |
| 8 | **Yarowsky's Decision List Score** | $\text{LLR}(f, s) = \log \dfrac{P(f \mid s)}{P(f \mid \neg s)}$ | $f$ = feature, $s$ = sense |
| 9 | **TF-IDF Weighting** | $\text{tfidf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log \dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | $N$ = total docs, $\text{df}$ = doc frequency |
| 10 | **Accuracy / F1** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{N}, \quad F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ | Standard classification metrics |
| 11 | **Conceptual Density (Agirre & Rigau)** | $\text{CD}(s, C) = \dfrac{\sum \text{depth}(s, h) \in \text{hyper}(s)}{\text{depth}(\text{lcs}(s, C))}$ | Measures density of a sense in WordNet graph |
| 12 | **MFS Baseline** | $\text{MFS\_Acc} = \max_i \dfrac{\text{count}(s_i)}{N}$ | Highest single-sense accuracy |

> **Constraint Note:** Vertical bars (e.g., for absolute value $\vert V \vert$) are rendered as `\vert` so they do not break the markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.9 Engineering Real-World Utility of WSD

| Application | How WSD Helps |
|---|---|
| **Google Translate / DeepL** | Selects correct target-language word for polysemous source terms (e.g., English "**bat**" → French "**chauve-souris**" vs "**batte de cricket**"). |
| **IBM Watson / Healthcare AI** | Disambiguates medical terms (e.g., "**cold**" as temperature vs common cold vs "cold" as in "cold feet"). |
| **Search Engines (Elasticsearch, Lucene)** | Query expansion using sense-specific synonyms improves precision by ~15–25%. |
| **Virtual Assistants (Siri, Alexa)** | Resolves user commands like "*play the bank*" (music album) vs "*play a song by The Bank*". |
| **Sentiment / Review Mining** | Distinguishes "**small** business" (positive) from "**small** screen" (potentially negative). |
