- String-match features (exact, head-word, lemma match)
- Syntactic features (same/different grammatical role, c-command)
- Semantic features (gender, number, animacy, named-entity type)
- Pronoun-specific features (definite NP vs pronoun)

### 2.4.2 Mention-Rank Model

- For each anaphor, **rank all preceding candidates**; the highest-scoring compatible one wins.
- Avoids the issue of pairwise over-prediction.
- Often trained with **maximum-margin rank loss**.

### 2.4.3 Entity-Mention Model

- Clusters are built incrementally. For each new mention, decide whether to:
  - (a) **Link** it to an existing cluster, or
  - (b) **Start** a new singleton cluster.
- Provides globally optimal decisions.

### 2.4.4 Clustering-Based Models

- **Agglomerative clustering** (Soon et al., 2001) — merge closest mention pairs iteratively.
- **Graph-based clustering** — mentions as nodes, edges weighted by coreference probability.
- **Neural models** (Lee et al., 2017; Joshi et al., 2019) — end-to-end **span-ranking** with BERT/transformer embeddings.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All key quantities required for KTU-level numerical or descriptive questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MUC Recall** | $R_{MUC} = \dfrac{\sum_i \bigl( m_i - p_i \bigr)}{\sum_i \bigl( m_i - 1 \bigr)}$ | $m_i$ = mentions in gold chain $i$, $p_i$ = predicted partition links |
| 2 | **MUC Precision** | $P_{MUC} = \dfrac{\sum_i \bigl( m_i - p'_i \bigr)}{\sum_i \bigl( m_i - 1 \bigr)}$ | $p'_i$ = predicted partition for gold chain $i$ |
| 3 | **MUC F1** | $F1_{MUC} = \dfrac{2 P_{MUC} R_{MUC}}{P_{MUC} + R_{MUC}}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 4 | **B³ Precision (per mention)** | $P_i = \dfrac{\vert C(g_i) \cap C(p_i) \vert}{\vert C(p_i) \vert}$ | $C(g_i)$ = gold cluster of $i$, $C(p_i)$ = predicted cluster |
| 5 | **B³ Recall (per mention)** | $R_i = \dfrac{\vert C(g_i) \cap C(p_i) \vert}{\vert C(g_i) \vert}$ | — |
| 6 | **B³ F1** | $F1_{B^3} = \dfrac{2 P_{B^3} R_{B^3}}{P_{B^3} + R_{B^3}}$ | Micro-averaged |
| 7 | **CoNLL F1 (Final Score)** | $F1_{CoNLL} = \dfrac{F1_{MUC} + F1_{B^3} + F1_{CEAF}}{3}$ | Average of 3 metrics |
| 8 | **Pairwise F1** | $F1_{pair} = \dfrac{2 TP}{2 TP + FP + FN}$ | Simple link-based metric |
| 9 | **Singletons** | $S = \sum_i \mathbb{1}\bigl[\vert C_i \vert = 1\bigr]$ | Count of clusters with 1 mention |
| 10 | **Antecedent Probability (Rank)** | $P(a_j \vert m_i) = \dfrac{\exp \bigl( s(m_i, a_j) \bigr)}{\sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \exp \bigl( s(m_i, a_k) \bigr)}$ | Softmax over antecedent scores |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse** "coreference" with "anaphora". Coreference is the *relation set*; anaphora is the *direction-dependent* relation. The two overlap but are not synonyms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Hobbs Algorithm on a Sample Sentence

Consider the classic demonstration sentence from Hobbs' 1978 paper:

> *"The daughter of the king **who** had **her** own ambitions was determined to rule the kingdom."*

**Task:** Resolve the pronoun **"her"**.

### Step 1 — Identify the pronoun and its parse position

We parse the sentence and locate "her" in the tree as a possessive pronoun inside the relative clause `who had her own ambitions`.

### Step 2 — Apply the algorithm's traversal from the current sentence

We begin traversing left-to-right, breadth-first, from the **S node above "her"**.

### Step 3 — Breadth-first search yields candidates

$$
\text{Candidate set at depth 1: } \{ \text{had}, \text{who}, \text{her}, \text{own}, \text{ambitions} \}
$$

No Noun Phrase (NP) here yet → we go up.

### Step 4 — Climb to parent and traverse left subtrees

We climb the tree. When we hit a parent **NP** (the relative clause's NP), we look at **left siblings** in breadth-first order:

$$
\text{Left sibling: } \{ \text{king}, \text{The}, \text{daughter of the king}, \text{of}, \text{the} \}
$$

The first NP encountered is **"the king"**.

### Step 5 — Apply Constraints (Binding Theory & Agreement)

- **Gender:** "her" is feminine ⟹ antecedent must be feminine. ❌ "the king" is masculine. **Reject.**

### Step 6 — Continue traversal

Next NP: **"The daughter of the king"** (the entire subject NP).

- **Gender:** feminine ✓
- **Number:** singular ✓
- **Semantic role:** "daughter" is animate ✓

### Step 7 — Select Antecedent

$$
\text{Antecedent}(\text{her}) = \text{"The daughter of the king"}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Hobbs' algorithm correctly handles the classic *picture-of-the-painting* ambiguity by binding **"her"** to the **daughter**, not the king. This is because Principle C of Binding Theory forbids an R-expression from binding a pronoun within its local domain.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mention Types vs Anaphoric Patterns

| Mention Type | Most Common Antecedent Type | Hardest Sub-case | Standard Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Personal pronoun (*he/she/it*) | Closest compatible NP | "It" (referential vs pleonastic) | Rule-based + neural classifier |
| Possessive pronoun (*his/her*) | Possessor NP | Reflexive "his own" | Binding theory constraints |
| Reflexive (*himself*) | Subject of same clause | Long-distance reflexives | Local c-command rule |
| Demonstrative (*this, that*) | Topic/focus NP | Discourse-deictic "this" | Centering Theory |
| Definite NP | Salient prior entity | Bridging anaphora | World knowledge |
| Proper noun alias | Full form earlier | Acronym expansion | String match + abbreviation dict |
| One-anaphora (*one, ones*) | Count noun NP | Set-membership inference | Type matching |

## 3.3 Derivation of the CoNLL F1 Score

Given three sub-metrics computed on the same output:

$$
F1_{MUC} = 65.2, \quad F1_{B^3} = 70.4, \quad F1_{CEAF} = 68.0
$$

The CoNLL F1 (the official score used in CoNLL-2011/2012 shared tasks) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F1_{CoNLL} &= \frac{1}{3} \left( F1_{MUC} + F1_{B^3} + F1_{CEAF} \right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{3} \left( 65.2 + 70.4 + 68.0 \right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{3} \times 203.6 \\[6pt]
&= 67.866\overline{6} \\[6pt]
&\approx 67.87
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The unweighted mean is the **de facto** standard; some variants use weighted averages, but the CoNLL shared task uses simple arithmetic mean.

## 3.4 Worked Example — B³ Metric Calculation

Suppose gold clusters and system-predicted clusters are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \bigl\{ \{a, b, c\},\ \{d, e\} \bigr\} \\
S &= \bigl\{ \{a, b\},\ \{c, d, e\} \bigr\}
\end{aligned}
$$

For each mention compute $P_i$ and $R_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \frac{\vert \{a,b,c\} \cap \{a,b\} \vert}{\vert \{a,b\} \vert} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.00 \\
R_a &= \frac{\vert \{a,b,c\} \cap \{a,b\} \vert}{\vert \{a,b,c\} \vert} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= \frac{2}{2} = 1.00 \\
R_b &= \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{\vert \{a,b,c\} \cap \{c,d,e\} \vert}{\vert \{c,d,e\} \vert} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \\
R_c &= \frac{\vert \{a,b,c\} \cap \{c,d,e\} \vert}{\vert \{a,b,c\} \vert} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= \frac{\vert \{d,e\} \cap \{c,d,e\} \vert}{\vert \{c,d,e\} \vert} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 \\
R_d &= \frac{\vert \{d,e\} \cap \{c,d,e\} \vert}{\vert \{d,e\} \vert} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e &= \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 \\
R_e &= \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Aggregate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{B^3} &= \frac{1.00 + 1.00 + 0.333 + 0.667 + 0.667}{5} = \frac{3.667}{5} \approx 0.733 \\
R_{B^3} &= \frac{0.667 + 0.667 + 0.333 + 0.667 + 0.667}{5} = \frac{3.001}{5} \approx 0.600
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F1_{B^3} = \frac{2 \times 0.733 \times 0.600}{0.733 + 0.600} = \frac{0.880}{1.333} \approx 0.660
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 3.66 / 5 working is the exact computation KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark derivation sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Coreference Resolution Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Tokenization and POS Tagging]
    B --> C[NP Chunking and Mention Detection]
    C --> D[Filter Non-Referential Mentions]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction Module]
    E --> F{Mention Pair Classifier}
    F -->|Yes| G[Mark as Coreferent Pair]
    F -->|No| H[Treat as Singleton]
    G --> I[Transitive Closure and Clustering]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Output Coreference Chains]
    J --> K[Evaluation via MUC, B-CUBED, CEAF]
```

## 4.2 Hobbs Algorithm Traversal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start at Pronoun Location]) --> Step1[Identify Parse Tree Node of Pronoun]
    Step1 --> Step2[Go Up to Parent Node]
    Step2 --> Step3{Is Parent an S node?}
    Step3 -->|Yes| Step4[Traverse Left Subtree Breadth-First]
    Step3 -->|No| Step5{Is Parent an NP node?}
    Step5 -->|Yes| Step6[Traverse Left Siblings of NP Breadth-First]
    Step5 -->|No| Step2
    Step4 --> Step7{Found Compatible NP?}
    Step6 --> Step7
    Step7 -->|Yes| End([Assign as Antecedent])
    Step7 -->|No| Step8[Move to Previous Sentence]
    Step8 --> Step1
```

## 4.3 Mention-Type Hierarchy and Resolution Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[All Mentions] --> B[Pronominal]
    A --> C[Nominal Definite NP]
    A --> D[Proper Noun]
    A --> E[Appositive]
    B --> B1[Hobbs Algorithm]
    B --> B2[Binding Theory Constraints]
    C --> C1[Centering Theory]
    C --> C2[World Knowledge Lookup]
    D --> D1[String Matching]
    D --> D2[Alias Dictionary]
    E --> E1[Trivial Coreference by Construction]
```

## 4.4 End-to-End Neural Coreference Architecture (eCoref / Lee et al. 2017)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Document] --> B[BERT Style Span Encoder]
    B --> C[All Possible Spans up to Length L]
    C --> D[Span Representation via Attention]
    D --> E[Mention Score Computation]
    D --> F[Pairwise Antecedent Score Matrix]
    E --> G[Prune Low Score Spans]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Softmax over Antecedents per Span]
    H --> I[Final Coreference Clusters]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagram Adaptations:** Since coreference resolution is a *linguistic* task (not a physical-mechanical one), all schematics have been rendered as **block-level functional flow diagrams** showing the data dependencies between stages of the pipeline. This is the standard depiction style used in NLP literature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *anaphora* with *coreference*. Anaphora is direction-specific (anaphor → antecedent), while coreference is symmetric (mention ↔ mention). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention **four types of mentions** (pronoun, nominal, proper noun, appositive) — partial answers lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Do NOT** compute the MUC score using the wrong formula. The denominator uses $\bigl( m_i - 1 \bigr)$ — not $m_i$.
> - **Do NOT** write "CoNLL F1 is the F1 of MUC". It is the *average of F1 scores of MUC, B³, and CEAF*.
> - **Do NOT** skip the **constraints** (gender, number, animacy, binding) when describing Hobbs algorithm.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define coreference resolution. Differentiate between anaphora and cataphora with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Coreference Resolution (1 mark):** It is the NLP task of identifying all mentions in a text that refer to the same real-world entity and clustering them together.
- **Anaphora (1 mark):** A later linguistic expression (anaphor) refers back to an earlier expression (antecedent). Example: *"Ravi bought a car. **He** loves driving it."* — "He/it" refer back to "Ravi/car".
- **Cataphora (1 mark):** A forward reference where the antecedent appears *after* the anaphor. Example: *"**Because he** was tired, Ravi went home."* — "he" refers to the later "Ravi".

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four surface types of mentions in coreference resolution. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Pronouns** (1 mark) — *"He, she, it, they"*
2. **Definite nominals** (1 mark) — *"the car, the young scientist"*
3. **Proper nouns** (1/2 mark) — *"Microsoft, Abdul Kalam"*
4. **Appositives** (1/2 mark) — *"Dr. Kalam, **the former President**, ..."*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Hobbs algorithm** for pronominal anaphora resolution. State the key constraints used during antecedent selection. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hobbs' Algorithm (1978) — Syntactic, Distance-Based, Rule-Based Approach (3 marks):**

The algorithm operates on the **parse tree** of the sentence containing the anaphoric pronoun. It works as follows:

1. **Start at the position of the pronoun** in the parse tree (Step 0).
2. **Go up the tree to the first NP or S node** encountered (Step 1).
3. **Traverse the left sub-tree** of that node in **breadth-first, left-to-right** order. For each NP encountered, treat it as a potential antecedent (Step 2).
4. If an **S node is reached**, traverse its left-subtree in breadth-first order (Step 3).
5. If an **NP node is reached**, traverse its **left siblings** in breadth-first order (Step 4).
6. If no antecedent is found in the current sentence, **move to the previous sentence** and repeat from Step 1.
7. The **first compatible candidate** is chosen.

**Constraints / Filters (2 marks):**

- **Number agreement** — "it" ↔ singular NP; "they" ↔ plural NP.
- **Gender agreement** — "he" ↔ masculine; "she" ↔ feminine.
- **Animacy compatibility** — "it" ↔ inanimate; "who" ↔ animate.
- **Binding Theory (Chomsky, 1981)** — Principle C blocks an R-expression from c-commanding a pronoun.
- **Selectional restrictions** — verbs restrict the type of their arguments.

**Advantages & Limitations (2 marks):**

- ✅ Simple, no training data needed.
- ✅ Works well for short, single-sentence cases.
- ❌ Ignores **discourse-level salience** and **world knowledge**.
- ❌ Fails on **non-pronominal** anaphora.
- ❌ Pre-supposes a **perfect parse tree** (error-prone).

---

**(b)** Discuss the **Mention-Pair model** for coreference resolution. List at least six features used by the classifier. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mention-Pair Model (4 marks):**

The Mention-Pair approach, pioneered by **Soon, Ng, and Lim (2001)**, treats coreference as a **pairwise binary classification** task.

- For every ordered pair of mentions $(m_i, m_j)$ with $i < j$, a classifier predicts: **coreferent** or **not coreferent**.
- The classifier is trained on annotated corpora (e.g., MUC, ACE, OntoNotes).
- A positive prediction creates a link; chains are formed via **transitive closure** of these links.

**Six Key Features (3 marks):**

1. **Sentence Distance** — Number of sentences between $m_i$ and $m_j$.
2. **Mention Distance** — Number of intervening mentions.
3. **String Match Features** — Exact, head-word, or lemma overlap.
4. **Gender / Number Agreement** — $m_i$ and $m_j$ must agree.
5. **Pronoun Type Indicator** — Is $m_j$ a personal, possessive, reflexive pronoun?
6. **Named-Entity Type Compatibility** — E.g., $m_i$ = PERSON, $m_j$ = ORGANIZATION is rejected.
7. **(Bonus)** Syntactic Role — Subject–Subject parallelism boosts coreference probability.
8. **(Bonus)** Appositive Feature — True if either mention is an appositive.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **MUC**, **B-CUBED**, and **CEAF** evaluation metrics for coreference. Why are all three needed? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. MUC Metric (Vilain et al., 1995) — 2 marks**

- Counts the **minimum number of link insertions/deletions** required to transform the system partition into the gold partition.
- **Recall:** $R_{MUC} = \dfrac{\sum_i (m_i - p_i)}{\sum_i (m_i - 1)}$
- **Precision:** $P_{MUC} = \dfrac{\sum_i (m_i - p'_i)}{\sum_i (m_i - 1)}$
- **Limitation:** It assigns the **best possible score to all-singletons output**, because no linking errors occur when no entity has multiple mentions. This artificially inflates systems that fail to merge.

**2. B-CUBED (Bagga & Baldwin, 1998) — 2 marks**

- Computes per-mention precision and recall, then averages.
- $P_i = \dfrac{\vert C(g_i) \cap C(p_i) \vert}{\vert C(p_i) \vert}$, where $C(\cdot)$ is the cluster containing mention $i$.
- **Improvement over MUC:** Properly penalizes **false splits** and **false merges**.
- **Limitation:** Still sensitive to cluster-size imbalance.

**3. CEAF (Constrained Entity-Alignment F-measure, Luo 2005) — 2 marks**

- Aligns system entities to gold entities with a **one-to-one constraint** using a similarity measure (e.g., $\phi_3$ or $\phi_4$).
- **Improvement:** Handles the many-to-many / many-to-one alignment ambiguity properly.

**Why all three are required (1 mark):**

- Each metric captures a **different error type** (link errors, split errors, merge errors).
- The official **CoNLL F1** is the simple average, giving a balanced view of all three.
- Reporting only one would let a system *game* the metric.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Binding Theory** principles of Chomsky. How do they help in coreference resolution of pronouns? Illustrate with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Binding Theory (Chomsky 1981) — 4 marks:**

Binding Theory specifies conditions on the **coindexation** of noun phrases. The relevant principles for coreference are:

1. **Principle A:** A reflexive (e.g., *himself, herself*) must be **bound** in its governing category.
2. **Principle B:** A pronoun (e.g., *he, she, it*) must be **free** in its governing category.
3. **Principle C:** An R-expression (a referring expression like a name) must be **free everywhere**.

*Binding:* NP A binds NP B if A c-commands B and A and B are coindexed. The *governing category* is typically the smallest clause containing both.

**How this helps coreference (2 marks):**

- **Eliminates impossible antecedents** syntactically, before any semantic processing.
- E.g., *Principle C* immediately rejects the reading where "Ravi" is coreferent with "he" inside the clause "Ravi said he was tired" in some syntactic configurations.

**Worked Example (1 mark):**

> *"Johnᵢ thinks that heᵢ is smart."*
- Here, "John" **c-commands** "he" across the embedded clause? In standard syntax, John is outside the governing category of "he", so Principle C is satisfied.
- Therefore, the coreference $i = i$ is **grammatical**.

> *"Johnᵢ said that Johnᵢ is smart."*
- The embedded "John" is c-commanded by the matrix "John" → violates Principle C → **ungrammatical** to read them as coreferent in the strict sense (in formal syntax).

**Why it's only a partial solution (extra):**

- Binding Theory does **not** address world knowledge, salience, or discourse coherence.
- It is used as a *hard filter* in modern neural resolvers, not the sole mechanism.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3**

- ✅ **Coreference Resolution** = grouping all mentions of the same entity in a text.
- ✅ **Mention types (4):** Pronoun, Definite Nominal, Proper Noun, Appositive.
- ✅ **Anaphora vs Cataphora:** Anaphora = backward reference; Cataphora = forward reference.
- ✅ **Hobbs Algorithm:** Traverse parse tree left-to-right, breadth-first; apply gender/number/animacy constraints.
- ✅ **Mention-Pair Model:** Binary classification of every pair (mᵢ, mⱼ); cluster by transitive closure.
- ✅ **Entity-Mention Model:** Greedy or global cluster-building, one mention at a time.
- ✅ **Neural SOTA:** Span-ranking models (Lee et al. 2017, Joshi et al. 2019) using BERT.
- ✅ **Three Core Metrics:** MUC, B³, CEAF — all required because each captures a different error type.
- ✅ **CoNLL F1** = simple arithmetic mean of MUC F1, B³ F1, CEAF F1.
- ✅ **Binding Theory:** Principle A (reflexive bound), B (pronoun free), C (R-expression free).
- ✅ **Pleonastic "it"** (e.g., "It is raining") is **non-referential** — must be filtered out.
- ✅ **Appositives** are trivially coreferent — both NPs go in the same cluster.
- ✅ **Singletons** are mentions with no other coreferent (e.g., "the sun" appearing once).
- ✅ **Centering Theory** governs nominal-anaphora by tracking *salient entities* across utterances.
- ✅ **Standard corpora:** MUC, ACE, OntoNotes, GAP, WSC, WinoBias — used for training and benchmarking.
- ✅ **Applications:** Question Answering, Summarization, Dialogue Systems, MT, Information Extraction.
- ✅ **Modern Pitfall:** Gender bias in resolvers — e.g., "doctor" → "he" (debiasing via WinoBias dataset).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discourse Analysis: Analysing Text Structure and Coherence, Including Anaphora and Discourse Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discourse Analysis: Analysing Text Structure and Coherence, Including Anaphora and Discourse Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Discourse Analysis — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discourse Analysis** is the systematic computational study of the organizational structure, semantic connectivity, and pragmatic function of language *beyond* the single sentence boundary. While **Syntax** studies sentence-internal rules and **Semantics** studies literal meaning, Discourse Analysis studies how sentences *interlock* to form a coherent, purposeful text.

The foundational objects of study in this module are:

1. **Text Structure (Discourse Structure)**: The hierarchical and sequential organization of clauses, sentences, and paragraphs into a single coherent text.
2. **Coherence**: The logical and semantic "stickiness" that makes a text hang together as a unified whole rather than a random list of sentences.
3. **Anaphora**: The linguistic phenomenon where a short reference (e.g., a pronoun) "points back" to an earlier entity in the discourse.
4. **Discourse Relations**: The semantic relationships (cause, contrast, elaboration, temporal sequence) that hold *between* units of text.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Reader

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a Bridge from Bricks**
> - **Sentences** = Individual bricks. Each one is perfectly well-formed on its own.
> - **Discourse** = The completed bridge. A pile of bricks is not a bridge — you need mortar, arches, and load-bearing structures.
> - **Coherence** = The mortar holding the bricks together.
> - **Anaphora** = The arrows drawn on the bridge (e.g., "this bridge" → "the Golden Gate Bridge" mentioned earlier).
> - **Discourse Relations** = The blueprint specifying *why* each brick is placed where it is (e.g., "this pillar SUPPORTS that beam", "that arch CONTRASTS with this one").

Without these "mortar" and "blueprint" mechanisms, a text becomes a disjoined heap of sentences — a *cohesive* text (grammatically linked) but **not** a *coherent* text (meaningfully linked).

### 1.3 Key Distinction: Coherence vs. Cohesion

| Property | Coherence | Cohesion |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Level** | Semantic / Pragmatic (meaning-level) | Lexical / Grammatical (surface-level) |
| **Question Answered** | *Why* does this text make sense as a whole? | *How* are sentences grammatically tied? |
| **Examples** | Logical relations, world knowledge, intention | Pronouns, conjunctions, lexical repetition |
| **Failure Mode** | A text is logically absurd or irrelevant | A text has dangling pronouns or broken syntax |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between **coherence** and **cohesion**. Memorize this table — it is a 3-mark classic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Architecture of Text Structure

Text structure can be analyzed at **three nested levels**:

- **Micro-level (Local)**: Sentence-to-sentence relations within a paragraph. Governed by anaphora and local coherence.
- **Meso-level (Paragraph)**: The thematic unity of a paragraph. A paragraph is treated as a single discourse unit representing one *proposition*.
- **Macro-level (Global)**: The overall rhetorical skeleton of the entire text. This is what Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST) and discourse parsers attempt to recover.

### 2.2 Coherence Relations — The Heart of Discourse

Coherence relations are semantic labels that explain **how** two text spans relate. The most influential taxonomies are:

1. **Mann & Thompson's Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST, 1988)**: ~30 relations including Elaboration, Contrast, Cause, Result, Background, Sequence, Condition.
2. **Hobbs' Coherence Relations**: A smaller, cognitively motivated set.
3. **Penn Discourse Treebank (PDTB) Hierarchy**: Three top-level classes:
   - **Comparison** (Contrast, Concession, Similarity)
   - **Contingency** (Cause, Condition, Purpose)
   - **Expansion** (Conjunction, Disjunction, Equivalence, Level-of-detail, Manner)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do these relations matter in NLP?**
> In computational linguistics, we cannot "understand" a long document by just reading sentences in isolation. To perform **question answering**, **summarization**, **machine translation (document-level)**, or **argumentation mining**, we MUST first parse the discourse structure. A machine that does not detect that sentence 2 *contradicts* sentence 1 will produce dangerously confident but logically invalid answers.

### 2.3 Anaphora — The Back-Pointer Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anaphora** is the phenomenon where a linguistic expression (the **anaphor**) derives its referent from context — typically by pointing *backward* to a previously mentioned entity (the **antecedent**).
> - Example: *"John bought a **new laptop**. **He** was excited."* → The pronoun "He" is the anaphor; "John" is the antecedent.
> - The reverse direction (pointing forward) is called **cataphora** (e.g., "*When **he** arrived, John smiled*").

**Categories of Anaphora in Computational Linguistics:**

- **Pronominal Anaphora**: He, she, it, they, this, that.
- **Definite Description Anaphora**: *the red car* → the car mentioned earlier.
- **One-anaphora**: *one / ones* referring to a prior noun (e.g., "I don't have a pen. Do you have *one*?").
- **Zero Anaphora** (pro-drop): Common in pro-drop languages like Hindi, Malayalam, Japanese, Italian.
- **VP-Ellipsis Anaphora**: *John can swim, and Mary can [swim] too.*

### 2.4 The Anaphora Resolution Pipeline

Anaphora resolution is broken into **three sub-tasks**:

1. **Anaphor Identification (Detection)**: Spotting all expressions that *might* be anaphoric. (Implemented via POS taggers + rule-based filters + neural classifiers).
2. **Antecedent Selection**: Choosing the correct referent from candidate antecedents in prior context.
3. **Coreference Chain Construction**: Linking *all* mentions that refer to the same entity into a single chain (e.g., "John" → "he" → "the manager" → "the man").

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Evaluation Metrics

For computational evaluation, anaphora and discourse parsers use these standard metrics. Memorize them — they appear in Part A numerical questions.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **MUC Score** | $Recall = \frac{\#\text{links in response \& key}}{\#\text{links in key}}$ | Measures how many coreference links the system found. |
| **B-CUBED F1** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ where $P = \frac{\sum \text{correct mentions}}{\sum \text{ predicted mentions}}$ | Per-mention precision & recall. |
| **CEAF (Entity-Aware)** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Treats each entity as a whole. |
| **Coherence Score (Barzilay & Lapata)** | $P(\text{entity} \mid \text{local context})$ | Probabilistic ranking of sentence ordering. |
| **Discourse Tree Parse Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{\#\text{correct relations}}{\#\text{all relations}}$ | Used in RST-style discourse parsing. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall**: When using the vertical bar in a formula like $\vert x \vert$, ALWAYS write `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, never `|`, or you will break the markdown table parser.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Translation**: Document-level translation requires anaphora resolution to maintain gender/number agreement across sentences.
- **Question Answering (QA)**: To answer "Who did what to whom?", a system must resolve pronouns across paragraphs.
- **Summarization**: Important sentences are selected based on their rhetorical role in the discourse tree (nuclei are preferred over satellites).
- **Chatbots & Dialogue Systems**: Tracking entities across turns is a coreference task. Failure → a chatbot that says "I don't know who *he* is."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Coherence Relations at a Glance

| Relation Type | Example Sentence Pair | Discourse Connective | Logical Form |
|---------------|----------------------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Elaboration** | *John fixed the bug. **He** rewrote the function.* | "in particular", "for example" | $S_1 \Rightarrow S_2 \text{ adds detail}$ |
| **Contrast** | *Mary loves coffee. **However**, John prefers tea.* | "however", "but", "yet" | $S_1 \oplus \neg S_2$ |
| **Cause** | *It rained. **Because** the roads were wet.* | "because", "since", "as" | $S_2 \rightarrow S_1$ |
| **Result** | *He studied hard. **As a result**, he passed.* | "therefore", "thus", "consequently" | $S_1 \rightarrow S_2$ |
| **Sequence** | *First, mix the flour. **Then** add water.* | "then", "next", "finally" | $S_1 \prec S_2$ |
| **Condition** | ***If** it rains, the match is cancelled.* | "if", "provided that" | $S_1 \Rightarrow S_2$ |
| **Background** | *John is a doctor. **In fact**, he saves lives daily.* | "in fact", "indeed" | $S_1 \text{ sets context for } S_2$ |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Anaphora Resolution via Centering Theory

Centering Theory (Grosz, Joshi & Weinstein, 1995) provides a deterministic algorithm to rank candidate antecedents. Here is the exhaustive derivation of the **CENTERING TRANSITION** classification.

> **Algorithm: Centering Theory for Anaphor Resolution**

**Step 1 — Identify Discourse Participants (Cb)**:
For each utterance $U_i$ in the discourse, list the *forward-looking centers* $C_f(U_i)$ — the entities evoked or mentioned in $U_i$, ranked by grammatical role.

**Step 2 — Compute the Preferred Center (Cp)**:
The **Preferred Center** $C_p(U_i)$ is the highest-ranked forward-looking center of $U_i$ (typically, the grammatical subject).

**Step 3 — Identify the Backward-Looking Center (Cb)**:
The **Backward-Looking Center** $C_b(U_i)$ is defined as the $C_p$ of the *previous* utterance that is *also* mentioned in the current utterance. Formally:

$$
C_b(U_i) = C_p(U_{i-1}) \quad \text{iff} \quad C_p(U_{i-1}) \in C_f(U_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Classify the Transition**:
Compare $C_b(U_i)$ to $C_p(U_i)$:

$$
\text{Transition}(U_i) = \begin{cases}
\text{Continue} & \text{if } C_b(U_i) = C_p(U_i) \\
\text{Retain} & \text{if } C_b(U_i) \neq C_p(U_i) \text{ but } C_b(U_i) \in C_f(U_i) \\
\text{Smooth-Shift} & \text{if } C_b(U_i) \neq C_p(U_i) \text{ and } C_b(U_i) \notin C_f(U_i) \\
\text{Rough-Shift} & \text{if } C_b(U_i) = \text{undefined}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: "Continue" means the topic hasn't changed (e.g., "John bought a car. **He** drove home."). "Retain" means the topic changed but is still mentioned. "Smooth-Shift" is a clean handoff. "Rough-Shift" is the worst — the listener is left confused.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: Pronominal Anaphora

> **Input Text:** *"Maria went to the market because **she** needed vegetables. **She** bought fresh tomatoes and brought **them** home."*

**Step A — Anaphor Detection**:
POS-tag the text. Identify pronouns with non-lexical meaning: *she, she, them*.
Result: $\text{Anaphors} = \{ \text{she}_1, \text{she}_2, \text{them} \}$

**Step B — Antecedent Candidate Filtering**:
- Filter by **Gender**: $\text{she}$ requires feminine antecedent → candidates = {Maria, market, vegetables, tomatoes}.
- Filter by **Number**: $\text{them}$ requires plural antecedent → candidates = {vegetables, tomatoes}.
- Filter by **Animacy**: $\text{she}$ requires animate → candidates = {Maria}.

**Step C — Salience Ranking (using Centering Theory)**:
Compute $C_b$ for each pronoun position. The previous $C_p$ was *Maria*. Therefore, by the Continue rule:

$$
C_b(U_2) = C_p(U_1) = \text{Maria}
$$

Since the highest-ranked $C_f$ of $U_2$ matching the pronoun "she" is "Maria", we assign the antecedent. For "them", we apply number agreement and recency:

$$
\text{Antecedent}(\text{them}) = \text{tomatoes}
$$

**Step D — Coreference Chain Construction**:

$$
\text{Chain}_1 : [\text{Maria}] \rightarrow [\text{she}_1] \rightarrow [\text{she}_2]
$$

$$
\text{Chain}_2 : [\text{tomatoes}] \rightarrow [\text{them}]
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Coherence via Entity Grid (Barzilay & Lapata, 2005)

The **Entity Grid** represents a text as a matrix where each row is a sentence, each column is a discourse entity, and each cell $G[s][e]$ records the grammatical role:

$$
G[s][e] = \begin{cases}
S & \text{if } e \text{ is the Subject of sentence } s \\
O & \text{if } e \text{ is the Object of sentence } s \\
X & \text{if } e \text{ appears in another role} \\
- & \text{if } e \text{ is absent in sentence } s
\end{cases}
$$

The local coherence score for a transition from sentence $s$ to sentence $s+1$ on entity $e$ is:

$$
P(e \mid s) = \frac{\#\{ \text{role } r \in \{S, O, X\} \text{ at } G[s+1][e] \mid G[s][e] \in \{S, O, X\}\}}{\text{total transitions}}
$$

The global coherence score is the weighted sum of all transition probabilities, ranked by grammatical role (Subject > Object > Other).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Discourse Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText[Raw Input Text] --> tok[Tokenization and POS Tagging]
    tok --> parser[Shallow Parsing and NER]
    parser --> anaphorDetect[Anaphor Detection Module]
    parser --> entityGrid[Entity Grid Construction]
    anaphorDetect --> candFilter[Antecedent Candidate Filtering]
    candFilter --> salienceRank[Salience Ranking and Centering]
    salienceRank --> corefChains[Coreference Chain Builder]
    corefChains --> discourseParser[Discourse Relation Parser]
    entityGrid --> discourseParser
    discourseParser --> rstTree[RST Discourse Tree]
    discourseParser --> coherenceScore[Coherence Score Output]
    rstTree --> downstream[NLP Downstream Tasks]
    coherenceScore --> downstream
    downstream --> qa[Question Answering]
    downstream --> summarization[Summarization]
    downstream --> mt[Document Level MT]
```

### 4.2 RST Tree Example for a Multi-Sentence Text

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Text: A and B go to the mall. They buy shoes. Then they eat lunch. Finally, they return home.]
    root --> n1[Nucleus 1: Shopping Activity]
    root --> n2[Nucleus 2: Dining Activity]
    root --> n3[Nucleus 3: Return Home]
    n1 --> s1[Satellite: Setup Clause A and B go to the mall]
    n1 --> n1a[Nucleus: They buy shoes]
    n2 --> s2[Satellite: Temporal Connector Then]
    n2 --> n2a[Nucleus: They eat lunch]
    n3 --> s3[Satellite: Temporal Connector Finally]
    n3 --> n3a[Nucleus: They return home]
    n1 --- seq1[Sequence Relation]
    n2 --- seq2[Sequence Relation]
    n3 --- seq3[Sequence Relation]
```

### 4.3 Anaphora Resolution Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Input Sentence with Anaphor] --> agree[Step 1: Agreement Filtering]
    agree --> gender{Gender Match?}
    gender -- No --> reject[Discard Candidate]
    gender -- Yes --> number{Number Match?}
    number -- No --> reject
    number -- Yes --> recency[Step 2: Recency Bias Score]
    recency --> salience[Step 3: Salience via Centering]
    salience --> role{Subject Role in Prior Sentence?}
    role -- Yes --> boost[Boost Salience Weight]
    role -- No --> neutral[Neutral Weight]
    boost --> final[Step 4: Highest Score Wins]
    neutral --> final
    final --> output[Resolved Antecedent]
```

### 4.4 Coherence Relation Hierarchy (PDTB-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Discourse Relations]
    root --> comp[Comparison]
    root --> cont[Contingency]
    root --> exp[Expansion]
    comp --> c1[Contrast]
    comp --> c2[Concession]
    cont --> c3[Cause]
    cont --> c4[Condition]
    cont --> c5[Purpose]
    exp --> c6[Conjunction]
    exp --> c7[Instantiation]
    exp --> c8[Equivalence]
    exp --> c9[Level of Detail]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define anaphora with a suitable example. Differentiate between anaphora and cataphora.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Anaphora is a discourse phenomenon in which a linguistic expression (the anaphor) refers back to a previously introduced entity (the antecedent) in the text. Example: *"Ravi bought a car. **He** drove it home."* — "He" is the anaphor; "Ravi" is the antecedent; "it" is another anaphor for "car".
**Cataphora** is the reverse: the anaphor precedes its antecedent. Example: *"**When he** arrived, John smiled."* — "he" points forward to "John".

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*List any THREE categories of coherence relations with one example each, as proposed in Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Elaboration** — *John solved the bug. **In fact**, he refactored the entire module.* (Second sentence elaborates the first.)
2. **Contrast** — *The system was fast. **However**, it was inaccurate.* (Opposing evaluation.)
3. **Cause / Result** — *It rained heavily. **Consequently**, the match was cancelled.* (Causal link.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 3 — Choose ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a discourse analysis system. With a neat block diagram, describe the major modules involved — Tokenization, Anaphor Detection, Salience Ranking, Coreference Chain Construction, and Discourse Relation Parsing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the Entity Grid model proposed by Barzilay and Lapata for measuring local coherence. Show how a coherence score is computed for a 3-sentence text. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Tokenization & POS Tagging (1 mark)**: The raw text is split into tokens, and each token receives a Part-of-Speech tag using a statistical model (e.g., HMM, CRF, or neural tagger).

2. **Shallow Parsing & NER (1 mark)**: Noun phrases are identified (NP chunks), and Named Entities are tagged to filter candidates like person names and pronouns.

3. **Anaphor Detection (1 mark)**: Pronouns, definite noun phrases, and one-anaphors are flagged. Rules: a pronoun is anaphoric if it has no antecedent in the *same* sentence.

4. **Antecedent Filtering (1 mark)**: Candidates are pruned via agreement constraints — gender, number, animacy, person. A pronoun "she" cannot resolve to "the car".

5. **Salience Ranking (1 mark)**: Surviving candidates are ranked using Centering Theory (recency + grammatical role). Subject > Object > Oblique.

6. **Coreference Chain Builder (1 mark)**: All mentions referring to the same entity are linked into a single chain, producing a one-to-one mapping between entities and mentions.

7. **Discourse Relation Parser (1 mark)**: Sentence pairs are classified into relation labels (PDTB/RST) using either rule-based connective cues or neural classifiers (e.g., transformer-based discourse parsers).

> Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the block architecture.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1** (1 mark): Construct the Entity Grid. For the text *"John bought a book. He read it. The book was thrilling."* the grid is:

$$
G = \begin{bmatrix}
 & \text{John} & \text{book} \\
S_1 & S & O \\
S_2 & S & O \\
S_3 & - & S
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2** (2 marks): Define grammatical role weights. Standard weights (from the original paper) are: $w_S = 1.0$, $w_O = 0.5$, $w_X = 0.2$.

**Step 3** (2 marks): Compute the transition probability for each sentence pair $(S_i, S_{i+1})$:

$$
P(\text{book} \mid S_1 \to S_2) = w_O = 0.5
$$

$$
P(\text{John} \mid S_1 \to S_2) = w_S = 1.0
$$

$$
P(\text{book} \mid S_2 \to S_3) = w_S = 1.0
$$

**Step 4** (2 marks): The global coherence score is the weighted average:

$$
\text{Coherence} = \frac{(1.0 + 0.5) + (1.0)}{2} = 1.25 \text{ (out of max } 1.0 \text{ per transition)}
$$

A high score indicates strong local coherence because the same entity (book, John) maintains a salient grammatical role across sentence boundaries.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define coherence. Differentiate between **coherence** and **cohesion** with three points of distinction and one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a worked example, derive the **Centering Theory transition classification** for a 3-sentence text. Show the computation of $C_b$, $C_p$, and $C_f$ at each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition (2 marks)**: Coherence is the semantic and logical unity of a text — the property that makes a sequence of sentences *meaningfully hang together* as a single communicative act. It depends on the listener's ability to infer logical relations, world knowledge, and speaker intention.

2. **Distinction 1 (1.5 marks)**: **Coherence is conceptual** (mind-internal, semantic), whereas **cohesion is grammatical** (surface-level syntactic ties). Coherence failure = illogical text; Cohesion failure = dangling pronoun.

3. **Distinction 2 (1.5 marks)**: Coherence uses **implicit signals** (world knowledge, inference); Cohesion uses **explicit signals** (pronouns, conjunctions, lexical repetition).

4. **Distinction 3 (1 mark)**: Coherence is **pragmatic / cognitive**; Cohesion is **linguistic / formal**.

5. **Example (1 mark)**:
   - Cohesive but incoherent: *"John is a fish. He ate lunch. Tuesday comes from Mars."* — Pronouns tie sentences, but meaning is absurd.
   - Coherent with low cohesion: *"John is a fish. He eats underwater. He has scales."* — No explicit pronoun in sentence 2, but logically coherent.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Text:** *"Rahul met Priya at the cafe. **She** was reading a novel. **The book** had a red cover."*

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Identify the entities in each utterance.

- $U_1$: $\text{Entities} = \{\text{Rahul}, \text{Priya}, \text{cafe}\}$
- $U_2$: $\text{Entities} = \{\text{She}, \text{novel}\}$
- $U_3$: $\text{Entities} = \{\text{The book}, \text{red cover}\}$

**Step 2 (1 mark)**: Compute the **Forward-Looking Centers** $C_f$ (ranked by grammatical role: Subject > Object > Other):

$$
C_f(U_1) = [\text{Rahul}, \text{Priya}, \text{cafe}]
$$

$$
C_f(U_2) = [\text{Priya}, \text{novel}]
$$

$$
C_f(U_3) = [\text{book}, \text{cover}]
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: Compute the **Preferred Center** $C_p$ (highest-ranked entity):

$$
C_p(U_1) = \text{Rahul}, \quad C_p(U_2) = \text{Priya}, \quad C_p(U_3) = \text{book}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks)**: Compute the **Backward-Looking Center** $C_b$:

- $C_b(U_2)$: Is $C_p(U_1) = \text{Rahul}$ present in $C_f(U_2)$? No. Is *any* $C_f$ of $U_1$ present in $C_f(U_2)$? Yes — *Priya*. So $C_b(U_2) = \text{Priya}$.
- $C_b(U_3)$: Is $C_p(U_2) = \text{Priya}$ present in $C_f(U_3)$? No. Is *any* $C_f$ of $U_2$ present in $C_f(U_3)$? No. So $C_b(U_3) = \text{undefined}$.

**Step 5 (2 marks)**: Classify transitions:

- $U_2$: $C_b = C_p = \text{Priya}$ → **Continue** (topic continues smoothly).
- $U_3$: $C_b = \text{undefined}$ → **Rough-Shift** (the topic abruptly leaves Priya and shifts to the book).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Coherence with Cohesion**: This is the #1 mark-loser. Coherence is meaning-level; cohesion is form-level. Examiners award 0 marks for vague or swapped definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting to rank $C_f$**: Centering Theory requires ranking by grammatical role. Listing entities alphabetically is wrong.
> 3. **Miscounting Antecedent Filters**: A common error is resolving "she" to "the novel". Always check **animacy** — "she" cannot be a novel.
> 4. **Missing the Continue vs. Retain distinction**: Continue requires $C_b = C_p$. Retain allows $C_b \neq C_p$ but requires $C_b \in C_f$.
> 5. **PDTB vs. RST Confusion**: PDTB relations attach to *connectives* (explicit). RST relations are *implicit* and structural. Examiners test this distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Discourse Analysis**

- ☐ **Discourse Analysis** studies text *beyond* the sentence boundary.
- ☐ **Coherence** = semantic unity; **Cohesion** = grammatical unity. Never confuse them.
- ☐ **Anaphora** = back-reference; **Cataphora** = forward-reference.
- ☐ Categories of anaphora: pronominal, definite description, one-anaphora, zero-anaphora, VP-ellipsis.
- ☐ Anaphora resolution pipeline = Detection → Filtering → Ranking → Chain Construction.
- ☐ Centering Theory uses $C_f$, $C_p$, $C_b$ to classify **Continue / Retain / Smooth-Shift / Rough-Shift**.
- ☐ **RST** relations are *structural* (Nucleus-Satellite); **PDTB** relations are *connective-based* (explicit + implicit).
- ☐ **Entity Grid** represents text as a matrix of (sentence × entity) with grammatical roles {S, O, X, -}.
- ☐ Evaluation metrics: **MUC**, **B-CUBED**, **CEAF** for coreference; **F1** for discourse parsers.
- ☐ Real-world applications: QA, Summarization, Document-level MT, Chatbots, Argumentation Mining.
- ☐ The transition from **Continue → Retain → Smooth-Shift → Rough-Shift** represents a decreasing coherence gradient.
- ☐ Pronoun resolution constraints: **Gender**, **Number**, **Person**, **Animacy**, **Recency**, **Salience** (GRAMPS mnemonic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pragmatic Enrichment: Integrating Context and World Knowledge into Computational Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pragmatic Enrichment: Integrating Context and World Knowledge into Computational Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pragmatic Enrichment** is the computational process of augmenting the literal, semantic meaning of a linguistic expression with non-linguistic information drawn from the *physical context* (situation of utterance), *discourse context* (prior utterances, shared knowledge between interlocutors), and *world knowledge* (encyclopedic facts, common-sense rules, cultural norms) so that a Natural Language Processing (NLP) system can derive the *intended* meaning rather than merely the *expressed* meaning.

Formally, given an utterance $U$ with semantic content $\llbracket U \rrbracket$ and a context $C$, pragmatic enrichment produces an enriched interpretation $I$ such that:

$$
I = f(\llbracket U \rrbracket, C, W)
$$

where:
- $\llbracket U \rrbracket$ = truth-conditional / compositional semantic representation
- $C$ = discourse + situational context model
- $W$ = world knowledge base (KB, ontology, knowledge graph)
- $f(\cdot)$ = pragmatic interpretation function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Pragmatics is the study of language use *in context*. It is the layer *above* semantics that resolves the gap between *what is said* and *what is meant*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are reading the sentence: **"The bank is closed."**

A naive computer reads "bank" and is stuck — *which* bank? financial bank? river bank? The literal meaning is incomplete. Now imagine your friend whispers: *"It's Saturday, you were robbed last month, and you live in Kochi."* Suddenly, "the bank" snaps into focus as the *SBI branch near your house*, and "closed" snaps into *closed due to a holiday*. This **automatic filling-in of missing information** is exactly what pragmatic enrichment does for a machine.

**The Iceberg Model of Meaning:**

```
                    ┌─────────────────────────────┐
                    │   WHAT IS MEANT (Pragmatics) │  ← Above the surface
                    ├─────────────────────────────┤
                    │   WHAT IS SAID (Semantics)   │  ← Water line
                    ├─────────────────────────────┤
                    │   WORDS / SYNTAX            │  ← Below the surface
                    └─────────────────────────────┘
```

Pragmatics deals with the *largest* portion — the submerged iceberg of unstated assumptions, shared knowledge, and contextual cues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction for Exams:**
> - **Semantics** → context-free, literal meaning (e.g., "He" = some male entity)
> - **Pragmatics** → context-dependent, intended meaning (e.g., "He" = *your brother*, based on prior sentence)

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold High-Yield Terms)

- **Deixis**: Words whose reference depends on the context of utterance (I, you, here, now, tomorrow).
- **Anaphora**: Referring back to a previously introduced entity (e.g., pronoun "she" referring to "Maria").
- **Presupposition**: Information the speaker assumes to be true to make the utterance meaningful (e.g., "John stopped smoking" presupposes *John used to smoke*).
- **Implicature**: Implicit meaning inferred beyond what is literally said (e.g., "Some students passed" implicates *not all students passed* — scalar implicature).
- **Speech Act**: The action performed by an utterance (assertion, question, request, promise).
- **Common-Sense Knowledge**: General everyday facts (e.g., *people eat food to survive, rain makes things wet*).
- **Knowledge Graph (KG)**: A structured representation of entities and their relations (e.g., Resource Description Framework / RDF triples).
- **Frame**: A structured script/schema representing a stereotyped situation (e.g., the "Restaurant" frame includes waiter, customer, menu, bill).

## 1.4 Why is Pragmatic Enrichment Hard for Machines?

Computational systems are *brilliant* at syntax and semantics but struggle with pragmatics because:

1. **Underspecification**: Sentences under-determine meaning. "Pick it up" requires knowing *what* "it" refers to.
2. **Implicit Information**: Up to 70% of the meaning in a conversation is *not* explicitly stated (per H. P. Grice's work on conversational maxims).
3. **Context-Dependence**: The same sentence means different things in different settings.
4. **World Knowledge Volume**: Encyclopedic + common-sense knowledge is effectively infinite.
5. **Cultural Variability**: Pragmatic norms differ across languages and cultures.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU answers:** Always start by stating the **semantics–pragmatics distinction**, then introduce the three pillars of pragmatic enrichment: **Deixis/Reference, Implicature, Presupposition**, and the world knowledge resources used (Frames, KGs, Common-Sense KBs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Three Layers of Pragmatic Enrichment

A KTU-mark-worthy answer must enumerate the **structured layers** through which a sentence is enriched:

### Layer 1 — Reference Resolution (Deixis & Anaphora)
- Identify *what* a referring expression points to.
- Sub-tasks: **Anaphora Resolution**, **Cataphora Resolution**, **Exophoric Reference**, **Demonstrative Resolution**.
- Computational approach: Centering Theory, Hobbs Algorithm, Logico-Linguistic models, End-to-End Neural Coreference (e.g., BERT-based coref).

### Layer 2 — Implicature & Presupposition Computation
- Apply **Gricean Maxims** to derive implicit meaning.
- Detect presuppositions via presupposition triggers (*again, stop, regret, even*).
- Resolve **scalar implicatures** (e.g., <all, some, none> ordered scale).

### Layer 3 — World & Common-Sense Knowledge Injection
- Inject structured facts from Knowledge Bases (ConceptNet, WordNet, Wikidata, ATOMIC).
- Use **Frame Semantics** (FrameNet) for situation-level scripts.
- Apply **Script-Based Reasoning** (Schank & Abelson) for event sequences.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (How Enrichment Works)

For an NLP pipeline to enrich an utterance, it executes the following sequence:

1. **Parse** the input into a syntactic tree (CFG / Dependency Parse).
2. **Build a Compositional Semantic Representation** (λ-calculus / AMR / UMR / FOL).
3. **Initialize Context Stack** $C_0$ with empty discourse + speaker + hearer + time + location.
4. **Resolve Referring Expressions** by binding each referring NP/pronoun to an entity in $C_t$.
5. **Detect Presuppositions** by scanning for trigger words and adding them to the *presupposition set* $P(U)$.
6. **Compute Implicatures** by applying the Gricean maxims to the logical form.
7. **Inject World Knowledge** by querying the KB for relations between entities (e.g., $KB \models \text{hasLocation(Kochi, India)}$).
8. **Perform Script/Frame Activation** to predict unstated events.
9. **Construct Enriched Logical Form** $L_{enriched}$ combining all layers.
10. **Output Final Interpretation** $I$ to the downstream task (QA, dialogue, MT, NLI).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for any 14-mark question on Pragmatic Enrichment.**

| # | Component | Purpose | Key Resource / Algorithm | Example Trigger |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Deixis Resolution | Bind I, you, here, now | Discourse model + temporal/spatial indexing | "Come here tomorrow" |
| 2 | Anaphora Resolution | Bind he/she/it/they | Centering Theory, BERT-coref | "Riya dropped *her* phone" |
| 3 | Presupposition | Extract hidden assumptions | Presupposition projection (Heim & Kratzer) | "Did John *stop* smoking?" |
| 4 | Implicature | Derive indirect meaning | Gricean Maxims, Scalar reasoning | "Some boys came" → not all |
| 5 | World Knowledge | Inject factual context | ConceptNet, WordNet, Wikidata | "He drank coffee" → morning context |
| 6 | Common-Sense KB | Add everyday facts | ATOMIC, CYC, GenericsKB | "She opened the umbrella" → it was raining |
| 7 | Frame / Script | Activate situational schema | FrameNet, PropBank | "He ordered a pizza" → delivery expected |
| 8 | Speech Act | Identify utterance function | Searle's Taxonomy, Intent Classifier | "Can you pass the salt?" = request |

## 2.4 Grice's Maxims of Conversation (Foundational)

Speaker and hearer cooperate under the **Cooperative Principle**. The hearer assumes the speaker obeys four maxims; any *flouting* generates an implicature.

| Maxim | Sub-Principle | Flouting → Implicature |
|-------|---------------|------------------------|
| Quantity | Give right amount of info | "I have *nothing* to say" → silence implicature |
| Quality | Be truthful | "He is *a fine friend*" (sarcastic) → he is not a fine friend |
| Relation | Be relevant | "A: Who won? B: The match was long." → B doesn't know / avoids answer |
| Manner | Be clear & brief | "He climbed up the upward-moving staircase" → clumsy phrasing implicates something odd |

## 2.5 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why reference resolution first?** All subsequent pragmatic layers (presuppositions, implicatures) refer to bound entities; without a resolved referent, nothing downstream makes sense.
- **Why separate presupposition from implicature?** Presuppositions survive under negation ("He didn't stop smoking" still presupposes he smoked); implicatures do not.
- **Why a knowledge graph over raw text?** Graphs offer *typed relations* (isA, hasProperty, locatedIn) that allow fast multi-hop reasoning; raw text requires costly inference.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Pragmatic Enrichment Used |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| **Virtual Assistants** (Alexa, Siri) | Speech act classification + frame activation |
| **Machine Translation** (Google Translate) | Anaphora + cultural context resolution |
| **Question Answering** (IBM Watson) | World knowledge + coreference chain |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Sarcasm detection via Quality maxim violation |
| **Dialogue Systems** (chatbots) | Common-sense + script-based prediction |
| **Legal / Clinical NLP** | Presupposition detection for missing info |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Model of Pragmatic Interpretation

We derive the **enriched interpretation** step-by-step. Let:

- $S$ = Sentence / Utterance
- $C$ = Context tuple $\langle S_p, H_p, T_p, L_p, D_t \rangle$
  - $S_p$ = Speaker, $H_p$ = Hearer, $T_p$ = Time, $L_p$ = Location, $D_t$ = Discourse state at turn $t$
- $W$ = World Knowledge Base
- $F$ = Frame/Script Library
- $I$ = Final Interpretation

### Step 1 — Semantic Composition

Compute the literal meaning $\llbracket S \rrbracket$ from syntax and lexical semantics:

$$
\llbracket S \rrbracket = \text{CompositionalSem}(S)
$$

For example, "She opened it":

$$
\llbracket \text{She opened it} \rrbracket = \lambda e.\, \text{Open}(e) \wedge \text{Agent}(e, x_{she}) \wedge \text{Theme}(e, x_{it})
$$

where $x_{she}$ and $x_{it}$ are unbound variables.

### Step 2 — Context Binding (Reference Resolution)

Apply the context $C$ to bind free variables to discourse entities:

$$
C(x_{she}) = e_1 \in D_t, \quad C(x_{it}) = e_2 \in D_t
$$

where $e_1$ is the most salient female entity and $e_2$ the most salient inanimate object in prior discourse.

### Step 3 — Presupposition Extraction

Scan $S$ for presupposition triggers $\text{Trig}(S)$:

$$
P(S) = \bigcup_{t \in \text{Trig}(S)} \text{Presup}(t)
$$

For $S$ = "She opened it *again*":

$$
P(S) = \{\text{She opened it before}\}
$$

### Step 4 — Implicature Generation

Apply the **Cooperative Principle + Quantity Maxim** to compute scalar implicature:

$$
\text{Impl}(S) = \llbracket S \rrbracket \wedge \neg(\text{stronger alternatives of } S)
$$

For "She opened *some* of the boxes":

$$
\text{Impl}(S) = \text{She opened some but not all boxes}
$$

### Step 5 — World Knowledge Query

Query $W$ for relations among bound entities:

$$
W \models \text{Open}(e) \rightarrow \text{HasContainer}(x_{it}) \wedge \text{Accessible}(x_{it}, e_1)
$$

This infers that $e_1$ had permission / ability to open $e_2$.

### Step 6 — Frame / Script Activation

Activate the most relevant frame $F^* \in F$:

$$
F^* = \arg\max_{F \in \mathcal{F}} P(F \mid \llbracket S \rrbracket, C)
$$

For "She opened it", $F^* = \text{Opening\_Object}$ with slots {Agent, Theme, Instrument, Location}.

### Step 7 — Combine Layers into Enriched Form

$$
I = \llbracket S \rrbracket \otimes P(S) \otimes \text{Impl}(S) \otimes W\text{-facts} \otimes F^*
$$

where $\otimes$ is a structured merge operator (e.g., graph union, λ-conjunction).

### Final Exhaustive Expression

$$
I = f(\llbracket S \rrbracket,\; C(S_p, H_p, T_p, L_p, D_t),\; W,\; F) = \text{Enriched Meaning}
$$

## 3.2 Numerical / Logical Worked Example

**Utterance:** *"She didn't open the door."*

**Step 1 — Semantics:**

$$
\llbracket S \rrbracket = \neg \exists e\,(\text{Open}(e) \wedge \text{Agent}(e, \text{she}) \wedge \text{Theme}(e, \text{door}))
$$

**Step 2 — Reference Binding (from prior discourse):**

$$
C(\text{she}) = \text{Mary}, \quad C(\text{door}) = \text{front door of the house}
$$

**Step 3 — Presupposition Extraction (trigger: negation does not block presupposition, but "open" itself is not a presupposition trigger; assume additional trigger "*anyway*":**

Suppose $S$ = "She didn't open the door *after all*":

$$
P(S) = \{\text{She was expected to open the door}\}
$$

**Step 4 — Implicature:**

No scalar trigger; check Manner maxim: implicature is that there is a *reason* she didn't (locked, busy, etc.).

**Step 5 — World Knowledge:**

$$
W \models \text{Door}(x) \rightarrow \text{OpenableByHand}(x) \vee \text{RequiresKey}(x)
$$

Inferred: maybe she lacked the key or the door was locked.

**Step 6 — Frame Activation:** $F^* = \text{Entering\_Building}$ (door + agent + intent).

**Step 7 — Enriched Interpretation:**

$$
I = \text{“Mary did not open the front door (contrary to expectation), and given world knowledge, the door was likely locked or she lacked the key, blocking her entry into the house.”}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pragmatic Phenomena vs. Computational Treatment

| Phenomenon | Linguistic Definition | Computational Detector | Knowledge Source |
|------------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Anaphora | Pronoun refers to prior NP | Coref resolver (Neural / Rule-based) | Discourse buffer |
| Cataphora | Pronoun refers to *later* NP | Forward-looking search | Sentence buffer |
| Exophora | Pronoun refers to *outside* text | Deictic resolver | $C$ (situation) |
| Presupposition | Backgrounded assumption | Trigger lexicon (Karttunen) | Semantic lexicon |
| Scalar Implicature | Negation of stronger term | Scalar ordering (Horn scales) | Lexicon + logic |
| Speech Act | Intent behind utterance | Intent classifier (SVM / NN) | Dialog act corpus |
| Sarcasm | Quality maxim violation | Sentiment + context + punctuation | Sentiment + KB |
| Metaphor | Cross-domain mapping | Word Sense Disambiguation | ConceptNet / embeddings |

## 3.4 Why a *Graph* Knowledge Base? — Formal Justification

A knowledge graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = N$ entities and $|E| = M$ edges allows reasoning in:

$$
O(d \cdot \log N)
$$

time using graph traversal (where $d$ = path depth), versus $O(N^2)$ for pairwise text lookup. This is why modern systems (e.g., Google's Knowledge Vault, IBM Watson) use KG-backed pragmatics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
