| **E-Commerce** | H&M, Sephora chatbots | Drives **conversion uplift of ~25%** |
| **Industry 4.0** | Voice-controlled manufacturing robots | Hands-free assembly-line operation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern dialogue systems are *always* integrated with at least one external knowledge base (KB) or API. A dialogue system that cannot ground itself in real data is essentially *fantasizing* — a phenomenon KTU examiners may refer to as **"hallucination"** in the LLM era.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix of Pipeline Modules

| Stage | Input | Output | Technique | Common Failure Mode |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ASR** | Audio waveform | Word sequence | Acoustic model + Language model | Background noise; OOV words |
| **NLU** | Word sequence | Semantic frame | Sequence labelling (BiLSTM/BERT) | Ambiguity; slang; code-switching |
| **DST** | Frame + history | Belief state $b_t$ | RNN/Transformer encoder | State drift across long dialogues |
| **DM (Policy)** | Belief state $b_t$ | System action $a_t$ | RL, rule engine, or LLM planner | Generic/unsafe actions |
| **NLG** | System action $a_t$ | Surface sentence | Seq2Seq / T5 / GPT | Hallucination; ungrammatical output |
| **TTS** | Surface sentence | Speech waveform | Tacotron 2, WaveNet | Robotic prosody |

## 3.2 Derivation: Computing the Optimal Discounted Return

The expected return of policy $\pi$ starting from state $s$ is the **state-value function**:

$$ V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Bigg[\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^{t} R(s_t, a_t) \,\Bigg\vert\, s_0 = s \Bigg] $$

We can split the expectation into the *immediate* reward plus the *future* reward:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Big[R(s_0, a_0) + \gamma \sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma^{t-1} R(s_t, a_t) \,\Big\vert\, s_0 = s\Big] \\
&= \sum_{a \in A} \pi(a \mid s)\;\Big[R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a)\;V^{\pi}(s')\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation** for dialogue policy evaluation. The *optimal* value function satisfies:

$$ V^*(s) = \max_{a \in A}\;\Big[R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a)\;V^*(s')\Big] $$

The greedy policy derived from $V^*$ is:

$$ \pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a \in A}\;\Big[R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a)\;V^*(s')\Big] $$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — BLEU Score Computation

**Problem (KTU-style, Dec 2023 pattern):**

> Candidate translation $c$ = `"the the the cat sat on the mat"` (length $c = 8$)
> Reference translation $r$ = `"the cat is on the mat"` (length $r = 7$)

Compute the **BLEU-1** score (using $N = 1$).

### Step 1: Compute modified unigram precision $p_1$

Raw unigram counts in candidate:
- `the`: 4 occurrences (but clipped to max reference count = 2)
- `cat`: 1, `sat`: 1, `on`: 1, `mat`: 1

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \frac{\text{clipped count}}{\text{total candidate words}} \\
&= \frac{2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute brevity penalty (BP)

Since $c = 8 > r = 7$:

$$ \text{BP} = 1 $$

### Step 3: Compute BLEU-1

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BLEU-1} &= \text{BP} \cdot \exp\big(w_1 \log p_1\big) \\
&= 1 \cdot \exp\big(\log 0.75\big) \\
&= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

A BLEU-1 score of **0.75** indicates that the candidate captures the unigram content reasonably well, but the redundancy of `the` (penalized by clipped counts) drags the score down — a classic KTU teaching point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the *clipping* step (max reference count) when computing BLEU. KTU examiners specifically test this. Always clip each n-gram count to the **maximum count of that n-gram in any single reference**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Reward Accumulation in MDP

**Problem:**

A dialogue policy receives the following sequence of rewards across a 4-turn conversation with $\gamma = 0.9$:

$$ R(s_0, a_0) = 1,\quad R(s_1, a_1) = 2,\quad R(s_2, a_2) = 3,\quad R(s_3, a_3) = 4 $$

Compute the cumulative discounted return $G_0$.

### Step-by-Step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_0 &= R(s_0, a_0) + \gamma R(s_1, a_1) + \gamma^{2} R(s_2, a_2) + \gamma^{3} R(s_3, a_3) \\
&= 1 + (0.9)(2) + (0.9)^{2}(3) + (0.9)^{3}(4) \\
&= 1 + 1.8 + 2.43 + 2.916 \\
&= 8.146
\end{aligned}
$$

The discounted return is **$G_0 = 8.146$**. Note that without discounting ($\gamma = 1$), the return would be $10$, confirming the design intuition that future rewards are weighted less to encourage faster task completion.

## 3.5 Slot Filling as Sequence Labelling (BIO Tagging)

Given the utterance:

> `"Book a flight from Delhi to Mumbai on 15th August"`

The corresponding **BIO-tagged slot sequence** is:

| Tokens | Book | a | flight | from | Delhi | to | Mumbai | on | 15th | August |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **BIO Tags** | O | O | O | O | B-from\_loc | O | B-to\_loc | O | B-date | I-date |
| **Intent** | | | | | | | `BookFlight` | | | |

Where:
- `B-from_loc` = *Begin* of `from_loc` slot
- `B-to_loc` = *Begin* of `to_loc` slot
- `B-date / I-date` = *Begin* / *Inside* of `date` slot
- `O` = Outside any slot

This tagging is the canonical input format for the **slot-filling head** of a joint NLU model.

## 3.6 Evaluation of Dialogue Systems — Analytical Comparison

| Metric | What it Measures | Task-Oriented | Open-Domain (Chitchat) | Range |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Task Success Rate** | Goal achievement | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | $[0,1]$ higher = better |
| **Slot F1** | Slot-filling accuracy | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | $[0,1]$ higher = better |
| **BLEU / METEOR** | Lexical overlap w/ reference | $\checkmark$ (sometimes) | $\checkmark$ | $[0,1]$ higher = better |
| **Perplexity** | LM fluency | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $(0, \infty)$ lower = better |
| **User Satisfaction (USS)** | Subjective rating | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $[1,5]$ higher = better |
| **Coherence (BERTScore)** | Semantic similarity | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $[0,1]$ higher = better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theoretical Note:** No single metric fully captures dialogue quality. This is known as the **"Beyond BLEU"** problem and is an active research area. Always pair an automatic metric with **human evaluation** in any dialogue system write-up.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Canonical Dialogue System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user1([User Utterance])
    asr1[ASR Module]
    nlu1[NLU Module]
    dst1[DST Module]
    dm1[DM Policy Module]
    nlg1[NLG Module]
    tts1[TTS Module]
    user2([System Response])
    kb1[(External Knowledge Base)]
    api1[(Backend API)]

    user1 -->|speech| asr1
    asr1 -->|text| nlu1
    nlu1 -->|semantic frame| dst1
    dst1 -->|belief state| dm1
    dm1 -->|system action| nlg1
    nlg1 -->|surface text| tts1
    tts1 -->|speech| user2

    nlu1 -. intent and slots .-> dm1
    dm1 -. query .- kb1
    dm1 -. call .- api1
    api1 -. response .- dm1
    kb1 -. facts .- dm1
```

## 4.2 Three Paradigms of Dialogue Management — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([User Input]) --> finiteQ{Finite-State DM}
    finiteQ -->|follows pre-defined script| finiteOut[Linear Path A to B to C]
    finiteOut --> endA([System Response])

    startB([User Input]) --> frameQ{Frame-Based DM}
    frameQ -->|identifies frame| slotCheck{All Slots Filled?}
    slotCheck -->|No| askSlot[Ask Missing Slot]
    askSlot --> slotCheck
    slotCheck -->|Yes| executeTask[Execute Backend Task]
    executeTask --> endB([System Response])

    startC([User Input]) --> planQ{Plan-Based DM}
    planQ -->|infers user plan| decompose[Decompose into Sub-Goals]
    decompose --> reason1[Belief Update]
    reason1 --> reason2[Desire Generation]
    reason2 --> reason3[Intention Selection]
    reason3 --> endC([System Response])
```

## 4.3 Joint NLU Architecture — Intent + Slot Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inText([User Utterance Tokens])
    embed[Embedding Layer]
    enc[BERT / BiLSTM Shared Encoder]
    intentHead[Intent Classification Head]
    slotHead[Slot Tagging Head BIO]
    intentOut([Intent Label])
    slotOut([Slot Tag Sequence])

    inText --> embed
    embed --> enc
    enc --> intentHead
    enc --> slotHead
    intentHead --> intentOut
    slotHead --> slotOut
```

## 4.4 Reinforcement Learning Dialogue Policy Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envState([Belief State bt])
    agentPolicy[Policy Network pi]
    action([System Action at])
    userEnv((User Simulator))
    rewardSignal([Reward rt])
    nextState([Next Belief bt+1])

    envState --> agentPolicy
    agentPolicy --> action
    action --> userEnv
    userEnv -->|user reply| nextState
    userEnv --> rewardSignal
    nextState --> agentPolicy
    rewardSignal --> agentPolicy
```

## 4.5 Hierarchy of Chatbot Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Dialogue System Taxonomy]
    rootA --> type1[Task Oriented]
    rootA --> type2[Open Domain Chitchat]
    rootA --> type3[Hybrid Chatbot]

    type1 --> implA1[Rule Based]
    type1 --> implA2[Statistical ML]
    type1 --> implA3[Neural End to End]

    type2 --> implB1[Retrieval Based]
    type2 --> implB2[Generative Neural]

    type3 --> implC1[FAQs and Skill Routing]
    implC1 --> routeQ{Confidence Above Threshold?}
    routeQ -->|Yes| skillMatch[Invoke Task Bot]
    routeQ -->|No| fallbackChit[Open Domain Chitchat]
    skillMatch --> skillOut([Task Response])
    fallbackChit --> skillOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every arrow direction encodes causality — `A --> B` means "A's output becomes B's input". Dashed arrows (`-.->`) in the architecture diagram represent *non-pipeline side-channels* (e.g., the DM querying the KB). This convention is the same one used in the official KTU module-end reference materials.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a dialogue system. Differentiate between a chatbot and a dialogue system with one example each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Dialogue System** is a computer-based system designed to engage in natural-language conversations with humans by interpreting user utterances, reasoning over dialogue context, and producing contextually appropriate responses, typically composed of NLU, DST, DM, and NLG modules.

A **Chatbot** is a conversational agent that simulates human conversation, usually over short textual interactions, and may be purely retrieval-based or pattern-matching without explicit state tracking.

**Differentiation:**

| Feature | Chatbot | Dialogue System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | Narrow, often single-turn | Broad, multi-turn state-aware |
| Components | Mostly NLG + retrieval | Full NLU + DST + DM + NLG |
| Example | A weather-query chatbot on a webpage | Amazon Alexa / Apple Siri |
| State Tracking | Absent or minimal | Mandatory (belief state) |

*(Word count ~110 — adequate for a 3-mark question.)*

---

### **Q2. List and briefly explain the four primary modules of a task-oriented dialogue system.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Natural Language Understanding (NLU):** Converts raw user utterance into a structured semantic frame comprising *domain*, *intent*, and *slots* (e.g., "book flight to Mumbai" → intent=`BookFlight`, slot=`to=Mumbai`).
2. **Dialogue State Tracker (DST):** Maintains a probabilistic *belief state* summarizing all information gathered so far across the dialogue turns.
3. **Dialogue Manager (DM):** Acts as the decision-making brain; takes the belief state as input and decides the next system action (which slot to ask, which API to query, or which answer to give).
4. **Natural Language Generator (NLG):** Converts the chosen system action into a fluent, grammatical natural-language sentence presented to the user.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three classical paradigms** of dialogue management — finite-state, frame-based, and plan-based. Compare them with a suitable diagram and discuss their relative merits and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **NLU module** of a task-oriented dialogue system in detail. Explain how **intent classification** and **slot filling** are performed jointly using modern neural architectures such as BERT. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 📝 Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Three Paradigms of Dialogue Management

**1. Finite-State (Script-Based) Dialogue Management:**

The dialogue is modelled as a **Directed Graph** where each *node* represents a system state (e.g., greeting, ask-destination, ask-date) and each *edge* represents a possible user input. The system follows a *pre-defined sequence* of states.

- ✅ **Merits:** Predictable, easy to engineer, deterministic, debug-friendly.
- ❌ **Limitations:** Cannot handle out-of-order user inputs; brittle to user improvisation; scalability is poor.

**2. Frame-Based Dialogue Management:**

The system maintains a **frame** (a structured collection of slots such as `origin`, `destination`, `date`, `time`). The system asks questions to fill empty slots in any order the user chooses.

- ✅ **Merits:** Flexible to user-driven slot ordering; easier to extend with new slots.
- ❌ **Limitations:** Cannot represent complex multi-step user plans; struggles with conditional intents.

**3. Plan-Based (Agent-Based) Dialogue Management:**

The system treats the user's request as a **plan** (a sequence of *actions*) that needs to be decomposed into sub-goals. Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) reasoning is used, and the system can dynamically adapt.

- ✅ **Merits:** Handles complex, multi-domain dialogues; supports reasoning and context-sensitive decisions.
- ❌ **Limitations:** Computationally heavy; requires rich world-knowledge modelling.

| Property | Finite-State | Frame-Based | Plan-Based |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| User-initiative | System-driven | Mixed | User-driven |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Flexibility | Low | Medium | High |
| Best for | IVR menus | Booking systems | Multi-domain agents |

**Diagram Reference:** Refer to **Section 4.2** for a comparative flow chart of the three paradigms.

---

#### Part (b) — NLU and Joint Intent Classification + Slot Filling with BERT

The NLU module takes raw text and outputs a **semantic frame** containing:

- **Domain** (e.g., `flight`, `hotel`)
- **Intent** (e.g., `BookFlight`)
- **Slots** (e.g., `from=Delhi`, `to=Mumbai`)

**Traditional (Pre-BERT) Approach:**

1. *Intent classification*: trained as a sentence-classification problem using BoW, TF-IDF, or CNN/LSTM encoders.
2. *Slot filling*: treated as a sequence-labeling task using BiLSTM + CRF with BIO tags.

**Modern Joint BERT Approach:**

```
Utterance -> BERT Token Embeddings -> Shared Encoder -> { Intent Classifier Head, Slot Tagging Head }
```

- The **shared encoder** is a 12-layer Transformer (BERT-base) that produces contextualized embeddings for every token.
- The **intent head** aggregates the `[CLS]` representation through a feed-forward layer to predict the intent class.
- The **slot head** classifies *each* token's BIO tag independently.

**Advantages of Joint BERT:**
1. **Shared representations** reduce parameter count.
2. **Contextual embeddings** handle polysemy better.
3. **Transfer learning** from large pre-trained corpora boosts low-resource performance.

**Worked Example:**

For `"Book a flight from Delhi to Mumbai on 15th"`:

| Tokens | Book | a | flight | from | Delhi | to | Mumbai | on | 15th |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Slot (BIO)** | O | O | O | O | B-from\_loc | O | B-to\_loc | O | B-date |

**Predicted intent:** `BookFlight`

The model jointly minimizes:

$$ \mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{intent}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{slot}} $$

where $\lambda$ balances the two losses (typically $\lambda = 1$).

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain how a dialogue system can be formulated as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**. Derive the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the dialogue policy and interpret its components in the dialogue context. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **evaluation metrics** used for dialogue systems. With suitable formulas, compute the **BLEU-2 score** for the following candidate-reference pair, given $N = 2$ and $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$:

- Candidate: `"a cat on mat"`
- Reference: `"the cat is on the mat"` **(7 Marks)**

---

### 📝 Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Dialogue as MDP and Bellman Optimality

A dialogue system operating under uncertainty (noisy ASR, ambiguous user input) is naturally modelled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**:

$$ \mathcal{M} = \langle S, A, P, R, \gamma \rangle $$

- $S$ — set of **belief states** summarising the dialogue so far.
- $A$ — set of **system actions** (e.g., `AskDate`, `ConfirmBooking`, `Greet`).
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — **transition probability** (probability that the user responds in a way that produces belief state $s'$ given the system just took action $a$ in state $s$).
- $R(s, a)$ — **reward** (e.g., $+10$ for successful booking, $-1$ for each turn, $-5$ for dialogue failure).
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$ — **discount factor** weighting future vs. immediate rewards.

**Policy:** A function $\pi: S \to A$ mapping each belief state to an action.

**Cumulative discounted return:**

$$ G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t} \gamma^{k} R(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) $$

**Derivation of Bellman Optimality:**

Starting from the state-value function:

$$ V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Big[\sum_{k=0}^{T} \gamma^{k} R(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) \mid s_t = s\Big] $$

Splitting the sum into immediate and future parts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Big[R(s_t, a_t) + \gamma \sum_{k=1}^{T} \gamma^{k-1} R(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k})\Big] \\
&= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)\Big[R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\;V^{\pi}(s')\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **optimal** value $V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$, the policy is greedy:

$$ V^*(s) = \max_{a \in A}\Big[R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\;V^*(s')\Big] $$

**Dialogue Interpretation:**
- $R(s,a)$ encodes *task success* (+ve) vs *failure* (-ve).
- $\gamma$ encourages the system to complete tasks in fewer turns.
- $\pi^*$ is the policy that maximizes long-term user satisfaction.

---

#### Part (b) — Evaluation Metrics and BLEU-2 Computation

**Overview of Metrics:**

| Metric | Type | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Task Success Rate** | Automatic | Task-oriented evaluation |
| **Slot F1** | Automatic | Slot-filling accuracy |
| **BLEU** | Automatic | Surface similarity |
| **METEOR** | Automatic | Synonym-aware similarity |
| **Perplexity** | Automatic | LM fluency |
| **User Satisfaction Score** | Human | End-user rating |

**BLEU-2 Computation:**

Given:
- Candidate $c$ = `"a cat on mat"` (length $c = 4$)
- Reference $r$ = `"the cat is on the mat"` (length $r = 6$)

**Step 1 — Unigram precision $p_1$:**

Unigrams in candidate: `{a, cat, on, mat}`. Max reference counts: `cat=1`, `on=1`, `mat=1`, `a=0` (no `a` standalone in reference), `the=1`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \frac{\min(1,1) + \min(1,1) + 0 + \min(1,1)}{4} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Bigram precision $p_2$:**

Bigrams in candidate: `{(a,cat), (cat,on), (on,mat)}`. Reference bigrams include `(cat,on)` and `(on,the), (the,mat)`.

- `(a,cat)` → 0 matches
- `(cat,on)` → 1 match (clipped to ref count 1)
- `(on,mat)` → 0 matches

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_2 &= \frac{0 + 1 + 0}{3} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Brevity Penalty:**

Since $c = 4 < r = 6$:

$$
\text{BP} = e^{1 - r/c} = e^{1 - 6/4} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
$$

**Step 4 — BLEU-2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BLEU-2} &= \text{BP} \cdot \exp\Big(w_1 \log p_1 + w_2 \log p_2\Big) \\
&= 0.6065 \cdot \exp\Big(0.5 \cdot \log 0.75 + 0.5 \cdot \log 0.3333\Big) \\
&= 0.6065 \cdot \exp\big(0.5 \cdot (-0.2877) + 0.5 \cdot (-1.0986)\big) \\
&= 0.6065 \cdot \exp(-0.6932) \\
&= 0.6065 \cdot 0.5 \\
&\approx 0.3033
\end{aligned}
$$

**BLEU-2 $\approx 0.30$** — indicating moderate similarity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clip n-gram counts** in BLEU computation (a guaranteed 2-mark deduction).
> 2. **Confusing** dialogue *state* with dialogue *act* — they are *not* the same. A *state* is a snapshot; an *act* is a communicative function.
> 3. **Writing `|x|` (bare pipe)** inside markdown tables — this breaks the table parser and may cost presentation marks.
> 4. **Stating "BLEU measures meaning"** — it does NOT. BLEU only captures lexical n-gram overlap. Mention this distinction explicitly.
> 5. **Omitting the brevity penalty** in BLEU derivation — examiners specifically test for it.
> 6. **Mixing up rule-based and retrieval-based chatbots** in answers — both are non-generative but differ in source of responses.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 A **chatbot** is typically a short, possibly retrieval-driven text interface; a **dialogue system** is a broader, state-aware, multi-turn conversational agent.
- 🔹 The canonical pipeline is **ASR → NLU → DST → DM → NLG → TTS** (text-only systems drop ASR & TTS).
- 🔹 **NLU** = Domain + Intent + Slot (BIO-tagged).
- 🔹 **DST** maintains the *belief state* $b_t$ — the system's running summary.
- 🔹 **DM** = Dialogue Policy + Backend Connector.
- 🔹 The **three paradigms** of DM are Finite-State, Frame-Based, Plan-Based — increasing in flexibility and complexity.
- 🔹 A dialogue can be **formulated as an MDP** $\langle S, A, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ and solved via **Bellman Optimality** or **Reinforcement Learning**.
- 🔹 **BLEU** uses clipped n-gram precision × brevity penalty; it measures lexical overlap, *not* meaning.
- 🔹 **Perplexity** = fluency metric; lower is better.
- 🔹 **Task Success Rate** and **Slot F1** are the standard metrics for task-oriented systems.
- 🔹 **Open-domain** systems need chitchat-fluency metrics (Perplexity, BERTScore, human USS).
- 🔹 **Joint BERT** is the modern standard for joint intent classification + slot filling.
- 🔹 Always pair an **automatic metric** with **human evaluation** — no single metric is sufficient.
- 🔹 **ELIZA (1966)** and **PARRY (1972)** are *de rigueur* historical names for any KTU question on chatbot evolution.
- 🔹 Be ready to write the **BIO tagging** of any sample slot-filling sentence in the exam.
- 🔹 The discount factor $\gamma \in [0.9, 0.99]$ in dialogue RL penalises unnecessarily long conversations.
- 🔹 End your answer with the **Bellman equation** whenever a question involves RL in dialogue — it earns a guaranteed 2 marks.

> 📌 **One-liner to revise:** *A dialogue system is an MDP-driven NLU-NLG pipeline whose optimal policy maximizes the discounted sum of task-completion rewards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Extraction and Summarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Text Extraction and Summarization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Text Extraction — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Text Extraction (Information Extraction, IE)** is the automatic process of deriving **structured, machine-readable information** (entities, relationships, events, attributes) from **unstructured or semi-structured natural language text**.

It is formally defined as a mapping function:

$$f_{\text{IE}} : \mathcal{T}_{\text{unstructured}} \longrightarrow \mathcal{S}_{\text{structured}}$$

where the input $\mathcal{T}$ is raw text (documents, articles, web pages) and the output $\mathcal{S}$ is a relational structure — typically tuples, knowledge graphs, or annotated spans.

The three principal sub-tasks in the KTU syllabus are:

| Sub-Task | Output Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Entity spans + types | "Kerala" → `LOCATION` |
| **Relation Extraction (RE)** | Entity-pair + relation label | ("Modi", "Delhi") → `born_in` |
| **Event Extraction (EE)** | Trigger + arguments + event type | "earthquake struck Nepal" → `DISASTER_EVENT` |

### 1.2 Text Summarization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Text Summarization** is the task of producing a **condensed version** of one or more source documents that preserves the **salient informational content** of the originals.

Formally, given a source document $D = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$ of $n$ sentences, a summarizer produces an output $S = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_m\}$ with $m \ll n$, such that:

$$\text{Relevance}(S, D) \;\geq\; \theta_{\text{min}} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Informativeness}(S) \;\text{is maximized}$$

There are two dominant paradigms:

- **Extractive Summarization** — selects verbatim sentences/phrases from $D$.
- **Abstractive Summarization** — generates novel sentences using Natural Language Generation (NLG).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — The Newspaper Editor:**
> - **Text Extraction** is like a **detective** going through piles of crime reports and pulling out only the names, dates, weapons, and locations into a clean spreadsheet.
> - **Text Summarization** is like a **newspaper sub-editor** who reads ten long court transcripts and writes a single half-column headline story.
> - The detective **selects specific slots** (extraction); the sub-editor **rephrases and condenses** (summarization).

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold — Board Favorites)

- **Precision** $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$
- **Recall** $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$
- **F1-Score** $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$
- **ROUGE-N** (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation)
- **BLEU** (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)
- **METEOR** (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Information Extraction Pipeline

The IE pipeline operates in **four sequential stages**:

1. **Sentence Segmentation** — split document into sentence units using delimiters or statistical models.
2. **Tokenization & POS Tagging** — break sentences into tokens; assign grammatical roles.
3. **Named Entity Recognition** — classify token spans into categories (PERSON, ORG, GPE, DATE, MONEY).
4. **Relation & Event Extraction** — connect entities with semantic predicates (e.g., `works_for`, `acquired_by`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this sequence?** Each stage **builds higher abstractions** on the previous stage's output. Skipping any stage compromises downstream accuracy because RE/EE depend on correctly identified entity boundaries.

### 2.2 Extractive Summarization — The "Why and How"

Extractive methods rank sentences by **salience**, then pick the top-$k$.

**Salience scoring approaches (KTU favorites):**

- **TextRank (Graph-based):** Builds a graph where nodes are sentences and edge weights are sentence similarity. Iteratively propagates PageRank-like scores.
- **LexRank:** Similar to TextRank but uses **TF-IDF cosine similarity** as edge weight.
- **Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA):** Applies **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** on the term-sentence matrix to find latent topics.
- **Centroid-based:** Ranks sentences by similarity to the **centroid** (mean TF-IDF vector) of the document.

**Why it works:** Sentences that frequently share vocabulary with many others are likely **topically central** — hence important.

### 2.3 Abstractive Summarization — The "Why and How"

Abstractive methods **generate** new text rather than copying. Key architectures:

- **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq):** Encoder–decoder with attention. Input: source tokens; output: summary tokens.
- **Pointer-Generator Network:** Hybrid that can **copy** from source OR **generate** new words.
- **Transformer-based Models:** BART, T5, PEGASUS — pretrained encoder-decoders fine-tuned on summarization corpora (CNN/DailyMail, XSum).

**Why it's harder:** Must handle **paraphrasing, fusion, and factual consistency** — risks producing *hallucinated* facts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Board Exam Lifesaver**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | TP = True Positives, FP = False Positives |
| **Recall** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | FN = False Negatives |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| **ROUGE-N Precision** | $P_{\text{ROUGE-N}} = \frac{\text{count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{n-gram})}{\text{count}(\text{n-gram in reference})}$ | N-gram overlap against reference |
| **ROUGE-N Recall** | $R_{\text{ROUGE-N}} = \frac{\text{count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{n-gram})}{\text{count}(\text{n-gram in candidate})}$ | N-gram overlap against candidate |
| **ROUGE-L (LCS)** | $F_{\text{LCS}} = \frac{(1+\beta^2) R_{\text{lcs}} P_{\text{lcs}}}{R_{\text{lcs}} + \beta^2 P_{\text{lcs}}}$ | $\beta$ typically $= 1$ |
| **BLEU** | $\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $BP$ = brevity penalty, $w_n = 1/N$ |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(u,v) = \frac{u \cdot v}{\Vert u \Vert \, \Vert v \Vert}$ | TF-IDF vector inner product |
| **TextRank Score** | $WS(V_i) = (1-d) + d \cdot \sum_{V_j \in \text{In}(V_i)} \frac{w_{ji}}{\sum_{V_k \in \text{Out}(V_j)} w_{jk}} WS(V_j)$ | $d$ = damping factor (default 0.85) |
| **SVD Reconstruction** | $A \approx U_k \Sigma_k V_k^T$ | $k$ = rank of approximation |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Mining Electronic Health Records (EHR) for adverse drug events.
- **Legal Tech:** Auto-extracting clauses (parties, dates, obligations) from contracts.
- **News Aggregation:** Google News, Inshorts — daily multi-document summarization.
- **Search Engines:** Snippet generation is essentially query-focused summarization.
- **Finance:** Real-time event extraction from earnings reports for trading signals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — ROUGE-1 Calculation (Board-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Evaluate a system summary against a reference summary using ROUGE-1 (unigram overlap).

**Reference Summary (R):**
> "the cat sat on the mat"

**System Summary (S):**
> "the dog sat on the rug"

**Step 1 — Tokenize both summaries.**

$$R = \{\text{the, cat, sat, on, the, mat}\}$$
$$S = \{\text{the, dog, sat, on, the, rug}\}$$

**Step 2 — Count overlapping unigrams (clipped by max count in reference).**

| Unigram | Count in $R$ | Count in $S$ | Min (clipped overlap) |
|---|---|---|---|
| the | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| cat | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| sat | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| on | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| mat | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| dog | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| rug | 0 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 3 — Sum the clipped overlaps.**

$$\text{count}_{\text{clip}} = 2 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROUGE-1 Recall (overlap / count in candidate $S$).**

$$R_{\text{ROUGE-1}} = \frac{4}{|S|} = \frac{4}{6} = 0.667$$

**Step 5 — Compute ROUGE-1 Precision (overlap / count in reference $R$).**

$$P_{\text{ROUGE-1}} = \frac{4}{|R|} = \frac{4}{6} = 0.667$$

**Step 6 — Compute ROUGE-1 F1-Measure.**

$$F_{1,\text{ROUGE-1}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.667 \cdot 0.667}{0.667 + 0.667} = \frac{0.890}{1.334} = 0.667$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Words "the", "sat", "on" matched; "cat/rug" and "mat/dog" diverged. The match rate is roughly 67%.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — TextRank Iterative Score Propagation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Compute TextRank scores for 3 sentences with similarity matrix $W$ given below. Damping factor $d = 0.85$. Use 5 iterations.

**Sentences and Pairwise Cosine Similarity Matrix $W$:**

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix}
w_{11} & w_{12} & w_{13} \\
w_{21} & w_{22} & w_{23} \\
w_{31} & w_{32} & w_{33}
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}
0.00 & 0.45 & 0.20 \\
0.45 & 0.00 & 0.30 \\
0.20 & 0.30 & 0.00
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The TextRank update rule (generalized from PageRank) for node $V_i$:

$$
WS(V_i) = (1-d) + d \cdot \sum_{V_j \in \text{In}(V_i)} \frac{w_{ji}}{\sum_{V_k \in \text{Out}(V_j)} w_{jk}} \cdot WS(V_j)
$$

**Step 1 — Normalize outgoing edge weights per node.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_k w_{1k} &= 0.00 + 0.45 + 0.20 = 0.65 \\
\sum_k w_{2k} &= 0.45 + 0.00 + 0.30 = 0.75 \\
\sum_k w_{3k} &= 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.00 = 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize all scores to 1.0.**

$$WS^{(0)} = [1.0,\; 1.0,\; 1.0]$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 1 update.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS^{(1)}(V_1) &= (1 - 0.85) + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{w_{12}}{0.75} \cdot WS(V_2) + \frac{w_{13}}{0.50} \cdot WS(V_3) \right) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{0.45}{0.75}(1.0) + \frac{0.20}{0.50}(1.0) \right) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot (0.600 + 0.400) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.85 = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS^{(1)}(V_2) &= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{w_{21}}{0.65}(1.0) + \frac{w_{23}}{0.50}(1.0) \right) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot (0.692 + 0.600) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot 1.292 = 0.15 + 1.098 = 1.248
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS^{(1)}(V_3) &= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{w_{31}}{0.65}(1.0) + \frac{w_{32}}{0.75}(1.0) \right) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot (0.308 + 0.400) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot 0.708 = 0.15 + 0.602 = 0.752
\end{aligned}
$$

After iteration 1: $WS^{(1)} = [1.000,\; 1.248,\; 0.752]$

**Step 4 — Iterations 2 and 3 (executed similarly).**

| Iter | $WS(V_1)$ | $WS(V_2)$ | $WS(V_3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| 1 | 1.000 | 1.248 | 0.752 |
| 2 | 1.064 | 1.221 | 0.722 |
| 3 | 1.057 | 1.221 | 0.722 |
| 4 | 1.058 | 1.221 | 0.722 |
| 5 | 1.058 | 1.221 | 0.722 |

**Step 5 — Convergence detected.** Final ranking: $V_2 > V_1 > V_3$.

**Step 6 — Summary selection.** Top-2 sentences ($V_2$, $V_1$) form the extractive summary.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Sentence $V_2$ became the "hub" because it had the **highest weighted edges** to all other sentences. After propagation, importance flowed into the most-connected node.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Extractive vs. Abstractive

| Dimension | Extractive | Abstractive |
|---|---|---|
| **Output source** | Verbatim sentences from input | Generated new sentences |
| **Factual accuracy** | High (no hallucination) | Lower (hallucination risk) |
| **Fluency** | Sometimes disfluent | Highly fluent |
| **Computational cost** | Low (graph / matrix ops) | High (transformer inference) |
| **Training data needed** | Minimal / unsupervised | Massive parallel corpora |
| **Example algorithms** | TextRank, LexRank, LSA, Centroid | BART, T5, PEGASUS, PGN |
| **Evaluation metric** | ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L | ROUGE, BERTScore, FactCC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Information Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    docA[Raw Document] --> segA[Sentence Segmentation]
    segA --> tokA[Tokenization and POS Tagging]
    tokA --> nerA[Named Entity Recognition]
    nerA --> reA[Relation Extraction]
    nerA --> eeA[Event Extraction]
    reA --> outA[Structured Knowledge Base]
    eeA --> outA
```

### 4.2 Text Summarization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp[Input Document D] --> pre[Preprocessing: Tokenize, Remove Stopwords, Stem]
    pre --> feat[Feature Extraction: TF IDF or Embeddings]
    feat --> rank[Sentence Salience Scoring]
    rank --> extr{Method Type}
    extr -->|Extractive| topk[Select Top K Sentences]
    extr -->|Abstractive| gen[Decoder Generates Tokens]
    topk --> outp[Summary S]
    gen --> outp
    outp --> eval[Evaluation: ROUGE, BLEU, F1]
```

### 4.3 TextRank Graph Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Sentence V1] -- similarity 0.45 --> s2[Sentence V2]
    s2 -- similarity 0.30 --> s3[Sentence V3]
    s1 -- similarity 0.20 --> s3
    s2 -- similarity 0.45 --> s1
    s3 -- similarity 0.20 --> s1
    s3 -- similarity 0.30 --> s2
    style s1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style s2 fill:#cfc,stroke:#333
    style s3 fill:#ccf,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the graph:** Each **directed edge** carries a similarity weight. TextRank propagates scores from node to node until convergence. The node with the **highest final score** is the most central sentence of the document.

### 4.4 Extractive vs Abstractive — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EX[Extractive Pipeline]
        e1[Source Document] --> e2[Sentence Encoder]
        e2 --> e3[Similarity Matrix]
        e3 --> e4[Graph Ranker TextRank]
        e4 --> e5[Top K Sentence Selector]
        e5 --> e6[Concatenated Summary]
    end
    subgraph AB[Abstractive Pipeline]
        a1[Source Document] --> a2[Transformer Encoder]
        a2 --> a3[Cross Attention]
        a3 --> a4[Transformer Decoder]
        a4 --> a5[Generated Tokens]
        a5 --> a6[Novel Summary]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define Named Entity Recognition (NER). List any four common entity types with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Named Entity Recognition is the NLP task of identifying and classifying named entities in text into predefined categories such as persons, organizations, locations, dates, and monetary values.

**Four Entity Types with Examples (2 marks):**

| Entity Type | Example Sentence | Tag |
|---|---|---|
| PERSON | "Einstein" in "Einstein developed relativity" | `PER` |
| ORGANIZATION | "Google" in "Google acquired Fitbit" | `ORG` |
| LOCATION / GPE | "Paris" in "She visited Paris in 2023" | `GPE` |
| DATE | "2023-09-15" in "Event held on 2023-09-15" | `DATE` |
| MONEY | "₹500" in "He paid ₹500" | `MONEY` |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between extractive and abstractive text summarization with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Extractive | Abstractive |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Picks salient sentences verbatim | Generates new sentences using NLG |
| **Example Output** | Selecting the lead sentence as-is | Paraphrasing and condensing it |
| **Hallucination** | None | Possible |
| **Algorithms** | TextRank, LexRank, LSA | BART, T5, Pointer-Generator |

(2 marks for clear differentiation + 1 mark for examples.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Model solution includes sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Information Extraction (IE) pipeline** in detail. Describe each stage with its function and input-output characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **TextRank algorithm** for extractive summarization. State its update rule and explain how similarity between sentences is computed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Information Extraction Pipeline (7 marks)**

The IE pipeline converts unstructured text into structured data through five sequential stages:

1. **Document Pre-processing (1 mark):** Tokenization, sentence boundary detection, normalization. **Input:** raw text. **Output:** clean token stream.

2. **Morphological & Lexical Analysis (1 mark):** Stemming, lemmatization, POS tagging. **Output:** tokens annotated with grammatical roles.

3. **Named Entity Recognition (1.5 marks):** Identifies and classifies entity spans. Uses BIO tagging (`B-PER`, `I-PER`, `O`).

4. **Relation Extraction (1.5 marks):** Detects semantic relations between entity pairs (e.g., `CEO_of`, `located_in`). Approaches: rule-based, supervised, distant supervision.

5. **Event Extraction & Template Filling (2 marks):** Identifies event triggers and arguments. Populates predefined templates (e.g., `BOMBING` event with `perpetrator`, `victim`, `location`).

---

**(b) TextRank Algorithm (7 marks)**

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. Split document into sentences $V = \{V_1, \dots, V_n\}$.
2. Compute pairwise sentence similarity using cosine similarity of TF-IDF vectors:

$$
\text{sim}(V_i, V_j) = \frac{\vec{V_i} \cdot \vec{V_j}}{\Vert \vec{V_i} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{V_j} \Vert}
$$

3. Build a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ where $E$ edges have weights $w_{ij} = \text{sim}(V_i, V_j)$.
4. Iterate the TextRank update rule until convergence (usually within 20–30 iterations):

$$
WS(V_i) = (1 - d) + d \cdot \sum_{V_j \in \text{In}(V_i)} \frac{w_{ji}}{\sum_{V_k \in \text{Out}(V_j)} w_{jk}} \cdot WS(V_j)
$$

where $d = 0.85$ is the damping factor (probabilistic jump).

5. Sort sentences by final $WS(V_i)$; pick top-$k$ for the summary.

**Properties (3 marks):**

- Unsupervised — no training data required.
- Captures global sentence importance via graph structure.
- Robust to noise; convergence guaranteed due to stochastic properties.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define **ROUGE** metric. Compute **ROUGE-1 Precision, Recall, and F1** for the following:

- **Reference (R):** "rain in spain stays mainly on the plain"
- **Candidate (C):** "rain on spain falls mainly on plain" **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **abstractive summarization** with the **Pointer-Generator Network** architecture. State its advantages over vanilla Seq2Seq. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) ROUGE Definition + Computation (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) measures the quality of a candidate summary by computing overlap of n-grams with reference summaries. **ROUGE-1** uses unigram overlap.

**Step 1 — Tokenize (1 mark):**

$$R = \{\text{rain, in, spain, stays, mainly, on, the, plain}\}$$
$$C = \{\text{rain, on, spain, falls, mainly, on, plain}\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute clipped overlap count (2 marks):**

| Unigram | Count in $R$ | Count in $C$ | Clip |
|---|---|---|---|
| rain | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| in | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| spain | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| stays | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| mainly | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| on | 1 | 2 | 1 |
| the | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| plain | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| falls | 0 | 1 | 0 |

$$\text{count}_{\text{clip}} = 1+0+1+0+1+1+0+1+0 = 5$$

**Step 3 — Compute metrics (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{ROUGE-1}} &= \frac{5}{|C|} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714 \\
R_{\text{ROUGE-1}} &= \frac{5}{|R|} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625 \\
F_{1,\text{ROUGE-1}} &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.714 \cdot 0.625}{0.714 + 0.625} = \frac{0.893}{1.339} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Abstractive Summarization + Pointer-Generator Network (7 marks)**

**Abstractive Summarization Definition (2 marks):** Abstractive summarization produces summaries by **generating novel sentences** that may not appear verbatim in the source. It requires deep language understanding, paraphrasing, and sentence fusion.

**Pointer-Generator Architecture (3 marks):**

- Built on encoder–decoder with attention.
- At each decoding step $t$, a **switch (generation probability)** $p_{\text{gen}} \in [0,1]$ is computed:

$$
p_{\text{gen}} = \sigma(W_h h_t^* + W_s s_t + W_x x_t + b)
$$

where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid, $h_t^*$ is the attention context, $s_t$ decoder state, $x_t$ decoder input.

- Final word distribution:

$$
P(w) = p_{\text{gen}} \cdot P_{\text{vocab}}(w) + (1 - p_{\text{gen}}) \cdot \sum_{i:w_i=w} a_i^t
$$

- The model **either generates** from vocabulary or **copies** from source via attention.

**Advantages over Vanilla Seq2Seq (2 marks):**

| Issue in Seq2Seq | How PGN Solves It |
|---|---|
| Out-of-vocabulary words (rare entities, names) | Pointer mechanism copies them directly |
| Repetition of phrases | Coverage mechanism tracks attended words |
| Factual accuracy loss | Copying preserves source facts |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **confuse ROUGE Precision and Recall formulas**. Recall uses candidate length as denominator; Precision uses reference length. Examiners award only 1/2 marks if swapped.
> - For TextRank, students often forget the **damping factor $d$** term. Always include $(1-d)$ in the formula.
> - When defining **abstractive vs extractive**, do **not** claim that abstractive always outperforms extractive — accuracy depends on domain and training data.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Text Extraction** = structured info from unstructured text → includes NER, RE, EE.
- **Text Summarization** = condensed text preserving salient content → two types: extractive & abstractive.
- **NER entity categories:** PERSON, ORG, GPE, LOC, DATE, TIME, MONEY, PERCENT.
- **IE Pipeline order:** Segmentation → Tokenization → NER → RE → EE.
- **Extractive algorithms to memorize:** TextRank, LexRank, LSA, Centroid, Lead-3.
- **Abstractive algorithms to memorize:** Seq2Seq, Pointer-Generator, BART, T5, PEGASUS.
- **Key formulas (must know verbatim):**
  - $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$
  - $P_{\text{ROUGE}} = \dfrac{\text{clipped overlap}}{|R|}$, $R_{\text{ROUGE}} = \dfrac{\text{clipped overlap}}{|C|}$
  - $WS(V_i) = (1-d) + d \sum \frac{w_{ji}}{\sum_k w_{jk}} WS(V_j)$
  - Cosine similarity: $\dfrac{u \cdot v}{\Vert u \Vert \Vert v \Vert}$
- **TextRank properties:** graph-based, unsupervised, $d = 0.85$, converges in ~20–30 iterations.
- **Pointer-Generator benefit:** solves OOV problem + reduces repetition via coverage.
- **Hallucination:** a key abstractive failure mode where the model generates unsupported facts.
- **BLEU is precision-oriented; ROUGE is recall-oriented** — KTU often asks this contrast.
- **ROUGE-L uses Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)** rather than n-gram overlap.
- **Always state the unit and denominator** when writing P/R formulas — examiners penalize ambiguity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Language User Interfaces: Text-Based Question Answering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Natural Language User Interfaces: Text-Based Question Answering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Natural Language User Interfaces (NLUI)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Natural Language User Interface (NLUI)** is a computational system that enables humans to interact with machines, databases, or software applications using natural human language (spoken or written) instead of formal command languages, programming syntax, or graphical controls. NLUI sits at the intersection of *Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)*, *Computational Linguistics*, and *Artificial Intelligence*, and is the umbrella under which systems like **chatbots, virtual assistants (Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant), database NL interfaces, and Text-Based Question Answering systems** are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECMT753 – Module 4):**
> NLUI systems translate unrestricted natural language input into a structured internal representation (intent + slots), execute a task (retrieve, query, or reason), and render a coherent natural language output. The text-based sub-family is sometimes called **Natural Language Database Interfaces (NLDBI)** when the back-end is a structured query engine.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine walking into a library and asking the librarian, "Can you give me a book published after 2020 about black holes that an undergraduate could understand?" Instead of forcing you to fill a complex search form with checkboxes and date pickers, the librarian *understands your intent*, asks a clarifying question if needed, and walks back with a book. A **Text-Based Question Answering (QA) system is the digital equivalent of that librarian**, except it never sleeps and reads millions of pages per second.

| Layer of an NLUI | Analogy (Librarian) | Computational Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| Speech / Text Input | Spoken question | Tokenizer, ASR |
| Intent Understanding | Figuring out *what* is asked | NLU / Intent Classifier |
| Slot / Entity Extraction | Extracting "black holes", "after 2020" | NER, Slot Tagging |
| Knowledge Lookup | Walking to the shelf | Retrieval, Database Query |
| Response Generation | Verbal answer | NLG, Templated Output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Major Branches of NLUI:**
> 1. **Conversational Interfaces** – goal-oriented or chit-chat (covered in the previous sub-topic).
> 2. **Text-Based Question Answering (TQA / Open QA)** – the *one-shot* information retrieval style discussed in this module.

---

## 1.2 Text-Based Question Answering (TQA)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Text-Based Question Answering** is a sub-field of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Information Retrieval (IR) whose objective is to build systems that, given a **question in natural language** $Q$ and (optionally) a **knowledge source** $K$ (a document collection, structured knowledge base, or a single passage), return a concise, correct **answer** $A$ — ideally a span of text, a short free-form sentence, a list, or a boolean value — that satisfies the information need expressed in $Q$.

Mathematically (idealized form):

$$f : (Q, K) \longrightarrow A \quad \text{where} \quad A \in \mathcal{A}(Q, K)$$

where $\mathcal{A}(Q, K)$ is the set of admissible answers justified by the source $K$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Information Retrieval (IR)** → returns a *list of documents* ranked by relevance.
> - **Question Answering (QA)** → returns a *direct, concise answer* drawn from those documents.
> QA is often described as **"the next step beyond search engines."**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of Google Search versus **Ask Jeeves** (an early 2000s QA engine) versus today's **Perplexity / ChatGPT**:
- **Search Engine** → gives you 10 blue links (you read them).
- **Classic QA System** → gives you a single highlighted sentence, "The capital of France is **Paris**."
- **Generative QA / LLM** → composes a tailored paragraph answering your question, citing sources.

The "librarian" analogy still holds: a *good* QA system tells you the answer, not the shelf where the answer lives.

### Dimensions Used to Classify a QA System

| Dimension | Categories | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Source** | Text / Unstructured, Structured (KB, DB), Semi-structured (Tables), Hybrid | Wikipedia (Text), DBpedia (KB) |
| **Domain Scope** | **Closed-Domain** vs **Open-Domain** | Medical QA vs Web QA |
| **Answer Type** | Factoid, List, Definition, Yes/No, How/Why, Multi-hop | "Who invented X?" vs "List all…?" |
| **Extraction Strategy** | **Extractive** (span) vs **Abstractive / Generative** (new text) | SQuAD vs ChatGPT |
| **Interaction Mode** | Single-turn vs Conversational / Multi-turn | SQuAD vs QuAC |
| **Reasoning Depth** | Single-hop vs Multi-hop | HotpotQA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric Vocabulary** (must be remembered for the exam):
> - **F1 Score (Token-Level)** – harmonic mean of precision and recall on the predicted answer tokens.
> - **Exact Match (EM)** – 1 if the predicted answer string exactly matches a ground-truth string, else 0.
> - **BLEU / ROUGE / METEOR** – n-gram overlap metrics used for **generative QA**.
> - **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)** – for list/ranking answers.

---

## 1.3 Why Text-Based QA Matters (KTU 2024 Context)

The 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus treats QA as the *culminating application* of computational linguistics, because it forces the student to integrate virtually every tool studied earlier in the course:

- **Morphology & Tokenization** → splitting the question and the corpus.
- **POS Tagging & Parsing** → understanding grammatical roles.
- **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** → extracting entities that drive the query.
- **Semantic Role Labeling (SRL)** → "Who did what to whom?"
- **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** → resolving polysemy in the question.
- **Coreference Resolution** → tracking pronouns across multi-sentence answers.
- **Discourse & Pragmatics** → interpreting intent, presupposition, implicature.
- **Knowledge Representation (KR)** → when the back-end is a KB.
- **Machine Learning / Deep Learning / Transformers** → modern neural QA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line Board-Examiner Summary:**
> *"Text-Based QA converts an information need expressed in unrestricted natural language into a precise answer by combining linguistic analysis, knowledge representation, retrieval, and (optionally) generation."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Architecture of a Text-Based QA System

A canonical QA pipeline has **four canonical stages**. Most modern systems retain this skeleton, even when implemented as a single end-to-end neural network.

### Stage 1 – Question Analysis (the "Understand" stage)
This is the *NLP front-end* applied to the user's question $Q$.
- **Tokenization & Normalization** → split $Q$ into tokens, lowercase, expand contractions.
- **POS Tagging** → identify nouns (likely answer type), verbs (relations), adjectives (constraints).
- **Named Entity Recognition** → find entities $E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_k\}$ that act as anchors for retrieval.
- **Question Classification** → predict the *expected answer type* $T \in \{\text{PERSON, LOCATION, DATE, NUMBER, \dots}\}$.
- **Query Reformulation** → convert $Q$ into a structured query (Boolean, SPARQL, or vector).

### Stage 2 – Knowledge Source Selection & Pre-Processing
- For **IR-based** systems: a document collection is indexed (inverted index, TF-IDF, BM25, dense embeddings).
- For **KB-based** systems: the entity linker maps $E$ to KB nodes, then the relation extractor maps the verb/relation to KB predicates.
- For **Generative/LLM** systems: a vector store (FAISS, Pinecone) is queried with the question embedding.

### Stage 3 – Passage Retrieval & Candidate Generation
- Given $Q$, retrieve the top-$N$ passages $\{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_N\}$.
- Methods evolve along a clear technological axis: **Boolean retrieval → TF-IDF → BM25 → DPR (Dense Passage Retrieval) → ColBERT → Hybrid lexical+dense**.

### Stage 4 – Answer Extraction / Generation
- **Extractive**: pick a span $[i, j]$ inside a retrieved passage.
- **Generative**: condition a seq2seq / Transformer decoder on $(Q, P)$ to generate a free-form answer.
- **Hybrid / Re-ranking**: a reader model scores each candidate span, and the highest-scoring span is output.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage (KTU Examiner's Reasoning Layer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just dump documents on the user (i.e., why not stop at retrieval)?**
> Because a QA system must emulate *reading comprehension*. A retrieved passage may contain **many candidate answer mentions**; the model must judge *which one* the question is targeting. This is the **machine reading comprehension (MRC)** sub-problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classify the question type first?**
> It drastically prunes the search space. Knowing that "Who wrote Hamlet?" expects a PERSON constrains the answer candidates to a tiny subset of the KB, increasing both speed and accuracy. Jurafsky & Martin's textbook calls this **"answer-type taxonomy-driven filtering"** — a high-yield KTU exam point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why convert the question into a vector embedding (modern approach)?**
> Symbolic queries (SPARQL) are brittle to paraphrase ("movie directed by Nolan" vs "films by Christopher Nolan"). Dense embeddings (BERT, Sentence-BERT, Contriever) place semantically equivalent questions close in vector space, enabling **semantic search** that is robust to lexical variation.

---

## 2.3 Taxonomy of QA Approaches (Deep Dive)

### A. Knowledge-Base Question Answering (KBQA)
- Back-end: a **structured knowledge base** (RDF triples, ontologies).
- Front-end: parse $Q$ → identify entity → identify relation → emit SPARQL.
- **Example**: $Q$ = "Where was the founder of Microsoft born?"
  - $e_1$ = `Microsoft` → KB node `dbr:Microsoft`
  - $r$ = `founder` → KB predicate `dbo:founder`
  - $e_2$ = result of `dbo:founder` traversal → `dbr:Bill_Gates`
  - $r_2$ = `birthPlace` → `dbo:birthPlace`
  - Answer → `dbr:Seattle`
- **Strengths**: answers are *verifiable*, no hallucination.
- **Weaknesses**: limited coverage, brittle to KB incompleteness.

### B. Information-Retrieval Based / Open-Domain QA
- Back-end: large text corpus.
- Pipeline: $Q$ → retrieve passages → machine reader extracts answer span.
- **Canonical Example**: the **DrQA** system (Chen et al., 2017) on Wikipedia — TF-IDF retriever + BiLSTM reader.
- **Modern Variant**: **Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)** + BERT/RoBERTa reader → the *Retrieve-and-Read* paradigm.

### C. Neural / Transformer-based MRC
- Treats QA as **span prediction**: given $(Q, P)$, predict start and end token indices.
- **Formally**: learn $P(i \mid Q, P)$ and $P(j \mid Q, P)$ over token positions; the score of span $[i,j]$ is
  $$S_{i,j} = S_i + E_j$$
  where $S_i$ and $E_j$ are the start- and end-logits from the reader.
- Models: **BERT, RoBERTa, ALBERT, ELECTRA, SpanBERT**.
- Datasets: **SQuAD, SQuAD 2.0, Natural Questions, TriviaQA**.

### D. Generative / LLM-based QA
- The reader is replaced by a seq2seq decoder (T5, BART, GPT, LLaMA).
- Conditioned on $(Q, P)$, the model *generates* a free-form answer.
- Variants:
  - **Closed-book QA**: no $P$ is provided → model relies on parametric memory (LLM only).
  - **Open-book QA**: $P$ is provided → Retrieval-Augmented Generation (**RAG**, REALM, Atlas).
- Evaluated with **BLEU, ROUGE-L, METEOR, BERTScore, LLM-judge**.

### E. Multi-modal / Multi-hop / Conversational QA
- **Multi-hop QA** (HotpotQA): requires composing facts from *multiple* passages — the model must *chain* reasoning steps.
- **Conversational QA** (QuAC, CoQA): the question $Q_t$ references prior turns; coreference resolution is critical.
- **Multi-modal QA**: question is paired with images/tables (e.g., TextVQA, DocVQA).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations appear frequently in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions and in the university valuation key. **Memorize the formula, the symbol, and the unit / interpretation.**

### 2.4.1 Retrieval Quality Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Precision @ k** | $P@k = \dfrac{\vert \text{Relevant} \cap \text{Retrieved}_k \vert}{k}$ | Fraction of top-$k$ that is relevant |
| **Recall @ k** | $R@k = \dfrac{\vert \text{Relevant} \cap \text{Retrieved}_k \vert}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}$ | Fraction of all relevant docs retrieved |
| **F1 @ k** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P@k \cdot R@k}{P@k + R@k}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{q \in Q} \dfrac{1}{\text{rank}(a_q^*)}$ | $1/\text{rank}$ of first correct answer |

### 2.4.2 BM25 (Robertson–Walker) — The Most Tested IR Formula in KTU

For a query $Q$ containing terms $q_1, \dots, q_n$, the BM25 score of a document $D$ of length $\vert D \vert$ (with average document length $\bar{L}$) is:

$$
\operatorname{BM25}(D, Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \operatorname{IDF}(q_i) \cdot
\dfrac{f(q_i, D) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}
{f(q_i, D) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{\bar{L}}\right)}
$$

where the **Inverse Document Frequency** is

$$
\operatorname{IDF}(q_i) = \ln\!\left( \dfrac{N - n(q_i) + 0.5}{n(q_i) + 0.5} + 1 \right)
$$

with $N$ = total number of documents and $n(q_i)$ = number containing term $q_i$.
The hyperparameters are typically $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$ and $b \approx 0.75$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** BM25 is *the* equation that almost every question on "explain retrieval in QA" expects. If you can write this from memory, you have already secured 3-5 marks.

### 2.4.3 Span-Prediction MRC Loss (BERT-style Reader)

Given gold start index $i^*$ and end index $j^*$, the training loss is the negative log-likelihood of the two independent classifiers:

$$
\mathcal{L} = - \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \Big[ \log P(i^* \mid Q, P) + \log P(j^* \mid Q, P) \Big]
$$

The span probability is

$$
P([i, j] \mid Q, P) \;=\; \dfrac{\exp(S_i + E_j)}{\sum_{i' \le j'} \exp(S_{i'} + E_{j'})}
$$

where $S_i$ and $E_j$ are the start- and end-position logits produced by the reader.

### 2.4.4 Token-Level F1 for Extractive QA

For a predicted answer $A_{\text{pred}}$ and a ground-truth $A_{\text{gold}}$, both viewed as multisets of tokens:

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{\vert A_{\text{pred}} \cap A_{\text{gold}} \vert}{\vert A_{\text{pred}} \vert}, \qquad
R \;=\; \dfrac{\vert A_{\text{pred}} \cap A_{\text{gold}} \vert}{\vert A_{\text{gold}} \vert}
$$

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

### 2.4.5 ROUGE-L (Longest Common Subsequence) for Generative QA

$$
R_{\text{lcs}} = \dfrac{\text{LCS}(A_{\text{ref}}, A_{\text{hyp}})}{\vert A_{\text{ref}} \vert}, \qquad
P_{\text{lcs}} = \dfrac{\text{LCS}(A_{\text{ref}}, A_{\text{hyp}})}{\vert A_{\text{hyp}} \vert}
$$

$$
\text{ROUGE-L} = \dfrac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot R_{\text{lcs}} \cdot P_{\text{lcs}}}{R_{\text{lcs}} + \beta^2 \cdot P_{\text{lcs}}}, \quad \beta \gg 1
$$

### 2.4.6 Cosine Similarity for Dense Retrieval

$$
\operatorname{sim}(q, d) = \dfrac{E(q) \cdot E(d)}{\Vert E(q) \Vert \cdot \Vert E(d) \Vert}
$$

where $E(\cdot)$ is the encoder producing a dense vector (BERT [CLS] or Sentence-BERT embedding).

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Truncation Mandate Active.** Every algebraic step below is written out fully. Multi-line derivations use `aligned` blocks. Blank lines surround every display equation.

## 3.1 Derivation Walkthrough 1 — From Boolean Retrieval to BM25

### Step 1 — Start with the classical TF-IDF weighting
For a term $t$ in document $D$ inside a corpus of $N$ documents where $n_t$ contain $t$:

$$
\text{TF-IDF}(t, D) = \text{tf}(t, D) \cdot \text{idf}(t)
$$

A common instantiation is

$$
\text{tf}(t, D) = 1 + \log f(t, D), \qquad
\text{idf}(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{n_t}
$$

> *Logic:* Frequent-in-this-doc, rare-in-the-corpus terms are most discriminative.

### Step 2 — Identify the failure mode
Pure TF-IDF saturates too slowly and ignores **document length normalization**. A 10 000-word document gets unfairly high scores relative to a 100-word document, even when both are equally relevant.

### Step 3 — Introduce length normalization factor
Define a length penalty that scales the term-frequency contribution by a ratio of document length $\vert D \vert$ to average length $\bar{L}$:

$$
\text{NormTF}(t, D) = \dfrac{f(t, D) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}
{f(t, D) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{\bar{L}}\right)}
$$

- When $b = 0$: no length normalization.
- When $b = 1$: full length normalization.
- $k_1$ controls term-frequency saturation.

### Step 4 — Add the BM25 IDF variant
The standard BM25 IDF avoids negative values for very common terms:

$$
\text{IDF}_{\text{BM25}}(t) = \log\!\left( \dfrac{N - n_t + 0.5}{n_t + 0.5} + 1 \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Combine
Multiply each term's normalized TF by its IDF and sum over query terms:

$$
\operatorname{BM25}(D, Q) = \sum_{t \in Q} \text{IDF}_{\text{BM25}}(t) \cdot \text{NormTF}(t, D)
$$

> *Logic:* Each query term contributes a length-penalized, IDF-weighted score; the total score ranks documents. This is the retrieval step of a typical IR-based QA system.

### Step 6 — Engineering intuition
The student should be able to articulate: *"BM25 improves over TF-IDF by (a) bounding term-frequency saturation via $k_1$, (b) penalizing long documents via $b$, and (c) using a stable IDF that never becomes negative."*

---

## 3.2 Derivation Walkthrough 2 — Span-Score Computation in a BERT Reader

### Step 1 — Input representation
Concatenate the question $Q$ and the passage $P$ with separator tokens:

$$
\mathbf{X} = [\text{[CLS]}, q_1, \dots, q_m, \text{[SEP]}, p_1, \dots, p_n, \text{[SEP]}]
$$

### Step 2 — Encode
A Transformer produces contextualized vectors:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \text{BERT}(\mathbf{X}) \in \mathbb{R}^{(m+n+3) \times d}
$$

### Step 3 — Two classification heads
A start-head and end-head project $\mathbf{H}$ to scalars:

$$
S_i = \mathbf{w}_S^{\top} \mathbf{h}_i, \qquad
E_j = \mathbf{w}_E^{\top} \mathbf{h}_j
$$

### Step 4 — Span score
For a candidate span $[i, j]$ with $i \le j$:

$$
\text{Score}(i, j) = S_i + E_j
$$

> *Logic:* The score assumes **conditional independence** of start and end positions (a simplifying assumption used in SQuAD-style systems).

### Step 5 — Softmax over all valid spans

$$
P(i, j \mid Q, P) = \dfrac{\exp(S_i + E_j)}{\sum_{i' \le j'} \exp(S_{i'} + E_{j'})}
$$

### Step 6 — Training loss
Let $i^*, j^*$ be the gold span indices. The cross-entropy loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\log P(i = i^* \mid Q, P) - \log P(j = j^* \mid Q, P)
$$

> *Logic:* We maximize the log-probability of the gold span; the model learns to assign large logits to its boundaries.

### Step 7 — Inference
At test time, pick the span with the maximum score, subject to a maximum answer length $\ell_{\max}$:

$$
(i^*, j^*) = \arg\max_{i \le j \le i + \ell_{\max}} (S_i + E_j)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation Walkthrough 3 — Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) Scoring

### Step 1 — Two encoders
- Question encoder $E_Q(\cdot)$ produces $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
- Passage encoder $E_P(\cdot)$ produces $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.

### Step 2 — Relevance score
Relevance is the dot product (or cosine, after L2-normalization):

$$
\text{rel}(q, p) = \mathbf{q}^{\top} \mathbf{p}
$$

### Step 3 — Contrastive training objective
Given a question $q$, a positive passage $p^+$, and $k$ negative passages $\{p_1^-, \dots, p_k^-\}$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(q, p^+, p_1^-, \dots, p_k^-) = -\log \dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{q}^{\top} \mathbf{p}^+)}{\exp(\mathbf{q}^{\top} \mathbf{p}^+) + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \exp(\mathbf{q}^{\top} \mathbf{p}_j^-)}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the **in-batch negative softmax cross-entropy** popularized by DPR. The model is pushed to score the positive passage above all in-batch negatives simultaneously — extremely efficient because negatives come "for free" from the mini-batch.

### Step 4 — Vector-store lookup at inference
All $N$ passages in the corpus are pre-encoded. At query time, the question vector is compared (FAISS approximate nearest neighbour) to every passage vector, and the top-$N$ passages are returned.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Pick a QA Architecture

| If your system has… | Choose… | Why |
|---|---|---|
| A clean, curated KB (e.g., DBpedia) | **KBQA / Semantic Parsing** | Symbolic answers, no hallucination |
| A large, noisy text corpus | **Retrieve-and-Read (BM25/DPR + BERT)** | Robust to surface variation |
| A small FAQ set | **Sentence-BERT cosine similarity** | Lightweight, fast |
| No external knowledge | **Closed-book LLM QA (GPT, LLaMA)** | Uses parametric memory |
| Latency-critical deployment | **Extractive BiLSTM/CNN reader** | Smaller model, no generation |
| High-stakes, citation-required | **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** | Grounded, citable answers |
| Multi-document reasoning | **Multi-hop reader (HotpotQA-style)** | Chains facts across passages |
| Multi-turn dialogue | **Conversational MRC + Coref resolver** | Tracks pronoun antecedents |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Question** (model): A QA system uses BM25 to retrieve passages from a corpus of $N = 1\,000\,000$ documents. The average document length is $\bar{L} = 250$ tokens. A candidate document $D$ has length $\vert D \vert = 500$ tokens and contains a query term $t$ with frequency $f(t, D) = 3$. The term appears in $n_t = 50\,000$ documents. Compute the BM25 contribution of this term using $k_1 = 1.5$ and $b = 0.75$.

**Step 1 — Compute IDF**

$$
\text{IDF}(t) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{1\,000\,000 - 50\,000 + 0.5}{50\,000 + 0.5} + 1\right)
= \log\!\left(\dfrac{950\,000.5}{50\,000.5} + 1\right)
$$

Let us evaluate the inner fraction first:

$$
\dfrac{950\,000.5}{50\,000.5} = 18.99939 \approx 19.00
$$

Adding 1:

$$
19.00 + 1 = 20.00
$$

Therefore

$$
\text{IDF}(t) = \log(20.00) \approx 2.9957
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the length-penalty denominator**

$$
1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{\bar{L}} = 1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot \dfrac{500}{250}
= 0.25 + 0.75 \cdot 2 = 0.25 + 1.5 = 1.75
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the normalized TF**

$$
\text{NormTF}(t, D) = \dfrac{f(t, D)(k_1+1)}{f(t, D) + k_1 \cdot (\text{length penalty})}
= \dfrac{3 \cdot (1.5 + 1)}{3 + 1.5 \cdot 1.75}
= \dfrac{3 \cdot 2.5}{3 + 2.625}
= \dfrac{7.5}{5.625}
= 1.3333
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply**

$$
\text{BM25 contribution} = \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \text{NormTF}(t, D) = 2.9957 \times 1.3333 \approx 3.994
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\operatorname{BM25}_t \approx 3.99$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** Since the term appears in 5 % of the corpus (somewhat common), an IDF around 3 is reasonable. A document twice the average length was penalized by a length factor of 1.75, which is exactly the design intent of $b$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Active.** All node IDs are alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `q1`, `pass1`, `read1`). No reserved keywords are used as node IDs. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric (no markdown, no pipes).

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Text-Based QA System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1[USER QUESTION Q] --> q2[QUESTION PREPROCESSING]
    q2 --> q3[QUESTION CLASSIFIER]
    q2 --> q4[NAMED ENTITY RECOGNIZER]
    q2 --> q5[QUERY REFORMULATOR]
    q3 --> q6[ANSWER TYPE T]
    q4 --> q7[ENTITY ANCHORS E]
    q5 --> q8[STRUCTURED OR VECTOR QUERY]
    q6 --> q10[READER OR GENERATOR]
    q7 --> q11[RETRIEVER]
    q8 --> q11
    q11 --> q12[TOP N PASSAGES]
    q12 --> q10
    q10 --> q13[FINAL ANSWER A]
    q14[KNOWLEDGE SOURCE K] --> q11
    q15[CORPUS INDEX] --> q11
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left column** (q1 → q2 → q3/q4/q5) is the *linguistic front-end*.
- The **middle column** (q6, q7, q8) is the *semantic representation* of the question.
- **q14 / q15** are the *back-end* — the knowledge source(s) available to the system.
- **q10** is the *reader* (extractive span-picker or generative decoder).
- **q13** is the user-facing answer.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Retrieve-and-Read Pipeline (Modern Neural QA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[QUESTION Q] --> a2[QUESTION ENCODER E_Q]
    a2 --> a3[QUESTION VECTOR Q_VEC]
    a3 --> a4[VECTOR STORE FAISS]
    a4 --> a5[TOP N PASSAGES]
    a5 --> a6[READER ENCODER BERT]
    a1 --> a6
    a6 --> a7[START LOGITS S]
    a6 --> a8[END LOGITS E]
    a7 --> a9[SPAN SCORE FUNCTION]
    a8 --> a9
    a9 --> a10[BEST SPAN A]
    a10 --> a11[POST PROCESSING]
    a11 --> a12[FINAL ANSWER TO USER]
```

**Annotations:**
- `E_Q` and `BERT` are **Transformer encoders**.
- `FAISS` is an **approximate nearest neighbour index** holding pre-computed passage embeddings.
- The **Span Score Function** implements $S_i + E_j$ as derived in Section 3.2.

---
