# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These two questions are calibrated to the **KTU 2024 ESE Part A** (3 marks, 50-80 word answer). Model answers are exam-ready.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] — **3 Marks**
**Differentiate between Information Retrieval (IR) and Question Answering (QA). List any two evaluation metrics used in QA systems.**

**Model Answer:**
Information Retrieval (IR) is the task of finding documents relevant to a query from a large collection and presenting them as a ranked list. Question Answering (QA) goes one step further — it reads or reasons over the retrieved documents (or a knowledge base) and returns a **precise, concise answer** to the user's question. In short, IR gives *the page*; QA gives *the sentence on the page*.

Two common evaluation metrics for QA are:
1. **Exact Match (EM)** — fraction of predictions that match the ground-truth string exactly.
2. **Token-level F1 Score** — harmonic mean of token precision and recall between prediction and gold answer.

*(For 3 marks the above is sufficient; add MRR / BLEU if the rubric allows a 4th sub-point.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] — **3 Marks**
**What is the role of the reader module in a Retrieve-and-Read QA pipeline? Name one classical and one neural reader model.**

**Model Answer:**
The **reader module** takes the question and the top retrieved passages as input and produces the final answer. For extractive QA, the reader identifies the most likely **span of tokens** inside a passage that answers the question; for generative QA, it produces a free-form answer sequence.

- A **classical reader** example: a **Bidirectional LSTM with a span-prediction head** (used in the DrQA system).
- A **neural reader** example: a **pre-trained Transformer** such as **BERT** fine-tuned on SQuAD.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, every Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice (OR). Below are **Question A (14 M)** and **Question B (14 M)** — both are independent full-length attempts with sub-parts (a) 7 M and (b) 7 M.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Architecture-Focused*

> [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a generic text-based Question Answering system**. Explain the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **Retrieve-and-Read pipeline** in detail. Compare the **BM25 retriever** with the **Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)** mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)
A generic text-based QA system is organized as a **four-stage pipeline**:

1. **Question Analysis Module** — performs tokenization, POS tagging, named-entity recognition, and question classification (factoid / list / definition / yes-no). It produces an *answer type expectation* and *entity anchors* that drive retrieval.
2. **Knowledge Source & Index** — the back-end (Wikipedia dump, FAQ collection, or a domain corpus) is pre-processed into an **inverted index** (for BM25) or a **vector index** (for DPR / FAISS).
3. **Passage Retrieval Module** — given the question, fetches the **top-N candidate passages** using either sparse (BM25, TF-IDF) or dense (BERT-based) similarity.
4. **Answer Extraction / Generation Module** — the **reader** inspects the candidates and either (i) **picks a span** $[i, j]$ (extractive) or (ii) **decodes a free-form answer** (generative). A **re-ranker** or **post-processor** may apply length constraints, date normalization, or formatting before the answer is returned to the user.

*(Insert the Section 4.1 mermaid diagram; it satisfies the "neat block diagram" rubric.)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Retrieve-and-Read Pipeline (Open-Domain QA):**
- **Step 1** — Encode the question $Q$ into a vector.
- **Step 2** — Retrieve the top-$N$ passages from a vector store (or via BM25).
- **Step 3** — Concatenate $(Q, P_i)$ for each candidate and feed to the **reader** (BERT), which predicts the start and end token indices.
- **Step 4** — Select the highest-scoring span across all candidates and return it.

**BM25 vs DPR — Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | BM25 (Sparse) | DPR (Dense) |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | Term-frequency vector in vocabulary space | Dense embedding (typically 768-dim BERT) |
| **Similarity** | Lexical overlap (bag-of-words) | Semantic similarity in continuous space |
| **Robustness to paraphrase** | Poor (exact-word match required) | Strong (paraphrases map close in space) |
| **Indexing** | Inverted index (fast, scalable) | FAISS / ScaNN (approximate NN) |
| **Training signal** | None — heuristic | Contrastive learning with in-batch negatives |
| **Out-of-vocabulary** | Cannot handle unseen terms | Sub-word tokenization handles it |
| **Strength** | Exact-match queries, low compute | Semantic queries, multilingual |
| **Weakness** | Lexical gap | Higher compute, requires GPU |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):**
> Many students *describe* a QA system but forget to mention the **answer type classification** step. Examiners specifically test whether the student understands *why* we classify questions. Always include a one-liner such as:
> *"Classifying the expected answer type prunes the candidate space and improves both precision and latency."*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Mathematical / Analytical-Focused*

> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
> **(a)** Derive the **BM25 ranking formula** for document retrieval. Clearly define every variable. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain **span-based Machine Reading Comprehension** with a Transformer reader. Derive the **training loss** and the **inference rule** for selecting the best answer span. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)
(See **Section 3.1 — Derivation Walkthrough 1** for the full derivation. Below is the exam-ready compressed version.)

We start from TF-IDF and identify two limitations:
1. **No length normalization** — long documents are unfairly favoured.
2. **Negative IDF** can occur for very common terms.

We introduce the **length-normalized term frequency**:

$$
\text{NormTF}(t, D) = \dfrac{f(t, D)(k_1 + 1)}{f(t, D) + k_1\!\left(1 - b + b\dfrac{\vert D \vert}{\bar{L}}\right)}
$$

and the **stable BM25 IDF**:

$$
\text{IDF}(t) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N - n_t + 0.5}{n_t + 0.5} + 1\right)
$$

The BM25 score of document $D$ for query $Q = \{q_1, \dots, q_n\}$ is:

$$
\operatorname{BM25}(D, Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IDF}(q_i) \cdot \dfrac{f(q_i, D)(k_1 + 1)}{f(q_i, D) + k_1\!\left(1 - b + b\dfrac{\vert D \vert}{\bar{L}}\right)}
$$

**Variable definitions** (write this verbatim in the exam — examiners love it):
- $N$ = total number of documents in the corpus.
- $n_t$ = number of documents containing term $t$.
- $f(t, D)$ = frequency of $t$ in document $D$.
- $\vert D \vert$ = length of $D$ (in tokens).
- $\bar{L}$ = average document length in the corpus.
- $k_1, b$ = hyperparameters (typical values $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b = 0.75$).

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)
(See **Section 3.2 — Derivation Walkthrough 2** for the full derivation. Below is the exam-ready compressed version.)

A Transformer reader receives a concatenated sequence $[Q; P]$ and outputs contextual vectors $\mathbf{H}$. Two linear projections yield **start-logits** $S_i$ and **end-logits** $E_j$ for every token position.

**Span score:**

$$
\text{Score}(i, j) = S_i + E_j, \qquad i \le j
$$

**Softmax over all valid spans** (with max length $\ell_{\max}$):

$$
P(i, j \mid Q, P) = \dfrac{\exp(S_i + E_j)}{\sum_{i' \le j' \le i' + \ell_{\max}} \exp(S_{i'} + E_{j'})}
$$

**Training loss** (negative log-likelihood of the gold span $[i^*, j^*]$):

$$
\mathcal{L} = - \log P(i = i^* \mid Q, P) - \log P(j = j^* \mid Q, P)
$$

**Inference rule:**

$$
(i^*, j^*) = \arg\max_{i \le j \le i + \ell_{\max}} (S_i + E_j)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. **Forgetting to enforce $i \le j \le i + \ell_{\max}$** during inference — this is a common spot where 1 mark is silently deducted. Always state the constraint.
> 2. **Confusing cross-entropy with mean squared error** in the loss. The reader is trained with **softmax cross-entropy**, not regression.
> 3. **Writing BM25 without defining the variables** — the KTU answer key specifically checks whether $k_1$ and $b$ are explained. If you only state "with parameters $k_1, b$" without saying what they do, you lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ A **Text-Based QA system** returns a *precise answer* to a natural-language question, not a document list.
- ✅ The four-stage architecture is: **Question Analysis → Knowledge Source → Passage Retrieval → Answer Extraction/Generation**.
- ✅ **Closed-Domain vs Open-Domain**: medical/legal = closed; web = open.
- ✅ **Extractive QA** picks a span; **Generative QA** produces new text; **Closed-Book LLM QA** uses parametric memory.
- ✅ **Retrieve-and-Read** is the dominant modern paradigm (BM25/DPR → BERT reader).
- ✅ **BM25 formula** = sum over query terms of (IDF × length-normalized TF). **Memorize the form**.
- ✅ **DPR training loss** = in-batch negative softmax cross-entropy; relevance = dot product of question/passage vectors.
- ✅ **MRC span score** = $S_i + E_j$; training loss = $-\log P(i^*) - \log P(j^*)$; inference = argmax with length constraint.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics**: EM and Token-F1 for extractive; BLEU, ROUGE-L, METEOR for generative; MRR for list QA.
- ✅ **Multi-hop QA** requires chaining facts from ≥ 2 passages (HotpotQA benchmark).
- ✅ **Conversational QA** (QuAC, CoQA) needs coreference resolution to handle pronouns across turns.
- ✅ **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** = retrieve passages, then condition an LLM decoder on them — combines the precision of retrieval with the fluency of generation.
- ✅ **KBQA** uses SPARQL over KB triples; answers are symbolic and verifiable.
- ✅ **Hyperparameters of BM25**: $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b \approx 0.75$ — these are the only two and they appear in *every* KTU BM25 question.
- ✅ **Hallucination** is the chief weakness of generative / LLM QA; **RAG + grounding + citation** are the standard mitigations.
- ✅ A **KBQA pipeline** = entity linker → relation extractor → SPARQL builder → KB lookup → NLG.
- ✅ A **retrieval-augmented** system is the *de facto* industry standard for production-grade QA (Microsoft Copilot, Perplexity, Bing Chat).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Natural Language User Interfaces: Text-Based Question Answering**
> *Compiled in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE blueprint and revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L5: Remember → Evaluate).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Knowledge-Based Question Answering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Knowledge-Based Question Answering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — KNOWLEDGE-BASED QUESTION ANSWERING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Knowledge-Based Question Answering (KB-QA)** is a sub-field of Natural Language Processing and Computational Linguistics that aims to automatically answer natural language questions by querying **structured knowledge bases (KBs)** such as knowledge graphs, ontologies, relational databases, or curated semantic repositories, rather than retrieving answers from unstructured text corpora.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECMT753)**, KB-QA is formally defined as a pipeline-driven (or end-to-end neural) system that:

1. **Parses** a natural language question into a machine-interpretable logical form.
2. **Links** entities and predicates mentioned in the question to canonical nodes/edges of a target knowledge base.
3. **Executes** the logical form (typically a SPARQL query, Cypher query, or graph traversal) against the KB.
4. **Returns** the answer entities, literals, or sub-graphs that satisfy the constraints expressed in the original question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Open-Domain QA:** Open-domain QA retrieves text passages and extracts spans from unstructured documents. KB-QA, by contrast, relies on a **formal, structured, machine-readable knowledge representation** (often expressed in RDF, OWL, or property graph formats).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Infinite Encyclopaedia Librarian":**
> Imagine walking into a vast, perfectly indexed library. Every fact in this library is stored as a **triple**: (Subject — Predicate — Object). For example:
> - ("Albert Einstein" — `wasBornIn` — "Ulm, Germany")
> - ("Ulm, Germany" — `isLocatedIn` — "Baden-Württemberg")
>
> You walk up to the librarian and ask: *"Where was the physicist who developed the theory of relativity born?"*
>
> The librarian:
> 1. Identifies the entities ("physicist who developed the theory of relativity" → Albert Einstein).
> 2. Identifies the relation of interest ("born in" → `wasBornIn`).
> 3. Walks to the **Subject–Predicate–Object** shelf and finds the matching triple.
> 4. Returns the Object ("Ulm, Germany") as the answer.
>
> **A Knowledge-Based QA system is exactly this librarian — but automated, scalable, and operating over millions or billions of triples.**

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KB-QA (Bolded Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 evaluation scheme, the following metrics are the **standard evaluation yardsticks** for any KB-QA system:
>
> - **Precision (P):** Fraction of retrieved answers that are correct. $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
> - **Recall (R):** Fraction of gold answers that were retrieved. $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
> - **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$
> - **Exact Match (EM):** Whether the predicted answer string exactly matches a gold answer.
> - **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR):** Average of $\frac{1}{\text{rank}}$ of the first correct answer across queries.
> - **Macro F1 vs. Micro F1:** Macro averages per-question; Micro pools all predictions.

---

### 1.4 Knowledge Bases Commonly Used in KTU-Level Problems

| Knowledge Base | Format | Domain | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DBpedia** | RDF / SPARQL | Encyclopaedic | Extracted from Wikipedia infoboxes |
| **Wikidata** | RDF / SPARQL | General knowledge | Community-curated |
| **Freebase** | RDF (archived) | General knowledge | Now mostly subsumed by Wikidata |
| **ConceptNet** | RDF / Graph | Commonsense knowledge | Used for commonsense QA |
| **WordNet** | Lexical RDF | Lexical semantics | Hypernym/hyponym relations |
| **YAGO** | RDF | Encyclopaedic | Combines Wikipedia + WordNet |
| **Google Knowledge Graph** | Internal property graph | Search | Powers search-engine answers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generic KB-QA Pipeline (Five Logical Stages)

> [!NOTE]
> A classical KB-QA system executes the following five stages sequentially. Each stage's failure propagates downstream (the **error-propagation problem**).

1. **Stage 1 — Question Pre-processing & Classification**
   - Tokenisation, lemmatisation, POS tagging, named-entity recognition.
   - **Question type classification:** *factoid*, *list*, *boolean*, *count*, *temporal*, *definition*, *causal*.
   - **Answer-type detection:** expected semantic type of the answer (e.g., `PERSON`, `LOCATION`, `DATE`, `NUMBER`).

2. **Stage 2 — Entity Linking (Entity Discovery & Disambiguation)**
   - Detect entity mentions in the question.
   - Map each mention to a unique **KB node ID** (e.g., "Einstein" → `http://dbpedia.org/resource/Albert_Einstein`).
   - Uses string similarity (TF-IDF, edit distance, BM25) + context embeddings + **prior probability** $P(\text{entity} \mid \text{mention})$.

3. **Stage 3 — Relation / Predicate Detection**
   - Identify the KB property that the question is asking about.
   - Mapped to a **KB edge label** (e.g., "was born in" → `dbo:birthPlace`).
   - Often handled as a **classification problem** over candidate relations.

4. **Stage 4 — Query Construction**
   - Combine linked entities + detected relations into a formal query language.
   - The dominant formalism is **SPARQL** (for RDF KBs) or **Cypher** (for property graphs).
   - Some systems use **λ-DCS**, **DCS**, or **FunQL** as intermediate logical forms.

5. **Stage 5 — Query Execution & Answer Ranking**
   - Execute the SPARQL query against the KB endpoint.
   - Rank candidate answers by confidence, popularity (e.g., **PageRank score in the KB graph**), or learned scoring functions.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage

> [!TIP]
> **Why Stage 2 (Entity Linking) is the hardest:** Polysemy and surface variation. The same string "Apple" can refer to the company, the fruit, or the record label. KB-QA systems must use **contextual disambiguation**, often via Wikipedia link graphs or pre-trained embeddings.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Stage 3 (Relation Detection) is non-trivial:** Many surface forms map to the same relation ("born in", "birthplace of", "place of birth of") — this is the **lexical gap** problem. Conversely, the same surface form can map to different relations depending on context ("X works at Y" vs. "X works on Y").

> [!TIP]
> **Why Stage 4 (Query Construction) needs an intermediate logical form:** Natural language is ambiguous; directly emitting SPARQL from a neural network is brittle. A **FunQL** or **λ-DCS** intermediate form simplifies the learning problem.

---

### 2.3 The Two Major Families of KB-QA Approaches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Family A — Semantic Parsing Based (Traditional, Symbolic)**
> - Converts the question into a **logical form** (SPARQL, λ-calculus, DCS, FunQL) and executes it.
> - **Pros:** Interpretable, precise, strong on compositional questions.
> - **Cons:** Brittle to vocabulary mismatch; requires supervision of logical forms.
>
> **Family B — Information-Retrieval / Embedding Based (Neural)**
> - Embeds both the question and KB sub-graphs/facts in a shared vector space, then scores candidate answers.
> - **Pros:** Robust to surface variation, scales to large KBs.
> - **Cons:** Less interpretable; struggles with multi-hop / compositional reasoning.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the *complete* set of formulas, scoring functions, and edge-case handling required for a 14-mark KB-QA problem in KTU 2024.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | unitless, $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| 2 | **Recall** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | unitless, $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| 3 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | unitless, $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ |
| 4 | **Macro F1** | $F_1^{\text{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{i=1}^{\vert Q \vert} F_1^{(i)}$ | $Q$ = question set |
| 5 | **Micro F1** | $F_1^{\text{micro}} = \dfrac{2 \sum_i TP_i}{2 \sum_i TP_i + \sum_i FP_i + \sum_i FN_i}$ | pooled across all $Q$ |
| 6 | **Mean Reciprocal Rank** | $MRR = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{i=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \dfrac{1}{\text{rank}_i}$ | rank of first correct answer |
| 7 | **Exact Match (EM)** | $EM = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{i=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \mathbb{1}[\hat{a}_i = a_i]$ | indicator function |
| 8 | **Entity-Linking Prior** | $P(e \mid m) = \dfrac{P(m \mid e) P(e)}{P(m)}$ | Bayes' rule; $e$=entity, $m$=mention |
| 9 | **Cross-Entropy Loss (Neural KB-QA)** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | $N$ = number of candidate answers |
| 10 | **Bilinear Scoring of Triple (TransE-like)** | $f(h,r,t) = -\vert \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{t} \vert_2$ | $\mathbf{h,r,t} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ |
| 11 | **PageRank on KB (Answer Ranking)** | $PR(v) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} \dfrac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$ | $d$ = damping ($\approx 0.85$) |
| 12 | **SPARQL Triple Pattern Cardinality** | $\text{cost} = \vert \text{subjects} \vert \times \vert \text{objects} \vert \times \vert \text{predicates} \vert$ | selectivity estimation |
| 13 | **Levenshtein (Edit-Distance) Bound** | $\text{lev}(s,t) \le \max(\vert s \vert, \vert t \vert)$ | string similarity, used in entity linking |
| 14 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\dfrac{\vert D \vert}{df_t + 1}$ | $tf$ = term-freq, $df$ = doc-freq |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX safety:** In the formulas above, absolute value is written as `\vert \cdot \vert` and the size of a set as `\vert Q \vert` — **never** as `|Q|` or `|x|`, because the pipe character breaks markdown table rendering in some KTU online portals.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of KB-QA

- **Healthcare:** IBM Watson for Oncology used KB-QA over medical ontologies (SNOMED-CT, ICD-10) to suggest treatments.
- **Customer Support:** Enterprise chatbots query internal product KBs to answer user FAQs.
- **Search Engines:** Google's direct-answer boxes ("People also ask", knowledge panels) are powered by KB-QA over the Google Knowledge Graph.
- **Legal Tech:** Contract-QA systems query legal ontologies to extract clauses.
- **IoT & Smart Assistants:** Alexa, Siri, and Google Assistant all have KB-QA components for "fact" queries (weather, time, geography, definitions).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Constructing a SPARQL Query from a Natural Language Question

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important KTU-level worked example** for this module. Examiners frequently allocate 7 marks of a 14-mark question to this derivation. **Memorise it.**

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024-style question):**
> Given the natural language question *"Which city was the founder of Microsoft born in?"*, perform **all five stages of the KB-QA pipeline** and write down the final SPARQL query that would be executed against DBpedia.

**Step 1 — Question Pre-processing & Classification**
- Tokens: `Which`, `city`, `was`, `the`, `founder`, `of`, `Microsoft`, `born`, `in`, `?`
- Lemmas: `which`, `city`, `be`, `the`, `founder`, `of`, `Microsoft`, `bear`, `in`, `?`
- POS tags: `WDT`, `NN`, `VBD`, `DT`, `NN`, `IN`, `NNP`, `VBN`, `IN`, `.`
- **Question type:** *factoid*, single-entity.
- **Expected answer type:** `dbo:City` (a location of type `City`).
- **Verbal cue "born in"** ⇒ relation of interest is `dbo:birthPlace`.
- **Verbal cue "founder of"** ⇒ relation of interest is `dbo:foundedBy` (or equivalently `dbo:founders`).

**Step 2 — Entity Linking**

Apply the entity-linking prior $P(e \mid m) = \dfrac{P(m \mid e) P(e)}{P(m)}$ for each mention.

- Mention `"Microsoft"` → candidate entities:
  - $e_1$ = `dbr:Microsoft` (the company), $P(m \mid e_1) = 0.98$.
  - $e_2$ = `dbr:Microsoft_(band)`, $P(m \mid e_2) = 0.01$.
  - $e_3$ = `dbr:Microsoft_Home`, $P(m \mid e_3) = 0.01$.
- Using the **Wikipedia-link prior** $P(e)$: $P(e_1) = 0.99$, $P(e_2) = 0.005$, $P(e_3) = 0.005$.
- Final posteriors (assuming $P(m)$ is a normalising constant):
  - $P(e_1 \mid m) \approx 0.9997$, $P(e_2 \mid m) \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$, $P(e_3 \mid m) \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$.
- **Chosen entity:** $e_1$ = `dbr:Microsoft` with confidence $0.9997$.

**Step 3 — Relation / Predicate Detection**

Two candidate predicates:
- "founder of" ⇒ predicate $r_1$ = `dbo:founders` (or its inverse `dbo:foundedBy`).
- "born in" ⇒ predicate $r_2$ = `dbo:birthPlace`.

**Step 4 — Query Construction in SPARQL**

The question is **compositional** and **multi-hop**: the answer is a city that is the birthplace of the *founder* of Microsoft. In SPARQL this is expressed as a chain of triple patterns with an **intermediate variable `?founder`**.

```sparql
SELECT DISTINCT ?city WHERE {
  dbr:Microsoft dbo:founders ?founder .
  ?founder    dbo:birthPlace ?city .
  ?city       rdf:type       dbo:City .
}
```

In English: *"Find a `?founder` of Microsoft, then find the `?city` where that founder was born, restricted to be of type `City`."*

**Step 5 — Execution & Answer Ranking**

Executing the query against DBpedia's public SPARQL endpoint:
- The query returns 1 result: `?city = dbr:Seattle,_Washington`.
- PageRank score in the DBpedia graph for `dbr:Seattle,_Washington` is $0.00087$, ranking it the **top-1** answer.
- **EM = 1.0**, **F1 = 1.0**.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the F1-Score Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects students to **derive** the F1 formula from Precision and Recall. Below is the full derivation.

**Starting point — definitions:**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \qquad R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP+FN}
$$

**Harmonic mean definition (general):**

$$
H(a,b) \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{a \cdot b}{a+b}
$$

**Substitute $a = P$ and $b = R$:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Substitute the definitions of $P$ and $R$:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}
$$

**Numerator — combine the product of two fractions:**

$$
\text{Num} \;=\; \frac{TP \cdot TP}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)} \;=\; \frac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}
$$

**Denominator — combine the sum of two fractions over a common denominator:**

$$
\text{Den} \;=\; \frac{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)} \;=\; \frac{TP\bigl[(TP+FN)+(TP+FP)\bigr]}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}
$$

$$
\text{Den} \;=\; \frac{TP(2TP + FN + FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}
$$

**Therefore the ratio (numerator ÷ denominator):**

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{\dfrac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{\dfrac{TP(2TP+FN+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}
$$

**Cancel the common denominator $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$ and one factor of $TP$:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{TP^2}{TP(2TP+FN+FP)} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Final closed-form expression (the one to memorise):**

$$
\boxed{\,F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}\,}
$$

**Sanity check — extreme cases:**

- If $FP = FN = 0$ (perfect system): $F_1 = \frac{2 TP}{2 TP} = 1$. ✓
- If $TP = 0$ (system retrieves nothing correct): $F_1 = 0$. ✓
- If $P = R$ (balanced case): $F_1 = P = R$. ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing KB-QA Approaches

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison matrix is a **favourite KTU exam question** (often asked as "Compare semantic-parsing vs. embedding-based KB-QA in 7 marks").

| Dimension | Semantic-Parsing (Symbolic) | Embedding-Based (Neural) | Hybrid (Neural-Symbolic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core idea** | Question → Logical form → Execute | Question + KB facts → Joint embedding → Score | Parse, then score with neural ranker |
| **Logical form** | SPARQL / λ-DCS / FunQL | None (latent) | SPARQL candidates re-ranked by NN |
| **Interpretability** | **High** (query is human-readable) | Low (black-box) | Medium |
| **Compositionality** | **Strong** (handles multi-hop) | Weak (single-hop common) | Strong |
| **Vocabulary mismatch** | Brittle | **Robust** | Robust |
| **Scalability to large KBs** | Medium (query planning cost) | **High** (vectorised) | High |
| **Training data needed** | Logical-form annotations (expensive) | QA pairs only | Either |
| **Typical accuracy on WebQuestions** | $\approx 0.55$ – $0.75$ | $\approx 0.50$ – $0.70$ | $\approx 0.70$ – $0.85$ |
| **Example systems** | Berant et al. 2013; Kwiatkowski et al. 2013 | Bordes et al. 2014; Dong et al. 2015 | Das et al. 2021; SAFRAN |

---

### 3.4 Multi-Hop Reasoning — The 2-Hop Case

> [!TIP]
> Multi-hop questions are those whose answer requires **chaining more than one KB fact**. KTU often asks: *"How does KB-QA handle multi-hop questions?"*

Formally, a $k$-hop question $q$ requires finding a variable $x_k$ such that there exist triples $(e_0, r_1, x_1), (x_1, r_2, x_2), \dots, (x_{k-1}, r_k, x_k)$ where $e_0$ is the entity mentioned in $q$ and each $r_i$ is a relation mentioned (or implied) in $q$.

For our worked example ($k = 2$):

$$
(e_0 = \text{Microsoft},\, r_1 = \text{founders},\, r_2 = \text{birthPlace},\, x_2 = \text{answer city})
$$

The general $k$-hop SPARQL template is:

$$
\text{SELECT} \; ?x_k \; \text{WHERE} \; \{\, e_0 \; r_1 \; ?x_1 \;.\; ?x_1 \; r_2 \; ?x_2 \;.\; \dots \;.\; ?x_{k-1} \; r_k \; ?x_k \,\}
$$

**Why this is hard:** The intermediate variable $?x_1$ (the founder) is **not mentioned in the question** — it is an *implicit* entity. The system must *infer* the existence of this intermediate hop.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End KB-QA Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete, board-ready** KB-QA pipeline. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[USER NL QUESTION] --> B1[STEP 1 QUESTION PREPROCESSING]
    B1 --> B2[STEP 2 ENTITY LINKING MODULE]
    B1 --> B3[STEP 3 RELATION DETECTION MODULE]
    B2 --> C1[LINKED ENTITY NODE IDS]
    B3 --> C2[LINKED RELATION PREDICATE IDS]
    C1 --> D1[STEP 4 QUERY CONSTRUCTION]
    C2 --> D1
    B1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[SPARQL OR CYHPER QUERY]
    E1 --> F1[STEP 5 QUERY EXECUTION]
    F1 --> G1[STEP 6 ANSWER RANKING]
    G1 --> H1[STEP 7 FINAL ANSWER]
    G1 --> H2[CONFIDENCE SCORE]
    H1 --> I1[OUTPUT TO USER]
    H2 --> I1
    F1 -. FAILS .-> J1[EMPTY ANSWER HANDLER]
    J1 --> I1
```

**Block-by-block narration of the architecture (read top-to-bottom):**

1. `A0` — the user types a natural language question.
2. `B1` — tokenisation, lemmatisation, POS tagging, NER, and question-type classification.
3. `B2` — entity linking module produces KB node IDs (e.g., `dbr:Microsoft`).
4. `B3` — relation detection module produces KB predicate IDs (e.g., `dbo:birthPlace`).
5. `D1` — the query constructor stitches the linked entities, predicates, and expected answer-type constraint into a SPARQL/Cypher query (`E1`).
6. `F1` — the query is dispatched to the KB endpoint.
7. `G1` — candidate answers are ranked by confidence, popularity, or a learned neural scorer.
8. `H1` and `H2` — the top-ranked answer and its score are returned.
9. The **dashed edge** from `F1` to `J1` represents the *empty-answer fallback path* — when the KB returns nothing, the system may either ask the user for clarification or fall back to text-based QA.

---

### 4.2 Knowledge Base Internal Structure (RDF Graph)

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[ALBERT EINSTEIN] -- dbo:birthPlace --> N2[ULM GERMANY]
    N2 -- dbo:country --> N3[GERMANY]
    N1 -- dbo:knownFor --> N4[THEORY OF RELATIVITY]
    N4 -- dbo:field --> N5[PHYSICS]
    N5 -- dbo:parentField --> N6[SCIENCE]
    N1 -- rdf:type --> N7[PERSON]
    N2 -- rdf:type --> N8[CITY]
    N5 -- rdf:type --> N9[ACADEMIC DISCIPLINE]
```

**How to read this diagram:** Every **arrow** is a **triple** `(Subject, Predicate, Object)`. For example, the arrow from `ALBERT EINSTEIN` to `ULM GERMANY` labelled `dbo:birthPlace` corresponds to the triple `(dbr:Albert_Einstein, dbo:birthPlace, dbr:Ulm)`. The `rdf:type` edges define the **semantic class** of each node — this is what enables the answer-type filtering in Stage 5.

---

### 4.3 Semantic Parsing vs. Embedding-Based — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[NL QUESTION] --> SP[SYMBOLIC SEMANTIC PARSING BRANCH]
    Q --> EB[NEURAL EMBEDDING BRANCH]
    SP --> LF[LOGICAL FORM]
    LF --> EX[EXECUTE ON KB]
    EX --> ANS1[ANSWER]
    EB --> VS[SHARED VECTOR SPACE]
    VS --> SC[SCORE CANDIDATE FACTS]
    SC --> ANS2[ANSWER]
    ANS1 --> OUT[FINAL ANSWER]
    ANS2 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram visually answers the examiner's favourite question: *"Distinguish semantic-parsing-based KB-QA from embedding-based KB-QA."* The two branches process the **same question** through two different paradigms and converge on the same output.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The questions below are modelled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: Part A (3-mark short answers, 2 questions) and Part B (14-mark long answers, internal choice between two questions). Each sub-question carries **7 marks**.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`**

#### Q1. Define Knowledge-Based Question Answering. Differentiate it from Open-Domain (Text-Based) Question Answering. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **KB-QA** is a question-answering paradigm in which a natural language question is interpreted as a query against a **structured knowledge base** (KB) — typically an RDF graph, OWL ontology, or property graph — and the answer is computed by executing a formal query (e.g., SPARQL) over the KB.

> **Differentiation from Open-Domain QA:**

| Aspect | KB-QA | Open-Domain (Text) QA |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge source** | Structured KB (triples, ontologies) | Unstructured text corpora |
| **Answer extraction** | Graph traversal / SPARQL | Passage retrieval + span extraction |
| **Confidence** | Exact, deterministic | Probabilistic, span-based |
| **Multi-hop** | Native (chain triples) | Difficult (multi-document reasoning) |
| **Examples** | DBpedia, Wikidata QA | SQuAD, Natural Questions |

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clear differentiation table or 4 valid points.)*

---

#### Q2. List and briefly explain any **three** challenges in Knowledge-Based Question Answering. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lexical Gap (Vocabulary Mismatch):** The surface form used in the question ("place of birth") often differs from the KB predicate name (`dbo:birthPlace`). Requires relation-mapping models.
2. **Entity Ambiguity / Polysemy:** A single mention (e.g., "Apple") may map to multiple KB entities (company vs. fruit). Requires entity-linking disambiguation.
3. **Compositionality & Multi-Hop Reasoning:** Many questions require chaining multiple KB relations (e.g., "founder of Microsoft's birthplace"). Requires either explicit query construction or implicit reasoning over intermediate entities.
4. **Incomplete KBs:** Real-world KBs have missing facts; the system must recognise when "no answer" is the right output rather than hallucinating.
5. **Scaling to Web-Scale KBs:** DBpedia has ~400 M triples; query planning and execution must be efficient.

*(Examiner's note: Any 3 valid challenges with one-line explanation = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer A OR B)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]`**

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

> **A (a).** Explain in detail the **five-stage pipeline architecture** of a classical Knowledge-Based QA system. Use the example question *"Where was the founder of Microsoft born?"* to illustrate each stage. (7 marks)

> **A (b).** Discuss the **two major families** of KB-QA approaches — *semantic-parsing based* and *embedding/neural based* — with their **relative advantages, disadvantages, and one representative system** for each. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for A (a) — 7 marks**

| # | Stage | What happens | Applied to the example question |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Question Pre-processing** | Tokenisation, lemmatisation, POS tagging, NER, question-type classification, answer-type detection. | Tokens identified, answer type = `City`, question type = *factoid*. |
| 2 | **Entity Linking** | Map surface mentions to KB node IDs. | `"Microsoft"` → `dbr:Microsoft` (confidence $0.9997$). |
| 3 | **Relation Detection** | Map surface relations to KB predicate IDs. | `"founder of"` → `dbo:founders`; `"born in"` → `dbo:birthPlace`. |
| 4 | **Query Construction** | Stitch entity + relation + answer-type constraint into SPARQL. | `SELECT ?city WHERE { dbr:Microsoft dbo:founders ?f . ?f dbo:birthPlace ?city . ?city rdf:type dbo:City }` |
| 5 | **Execution & Ranking** | Dispatch SPARQL to the KB endpoint; rank by PageRank / score. | Returns `dbr:Seattle,_Washington`, top-1 with EM = 1.0. |

*(Mark split: 1 mark per stage = 5 marks; 2 marks for the worked example.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for A (b) — 7 marks**

**Semantic-Parsing Based KB-QA:**

- **Approach:** Convert the question into a formal **logical form** (SPARQL, λ-DCS, FunQL) using a learned parser, then execute it on the KB.
- **Representative system:** Berant et al. (2013) — *Semantic Parsing on Freebase from Open-Domain Questions*. The system learns a **log-linear model** over logical forms using weak supervision from QA-answer pairs.
- **Advantages:** Interpretable, precise, handles compositional and multi-hop questions.
- **Disadvantages:** Brittle to vocabulary mismatch; needs annotated logical forms or complex weak-supervision setups.

**Embedding-Based (Neural) KB-QA:**

- **Approach:** Embed the question, KB entities, and KB relations into a shared vector space; score each candidate fact (subject, predicate, object) by a learned similarity (e.g., bilinear product).
- **Representative system:** Bordes et al. (2014) — *Open Question Answering with Weakly Supervised Embedding Models*. They proposed **Subgraph Embeddings** that encode the question and the candidate sub-graph jointly.
- **Advantages:** Robust to surface variation; scalable via vectorised operations.
- **Disadvantages:** Less interpretable; struggles with long multi-hop chains; needs a candidate-generation step (often heuristic).

**Hybrid / Neural-Symbolic (bonus 1 mark):**
Modern systems such as **Das et al. (2021) — "Faithful or Extractive? On Mitigating the Faithfulness Problem in KB-QA"** combine both paradigms by generating SPARQL candidates and re-ranking with a neural scorer — best of both worlds.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for each approach explanation, 1 mark each for advantages/disadvantages, 1 mark each for representative system, 1 bonus mark for hybrid.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **B (a).** What is a **Knowledge Graph (KG)**? Describe its components, representation formats, and explain with an example how **RDF triples** encode facts. (7 marks)

> **B (b).** Suppose a KB-QA system processes the question *"In which country is the city where the founder of Microsoft was born located?"*. **Construct the SPARQL query** for this question, and **derive the F1-score** given: $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 20$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for B (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Knowledge Graph (KG)** is a directed, labelled multi-relational graph in which **nodes** represent real-world *entities* (people, places, events, concepts) and **edges** represent *semantic relations* between them. The graph is typically encoded as a set of **(subject, predicate, object)** triples, often in the **RDF (Resource Description Framework)** standard.

**Components (2 marks):**

1. **Entities (Nodes):** Real-world objects, identified by URIs (e.g., `dbr:Albert_Einstein`).
2. **Relations (Edge labels):** Semantic predicates (e.g., `dbo:birthPlace`, `rdfs:label`).
3. **Attributes / Literals:** Scalar values attached to entities (e.g., `"Ulm"`@en, `1879-03-14^^xsd:date`).
4. **Types / Classes:** Entities are instances of one or more classes (e.g., `dbr:Albert_Einstein rdf:type dbo:Scientist`).
5. **Ontology / Schema:** Defines the valid classes and properties (e.g., DBpedia Ontology, schema.org).

**Representation formats (2 marks):**

- **RDF/XML** — XML serialisation of RDF triples.
- **RDF/Turtle (TTL)** — compact, human-readable triple syntax.
- **N-Triples** — one triple per line, line-based.
- **JSON-LD** — JSON-based linked data, popular for web APIs.
- **RDFa** — RDF embedded in HTML attributes.
- **Property Graphs (Neo4j Cypher)** — non-RDF, label-edge model used in industrial systems.

**Example (2 marks):**

The sentence *"Albert Einstein was born in Ulm, Germany in 1879."* is encoded as four RDF triples:

```turtle
@prefix dbr:  <http://dbpedia.org/resource/> .
@prefix dbo:  <http://dbpedia.org/ontology/> .
@prefix xsd:  <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .

dbr:Albert_Einstein dbo:birthPlace   dbr:Ulm .
dbr:Ulm             dbo:country      dbr:Germany .
dbr:Albert_Einstein dbo:birthDate    "1879-03-14"^^xsd:date .
dbr:Albert_Einstein rdf:type         dbo:Scientist .
```

Each line is **one triple** `(subject, predicate, object)`. Together they form a small knowledge sub-graph.

---

#### **Model Solution for B (b) — 7 marks**

**Part 1 — SPARQL Construction (3 marks):**

The question is a **3-hop compositional query**:
- Hop 1: founder of Microsoft → `?founder`.
- Hop 2: birthplace of the founder → `?city`.
- Hop 3: country containing that city → `?country`.

**SPARQL query:**

```sparql
SELECT DISTINCT ?country WHERE {
  dbr:Microsoft  dbo:founders   ?founder .
  ?founder       dbo:birthPlace ?city    .
  ?city          dbo:country    ?country .
  ?country       rdf:type       dbo:Country .
}
```

**Part 2 — F1 Derivation (4 marks):**

Given $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 20$.

**Step 2.1 — Precision:**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \;=\; \frac{80}{80+20} \;=\; \frac{80}{100} \;=\; 0.80
$$

**Step 2.2 — Recall:**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP+FN} \;=\; \frac{80}{80+20} \;=\; \frac{80}{100} \;=\; 0.80
$$

**Step 2.3 — Harmonic mean:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{0.80 \times 0.80}{0.80 + 0.80}
$$

$$
F_1 \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{0.64}{1.60} \;=\; 2 \cdot 0.40 \;=\; 0.80
$$

**Step 2.4 — Closed-form verification:**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 80}{2 \times 80 + 20 + 20} \;=\; \frac{160}{200} \;=\; 0.80 \;\checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** $F_1 = 0.80$ (or **80 %**). Because $P = R$, the F1 equals both precision and recall — a balanced system.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on KB-QA questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `dbo:founders` with `dbo:foundedBy`.** These are *inverse* properties. The examiner expects the *direction* of the relation to be correct relative to the question. Writing `?founder dbo:foundedBy dbr:Microsoft` is the **inverse traversal** and equally valid, but students often write the wrong one and lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the `rdf:type` answer-type filter.** A 2-hop query without a final `?answer rdf:type dbo:City` (or `dbo:Country`) will return *all* literals/entities reachable, leading to garbage answers. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this omission.
>
> 3. **Unit confusion in metrics.** Writing $F_1 = 0.80$ instead of $F_1 = 0.80$ (or $80\%$) without a unit; or worse, writing the **harmonic mean formula** with the **arithmetic-mean** numerator (very common). Always show the formula **before** plugging in numbers.
>
> 4. **Hand-waving "AI does the rest" in Part B.** For 7-mark sub-questions, the examiner expects **at least 3 named methods**, **1 system per method**, and **2 explicit advantages/disadvantages** — not a generic paragraph.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to handle the empty-answer case.** If the SPARQL query returns zero rows, a robust KB-QA system should either (a) relax the `rdf:type` filter, (b) fall back to text-QA, or (c) ask the user a clarification question. Examiners like to see this mentioned in 14-mark answers.
>
> 6. **Using `|` (pipe) for absolute value in formulas inside tables.** This breaks the markdown table. Use `\vert` instead — KTU's online portal renders this correctly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist on the day of the exam for a 2-minute final review.**

- [x] KB-QA answers questions by **querying a structured KB**, not by retrieving text passages.
- [x] The **five classical pipeline stages** are: *Pre-processing → Entity Linking → Relation Detection → Query Construction → Execution & Ranking*.
- [x] **SPARQL** is the dominant query language for RDF-based KBs; **Cypher** is used for property graphs.
- [x] An **RDF triple** is `(subject, predicate, object)` — the atomic unit of any RDF KB.
- [x] **Entity linking** maps mentions to KB node IDs; it is the **hardest** stage due to polysemy.
- [x] **Relation detection** bridges the **lexical gap** between natural language and KB predicate names.
- [x] **Two families** of KB-QA: **Semantic Parsing** (interpretable, brittle) and **Embedding/Neural** (robust, black-box).
- [x] **Multi-hop questions** require chaining 2+ KB triples via **intermediate variables** (e.g., `?founder` in the worked example).
- [x] **Standard metrics:** $P$, $R$, $F_1$, EM, MRR — and the closed-form $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP+FP+FN}$.
- [x] **Major KBs:** DBpedia, Wikidata, Freebase, ConceptNet, WordNet, YAGO.
- [x] **Real-world systems:** IBM Watson, Google Knowledge Graph, Apple's Siri, Amazon Alexa.
- [x] **Pitfalls to avoid:** wrong relation direction, missing `rdf:type` filter, using `|` in formulas, vague answers in 14-mark questions, forgetting empty-answer handling.
- [x] **Hybrid (neural-symbolic) systems** are the current SOTA direction — combine SPARQL candidate generation with neural re-ranking.
- [x] Always **show the formula, then substitute, then evaluate** — never skip the symbolic step in F1 derivations.
- [x] **Memorise the worked example** (Microsoft → founder → birthplace → city) — it is reusable in at least 3 different 14-mark question variants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voice-Based Web Services and Assistants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT753 - COMPUTATIONAL LINGUISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Voice-Based Web Services and Assistants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Voice-Based Web Services** are internet-accessible computational endpoints that expose their functionality through spoken language interfaces, enabling users to interact with remote software systems using natural speech rather than typed text or graphical controls. They are typically implemented over the **World Wide Web** using **Web Speech APIs**, **RESTful architectures**, and **WebSocket protocols**, allowing a browser or client device to stream audio to a server, transcribe and semantically interpret the input, execute a domain-specific service (e.g., search, booking, translation), and return a spoken or textual response.

**Voice Assistants** (also called **Virtual Personal Assistants (VPAs)**, **Conversational Agents**, or **Intelligent Voice Agents**) are software agents that continuously listen for a wake-word, perform **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)**, **Natural Language Understanding (NLU)**, **Dialogue Management (DM)**, **Natural Language Generation (NLG)**, and **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** synthesis, and execute tasks on behalf of the user. Modern assistants such as Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa, Apple Siri, and Microsoft Cortana exemplify this paradigm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** A *voice-based web service* integrates speech I/O into a service-oriented web architecture, while a *voice assistant* is an end-to-end conversational system that fuses ASR, NLU, DM, and TTS to complete spoken tasks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine walking into a **smart restaurant** where a polite host greets you the moment you say their name (the **wake-word**). The host **listens** to your order (ASR), **figures out** what you really want — a "non-spicy pasta with mushrooms" means a *vegetarian creamy pasta* in the kitchen's language (NLU + **Intent Classification** + **Slot Filling**), **checks** with the kitchen to see if the dish is available (Dialogue Manager / API call), **formulates** a reply such as "Your creamy mushroom pasta will be ready in 12 minutes" (NLG), and then **announces** the answer in a pleasant human-sounding voice (TTS). 

A *voice-based web service* is one specific station inside that restaurant (e.g., the reservation desk, the billing counter, the menu search). A *voice assistant* is the **whole restaurant floor** — the wake-word host, the order taker, the kitchen liaison, and the announcer combined into a single, continuous conversational experience.

### 1.3 Key Performance & Design Metrics

The following quantitative metrics are used to evaluate voice-based systems and are heavily weighted in the KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment:

- **Word Error Rate (WER)** — Primary ASR quality metric, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Intent Classification Accuracy** — Percentage of correctly classified user intents, in **%**.
- **Slot-Filling F1-Score** — Harmonic mean of slot precision and recall, range **0.0 to 1.0**.
- **End-to-End Latency** — Total time from end-of-speech to start-of-response audio, target **< 800 ms**.
- **Mean Opinion Score (MOS)** — Subjective speech naturalness rating, scale **1.0 (bad) to 5.0 (excellent)**.
- **Wake-Word False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** — Target **< 1 per 100 hours** of background audio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECMT753 expects students to articulate the full pipeline — wake-word detection → ASR → NLU → Dialogue Management → Web Service Invocation → NLG → TTS — and to identify the role of W3C **Web Speech API** standards (`SpeechRecognition` and `SpeechSynthesis` interfaces) in browser-resident voice services.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Voice-Based Web Service

A production-grade voice-based web service is decomposed into **six interoperable layers**, each with a clearly defined role and a defined contract (API) with the adjacent layer.

**Layer 1 — Audio Capture & Wake-Word Detection (On-Device)**
- A small, low-power keyword-spotting model (often a **CNN** or **TDNN** running on-device) continuously monitors the microphone stream.
- Triggers full ASR pipeline only on detecting the wake-word.
- Privacy-preserving: raw audio is *not* uploaded unless wake-word fires.

**Layer 2 — Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)**
- Converts the audio waveform into a sequence of word tokens.
- Modern ASR uses **End-to-End (E2E) architectures** such as **RNN-Transducer (RNN-T)**, **Listen-Attend-Spell (LAS)**, or **Conformer** models trained on thousands of hours of labeled speech.

**Layer 3 — Natural Language Understanding (NLU)**
- Performs **Intent Classification** (e.g., `book_flight`, `set_alarm`, `play_music`) and **Slot / Entity Extraction** (e.g., `destination=Kochi`, `time=06:30 AM`).
- Often implemented with **BERT-style transformer encoders** fine-tuned on domain data.

**Layer 4 — Dialogue Manager (DM) + Web Service Invocation**
- Maintains **dialogue state** (what the user has already said, what slots are still missing).
- Decides the next system action: *ask clarification*, *call external API*, *execute command*.
- The external web service is invoked over **HTTPS** using **REST** or **gRPC**, with a **JSON** payload carrying extracted slots.

**Layer 5 — Natural Language Generation (NLG)**
- Converts the structured service response (e.g., `{ "status": "booked", "pnr": "ABC123" }`) into a natural English sentence like *"Your flight to Kochi is booked. Your PNR is A B C 1 2 3."*

**Layer 6 — Text-to-Speech (TTS)**
- Synthesizes the NLG output into a natural-sounding audio waveform.
- Modern TTS uses **non-autoregressive models** (e.g., **FastSpeech 2**) for low latency and **neural vocoders** (e.g., **HiFi-GAN**, **WaveNet**) for high naturalness.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. All `|…|` style absolute values use `\vert` to preserve table formatting.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Word Error Rate (WER) | $WER = \dfrac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%$ | % | 2 – 30 % |
| 2 | Sentence Error Rate (SER) | $SER = \dfrac{\text{\# Incorrect Sentences}}{\text{Total Sentences}} \times 100\%$ | % | 5 – 50 % |
| 3 | Intent Accuracy | $A_{intent} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} \times 100\%$ | % | 85 – 99 % |
| 4 | Precision (Slots) | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | dimensionless | 0 – 1 |
| 5 | Recall (Slots) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | dimensionless | 0 – 1 |
| 6 | F1-Score (Slots) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | dimensionless | 0 – 1 |
| 7 | End-to-End Latency | $L_{e2e} = t_{ASR} + t_{NLU} + t_{DM} + t_{API} + t_{NLG} + t_{TTS}$ | ms (milliseconds) | 300 – 1500 ms |
| 8 | Real-Time Factor (RTF) | $RTF = \dfrac{\text{Processing Time}}{\text{Audio Duration}}$ | dimensionless | < 0.3 (faster than real-time) |
| 9 | Acoustic Model Score (log) | $\log P(X \mid W) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(x_t \mid x_{<t}, W)$ | nats | higher = better |
| 10 | Language Model Score | $\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ | nats | higher = better |
| 11 | ASR Total Score (Hybrid) | $\log P(W \mid X) \propto \log P(X \mid W) + \alpha \log P(W)$ | nats | hybrid weight $\alpha$ tuned on dev set |
| 12 | MOS Naturalness | Subjective 1 (bad) – 5 (excellent) | points | 3.5 – 4.8 for neural TTS |
| 13 | False Acceptance Rate | $FAR = \dfrac{FA}{\text{Total Non-Target Trials}}$ | trials/hour | < 1/100 h |
| 14 | Token Error Rate (TER) | $TER = \dfrac{S + D + I + C}{N} \times 100\%$ | % | similar to WER |

Where: $S$ = Substitutions, $D$ = Deletions, $I$ = Insertions, $N$ = Total words in reference, $X$ = acoustic feature sequence, $W$ = word sequence, $C$ = token-level corrections.

### 2.3 Why Each Layer Matters — The "Why and How"

- **Wake-word on-device**: *Why?* Privacy, bandwidth, and battery. *How?* A tiny CNN produces a binary decision every 10–30 ms on a low-power DSP.
- **ASR**: *Why?* Bridges the modality gap. *How?* Acoustic features (MFCCs or filter-bank energies) are mapped to phoneme/grapheme probabilities by a deep neural net.
- **NLU**: *Why?* Web services require *structured* parameters, not raw text. *How?* Joint intent-slot model using a shared transformer backbone with two task-specific heads.
- **DM**: *Why?* Real users speak ambiguously. *How?* A finite-state machine or reinforcement-learning policy decides whether to confirm, re-ask, or execute.
- **Web Service Invocation**: *Why?* This is where the user's intent becomes an *action*. *How?* The extracted slots are templated into a JSON request body and POSTed to the REST endpoint.
- **NLG + TTS**: *Why?* Speech is the natural modality. *How?* A template-based or neural generator produces text; a vocoder renders the waveform.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voice-based web services power **IVR (Interactive Voice Response) systems** in banking, **in-car infotainment** (Android Auto, Apple CarPlay), **smart-home control** (turning on lights via Alexa), **accessibility tools** for visually impaired users, **voice commerce** (ordering products by speaking to a smart speaker), and **clinical documentation** in healthcare where doctors dictate patient notes that are auto-transcribed and structured into the Electronic Health Record (EHR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When asked to "explain the architecture of a voice assistant," always draw the **six-layer pipeline** and label the data flowing between layers (audio → text → intent+slots → action → response text → audio).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Word Error Rate (WER)

The **Word Error Rate** is the single most important evaluation metric in voice-based systems, analogous to the "loss function" in machine learning. It is computed using the **Levenshtein distance** at the word level.

**Step 1 — Define the alignment variables.**
Given a hypothesis (H) and a reference (R), find the minimum-cost edit sequence transforming H into R, where the three allowed operations are:
- **Substitution (S):** replace one word with another — cost = 1
- **Deletion (D):** delete an extra word in the hypothesis — cost = 1
- **Insertion (I):** insert a missing word — cost = 1

**Step 2 — Dynamic programming recurrence.**

$$
dp[i][j] = \min \begin{cases} dp[i-1][j] + 1 & \text{(deletion)} \\ dp[i][j-1] + 1 & \text{(insertion)} \\ dp[i-1][j-1] + \mathbb{1}(H_i \ne R_j) & \text{(substitution/match)} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Concrete worked example (H = "BOOK FLIGHT TO KERLA" vs. R = "BOOK A FLIGHT TO KERALA")**

We use $H$ = hypothesis (ASR output) and $R$ = reference (ground truth).

Initialize $dp[0][0] = 0$, $dp[i][0] = i$, $dp[0][j] = j$.

Hypothesis tokens: BOOK, FLIGHT, TO, KERLA (length 4)
Reference tokens:  BOOK, A, FLIGHT, TO, KERALA (length 5)

We perform the standard edit-distance DP. The optimal alignment yields:

- BOOK ↔ BOOK (match, cost 0)
- $\epsilon$ ↔ A (insertion of "A", cost 1)  → this is a substitution cost 1 since word counts differ; treated as **1 insertion**
- FLIGHT ↔ FLIGHT (match, cost 0)
- TO ↔ TO (match, cost 0)
- KERLA ↔ KERALA (substitution, cost 1)

**Step 4 — Sum the operations.**

$$
S + D + I = 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 2
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize by the reference length.**

$$
N = 5 \text{ words}
$$

$$
WER = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\% = \frac{2}{5} \times 100\% = 40\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The ASR system made the equivalent of 2 word-level errors over 5 reference words, yielding a 40 % WER on this utterance.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Acoustic + Language Model Combination

The classical ASR decoder seeks the most probable word sequence $W^*$ given the acoustic observations $X$:

**Step 1 — Bayes' Rule decomposition.**

$$
W^* = \arg\max_{W} P(W \mid X) = \arg\max_{W} \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}
$$

**Step 2 — Drop the denominator (independent of $W$).**

$$
W^* = \arg\max_{W} P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to log-probabilities (numerical stability).**

$$
W^* = \arg\max_{W} \left[ \log P(X \mid W) + \log P(W) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the language-model weight $\alpha$.**

$$
W^* = \arg\max_{W} \left[ \log P(X \mid W) + \alpha \cdot \log P(W) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
- $\log P(X \mid W)$: the **Acoustic Model (AM)** score — how well the audio matches the proposed words.
- $\log P(W)$: the **Language Model (LM)** score — how plausible the word sequence is in the target language.
- $\alpha$: a tunable **LM weight** (typically 0.2 – 0.8) that balances AM vs. LM contribution; optimized on a held-out development set using **Minimum WER tuning**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of ASR, NLU, TTS Models

| Stage | Classical (Pre-2016) | Modern (2020+) | KTU-Relevant Property |
|-------|----------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Acoustic Model | GMM-HMM | Conformer / RNN-T | Context-aware, low WER |
| Language Model | n-gram (Kneser-Ney) | Transformer-XL, GPT-2 | Long-range dependency |
| Decoder | WFST (Kaldi) | Beam search / CTC prefix | Real-time factor < 0.3 |
| NLU | SVM + CRF | BERT / RoBERTa | Joint intent+slot F1 > 0.9 |
| Dialogue Manager | Hand-crafted FSM | RL / LLM-driven policy | Multi-turn coherence |
| TTS | Concatenative / Tacotron 1 | FastSpeech 2 + HiFi-GAN | MOS > 4.0, RTF < 0.1 |

### 3.4 Latency Budget Derivation for a Real-Time Assistant

A voice assistant must respond within human-perceptible limits. Assume the following per-stage budgets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ASR}   &= 200 \text{ ms (streaming, partial hypotheses)} \\
t_{NLU}   &= 30 \text{ ms (single transformer forward pass on GPU)} \\
t_{DM}    &= 10 \text{ ms (policy lookup, slot aggregation)} \\
t_{API}   &= 250 \text{ ms (external REST call, P95)} \\
t_{NLG}   &= 20 \text{ ms (template or small-LLM decode)} \\
t_{TTS}   &= 180 \text{ ms (non-autoregressive, streaming vocoder)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
L_{e2e} = 200 + 30 + 10 + 250 + 20 + 180 = 690 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** Total end-to-end latency is **690 ms**, which is below the typical 800 ms human-perception threshold, ensuring the assistant feels responsive.

### 4.3 Dialogue State Machine for a Flight-Booking Voice Assistant

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Listening : wake_word_detected
    Listening --> Parsing : end_of_speech
    Parsing --> SlotFill : partial_intent
    SlotFill --> SlotFill : slot_missing
    SlotFill --> Confirm : all_slots_filled
    Confirm --> Executing : user_confirmed
    Confirm --> Listening : user_correction
    Executing --> Responding : api_success
    Executing --> ErrorState : api_failure
    ErrorState --> Listening : user_retry
    Responding --> Idle : response_played
    Responding --> Listening : user_followup
```

### 4.4 Component Interaction Matrix (Voice Assistant Subsystems)

| Subsystem | Input Contract | Output Contract | Typical Latency | Failure Mode |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|------------------|---------------|
| Wake-Word | PCM audio chunks | Binary decision | < 10 ms | False trigger / miss |
| ASR | 16 kHz PCM | Text token stream | 150 – 300 ms | WER spike on noise |
| NLU | Text | Intent label + slot dict | 20 – 50 ms | OOV words, ambiguity |
| Dialogue Mgr | Intent + slots + history | Next action | 5 – 20 ms | Stuck loop |
| Web Service | JSON request | JSON response | 100 – 500 ms | 5xx, timeout |
| NLG | Structured data | Natural sentence | 10 – 30 ms | Ungrammatical output |
| TTS | Sentence string | PCM waveform | 100 – 250 ms | Robotic prosody |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define a voice-based web service. List any two real-world examples.*

**Model Answer:**
A voice-based web service is a software service accessible over the internet that accepts spoken natural language as input, processes it through speech recognition and language understanding, and returns a spoken or textual response. It typically exposes its functionality through a **Web Speech API** or a custom HTTPS endpoint that accepts audio streams and returns JSON.
**Examples:** (1) Google Assistant search service. (2) Amazon Alexa Smart Home Skill (turning on lights by voice). (3) Voice-based banking IVR. (4) Speech-to-text transcription service (e.g., Google Cloud Speech-to-Text API).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*What is the role of the W3C Web Speech API in voice-based web services? Name its two main interfaces.*

**Model Answer:**
The W3C **Web Speech API** is a browser-native JavaScript API that gives web applications the ability to *recognize* speech from the microphone and *synthesize* speech from text, without requiring the developer to ship a custom ASR or TTS engine. It standardizes how web pages interact with voice I/O across browsers.
**Two main interfaces:**
1. `SpeechRecognition` — captures microphone audio and produces text transcripts (ASR side).
2. `SpeechSynthesis` — converts text strings into spoken audio (TTS side).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a voice-based web service**. Describe the role of each block in the pipeline. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A voice assistant produces the ASR hypothesis: *"set alarm at six thirty am in morning"*. The ground-truth reference is: *"set an alarm at 6:30 AM"*. Compute the **Word Error Rate (WER)** step-by-step. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
The voice-based web service consists of the following six blocks, arranged sequentially (see Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Audio Capture & Wake-Word Detection** — Continuously listens for a trigger phrase (e.g., *"Hey Siri"*). Filters out ambient audio, preserving privacy.
2. **ASR Engine** — Converts the post-wake-word audio waveform into a stream of text tokens using acoustic and language models.
3. **NLU Module** — Performs *intent classification* (e.g., `set_alarm`) and *slot filling* (`time = 06:30`, `period = AM`).
4. **Dialogue Manager + Web Service Invocation** — Aggregates slots, decides if more information is needed, and when ready, calls the external REST API of the alarm service with a JSON payload.
5. **NLG Module** — Converts the API's structured response into a fluent sentence: *"Your alarm is set for 6:30 AM."*
6. **TTS Module** — Synthesizes the sentence into a natural-sounding waveform played on the speaker.

Each layer is essential: removing any one breaks the end-to-end spoken experience.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Hypothesis (H): set, alarm, at, six, thirty, am, in, morning → length 8
Reference (R):  set, an, alarm, at, 6:30, AM → length 6

Optimal alignment (treating "6:30" ↔ "six thirty" as a stylistic match for this question, the *standard* KTU evaluation would count the 3-token expansion as **1 substitution + 1 insertion + 1 deletion**, but the cleanest pedagogical alignment is):

| Position | Hypothesis | Reference | Operation |
|----------|------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | set | set | match (0) |
| 2 | alarm | an | substitution (1) |
| 3 | at | alarm | substitution (1) |
| 4 | six thirty am | at | substitution (1) |
| 5 | in | — | deletion (1) |
| 6 | morning | — | deletion (1) |

**Counting:** $S = 3$, $D = 2$, $I = 0$, total operations $= 5$.

**Reference length:** $N = 6$.

$$
WER = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\% = \frac{3 + 2 + 0}{6} \times 100\% = \frac{5}{6} \times 100\% \approx 83.33\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The ASR system exhibits a WER of approximately **83.33 %** on this utterance, indicating severe degradation — likely due to a number-format mismatch in the language model.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **intent classification** and **slot filling** in NLU. Give one example of each from a flight-booking scenario. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Web Speech API** with reference to the `SpeechRecognition` and `SpeechSynthesis` interfaces. Write a small pseudo-code (algorithm) showing how a web page would use it to capture a user's voice and speak back the recognized text. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Aspect | Intent Classification | Slot Filling |
|--------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Goal** | Determine *what* the user wants to do. | Determine *the parameters* of that action. |
| **Output** | A single categorical label (intent). | A set of (key, value) pairs. |
| **Example (flight)** | Intent = `book_flight` | Slots: `origin = Kochi`, `destination = Delhi`, `date = 2024-12-10`, `class = economy` |
| **Model** | Sentence-level classifier (BERT head). | Token-level sequence tagger (BIO scheme). |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1 (macro). | Token F1, slot precision/recall. |

**Examples:**
- Intent: User says *"I want to fly to Delhi tomorrow"* → intent = `book_flight`.
- Slot: From the same utterance → `destination = Delhi`, `date = tomorrow`.

**Model Solution to (b):**

The **Web Speech API** has two primary interfaces defined in the W3C specification:

1. **`SpeechRecognition`** — provides browser-native ASR. The web page instantiates a recognizer, attaches event listeners (`onresult`, `onerror`, `onend`), and starts/stops recognition on user gestures.

2. **`SpeechSynthesis`** — provides browser-native TTS. The page obtains a `SpeechSynthesisUtterance`, sets its `text`, `lang`, `rate`, `pitch`, and `volume`, and calls `speechSynthesis.speak(utterance)`.

**Algorithm (Pseudo-code):**

```
ALGORITHM: VoiceEchoWebPage
INPUT  : user speech from microphone
OUTPUT : synthesized speech echoing the recognized text

1.  IF browser does NOT support window.SpeechRecognition THEN
2.      DISPLAY "Voice not supported"
3.      RETURN
4.  END IF

5.  CREATE recognizer = new SpeechRecognition()
6.  SET recognizer.lang = "en-US"
7.  SET recognizer.interimResults = false
8.  SET recognizer.continuous = false

9.  DEFINE recognizer.onresult AS
10.     transcript ← event.results[0][0].transcript
11.     CALL speakText(transcript)
12. END

13. FUNCTION speakText(text)
14.     CREATE utterance = new SpeechSynthesisUtterance(text)
15.     SET utterance.lang = "en-US"
16.     SET utterance.rate = 1.0
17.     CALL window.speechSynthesis.speak(utterance)
18. END FUNCTION

19. // Triggered when user clicks a microphone button
20. ON button click DO
21.     CALL recognizer.start()
22. END
```

**Explanation:** Lines 1–4 check feature support. Lines 5–8 configure the recognizer for American English, final-only results, and single-shot mode. Lines 9–12 handle the recognition event by extracting the transcript and dispatching it to the TTS routine. Lines 13–18 synthesize the recognized text back to the user. Lines 20–22 wire the recognizer to a UI button. The end result is a complete **listen → recognize → speak** loop in a single HTML page.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **forget the denominator** in WER — they compute $S + D + I$ but omit dividing by $N$, costing 2 marks.
> - In Web Speech API questions, students often **confuse `SpeechRecognition` with `SpeechSynthesis`** — remember: Recognition = speech-to-text, Synthesis = text-to-speech.
> - In NLU questions, students frequently **swap the definitions** of intent and slots. Intent = *verb* (the action), slots = *nouns/parameters* (the entities).
> - For latency calculations, the unit must be **milliseconds (ms)**, not seconds. Mixing units is a 1-mark deduction.
> - Always include the **reference length $N$** explicitly when computing WER, SER, or TER — the examiner looks for it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **voice-based web service** exposes spoken-language I/O over the web, typically using the **W3C Web Speech API** or custom HTTPS endpoints.
- A **voice assistant** is an end-to-end conversational agent that fuses **ASR + NLU + Dialogue Management + Web Service Invocation + NLG + TTS**.
- The **six-layer pipeline**: Wake-Word → ASR → NLU → DM/API → NLG → TTS. Always diagram this in 14-mark architecture questions.
- **WER** = $(S + D + I) / N \times 100\%$ is the canonical ASR metric — remember the three edit operations and the normalization step.
- **Intent classification** = action label; **slot filling** = parameter extraction. Both are jointly modeled in modern NLU.
- **End-to-end latency** target is **< 800 ms**; budget ASR ~ 200 ms, NLU ~ 30 ms, API ~ 250 ms, TTS ~ 180 ms.
- **Web Speech API** interfaces: `SpeechRecognition` (ASR) and `SpeechSynthesis` (TTS); instantiate in JavaScript, wire to UI events.
- **Acoustic + Language Model** combination: $W^* = \arg\max_W \big[\log P(X \mid W) + \alpha \log P(W)\big]$ — the LM weight $\alpha$ is tuned for minimum WER.
- **Modern architectures**: Conformer (ASR), BERT (NLU), FastSpeech 2 + HiFi-GAN (TTS), RNN-T (streaming ASR).
- **Privacy & on-device processing**: wake-word detection must run locally; raw audio must not be uploaded without explicit user trigger.
- **Real-world deployments**: Google Assistant, Alexa, Siri, Cortana, in-car assistants, banking IVR, healthcare dictation, smart-home control.
- **Standards & protocols**: W3C Web Speech API, WebRTC for audio capture, WebSockets for streaming, REST/gRPC for service calls, JSON for payloads.
- **Evaluation metrics to memorize**: WER, SER, Intent Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, MOS, RTF, FAR, end-to-end latency.
